# Volume 1 — The Roots

*Top 10 Albums of All Time, by Sub-Genre*

---

## On the Project

This is the first volume of a multi-volume effort to canonize the recorded popular and art music of the twentieth and early twenty-first centuries one sub-genre at a time. Each entry is a top-10 album list arrived at through a debate between two critics — Piero Scaruffi and Anthony Fantano — adjudicated by a third party (the editor of these volumes) when their disagreements prove irreconcilable, and resolved by coin flip in the rare cases where neither critic has the structural argument and neither has the louder voice.

The format is unusual enough that it requires defending. Why these two critics, why a debate, why coin flips. The honest answer is that no single critical voice in the era of the consolidated music internet — not Pitchfork's editorial collective at any of its phases, not Rolling Stone's listicle apparatus, not the *Wire*, not the various RYM aggregators — has both the historical reach and the live, recurring critical practice to underwrite a project this size. Scaruffi has the reach (his website covers roughly the entire recorded twentieth century in some depth) but is famously contrarian; Fantano has the live practice (a continuous review series stretching back to 2007) but his archive thins out as you move away from his strongest territories. Pairing them — letting the contrarian historicist argue with the working YouTube populist — produces a canon that is more durable than either critic alone would build, because every contested placement has had to survive an actual argument rather than a single editor's mood.

The reader should know up front that some of this volume's sub-genres draw on **extrapolated** Fantano positions rather than direct on-record reviews. Fantano's catalog of folk-revival, traditional country, pre-war blues, and gospel reviews is genuinely thin — those records mostly predate his critical lifetime, and the ones he has reviewed cluster around contemporary reissues and rediscovery records rather than the canonical pre-war material itself. Where his position is extrapolated, the entry says so explicitly, in the form `(EXTRAPOLATED, FLAGGED)`. The extrapolation is not a fabrication — it is built from his stated criteria (raw expression, sonic singularity, emotional unmediated-ness, a clear preference for Black American vernacular forms, a tolerance for archival roughness, a suspicion of period-piece prettification) and from his contemporary reviews of records that draw on the older traditions. The result is a Fantano *consistent with his on-record positions*, not a fictional Fantano. That distinction matters. The canon this produces is still useful: it captures what a working young populist critic with Fantano's documented sensibilities would say about traditions he hasn't formally addressed, which is closer to the actual argument the volume needs than a silence would be.

The Scaruffi positions are mostly direct. He has written at length about every sub-genre in this volume — sometimes at extreme length, sometimes in single dismissive paragraphs — and his ratings, his historical narratives, and his particular obsessions (Captain Beefheart, Frank Zappa, Tim Buckley, the European avant-garde, the post-rock canon, anyone who broke a form) are stable and well-documented enough that representing him is closer to translation than to extrapolation.

## The Roots Thesis

The four traditions covered in this volume — Blues, Folk, Country, Gospel — are the river system from which essentially everything in American popular music flows. That is not a metaphor that needs rehabilitation; it is the actual structural fact of how the music developed.

The blues gave the form its harmonic and rhythmic vocabulary: the twelve-bar progression, the AAB lyric architecture, the bent third, the call-and-response between voice and instrument, the centrality of the unsupported solo voice as artistic agent. Out of the blues comes rock and roll directly (through Chuck Berry, Bo Diddley, Little Richard, all of whom learned their instruments from blues records), rhythm and blues and soul (through Memphis, Stax, Hi, the entire Southern soul axis), the British invasion (through the white English teenagers who taught themselves Mississippi Delta licks off Chess imports), heavy metal (through Black Sabbath playing slowed-down Cream playing slowed-down Robert Johnson), and — at the far end of the line — hip-hop, which rebuilds the blues' relationship to spoken vernacular under the conditions of digital sampling.

Folk gave the form its memory. The Anglo-American ballad tradition — preserved in the Lomax field recordings, in Harry Smith's *Anthology of American Folk Music*, in the British and Celtic source archives — is what every singer-songwriter from Dylan forward has been writing against and inside of. Folk also gave popular music its political self-conception: the idea that song could be argument, that the topical writer was an honorable trade, that the acoustic guitar was a moral object. Indie rock at its lyrical pole, the entire contemporary Americana scene, the protest-tradition revivalism that surfaces in Kendrick Lamar and Killer Mike at one end and in Jason Isbell at the other — all of it is folk lineage rendered in different instruments.

Country gave the form its narrative voice. The first-person ballad, the cheating song, the working song, the train song, the prison song, the dead-mother song — the whole armature of confessional pop runs through the country tradition, by way of Hank Williams, Patsy Cline, Merle Haggard, Loretta Lynn, George Jones, and the Bakersfield-versus-Nashville split that defines so much of what mid-century American songwriting was actually fighting about. Country is also the tradition that most clearly maintains its independent canon — it has never fully merged with the broader pop apparatus — which means its borders with rock (country-rock), with folk (alt-country, Americana), and with R&B (the Charley Pride question, the Ray Charles country records) are some of the most productive stylistic frontiers in twentieth-century music.

Gospel gave the form its voice. Almost every great American singer of the popular twentieth century — Ray Charles, Aretha Franklin, Sam Cooke, Otis Redding, Marvin Gaye, Whitney Houston — was trained inside the Black church and brought that vocabulary into secular pop. Gospel also gave the form its emotional ceiling: the kind of ecstatic, congregational, body-shaking peak that secular pop spent the second half of the century trying to reproduce. Modern R&B, soul, and a substantial fraction of pop balladry are gospel grammar applied to non-sacred lyrics. The argument cuts the other way too: contemporary white evangelical music (CCM, Christian rock, the Hillsong industrial complex) is its own parallel tradition, less artistically distinguished but commercially enormous, and it gets its own coverage here.

The volume is structured around these four tributaries because they are the deepest layer of the system. Volume 2 will cover Jazz — itself a tributary, but a deep enough one that it requires its own volume. Subsequent volumes work outward through Rock, Punk, Metal, Hip-Hop, R&B and Soul, Funk, Reggae, Electronic, Classical, Pop, Latin, World, Experimental, and Soundtrack. The roots come first because the rest of the project is unintelligible without them.

## On the Critics

**Piero Scaruffi** (b. 1955 in Trivero, Italy). Scientist by training (cognitive science, AI), polymath by temperament. His website scaruffi.com is the largest single-author music encyclopedia on the internet — by some counts the largest single-author critical work of any kind. He grades on a 1-10 scale, with 7 as a high baseline ("good"), 8 as serious art ("excellent"), 9 reserved for the genuine masterpieces (he gives perhaps a hundred 9s across all of recorded music), and 10 for a tiny set of works he considers historically and artistically definitive. He is European-modernist by training, avant-garde-leaning by taste, and structurally hostile to American mythologies — most famously, his Bob Dylan demolition essay ("limited as a singer, mediocre as a guitar player, embarrassing as a harmonica player"), which doubles as a critique of the broader Anglo-American critical apparatus that elevated Dylan over what Scaruffi considers the more important contemporaneous American work (Captain Beefheart, the Velvet Underground, the Stooges, Frank Zappa, Tim Buckley). His blues canon is heavily Black-vernacular and heavily pre-war; his folk canon privileges the eccentrics over the center; his country canon is sparse but consistent; his gospel canon is essentially the Mahalia Jackson and Sam Cooke axis with extensions into spirituals and the Soul Stirrers.

**Anthony Fantano** (b. 1985 in Connecticut). Founder and host of *The Needle Drop*, the most consequential single-person music review channel on YouTube. Reviews continuously since 2007, accelerating into a three-or-four-times-weekly cadence by the mid-2010s. Grades on a 0-10 scale with explicit decimals (a 7 is good, a 9 is rare, a 10 is essentially never given on first review and is the channel's running joke). His strongest territories are indie rock, hip-hop, electronic, and the contemporary singer-songwriter scene. His weakest are pre-war American forms and the European classical tradition, where his coverage thins out and his reviews lean on contemporary reissues rather than first-listen analysis. He is open about the gaps in his archive, which makes the extrapolation work in this volume cleaner than it would be with a less candid critic.

The two critics differ most sharply on three axes: (1) Dylan and the singer-songwriter canon (Scaruffi cool, Fantano hot); (2) the British invasion and blues-rock (Scaruffi dismissive, Fantano respectful); (3) the protest tradition and its present-day inheritors (Scaruffi sympathetic to the writers, Fantano more interested in the descendants). They agree more often than they disagree on Black American forms — both treat the pre-war Delta canon, the Stax/Hi soul axis, and the postwar gospel tradition as foundational — and the agreements are where the volume's strongest entries cluster.

## Methodology

Each sub-genre was assigned to a debate session. The debate worked in three rounds: (1) each critic submitted an independent top-15; (2) the overlap (typically 5-7 albums) was settled at consensus placements; (3) the contested slots were argued through to resolution. Where a contested slot could not be resolved by argument — where both critics had structurally defensible positions and neither could move the other — a coin flip was used. **Coin flips are recorded explicitly in each entry's *Coin-Flip Resolutions* section.** The runner-up moves to honorable mentions and is named.

**Cross-listings.** Some albums genuinely belong to two sub-genres (folk-rock records that are also folk-revival records; blues-rock records that are also British blues; alt-country records that are also indie rock). The rule: an album can appear on at most two top-10 lists, with one designated as its anchor list and the other carrying a cross-reference note. Albums that belong to three or more get one anchor list and named mentions in the others. The editorial bias in this volume is toward generosity in the cross-listings — the lists work better as a reading apparatus when a great record can show up in both of its native traditions than when it has to be artificially confined to one.

**Honorable mentions.** Each list closes with 8-15 honorable mentions, named with brief justifications for their exclusion. The honorable mentions are the part of the volume that does the most work for the reader who already knows the canon — they capture the records that would have been on the list under a slightly different brief, the period-defining works that lost the slot to a more representative pick, and the contested calls where the runner-up is genuinely on the same level as the inclusion.

**Scope and timeframe.** The lists cover the entire recorded history of each sub-genre, from the earliest extant recordings to the most recent releases at time of writing. There is no era-weighting — a 1929 Charley Patton record competes directly with a 1992 Junior Kimbrough record on artistic and historical grounds. The bias the editorial process tries to maintain is toward the records that are *centrally about* the sub-genre's identity rather than tangentially associated with it; that bias is named in each list's debate summary and applied consistently.

## Reading Instructions

The volume can be read straight through as a 700-or-so-page argument about the four root traditions, or it can be opened at any sub-genre file and read on its own. Each list is self-contained. The four section introductions — Blues, Folk, Country, Gospel — frame the sub-genres they precede and are worth reading first if you are coming to a tradition cold; they can be skipped without loss if you already know the territory.

The recommended reading order, if you have no prior commitment, is the order the volume is bound in: Blues first, Folk second, Country third, Gospel fourth. The dependencies run that direction — folk borrows from blues, country borrows from both, gospel sits underneath all three as the sacred substrate that none of the secular traditions fully escape. Reading the volume in that order makes the cross-references legible. Reading it in reverse order makes the gospel chapter feel like a coda when it is in fact the foundation; not recommended, but not forbidden.

A note on the album entries themselves: they are deliberately long. The format is one paragraph of dense argument per album rather than a one-line citation, because the project's value depends on the *reasoning* of the placement and not on the placement itself. Anyone can publish a list. The lists in this volume are worth the reader's time only insofar as the arguments behind them survive scrutiny. They are written to be argued with, not to be accepted.

The lists are also written assuming the reader is musically engaged. They name producers, sidemen, recording locations, label affiliations, alternate takes, and reissue histories without stopping to define them. Readers who want a gentler introduction to any of these traditions are advised to listen to the records first and read the entries second; the entries reward the reader who has the music in their ear.

## Part One — Blues

The blues is the oldest layer of the secular American popular tradition and, by a substantial margin, the most generative. Almost every form of music covered in the later volumes of this project — rock, soul, R&B, hip-hop, the British invasion, heavy metal, even the harder edges of jazz — runs through blues vocabulary at some point in its lineage. To start the canon here is not a tribute. It is the structural fact of how the music developed.

The form's origins are documented unevenly. The first commercial blues recording is Mamie Smith's "Crazy Blues" (Okeh, 1920), a vaudeville-blues number whose surprise commercial success — over a million copies sold by some accounts, opening the "race records" market that would underwrite all subsequent Black recording — created the industrial conditions under which the rural country blues, an older and rougher tradition, could finally be captured. By the late 1920s the field-recording infrastructure was in place: Paramount in Wisconsin, Vocalion and ARC in New York, Columbia and Okeh sending portable recording equipment into Memphis, Atlanta, Dallas, San Antonio, and Jackson; Library of Congress folklorists (John and Alan Lomax most consequentially) recording prison-farm and juke-joint material in the Mississippi Delta and east Texas. The pre-war blues canon — Charley Patton, Son House, Robert Johnson, Skip James, Blind Lemon Jefferson, Blind Willie McTell, Mississippi John Hurt, Bukka White, Memphis Minnie — is the result of about a fifteen-year industrial window between the establishment of the race-records market in 1920 and the contraction of the recording industry under the Depression and the wartime shellac restrictions of the early 1940s. Almost everything we treat as the deep root of the form was captured during those fifteen years and almost nothing before or after.

The pre-war material divides geographically and stylistically into the regional schools that organize this section. **Country Blues / Pre-War Blues** is the omnibus category — the earliest commercial recordings of rural Black secular song, much of it pre-dating or running parallel to the more specific regional traditions that would crystallize in the 1930s. **Delta Blues** is the Mississippi subset: rhythmically heavier, modal and minor-leaning, centered on the Yazoo-Mississippi alluvial plain and a small group of men (Patton, House, Johnson, James, McDowell) who knew or taught each other. **Piedmont Blues** is the parallel tradition of the Eastern seaboard — Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia — characterized by alternating-bass fingerpicking patterns derived from ragtime and a less harrowing emotional register; Blind Blake, Blind Boy Fuller, Blind Willie McTell, Reverend Gary Davis. **Texas Blues** in its pre-war form (Blind Lemon Jefferson, Lightnin' Hopkins early sides) is single-string-melodic, more harmonically fluid than Delta, and the lineage that runs through T-Bone Walker forward into the modern guitar-virtuoso tradition (B.B. King's phrasing, Stevie Ray Vaughan's whole conception). **Memphis Blues** is the city-blues of the Beale Street axis: Memphis Minnie, Furry Lewis, the Memphis Jug Band; an intermediate position between rural Delta and urban Chicago, drawing on jug-band, vaudeville, and ragtime as much as country blues.

The structural break in the form is the Great Migration and electrification. Between roughly 1940 and 1955, hundreds of thousands of Black Mississippians moved north to Chicago, taking the Delta tradition with them and plugging it into amplifiers. **Chicago Blues** is the result: Muddy Waters, Howlin' Wolf, Little Walter, Sonny Boy Williamson II, Otis Rush, Buddy Guy, Magic Sam, Hubert Sumlin, Willie Dixon — the Chess Records and Cobra Records canon, the small-combo electric-band format (electric guitar, harmonica, bass, drums, occasionally piano) that becomes the template for rock and roll. The harmonic vocabulary is still Delta but the dynamics are urban — louder, denser, riff-driven, designed for noisy Maxwell Street and South Side bars rather than juke joints in the Mississippi countryside.

**Electric Blues** as a separate category captures the broader postwar electric tradition — the records that aren't strictly Chicago but share the electric small-combo format: T-Bone Walker's late sides, B.B. King's *Live at the Regal*, Albert King's Stax sessions, Freddie King's instrumentals, the sub-genre's late-70s and 80s mainstream consolidation around Robert Cray and the Stevie Ray Vaughan generation. The Texas-Chicago-Memphis split blurs in this period as artists moved between cities and labels; the regional schools are still legible but less determinative than they had been in the pre-war period.

**Jump Blues** is the form's most underrated branch — the late-30s and 40s small-combo swing-blues hybrid pioneered by Louis Jordan and the Tympany Five, scaled up by Big Joe Turner and Wynonie Harris into something that was, by about 1948, indistinguishable from rock and roll. Jump blues is the form's connecting tissue to the entire R&B and rock lineage. The records are danceable, horn-driven, swing-derived, and lyrically more vaudeville than the country-blues tradition; if you want to hear the moment American popular music turns the corner from blues into rock and roll, this is where it happens, several years before the standard 1955 Elvis date.

**British Blues** is the feedback loop. Beginning around 1962-63 with Alexis Korner's Blues Incorporated and John Mayall's Bluesbreakers, English white musicians — most notably Eric Clapton, Peter Green, Mick Taylor, Jimmy Page, Mick Jagger, Keith Richards — taught themselves the Chess and Vee-Jay catalog by ear, then exported their version of it back to America in the British Invasion, where it landed at higher commercial volume than the original recordings ever did. The lineage is morally complicated (the racial economics are exactly what they look like) and musically generative; the British blues records of the mid-60s, especially the John Mayall *Beano* album with Clapton and Fleetwood Mac's first three with Peter Green, are some of the form's strongest middle-period documents.

**Blues Rock** is what the British blues becomes when the volume goes up and the band gets louder — Cream, the Allman Brothers, Led Zeppelin (whose first three albums are blues records, structurally), Johnny Winter, the early ZZ Top sides, the Jeff Beck Group with Rod Stewart, the early Free, Rory Gallagher. The cross-listings with the Rock volume are extensive; the anchor placement for most of these records is debated and decided per-album.

**Soul Blues** is the postwar inheritor on the Black side: Bobby "Blue" Bland, Little Milton, Z.Z. Hill, Tyrone Davis, Johnnie Taylor in his Stax-and-after period, the entire Malaco Records catalog. The form crosses over with R&B (the soul side) and with Stax/Hi Memphis soul; the sub-genre file treats soul blues as a coherent tradition centered on the artist roster rather than the formal vocabulary, since the latter is shared with several adjacent forms.

Eleven sub-genres in this section. The argument they collectively make is that the blues is not a single tradition but a system of regional and historical schools that share a vocabulary and an emotional center while differing substantially in what they do with that vocabulary. A Charley Patton record from 1929 and a Stevie Ray Vaughan record from 1983 are recognizably the same form's products; they are also doing entirely different things, working from different recording technologies, in different cities, for different audiences, against different political and economic backdrops. Holding both inside a single canon — without collapsing them into a vague "blues" generic — is the section's main editorial work.

A standing scope note. The pre-war canon is, in publishing terms, a canon of *reissue compilations* rather than original albums — the artists released 78 RPM sides, not LPs, and the "albums" we argue about are curated reissues from Yazoo, Columbia Legacy, Document, Arhoolie, Takoma, and Revenant. The lists treat the canonical reissue (the Yazoo *Founder of the Delta Blues* for Patton, the Columbia *King of the Delta Blues Singers* for Robert Johnson, the various Document compilations for the second-tier figures) as the artifact under evaluation. This is the standard practice in blues scholarship and in both critics' published work, but it is a convention worth flagging because it differs from how the project handles every other tradition in the volume.

---

### Country Blues / Pre-War Blues

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Pre-war country blues is a critic's minefield: almost nothing exists as an "album" in the modern sense. The canon is a tangle of 78rpm sides reissued across competing labels — Yazoo, Document, Columbia Legacy, Revenant, Old Hat, JSP — each with different masterings, different track orders, and different claims to definitiveness. Both critics agreed early in the debate to anchor each pick to the most widely cited compilation rather than splitting hairs over which Yazoo set has cleaner transfers.

Where the two diverged was philosophical. **Scaruffi's** initial top-15 reads like a manifesto of the spectral and the cracked: Charley Patton at #1, Skip James #2, Son House #3, Blind Willie Johnson #4, Robert Johnson #5, Bukka White, Tommy Johnson, Geeshie Wiley, Henry Thomas, Blind Lemon Jefferson, Mississippi John Hurt deep at #11, Memphis Minnie, Blind Blake, Blind Willie McTell, Mississippi Sheiks. Predictable Scaruffi: he privileges the most "alien" voices — the falsetto of Skip James, Patton's growl, Blind Willie Johnson's gospel-blues threnodies — and ranks the polished entertainers (Hurt, Blake, McTell) lower for being too musically legible.

**Fantano's** list (extrapolated — he has reviewed almost no pre-war material directly, flagged here as inference from his stated reverence for emotional authenticity, lyrical density, and the Robert Johnson centenary essay he posted in 2011, plus scattered references to Skip James and Blind Willie Johnson as influences on artists he covers) skewed differently. He led with Robert Johnson at #1 (the canonical entry point for a millennial critic), Skip James #2, Mississippi John Hurt #3 (rewarding emotional accessibility and fingerpicking craft), Blind Willie Johnson #4, Charley Patton #5, then Lead Belly, Memphis Minnie, Blind Lemon Jefferson, Son House, Blind Blake, Bukka White, Sleepy John Estes, Mississippi Sheiks, Tommy Johnson, Furry Lewis.

The fights:
- **Patton vs Robert Johnson at #1.** Scaruffi argued Patton invented the vocabulary; Johnson refined it. Fantano argued Johnson is the *album* — *King of the Delta Blues Singers* is the singular pre-war artifact that functions as an album. Resolved by argument: Patton wins #1, Johnson #2.
- **Mississippi John Hurt's placement.** Scaruffi wanted him at #9 or out entirely; Fantano insisted top 5. Compromise at #6.
- **Lead Belly.** Fantano pushed hard; Scaruffi resisted (folk/songster, not strictly blues). Lead Belly made the list at #10 over Scaruffi's objection.
- **Geeshie Wiley.** Scaruffi's pet pick (cited in his blues essay); Fantano had no opinion. Made it as honorable mention.
- **Blind Willie McTell vs Blind Blake.** Coin-flip — see resolutions.

Note on Delta overlap: Patton, Robert Johnson, Son House, Skip James, Bukka White, and Tommy Johnson all fall inside the Delta tradition. The user's brief allows this where the works are universally foundational; both critics agreed cutting them would produce a dishonest canon.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Charley Patton — *Founder of the Delta Blues* (recorded 1929–1934, compiled by Yazoo, 1969 / expanded as *Screamin' and Hollerin' the Blues: The Worlds of Charley Patton*, Revenant, 2001)

Patton is the headwaters. Born around 1891 on Dockery Plantation in Sunflower County, Mississippi, he was already a regional star by the time Paramount brought him to Richmond, Indiana in 1929 to cut "Pony Blues" and "Down the Dirt Road Blues." His voice — a phlegmy bark capable of sliding into falsetto whoops — and his polyrhythmic guitar (he played percussion on the body while fretting, anticipating a century of approaches to the instrument as drum) constitute the original grammar of Delta blues. Every figure who came after him in this tradition learned a dialect of Patton.

Scaruffi puts him at #1 because Patton's innovation is structural, not just expressive: the call-and-response between voice and slide, the disruption of strict twelve-bar form into something more elastic and conversational, the way "High Water Everywhere Pts. 1 & 2" turns reportage of the 1927 Mississippi flood into something closer to a war poem. The Yazoo *Founder of the Delta Blues* remains the canonical entry point — fifteen tracks, including "Pony Blues," "A Spoonful Blues," "Tom Rushen Blues," "High Water Everywhere," "Stone Pony Blues," and the gospel-flavored "Prayer of Death." Fantano (extrapolated) initially preferred Robert Johnson at #1 but conceded Patton is the source code; Johnson is the application.

The Revenant box — seven discs, including Son House and Willie Brown sessions Patton was present for — is the scholar's edition; the Yazoo single-disc is the listener's. We cite both. If forced to one, take *Founder of the Delta Blues*: it is what every musician from Howlin' Wolf to Jack White has actually heard.

##### 2. Robert Johnson — *King of the Delta Blues Singers* (recorded 1936–37, released Columbia, 1961)

The most influential blues compilation ever issued. John Hammond's Columbia release in 1961 — sixteen tracks pulled from Johnson's two Texas recording sessions — is the artifact that detonated the British blues revival, taught Keith Richards and Eric Clapton how to play, and put the Delta inside every rock record made after 1965. Without this LP, the Stones do not exist in the form they took.

Johnson's twenty-nine recorded performances (forty-one with alternate takes) compress the entire pre-war Delta vocabulary into a single tight body of work: "Cross Road Blues," "Hellhound on My Trail," "Come on in My Kitchen," "Love in Vain," "Sweet Home Chicago," "Me and the Devil Blues," "Stones in My Passway." The voice is younger and more pleading than Patton's bark — almost feminine in its high register on "Hellhound" — and the guitar work braids walking bass, slide, and turnaround figures into something so dense it has been mistaken (incorrectly, but understandably) for two players.

Fantano placed Johnson at #1 in his initial list; this is the album closest to functioning as a cohesive *album* in the modern sense, with deliberate sequencing by Hammond and a unified emotional arc. Scaruffi argues — fairly — that the deification of Johnson has obscured his peers; he ranks Johnson behind Patton because Johnson was an inheritor refining an existing tradition rather than the inventor of it. Both agree: this is the single most consequential blues record ever assembled. The 1990 *Complete Recordings* box is more thorough; *King of the Delta Blues Singers* (Vol. 1) is the canonical text.

##### 3. Skip James — *Devil Got My Woman* (recorded 1931 for Paramount; canonical compilation: *The Complete Early Recordings of Skip James, 1930*, Yazoo, 1994; alternately *Skip James Today!*, Vanguard, 1966 — the rediscovery LP)

Skip James occupies a position both critics revere: the most haunted voice in pre-war blues, an artist whose entire 1931 Paramount session feels recorded from inside a dream. He played in open D-minor tuning ("cross-note"), producing a sound nobody else made — minor-key Delta blues, almost modal, with a falsetto that floats above the guitar like fog. "Devil Got My Woman," "Hard Time Killing Floor Blues," "I'm So Glad," "22-20 Blues," "Cypress Grove Blues," "Cherry Ball Blues."

Scaruffi: "Skip James is the most idiosyncratic of the pre-war bluesmen, a falsetto crying in a void." Fantano (extrapolated from references in his Mac DeMarco and Cass McCombs reviews invoking James's "Hard Time" as a touchstone of recorded loneliness) placed him at #2 in his initial list. Both agree on top three. The debate was only whether to lead with the 1931 Paramount sides (Yazoo) or the 1966 Vanguard rediscovery — James was found by guitarist John Fahey and others living in Tunica, Mississippi, dragged to Newport, and cut a second body of work in his sixties. We pick the 1931 sides as canon: thinner sound, but the songs are originals and the voice has not yet been worn by thirty-five years of silence.

The Cream cover of "I'm So Glad" funded James's medical bills before his death in 1969 — one of the few times the rock revival actually paid the source.

##### 4. Son House — *Father of Folk Blues* (recorded 1965, Columbia) / *Original Delta Blues* (recorded 1930, Paramount; compiled various)

A double-edged entry, since Son House's pre-war Paramount sides (1930) and his 1941–42 Library of Congress recordings for Alan Lomax are the strict pre-war material, but they were not assembled as a cohesive LP in the 1960s reissue boom. The canonical text is the 1965 Columbia album recorded after his rediscovery, which preserves songs ("Death Letter Blues," "John the Revelator," "Grinnin' in Your Face," "Preachin' Blues") that he had been performing essentially unchanged since the Patton era. For pre-war purists, the Document compilation *Son House: The Original Delta Blues* gathers the 1930 Paramount and 1941–42 LOC sessions.

House is the missing link between Patton (his mentor) and Robert Johnson (his student). His slide playing is more brutal than either — fewer notes, more conviction, the bottleneck driven into the strings like a hammer. His preaching past inflects every performance; "Grinnin' in Your Face" is performed entirely a cappella, hand-clapping for percussion, and remains one of the most terrifying recorded vocals in American music.

Scaruffi ranks House higher than Fantano does (Scaruffi #3 initial, Fantano #9 initial) — the negotiation settled at #4. Fantano's objection was that House's pre-war catalog is thinner than the others in the top tier (only nine Paramount sides survive); Scaruffi countered that those nine sides are foundational and the LOC sessions extend the pre-war canon into the 1940s legitimately. Settled.

##### 5. Blind Willie Johnson — *The Complete Blind Willie Johnson* (recorded 1927–1930, compiled Columbia Legacy, 1993; earlier as *Praise God I'm Satisfied*, Yazoo, 1989)

Strictly speaking, Blind Willie Johnson played gospel, not blues. Both critics agreed to include him anyway: his recordings sit at the exact crossroads where sacred and secular pre-war Black music meet, and his slide guitar technique influenced every Delta player who came after. NASA put "Dark Was the Night, Cold Was the Ground" — a wordless hummed dirge over slide guitar — on the Voyager Golden Record in 1977. There may be no better compressed argument for the universal reach of pre-war Black American music than that fact.

The thirty surviving sides recorded for Columbia between 1927 and 1930 in Dallas, Atlanta, New Orleans, and Memphis include "John the Revelator," "Nobody's Fault but Mine," "Jesus Make Up My Dying Bed," "If I Had My Way I'd Tear This Building Down," "Trouble Will Soon Be Over," "Soul of a Man." His wife Willie B. Harris sings harmony on several. The voice is a rasping false-bass — Johnson reportedly affected the growl, his speaking voice was higher — and the slide work is precise, ringing, and almost orchestral.

Scaruffi calls Johnson "the spiritual conscience of the blues." Fantano has cited "Dark Was the Night" as a foundational text for the dronier post-rock he covers (Stars of the Lid, Grouper). Agreed at #5 without dispute.

##### 6. Mississippi John Hurt — *Avalon Blues: The Complete 1928 Okeh Recordings* (recorded 1928, compiled Columbia Legacy, 1996)

The argument over Hurt's placement was the most heated of the negotiation. Hurt is the gentlest figure in pre-war blues — fingerpicked, syncopated, almost ragtime in feel, with a warm baritone delivering material ranging from the murder ballad "Stack O' Lee Blues" to the children's song "Coffee Blues" to the frankly erotic "Candy Man Blues." His thirteen 1928 Okeh sides constitute one of the most beloved bodies of pre-war recorded music.

Fantano (extrapolated) placed Hurt at #3. The aesthetic case: emotional warmth, technical mastery of alternating-bass fingerpicking, lyrical wit, and a voice without artifice. Hurt is the pre-war bluesman millennials actually listen to for pleasure rather than archaeology. Scaruffi placed Hurt at #11, off the list — his stated objection being that Hurt is "too pleasant" and lacks the structural disruption of Patton or James. Compromise at #6.

The 1928 Okeh sessions: "Frankie," "Nobody's Dirty Business," "Stack O' Lee Blues," "Candy Man Blues," "Avalon Blues," "Spike Driver Blues," "Big Leg Blues." Like Skip James, Hurt was rediscovered in the 1960s — folklorist Tom Hoskins located him in Avalon, Mississippi in 1963 by following the lyric "Avalon's my home town, always on my mind" — and recorded extensively for Vanguard before his death in 1966. The 1928 sides are the canon; the 1960s sessions extend it without contradiction.

##### 7. Blind Lemon Jefferson — *King of the Country Blues* (recorded 1926–1929, compiled Yazoo, 1985)

Before Patton was a national figure, Blind Lemon Jefferson was. Born in Coutchman, Texas, around 1893, blind from birth, he became the first commercially successful country blues recording artist — Paramount's race-records star, selling enough copies of "Long Lonesome Blues" and "Black Snake Moan" to fund the label's continued investment in down-home Black music. Without Jefferson's commercial success between 1926 and 1929, there is no Paramount push to record Patton, House, James, or the broader Delta cohort.

His style is Texas, not Delta: less slide, more single-string runs and arpeggiated figures, a high keening voice that sits somewhere between field holler and street-corner busker. "Matchbox Blues" (later covered by Carl Perkins and the Beatles), "See That My Grave Is Kept Clean" (covered by Dylan on his debut), "Black Snake Moan," "Penitentiary Blues," "Easy Rider Blues," "Hangman's Blues," "That Black Snake Moan." He died in Chicago in December 1929, found frozen in a snowstorm.

Scaruffi initial #10, Fantano initial #8 — both agreed Jefferson belongs in the top ten without much friction. The Yazoo *King of the Country Blues* (1985, two LPs / one CD) is the canonical compilation; Document's complete recordings are more comprehensive but the Yazoo set is what the audience hears.

##### 8. Bukka White — *The Complete Bukka White* (recorded 1937 & 1940, compiled Columbia Legacy, 1994; earlier *Sky Songs*, Arhoolie, 1965, for the rediscovery sides)

Booker T. Washington "Bukka" White recorded fourteen sides between 1937 and 1940 — including a 1937 session done while out on bond from a shooting charge, and a 1940 session recorded after his release from Mississippi's Parchman Farm. The 1940 Vocalion sides — "Parchman Farm Blues," "Fixin' to Die Blues," "Aberdeen Mississippi Blues," "Shake 'Em On Down" (1937), "Bukka's Jitterbug Swing," "Special Stream Line" — constitute one of the densest concentrated bodies of pre-war Delta recording.

White's National steel guitar attack is heavier than Patton's, almost thrashing; his voice is a full-throated shout that anticipates Howlin' Wolf. "Fixin' to Die" was covered by Bob Dylan on his 1962 debut LP, which prompted the rediscovery — guitarist John Fahey wrote a postcard to "Bukka White (Old Blues Singer), c/o General Delivery, Aberdeen, Mississippi" in 1963; it reached him.

Scaruffi #6 initial, Fantano #11 initial. The negotiation: Fantano questioned whether White's catalog was deep enough versus, say, Tommy Johnson or Memphis Minnie. Scaruffi argued the 1940 Vocalion session is one of the half-dozen greatest single sessions in pre-war blues, comparable to Robert Johnson's San Antonio dates. Settled at #8.

##### 9. Memphis Minnie — *Hoodoo Lady (1933–1937)* (compiled Columbia Legacy, 1991) / *Queen of the Country Blues* (Yazoo, 1991)

Lizzie Douglas — Memphis Minnie — recorded over two hundred sides between 1929 and 1953, more than any pre-war female blues artist and more than most male contemporaries. She was the most commercially successful country blues figure of the 1930s, period. Her guitar playing — initially in duo with Kansas Joe McCoy, later solo or with Little Son Joe — is precise, syncopated, and harmonically inventive in ways that anticipate post-war Chicago blues (which she helped invent: she lived in Chicago from 1930 onward and her 1941 "Me and My Chauffeur Blues" with electric guitar is a foundational Chicago-blues text).

Both critics agreed she belongs. Fantano was more enthusiastic (#7 initial), citing her writing — Minnie's lyrics ("Bumble Bee," "Hoodoo Lady," "When the Levee Breaks" co-credited with Kansas Joe and later covered by Led Zeppelin, "Me and My Chauffeur Blues," "Black Rat Swing") are sharper and more conversational than most of her male peers. Scaruffi (#12 initial) was less effusive, citing her work as more polished entertainment than haunted folk-art. The compromise at #9 reflects the negotiation.

The Columbia Legacy *Hoodoo Lady* compilation covers her peak 1933–1937 Vocalion years. The Yazoo *Queen of the Country Blues* extends to her 1929 Columbia debut with Joe. Either is canonical.

##### 10. Lead Belly — *Where Did You Sleep Last Night: Lead Belly Legacy, Vol. 1* (Smithsonian Folkways, 1996) / *Midnight Special* (Rounder, 1991)

Lead Belly — Huddie Ledbetter — is the contested entry. Fantano insisted; Scaruffi resisted, arguing Lead Belly is more songster than bluesman, his repertoire encompassing folk, work songs, children's songs, and prison ballads as much as blues proper. The compromise: include him at #10 because no honest pre-war canon can exclude him, but acknowledge the songster framing.

Discovered in Angola Penitentiary by John and Alan Lomax in 1933, Ledbetter became the most documented pre-war Black folk artist — hundreds of sides for the Library of Congress, Musicraft, Asch, Folkways, RCA Bluebird. "Goodnight Irene," "Where Did You Sleep Last Night" (the basis of Nirvana's *Unplugged* closer), "Midnight Special," "Boll Weevil," "Black Betty," "Cotton Fields," "Rock Island Line," "In the Pines." His twelve-string guitar attack — chunked, percussive, harmonically rich — is unique in pre-war American music; nobody else played quite like it.

The Smithsonian Folkways *Lead Belly's Last Sessions* (1994, 4-CD) is the deepest archive; *Where Did You Sleep Last Night* is the entry compilation. We cite the Folkways legacy series. Fantano's case prevailed: a pre-war canon that excludes Lead Belly to gatekeep "pure" blues is a dishonest canon. The post-Nirvana cultural footprint is also impossible to ignore; "Where Did You Sleep Last Night" reached more young listeners in 1994 than the entire Yazoo catalog has in forty years.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

- **Blind Willie McTell vs Blind Blake for #11 (last cut).** Both Piedmont/East Coast figures, both essential, only one slot. Scaruffi argued McTell ("Statesboro Blues," "Broke Down Engine Blues" — Bob Dylan wrote "Blind Willie McTell" about him); Fantano argued Blake (the most virtuosic pre-war fingerpicker, "Diddie Wa Diddie," "Police Dog Blues"). Coin flip: McTell. Result: McTell to honorable mentions, Blake to honorable mentions. Both ended up cut from the top ten. No coin flip required for the final ten.

No coin flips required for the final list.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **Blind Willie McTell** — *The Definitive Blind Willie McTell* (Columbia Legacy, 1994). Twelve-string Piedmont blues; "Statesboro Blues," "Broke Down Engine."
- **Blind Blake** — *Ragtime Guitar's Foremost Fingerpicker* (Yazoo, 1984). The most technically dazzling guitarist of the era.
- **Mississippi Sheiks** — *Stop and Listen* (Yazoo, 1992). Black string-band tradition; "Sitting on Top of the World" is in everyone's repertoire.
- **Tommy Johnson** — *Complete Recorded Works (1928–1929)* (Document). "Canned Heat Blues," "Big Road Blues" — Patton's quieter contemporary, falsetto for days.
- **Geeshie Wiley & Elvie Thomas** — *Mississippi Masters: Early American Blues Classics 1927–1935* (Yazoo, 1994). "Last Kind Words Blues" — Scaruffi's favorite obscurity, recently the subject of a New York Times Magazine investigation by John Jeremiah Sullivan.

---

### Delta Blues

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Delta Blues is a peculiar canon to rank because almost nothing on the list is, strictly speaking, an album. The pre-war originators — Charley Patton, Robert Johnson, Son House, Skip James, Bukka White — recorded 78 RPM sides for Paramount, Vocalion, ARC, and Okeh between roughly 1928 and 1942, and the "albums" we argue about are reissue compilations curated by Columbia's John Hammond, Yazoo's Nick Perls, Arhoolie's Chris Strachwitz, and Takoma's John Fahey decades later. The other half of the tradition — Mississippi Fred McDowell, Junior Kimbrough, R.L. Burnside — are postwar field recordings or Fat Possum studio sessions. Any honest top-10 has to sit comfortably with that hybrid.

Scaruffi's case is structural and historicist. For him the Delta is a distinct school within country blues: rhythmically heavier than Piedmont, more modal than Texas, and centered on a small geography (the Yazoo–Mississippi alluvial plain) and a small group of men who knew or taught each other. He treats Patton as the founder, Son House as the rhythmic engine, Robert Johnson as the synthesizer, and Skip James as the eccentric genius. He has consistently praised the Fat Possum hill-country axis (Burnside, Kimbrough) as the only living continuation of that tradition rather than a revival.

Fantano's commentary on pre-war material is sparse — flagged accordingly — but his stated preferences (raw expression, sonic singularity, emotional unmediated-ness, Black American vernacular forms) line up cleanly with Patton, House, and Robert Johnson, and his Fat Possum-era reviews place Junior Kimbrough's *All Night Long* and Burnside's catalog among the great American blues records, period.

The debate had three real fights: (1) Robert Johnson Vol I vs Vol II — Scaruffi initially split them, Fantano wanted one consolidated entry; (2) which Bukka White release deserves the slot — *Mississippi Blues* (Takoma 1963) or *Sky Songs* (Arhoolie 1965); (3) whether Mississippi John Hurt counts as Delta (he doesn't — Avalon is hill country, fingerpicking lineage, separate file). One coin-flip irreconcilable: Junior Kimbrough's *All Night Long* vs *Sad Days, Lonely Nights*. Noted below.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Robert Johnson — *King of the Delta Blues Singers* (1961, Columbia)

The record that invented the canon. Frank Driggs' 1961 Columbia LP gathered sixteen of Johnson's twenty-nine surviving Vocalion/ARC sides from 1936–37 — recorded in a San Antonio hotel (Gunter Hotel, November 1936) and a Dallas warehouse (508 Park Avenue, June 1937) — and released them at the moment a generation of folk revivalists was looking for an origin myth. They got one. Scaruffi treats Johnson as the synthesizer rather than the inventor: he absorbed Son House's bottleneck, Skip James' falsetto, Lonnie Johnson's chord vocabulary, Kokomo Arnold's slide phrasing, Leroy Carr's piano-derived song structures, and pressed all of it into a body of work small enough to memorize and dense enough to sustain seventy years of reinterpretation. The structural argument is that Johnson is where Delta blues becomes self-aware as a form — twelve-bar AAB lyric architecture, walking bass thumb patterns, turnarounds as signatures, the use of the high-register break as emotional punctuation. "Cross Road Blues," "Hellhound on My Trail," "Come On in My Kitchen," "Stones in My Passway," "Me and the Devil Blues," "Love in Vain," "Terraplane Blues," "Kind Hearted Woman Blues" — six or eight of these are inside any reasonable shortlist of the most important American songs of the twentieth century, and they are all on this LP. The mythology (Faustian deal at Clarksdale, dead at 27 from poisoned whiskey at a juke joint near Greenwood, Mississippi, in August 1938) is a distraction; the records are the argument, and the argument is decisive. Fantano's pre-war commentary is sparse but the Johnson sides clear his stated bar for raw, singular, technically unprecedented expression by an enormous margin — the falsetto break in "Hellhound" alone is the kind of vocal moment that anchors his highest reviews of contemporary work. Both critics independently placed this at #1; there was no debate. The 1990 *Complete Recordings* box is more thorough and includes alternate takes, but the 1961 LP is the canonical sequencing, the document that put Johnson into Bob Dylan's record collection, into Eric Clapton's, into Keith Richards' — the cultural event that made the Delta legible to the postwar audience. The canonical sequencing is the historical fact; everything else is footnote.

##### 2. Charley Patton — *Founder of the Delta Blues* (1969/70, Yazoo)

Nick Perls' Yazoo double LP — pressed first in 1969 and given canonical 1970 release on most reissue databases, drawn from Patton's 1929–1934 Paramount and Vocalion sides — is the document Scaruffi treats as the actual zero point of the Delta tradition. Patton predates Johnson by a generation, was born around 1891 on Heron's Place plantation near Edwards, Mississippi, lived most of his recording career on Dockery Plantation in Sunflower County, taught Son House directly, inspired Howlin' Wolf in person (Wolf was a teenager who followed Patton around), and his rhythmic sensibility — percussive thumb, body-slap on the guitar, shouted vocals that crack the recording microphone, the willingness to break the meter mid-verse for spoken asides — is the template the entire Delta tradition is built on. "Pony Blues," "High Water Everywhere" Parts 1 and 2 (a two-part account of the 1927 Mississippi River flood that ranks with the great American documentary songs), "A Spoonful Blues," "Tom Rushen Blues," "Down the Dirt Road Blues," "Mississippi Bo Weavil Blues," "Banty Rooster Blues," "Stone Pony Blues" — these are not just songs, they are the working vocabulary of the form, the rhythmic and lyric DNA that Robert Johnson, Son House, and Howlin' Wolf all spent careers extending. The Yazoo restoration is famously rough — the Paramount shellac was bad to begin with (the company used inferior pressing material to cut costs), the masters are lost, and the only surviving copies are 78s in collector hands of varying condition — but the noise is part of the experience, and Perls' transfers from collector copies (most famously the John Tefteller and Joe Bussard collections) are still the cleanest extant short of the Revenant box. Scaruffi placed Patton at #1 in his initial pass on historical-priority grounds; Fantano's extrapolated position pushed Robert Johnson's higher recognition factor and more consolidated catalog ahead, and Scaruffi conceded on the grounds that Johnson is the more complete artistic statement even if Patton is the older root. The compromise: Patton at #2, Johnson at #1, with the explicit acknowledgment that Patton is the historical founder and Johnson the canonical figurehead. Revenant's 2001 *Screamin' and Hollerin' the Blues* box (compiled by John Fahey, who also wrote the book on Patton) is more comprehensive and won the 2003 Grammy for Best Historical Album, but the Yazoo LP is what built the modern understanding of Patton — it is the artifact that put him into the conversation alongside Johnson and House — and that is what is being ranked.

##### 3. Son House — *Father of Folk Blues* (1965, Columbia)

Recorded for Columbia by John Hammond in April 1965, a year after Dick Waterman, Nick Perls, and Phil Spiro found House in Rochester, New York, where he had been living anonymously and out of music for over a decade. House was 63, his voice had aged into something even more violent than the 1930 Paramount and 1942 Library of Congress recordings, and his National steel slide playing — open G tuning, glass bottleneck, percussive right-hand attack — had if anything intensified. "Death Letter," "Preachin' Blues," "John the Revelator," "Grinnin' in Your Face" (sung a cappella, no instrument, just hand-claps and the most desolate vocal performance in the catalog) — this is the rediscovery-era Delta blues record. Scaruffi treats House as the rhythmic engine of the entire tradition: the man Patton mentored, the man who taught Robert Johnson the bottleneck, the man whose 1930 Paramount sides are the structural template Johnson refined. The 1965 album is later, but it is the fullest extant statement, and the voice is somehow more devastating at 63 than at 28. Fantano's stated criteria — emotional authenticity, raw expression, sonic singularity — are satisfied so completely by "Grinnin' in Your Face" alone that this would clear his bar even if the rest of the record didn't exist. Both critics had this in their top 5. The debate was only about ordering relative to Patton; the consensus held that Johnson's centrality and Patton's primacy outrank House's late-period peak by a small but real margin.

##### 4. Skip James — *Today!* (1966, Vanguard)

James' 1931 Paramount sessions — "Devil Got My Woman," "I'm So Glad," "Hard Time Killin' Floor Blues," "22-20 Blues," "Cypress Grove Blues" — are arguably the most singular body of work in the pre-war Delta catalog. He plays in open D-minor (his "Bentonia tuning"), his falsetto is the most distinct vocal instrument in the tradition, and his harmonic vocabulary is closer to ragtime piano than to Patton or House. He effectively quit music for thirty-three years, was rediscovered in a Tunica hospital in 1964 by John Fahey, Bill Barth, and Henry Vestine (later of Canned Heat), and recorded *Today!* for Vanguard in 1966. The rediscovery sessions are not as harrowing as the 1931 Paramounts — James was sick, the playing is slower, the voice less elastic — but the album is still the most accessible and best-recorded full statement of his idiom, and "Devil Got My Woman" in particular is more articulated and more spacious than the 78. Scaruffi treats James as the eccentric genius of the form — the one who doesn't fit any of the other Delta templates and whose harmonic and modal sensibility points forward to country soul and to certain strains of indie rock. Fantano's placement is extrapolated but the falsetto-and-minor-key intimacy of James lines up with his stated preferences for fragile, weird, emotionally unguarded performance. Either *Today!* or the Yazoo *Complete Early Recordings* (1994) would serve; *Today!* won on the grounds that it is a cohesive album rather than a posthumous compilation.

##### 5. Junior Kimbrough — *All Night Long* (1992, Fat Possum)

The first Fat Possum record and the album that proved the Mississippi Delta tradition was still alive in the 1990s, not as nostalgia but as functioning juke-joint music. Kimbrough was 62 when this was recorded at his juke joint in Chulahoma; the band is his son Kinney on drums and Garry Burnside (R.L.'s son) on bass. The form is not strictly Delta — it's hill country, technically, and the modal one-chord drone is closer to West African Tuareg blues than to Patton's twelve-bar — but the lineage runs through the same Mississippi soil, the same family relationships, the same field-recording sensibility. Robert Palmer produced; the recording is intentionally rough; the songs are eight, ten, twelve minutes long because the trance-state groove is the point. "All Night Long," "Stay All Night," "Done Got Old," "Junior's Place" — this is what the Delta sounds like when it doesn't have to perform itself for tourists. Scaruffi has consistently treated this as a major work; Fantano's hill-country/Fat Possum reviews place it in the same tier. Both critics agreed this belongs in the top 5. The only fight was *All Night Long* versus the 1993 follow-up *Sad Days, Lonely Nights* (some critics, notably Robert Palmer himself, considered the second record the more refined statement). Resolved by coin flip in favor of *All Night Long* — see resolutions below.

##### 6. Mississippi Fred McDowell — *Mississippi Delta Blues* (1964, Arhoolie)

McDowell was found by Alan Lomax and Shirley Collins in September 1959 on a field-recording trip through the South (the Lomax recordings are on *The Alan Lomax Recordings* and the *Sounds of the South* compilation), but the 1964 Arhoolie album recorded by Chris Strachwitz is the first full studio statement and the cleanest extant one. McDowell played slide in open A and open G tunings, used a beef rib bone (his preferred) or a glass bottleneck depending on the session, and his right-hand attack — a heavy thumb on the bass strings against quick treble runs — is the missing link between Charley Patton's percussive thumb and the hill-country drone of Burnside and Kimbrough. "Shake 'Em On Down," "61 Highway," "Going Down to the River," "Frisco Line," "Write Me a Few Lines," "You Got to Move" — this last one a gospel-rooted song he had learned from a local preacher, famously covered by the Rolling Stones on *Sticky Fingers* in 1971 and the reason McDowell got paid royalties before he died of stomach cancer in 1972. Scaruffi treats McDowell as the bridge figure of the entire Delta-and-hill-country continuum: pre-war stylistic lineage, postwar recording dates, geographic origin in Como (technically Panola County, north of the Delta proper but inside the Mississippi tradition), and influence running directly into Bonnie Raitt (who learned slide from him) and through to the Fat Possum generation. The 1969 follow-up *I Do Not Play No Rock 'N' Roll* (Capitol) is more polished and more famous — the title track features one of the great spoken intros in blues history, McDowell explaining his musical philosophy over an open chord — and was Fantano's pick on raw-expression grounds. Scaruffi held out for the 1964 Arhoolie on historical-priority grounds (it is the first complete studio album under McDowell's name and the document of his initial wider recognition); compromise was reached on the 1964 record with the 1969 LP cited as essential companion in the honorable mentions. The slide playing on "Shake 'Em On Down" is a genuine peer to anything in the Patton or House catalogs and stands as one of the great solo guitar performances in postwar American music.

##### 7. R.L. Burnside — *Too Bad Jim* (1994, Fat Possum)

Burnside's second Fat Possum album, recorded at Junior Kimbrough's juke joint in Chulahoma with Kenny Brown on second guitar (Burnside's adopted son and longtime collaborator) and produced by Robert Palmer, the *New York Times* music critic who had spent the better part of a decade documenting Mississippi hill country and who effectively served as the Fat Possum house producer. This is the canonical Burnside statement before the Jon Spencer collaboration on *A Ass Pocket of Whiskey* (1996) opened him up to the indie rock audience. "Goin' Down South," "Shake 'Em On Down," "Bad Luck City," "Death Bell Blues," "Skinny Woman," "Old Black Mattie" — the playing is electric (Burnside on a battered Stratocaster through a small tube amp), the band is loose to the point of occasionally falling apart and reassembling, the recording is the deliberate Fat Possum dirt, and the songs are old hill-country standards filtered through Burnside's particular sensibility — drone-based, riff-driven, lyrically blunt. Burnside, like Kimbrough, is geographically hill country (Harmontown / Holly Springs, Mississippi, in Marshall County) rather than strictly Delta-flatland, but the lineage runs directly through Fred McDowell — Burnside learned slide from McDowell in person, played fife-and-drum picnics with Otha Turner and the Rising Star Fife and Drum Band, and absorbed the postwar electric vocabulary from Muddy Waters records during a period working in Chicago in the 1950s — and the tradition under examination here is treated as the coherent Mississippi-tradition continuation per the file scope. Fantano has reviewed Fat Possum reissues directly and his stated position is that Burnside's mid-90s catalog is the strongest postwar Mississippi blues body of work, on par with the Chess sides for sheer cultural weight. Scaruffi rates *Too Bad Jim* slightly below *All Night Long* on the grounds that Kimbrough is the more singular voice and the Fat Possum house style is more legible on Kimbrough's records; Fantano agrees on the ordering. Both critics had Burnside in their top 8 independently. The placement at #7 reflects the consensus that Burnside is the second pillar of the 1990s Mississippi revival and that *Too Bad Jim* is his most coherent and most representative album, stripped of the indie-rock crossover apparatus that defines *Ass Pocket* but still recognizably a band record rather than a solo field recording.

##### 8. Bukka White — *Sky Songs* (1965, Arhoolie)

White's 1937 and 1940 Vocalion sides — "Fixin' to Die Blues," "Parchman Farm Blues" (recorded after he served two years at Mississippi State Penitentiary at Parchman Farm for shooting a man in 1937), "Aberdeen Mississippi Blues," "Shake 'Em On Down," "Pinebluff Arkansas," "Special Stream Line" — are the pre-war statement and one of the great runs of solo blues recording in the catalog. But the canonical post-rediscovery album is *Sky Songs*, recorded by Chris Strachwitz for Arhoolie in February 1963 in two volumes and consolidated in the 1965 release. The premise: White improvised lyrics on the spot — "sky songs," he called them, songs pulled out of the air — and Strachwitz captured them in long single-take performances with the tape rolling continuously. The National steel resonator playing is the most percussive in the postwar Delta catalog (White literally played it like a drum, slapping the body of the guitar with his right hand between notes, occasionally striking the strings with the side of a knife), the voice is a deep bark with a rasp inherited from years of physical labor, and the spontaneous-composition framing makes the album closer to a field recording than to a studio statement — White is composing in real time, working out melodies and verses against his own playing. John Fahey, who along with ED Denson had rediscovered White in 1963 by sending a letter addressed to "Booker T. Washington White, Old Blues Singer, c/o General Delivery, Aberdeen, Mississippi" (it actually reached him), considered him the equal of Patton on rhythmic terms and wrote the liner notes treating him as such. *Mississippi Blues* (Takoma 1963), Fahey's own production with more focused song selection, was the alternative candidate; the debate went to *Sky Songs* on the grounds that it is the more characteristic White performance — looser, longer, more self-directed, and closer to how White actually played in life. Scaruffi pushed for inclusion against internal pressure to slot in a second Robert Johnson volume; Fantano's extrapolated position favored White on raw-expression grounds (the spontaneous-composition format especially). White is the most underrated of the Paramount-era Delta originators on the post-1960s reissue circuit — overshadowed by Patton, House, and Johnson — and a top-10 list without him would be visibly incomplete. He was also B.B. King's older cousin and gave King his first guitar, a fact that places him at a structural node in the entire postwar blues lineage.

##### 9. Robert Johnson — *King of the Delta Blues Singers, Vol. II* (1970, Columbia)

The 1970 Columbia follow-up gathered the remaining Johnson sides not on Vol. I — "Sweet Home Chicago," "Walking Blues," "Stop Breakin' Down Blues," "Last Fair Deal Gone Down," "Honeymoon Blues," "From Four Until Late," "Little Queen of Spades," and ten others. Scaruffi initially argued for separate placement on the grounds that Vol. II contains "Sweet Home Chicago" (the song that defined urban blues migration) and "Walking Blues" (the rhythmic template that becomes Muddy Waters' "Country Blues" and the entire postwar Chicago school). Fantano's extrapolated objection was that splitting Johnson into two slots crowds out a Patton or a Burnside; better to consolidate with the 1990 *Complete Recordings* box and free a slot. Scaruffi's argument carried on the grounds that the canonical sequencing of Vol. I and Vol. II as the two-LP introduction-to-Johnson is the historical document that built the canon, and that the 1990 box, while more thorough, is not how the canon was built. Vol. II is structurally the lesser of the two — Vol. I has the more iconic shortlist — but it contains enough top-tier material that excluding it from a top-10 of Delta blues is harder to defend than the alternative. The placement at #9 reflects the compromise: Johnson gets two slots, but the second one ranks below all the other artist-slots above it.

##### 10. R.L. Burnside — *A Ass Pocket of Whiskey* (1996, Matador / Fat Possum)

The Jon Spencer Blues Explosion collaboration — Burnside, Kenny Brown, and the JSBX band recorded over two days at Easley Studios in Memphis with no overdubs. Purists hated it on release; everyone has come around. The album is the moment hill-country Mississippi blues opens up to the indie rock audience that would eventually bring it to White Stripes / Black Keys / Heartless Bastards / Daniel Lanois territory, and Burnside, then 69, plays as if he is twenty years younger. "Goin' Down South," "Snake Drive," "Tojo Told Hitler," "Shake 'Em On Down" — the songs are mostly Burnside standards, but the band attack is closer to Pussy Galore than to Fat Possum, and that contrast is the whole point. Scaruffi's stated preference for rawness over polish lines up cleanly with this record, and he placed it in his top 12; Fantano's extrapolated position (Fat Possum-era hill-country, raw expression, sonic innovation, crossover into indie rock) places it considerably higher. The compromise at #10 reflects the disagreement: Scaruffi wanted *Sad Days, Lonely Nights* (Junior Kimbrough's second album) in this slot for stylistic-purity reasons; Fantano wanted *Ass Pocket* on raw-expression / sonic-innovation grounds. The argument was that Burnside is already represented at #7 with *Too Bad Jim*, so a second Burnside slot is harder to justify than a second Kimbrough — but Fantano's case held that *Ass Pocket* is genuinely a different record from *Too Bad Jim* (it is — different band, different aesthetic, different audience) and that excluding it removes the Mississippi-tradition continuation arc from the list at the moment of its widest cultural reach. Resolved by coin flip in favor of *Ass Pocket* — see resolutions below.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Two genuine irreconcilables required coin flips:

**#5: Junior Kimbrough — *All Night Long* (1992) vs *Sad Days, Lonely Nights* (1993).** Scaruffi argued *Sad Days* is the more refined statement, the band tighter and the production cleaner; Fantano (extrapolated) and Robert Palmer's contemporaneous reviews favored *All Night Long* as the rawer, more characteristic juke-joint document. Coin flip resolved in favor of *All Night Long* on the grounds that it is the founding Fat Possum release and the historical document of Kimbrough's discovery.

**#10: R.L. Burnside — *A Ass Pocket of Whiskey* (1996) vs Junior Kimbrough — *Sad Days, Lonely Nights* (1993).** Scaruffi wanted Kimbrough's second album for stylistic-purity reasons; Fantano wanted the Burnside / Jon Spencer collaboration on raw-expression and crossover-significance grounds. Coin flip resolved in favor of *A Ass Pocket of Whiskey*. *Sad Days, Lonely Nights* moves to honorable mentions.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **Junior Kimbrough — *Sad Days, Lonely Nights* (1993, Fat Possum).** The runner-up to *All Night Long* on coin flip. Some critics, notably Robert Palmer, considered this the more refined statement.
- **Robert Johnson — *The Complete Recordings* (1990, Columbia).** The Grammy-winning two-disc box that consolidates Vol. I and Vol. II plus alternate takes. Excluded on the grounds that the 1961/1970 LPs are the canonical sequencing; included here as the recommended single purchase for new listeners.
- **Charley Patton — *Screamin' and Hollerin' the Blues: The Worlds of Charley Patton* (2001, Revenant).** John Fahey's seven-disc Patton box, Grammy-winning, more comprehensive than the Yazoo LP. Excluded on canonical-sequencing grounds.
- **Son House — *Delta Blues and Spirituals* (1995, Capitol).** The 1970 Café Au Go Go live recording, released posthumously. A worthy companion to *Father of Folk Blues*.
- **Mississippi Fred McDowell — *I Do Not Play No Rock 'N' Roll* (1969, Capitol).** The more famous and more polished McDowell statement; lost the slot to the 1964 Arhoolie on historical-priority grounds.
- **Bukka White — *Mississippi Blues* (1963, Takoma).** John Fahey's production. The alternative to *Sky Songs* and arguably the more focused album.
- **Howlin' Wolf — *Moanin' in the Moonlight* (1959, Chess).** Wolf was Patton's direct disciple and a Mississippi Delta product, but his canonical work is Chicago blues, treated in a separate file.
- **T-Model Ford — *Pee-Wee Get My Gun* (1997, Fat Possum).** Third-tier Fat Possum but a real document of the 1990s Mississippi scene.
- **Asie Payton — *Worried* (1999, Fat Possum).** Posthumous; raw to the point of unfinished. Cult favorite among Fat Possum partisans.
- **Various Artists — *The Story of the Blues* (1969, Columbia, comp. Paul Oliver).** The other foundational reissue alongside *King of the Delta Blues Singers*; broader scope than Delta-only, hence excluded.
- **Robert Petway — *Catfish Blues* sides (1941–42, Bluebird).** The "Catfish Blues" original, source of Muddy Waters' "Rollin' Stone." No standalone canonical album — sides scattered across Delta blues compilations.
- **Tommy Johnson — Yazoo *Complete Recorded Works* compilations.** The other Johnson; "Canned Heat Blues," "Big Road Blues," "Cool Drink of Water Blues." Lost the slot to maintain artist diversity.

---

*Note on scope: Mississippi John Hurt was considered and excluded as Avalon hill-country fingerpicking, not Delta lineage — see the separate Country Blues / Pre-War Blues file. Howlin' Wolf and Muddy Waters were considered and excluded as Chicago blues despite their Delta origins. The list prioritizes the Mississippi Delta tradition as a coherent geographic-musical lineage rather than all pre-war rural blues.*

---

### Piedmont Blues

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Piedmont blues is a critic's puzzle. Unlike Delta blues, where the canon hardened around a handful of monumental figures (Patton, House, Johnson, Hurt), the East Coast tradition spreads its genius across more players, more idioms, and more sessions, much of it cut between 1927 and 1941 and reissued in confusing, overlapping compilations. There is no *King of the Delta Blues Singers* equivalent — no single LP that crystallized the form for the folk revival. Instead there are decades of Yazoo, Document, Bluebird, Folkways, and Smithsonian compilations, plus the 1960s revival recordings that revived the players themselves. Any honest top-ten list is therefore a list of *issued documents* — compilations, field recordings, revival LPs — and we have been explicit when an album is a compilation. We have noted "compilation" rather than treating these as if they were originally conceived as albums in the rock-era sense.

Both critics agree that **Blind Willie McTell** and **Reverend Gary Davis** are the twin peaks of the tradition. Scaruffi's general blues writing places McTell in his highest tier of bluesmen, alongside Patton and Johnson, and treats Davis as the most technically sophisticated of all pre-war guitarists. Fantano has not written sustained criticism on Piedmont blues — we flag this explicitly — but his stated reverence for technical guitar (he has gone long on Jeff Beck, John Fahey, Bert Jansch, and the ECM jazz-guitar lineage) and his repeated emphasis on emotional authenticity in folk forms (his Karen Dalton and Jackson C. Frank takes) make extrapolation defensible. We are not putting words in his mouth on individual records he has never reviewed; we are saying that his demonstrated values map cleanly onto Piedmont's intricate-thumb-and-finger virtuosity and its devotional/secular tension.

The disagreements were narrower than expected. Scaruffi pushed harder for Buddy Moss and for the more obscure Atlanta and Carolina players (Curley Weaver, Willie Walker via reconstruction). Fantano (extrapolated) leaned on the cleaner, more sonically present revival-era recordings — the ones a young listener can actually hear without 78 surface noise as a barrier — and argued for Etta Baker's *One-Dime Blues* as essential because of how it positions a woman fingerpicker at the center of a tradition usually framed as male. Both critics agreed Brownie McGhee and Sonny Terry as a duo earned a slot, though they argued about which compilation. Both flagged that the genre's "albums" problem is real and that any list is a list of documents, not of LPs in the album-as-statement sense.

Four rounds of debate followed. Coin-flips were used twice and are noted in their own section. Honorable mentions catch the records that lost narrowly.

A note on the geography. "Piedmont" technically refers to the plateau between the Appalachian foothills and the Atlantic coastal plain — Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia — but in blues criticism the term is also a stylistic label that can stretch to include adjacent traditions. We have kept the list tight to the East Coast fingerpicking tradition: alternating thumb bass against ragtime-derived treble figures, songster repertoire mixed with twelve-bar blues, often sacred-secular bilingual, often built around the I-IV-V harmony of "Step It Up and Go," "Hesitation Blues," and the rag-blues forms. We have explicitly excluded Mississippi John Hurt (a closer-to-Delta songster whose fingerpicking is sometimes lumped in), Lead Belly (a Texas/Louisiana songster), and Blind Blake (whose Chicago recordings are stylistically Piedmont-adjacent but geographically and biographically separate). Blake gets an honorable-mention footnote with the reasoning explicit. Hurt and Lead Belly belong to other entries in this volume.

A note on the format. Several of the entries below are compilations rather than albums-as-statements. We have treated this as a feature of the genre rather than a flaw of the list: the pre-war Piedmont catalogue exists in 78-rpm units, not LPs, and the canonical "albums" are therefore the curated reissues that organized those units into listenable sequences. We have preferred reissues that were curated with care (Columbia/Legacy, Yazoo, Smithsonian Folkways, Document) and we have flagged when an entry is closer to a discographical reconstruction than to a conceived album.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Blind Willie McTell — *The Definitive Blind Willie McTell* (Columbia/Legacy, 1994; recordings 1927–1935)

McTell is the consensus number one. Scaruffi treats him as a structural genius — a twelve-string player whose harmonic vocabulary is wider than anyone in the pre-war tradition, capable of a ragtime stride one minute and a slow modal lament the next. "Statesboro Blues," "Broke Down Engine," "Southern Can Is Mine," "Mama, 'Tain't Long Fo' Day," "Travelin' Blues" — these are the foundation texts. He recorded for Victor, Columbia, Decca, and Vocalion under different names (Blind Sammie, Georgia Bill, Hot Shot Willie), and any single label compilation tells only part of the story; the Columbia/Legacy *Definitive* set is the most widely available curated entry point and is what we list, with the caveat that the completist will eventually want the Document chronologicals and the 1940 Library of Congress sessions Alan Lomax cut in an Atlanta hotel room.

What makes McTell decisive for both critics is that he refuses the usual Piedmont categories. He is not strictly a ragtime fingerpicker like Fuller; he is not a gospel virtuoso like Davis; he is not a duo player like McGhee. He is a soloist with a twelve-string and a singing voice that moves between confidential murmur and full sermon, and he rewrites the form every few bars. Scaruffi explicitly ranks him as one of the great blues vocalists, not just guitarists. Fantano's extrapolated case rests on the same intricacy-plus-feeling axis he applies to Jansch and Fahey: McTell sounds composed in real time, and the emotional weight is delivered without stagecraft. The Columbia compilation also contains the cleanest available transfers of the Vocalion sides, which matters when you are trying to actually hear the inner voices of a twelve-string. This is the album anyone unfamiliar with Piedmont should hear first, and the album anyone familiar with it returns to most.

##### 2. Reverend Gary Davis — *Harlem Street Singer* (Bluesville/Prestige, 1960)

Recorded in a single August 1960 afternoon in Englewood Cliffs, *Harlem Street Singer* is the Davis album that captures him at his most fully realized: solo voice and solo guitar, no band, no duets, no compromises. Davis had moved from the Carolinas to New York in the 1940s and made his living as a street preacher with a guitar; by 1960 his technique had reached the level that would make him the teacher of Stefan Grossman, Jorma Kaukonen, Dave Van Ronk, Ry Cooder, and effectively the entire 1960s American fingerstyle school. "Samson and Delilah," "Twelve Gates to the City," "Death Don't Have No Mercy," "I Belong to the Band" — these are not just gospel-blues touchstones; they are masterclasses in three-finger Piedmont attack used as religious witness.

Scaruffi rates Davis as the most technically advanced of the pre-war players and treats *Harlem Street Singer* as the document that proves it, since the 1935 sides for ARC are too few and too stylistically constrained (Davis insisted on recording mostly sacred material and walked when producers pushed him toward secular blues). Fantano has not reviewed this record specifically — flagged — but his repeated praise of Fahey and his framing of John Frusciante, Jansch, and Robbie Basho as carriers of a "real" guitar tradition all trace back, historically, to Davis. The 1960 fidelity is excellent, the performances are unhurried, and Davis's voice — gravelly, declarative, half-shouting from the corner of 138th Street — carries the conviction. Both critics agreed this is the Davis record. The runner-ups (*Pure Religion and Bad Company*, *Say No to the Devil*) are honorable mentions; *Harlem Street Singer* is the one.

##### 3. Blind Boy Fuller — *East Coast Piedmont Style* (Columbia/Legacy, 1991; recordings 1935–1940)

Fuller (Fulton Allen) is the commercial center of pre-war Piedmont — the player whose ARC and Vocalion sides between 1935 and his death in 1941 effectively defined what record buyers in the Carolinas understood Piedmont blues to sound like. He sold more 78s than any other East Coast bluesman of his era. *East Coast Piedmont Style* is Columbia/Legacy's curated selection: "Step It Up and Go," "Truckin' My Blues Away," "Rag, Mama, Rag," "Get Your Yas Yas Out" (the original Fuller cut, not the Stones), "Mamie." Bull City Red on washboard, Sonny Terry on harmonica from 1937 onward, occasionally Gary Davis on second guitar.

The critics split mildly here. Scaruffi prefers the more idiosyncratic players — McTell, Davis, Buddy Moss — and views Fuller as the populist, the form-codifier rather than the form-stretcher. Fantano (extrapolated) values Fuller for exactly that reason: the playing is clean, the rhythmic propulsion is undeniable, and the songs are short, structured, repeatable. There is no question that Fuller is on the list; the question was what slot. Scaruffi argued sixth, Fantano argued second. They settled at third on the strength of Fuller's historical centrality — without him, the Piedmont commercial pipeline through Memphis-via-J.B. Long does not function, and Brownie McGhee is literally repackaged as "Blind Boy Fuller No. 2" after Fuller's death. The Columbia compilation offers cleaner audio than Document; the Document chronologicals (Vols. 1–6) are more complete but more punishing for casual listening. *East Coast Piedmont Style* is the entry point.

##### 4. Brownie McGhee & Sonny Terry — *Sonny Terry & Brownie McGhee Sing* (Folkways, 1958; reissued Smithsonian Folkways)

The McGhee/Terry partnership ran from the mid-1940s to the mid-1970s and produced more albums than any other Piedmont act, which is also the problem: the catalogue is uneven, padded, and frequently aimed at white folk audiences in ways that smoothed off the edges. Both critics agreed they earned one slot; the argument was which record.
