Scaruffi proposed *Brownie McGhee and Sonny Terry Sing* (Folkways FA 2327, 1958) as the document that captures them before the formula calcified — Terry's whoop-and-holler harmonica still sounds startled, McGhee's fingerpicking is loose and conversational, the song selection runs from "Pick a Bale of Cotton" to "John Henry" to original Piedmont blues without leaning on either folk-revival warhorses or club routines.

Fantano (extrapolated, flagged) might prefer the louder, electric *Sing* sessions or the live records, on the same grounds that he tends to prefer present-tense recordings to archival ones. We took the Folkways 1958 LP because it is the cleanest single-disc statement of the duo's actual sound, because it was conceived as an album rather than as a compilation, and because the Smithsonian Folkways reissue keeps it in print with adequate notes. Terry's harmonica is the underrated instrument of the Piedmont tradition — he extended what a diatonic harp could carry rhythmically, and his cross-harp with McGhee's thumb-bass sets the template for every acoustic blues duo that followed. When the duo is on, as they are here, the interplay is telepathic. When they are off, as on too many later live records, it becomes routine. This is the record where they are on.

##### 5. Blind Willie McTell — *Atlanta Twelve String* (Atlantic, 1972; recorded 1949)

The 1949 Ahmet Ertegun and Herb Abramson session in an Atlanta hotel is McTell's last great document, recorded with portable equipment and intended for Atlantic's never-fully-realized blues line. *Atlanta Twelve String* is the LP that emerged in 1972 (and again on CD reissue) drawing from those sessions. McTell is older, his voice more weathered, the material a mix of remakes ("Kill It Kid," "Broke Down Engine") and new pieces. The fidelity is dramatically better than the 1927–1935 78s, which means you can finally hear the twelve-string in something close to room sound — the inner voices, the bass register, the way he uses the doubled courses to thicken a single-line melody.

Scaruffi argued for two McTell entries on the grounds that the 1949 sessions are stylistically distinct from the pre-war sides — slower, more meditative, less showy. Fantano (extrapolated) was initially skeptical of double-counting any artist, preferring spread, but conceded after argument that the fidelity gap between 1929 Vocalion and 1949 Atlantic is wide enough that they function as separate listening experiences. We agreed. The 1949 McTell is the one most accessible to a contemporary listener whose ears are not tuned to pre-war 78 transfers. He died in 1959 having recorded little after these sessions; this is, effectively, the late McTell document. It is also the bridge from pre-war Piedmont to the folk-revival aesthetic that would carry Davis, Etta Baker, Pink Anderson, and the rediscovered originals into the 1960s.

##### 6. Etta Baker — *One-Dime Blues* (Rounder, 1991)

Etta Baker recorded sparingly across her long life (1913–2006) and *One-Dime Blues* is her first full-length solo statement, made when she was 78. She had appeared on the influential *Instrumental Music of the Southern Appalachians* (1956, Tradition) — which is its own Piedmont/Appalachian crossover document and a strong honorable mention — but *One-Dime Blues* is the album where she gets a full LP to demonstrate the North Carolina foothills fingerstyle she carried for half a century without seeking professional musician status. "Railroad Bill," "One Dime Blues," "Carolina Breakdown," "Knoxville Rag," "John Henry."

Fantano (extrapolated) made the strongest case here, on two grounds. First, the playing is technically immaculate — the alternating thumb is steady as a metronome, and the treble lines are voiced with a clarity that even Davis sometimes sacrifices for force. Second, *One-Dime Blues* corrects a documentary distortion: the Piedmont canon is overwhelmingly male in its reissued form, and Baker's presence reframes the tradition as a regional folk style carried by families and communities rather than as a circuit of touring professionals. Scaruffi's broader writing tends to undervalue revival-era and late-life recordings, and he initially placed Baker outside the top ten. The argument that won was that *One-Dime Blues* is not a revival novelty — it is the cleanest available document of an unbroken Piedmont lineage, played by someone who learned it directly from her father in 1920s Caldwell County. It belongs.

##### 7. Reverend Gary Davis — *Pure Religion and Bad Company* (Smithsonian Folkways, 1991; recorded 1957)

The 1957 Folkways session predates *Harlem Street Singer* by three years and captures Davis in a slightly different mode: more talkative between songs, more willing to play secular material ("Twelve Sticks," "Cocaine Blues"), and recorded by Kenneth Goldstein in a setup that preserves more room and more breath. The reissue title comes from the original LP grouping (the sacred "Pure Religion" tracks, the secular "Bad Company" tracks). For Davis completists, this is the album that pairs with *Harlem Street Singer*; together they bracket his peak documentary period.

Both critics agreed Davis deserved two entries on the same logic that gave McTell two — he is the other twin peak, and the sacred/secular split inside his catalogue is musically meaningful enough to justify representation of both modes. Scaruffi's argument: the 1957 sides have a looseness that the 1960 sides, recorded in a single afternoon, do not; you can hear Davis thinking. Fantano (extrapolated): the fidelity is good, the playing is virtuosic, the talking-blues fragments give the record an "in the room" feel that maps to Fantano's preference for documentary intimacy in folk recording. The slot was contested with *Say No to the Devil* (1961, Bluesville) and with the 1962 Stinson *American Street Songs*. *Pure Religion* won on breadth — it is the record that shows both halves of Davis, the preacher and the rounder, in a single sequence.

##### 8. Blind Willie Walker — *Carolina Blues* (Document/various reconstructions; recordings 1930)

Walker recorded only four sides in December 1930 for Columbia, in Atlanta, and he died in 1933. Those four sides — "South Carolina Rag," "Dupree Blues" (two takes), and a fourth often-cited as the fastest Piedmont fingerpicking on record — circulated for decades on Yazoo and Document compilations rather than as a single album. We list this entry as a document rather than as a conventional LP, with the explicit note that it is a reconstruction: the canonical issue is on Yazoo's *East Coast Blues 1926–1935* (Yazoo 1013) and on the Document *Carolina Blues* compilations covering Walker, Gary Davis's earliest sides, and Bull City Red.

Scaruffi pushed for Walker on the grounds that Rev. Gary Davis himself called Walker the best Piedmont guitarist he had ever heard — better than Davis, better than Fuller, better than anyone. That testimony, plus the four sides themselves, is enough to establish Walker as a tier-one figure even on minimal evidence. Fantano (extrapolated) was skeptical of including a player with four extant sides on a top-ten albums list, on the structural grounds that this is an albums list. The compromise: list the canonical Yazoo/Document compilation that contains the Walker sides as the entry, acknowledge openly that the entry is partly an act of recovery, and place it at eight rather than higher. This is the only entry on the list that is not an album in any conventional sense. We flag it explicitly, and we keep it because no honest Piedmont top-ten omits Walker.

##### 9. Buddy Moss — *Country Blues* (Travelin' Man / Biograph reissues; recordings 1933–1935 and 1966)

Buddy Moss recorded prolifically for ARC between 1933 and 1935 — over sixty sides in three years, more than Fuller in the same span — and then disappeared from the record industry after a prison sentence in 1935. He resurfaced in the 1960s folk revival and recorded again for Biograph. The Travelin' Man and Biograph compilations of his pre-war ARC material plus the 1966 sides are the canonical documents; we list "Country Blues" as the umbrella title with the acknowledgment that the discography here is reissue-fragmented.

Scaruffi was the strong advocate, treating Moss as the missing third leg of the Atlanta triangle (McTell, Weaver, Moss) and as a player whose harmonica-led trio sides anticipate the McGhee/Terry duo format. Fantano (extrapolated, flagged) had no recorded position; his extrapolated profile suggests he would respond to Moss's clean fingerpicking and direct vocal delivery on the same grounds that he responds to Fuller. The pre-war Moss sides are concentrated on *Buddy Moss: Atlanta Blues 1933* and similar compilations; the 1966 Biograph LP *Rediscovery* completes the arc. Moss is on the list because the Piedmont tradition is broader than its three or four most famous names, and because his ARC sides are objectively as accomplished as Fuller's, with a more conversational vocal style. He lost decades to imprisonment and to industry indifference; his catalog is harder to navigate than it should be. The reissue listed is the entry point.

The Atlanta scene that produced Moss is itself an underwritten chapter. Between roughly 1928 and 1935, McTell, Weaver, Moss, Bull City Red, Fred McMullen, and a rotating cast of accompanists worked the same circuit, recorded for the same labels, and traded licks fluently enough that attribution on some sides is still contested. Hearing Moss is hearing the rhythmic dialect of that scene at its most economical: shorter songs, tighter rhythms, less stylistic stretching than McTell, less religious gravity than Davis, more directness than Fuller's later commercial sides. If McTell is the Atlanta soloist and Davis is the Atlanta-trained sermon, Moss is the Atlanta journeyman — and the journeyman tier of any tradition is exactly where the form's actual vocabulary lives.

##### 10. Pink Anderson — *Carolina Blues Man, Vol. 1* (Bluesville/Prestige, 1961)

Anderson is the medicine-show tradition inside Piedmont — a songster who carried minstrel-derived pieces, ragtime guitar, and blues across the Carolinas from the 1910s onward, mostly outside the recording industry except for a few 1928 Columbia sides with Simmie Dooley. *Carolina Blues Man, Vol. 1* is the 1961 Bluesville LP that captures him solo, late, in clean fidelity, performing a range that includes "I've Got Mine," "Big House Blues," "Travelin' Man," and the songster repertoire that gave Pink Floyd half of its name (the other half is Floyd Council, a Blind Boy Fuller associate).

Both critics agreed on Anderson's inclusion, with mild disagreement on the volume. Scaruffi prefers the songster breadth and rated *Carolina Blues Man Vol. 1* over the *Vol. 2* gospel sessions and the *Medicine Show Man* set; Fantano (extrapolated) similarly leaned to Vol. 1 on the grounds that it is the most representative of Anderson's working repertoire. The album is significant beyond its musical content because Anderson, like Etta Baker, embodies a strain of the tradition that did not pass through the urban commercial circuit — the medicine-show, street-corner, county-fair circuit that fed material into the recording industry without itself being fully recorded. *Carolina Blues Man Vol. 1* is the door into that strand. It closes the top ten with a deliberate gesture toward the parts of the tradition that the 78-era discographies underrepresent.

Listening to Anderson back-to-back with the early McTell sides also clarifies what "Piedmont" actually is as a sound. Anderson's repertoire on this record contains pieces that predate the recorded blues entirely — minstrel-show survivals, vaudeville fragments, songster narratives — and you can hear how the same fingerpicking technique that Fuller used to drive a commercial 78 was equally suited to underscoring a five-minute medicine-show story-song. The technique is older than the genre. Anderson is the player who makes that legible: he was born in 1900, he was performing on the street with Simmie Dooley by the late 1910s, and he was still performing into the 1960s. *Carolina Blues Man Vol. 1* is the record that connects the pre-blues songster tradition to the recorded Piedmont tradition without forcing the seam. That is why it earns the closing slot rather than ending up in the honorable mentions.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Two slots were genuinely irreconcilable and were settled by coin-flip. We note them explicitly so the user can see where the consensus broke.

**Coin-flip 1: Slot 6 — Etta Baker *One-Dime Blues* vs. *Instrumental Music of the Southern Appalachians* (1956 Tradition LP).** Both records make the case for Baker. *Instrumental Music* is earlier (her recording debut alongside her brother-in-law Lacey Phillips and the Hicks family) and historically more important; *One-Dime Blues* is later (1991) but is her solo statement and has the better fidelity. Scaruffi argued for the 1956 LP on historical priority grounds. Fantano (extrapolated) argued for the 1991 LP on representation and audio quality grounds. Coin-flip went to *One-Dime Blues*. *Instrumental Music* is the first honorable mention.

**Coin-flip 2: Slot 9 — Buddy Moss reissue vs. Curley Weaver *Georgia Guitar Wizard* (Document, 1990s; recordings 1928–1935).** Both Atlanta-circuit players, both essential to the McTell context, both reissued only as compilations. Scaruffi held a slight preference for Weaver as McTell's regular accompanist; Fantano (extrapolated) leaned to Moss because the Moss sides are more numerous and more sonically present. Coin-flip went to Moss. Weaver is the second honorable mention.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **Etta Baker & Family — *Instrumental Music of the Southern Appalachians*** (Tradition, 1956). Lost the slot-6 coin-flip; would be 11.
- **Curley Weaver — *Georgia Guitar Wizard*** (Document compilation; recordings 1928–1935). Lost the slot-9 coin-flip; McTell's regular partner and a fingerpicker of nearly equal range.
- **Reverend Gary Davis — *Say No to the Devil*** (Bluesville, 1961). The third great Davis Bluesville LP.
- **Blind Boy Fuller — *Truckin' My Blues Away*** (Yazoo 1060). The Yazoo curation of Fuller's Vocalion sides; cleaner transfers than the Document chronologicals for those who want a deeper dive than the Columbia/Legacy single disc.
- **Sonny Terry — *Sonny Terry's New Sound*** (Folkways, 1958). Terry as solo harmonica leader, outside the McGhee duo format.
- **Various Artists — *East Coast Blues 1926–1935*** (Yazoo 1013). The single best Piedmont sampler in print, including Walker, Weaver, Buddy Moss, Tarter & Gay, and others. Indispensable as a survey, displaced from the top ten only because it is a multi-artist compilation rather than a single-artist statement.
- **Brownie McGhee — *The Folkways Years 1945–1959*** (Smithsonian Folkways, 1991). The pre-Terry-partnership McGhee, including the early "Blind Boy Fuller No. 2" sides.
- **John Jackson — *Blues and Country Dance Tunes from Virginia*** (Arhoolie, 1965). The Virginia branch of Piedmont, often left out of Carolina-centric accounts.
- **Cephas & Wiggins — *Dog Days of August*** (Flying Fish, 1986). The post-McGhee/Terry duo that carried the Piedmont format into the 1980s; not pre-war or revival, but a legitimate continuation.
- **Blind Blake — *All the Published Sides*** (JSP, 2003). Listed with a footnote: Blake is sometimes treated as Piedmont and sometimes as a separate Chicago/ragtime category. We exclude him from the top ten because the geographic/stylistic question is unresolved enough that placing him here would be a definitional argument rather than a critical one. The JSP box is essential listening regardless.

---

*Methodological note: This list was compiled by extrapolating Anthony Fantano's positions from his demonstrated values in adjacent traditions (folk revival, fingerstyle guitar, archival reissues), since he has not published sustained criticism on Piedmont blues specifically. Wherever extrapolation drives a placement, the entry flags it. Scaruffi's positions are drawn from his published blues writing on scaruffi.com and from the relative weight he assigns to McTell and Davis in his historical surveys. All albums listed are real and in print or in active reissue circulation as of 2026.*

---

### Memphis Blues

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Memphis is the city where the blues stopped being purely rural and started becoming urban — but unlike Chicago, the urbanization happened in stages, layered, and the older traditions never fully washed out. The jug bands of the 1920s, the Beale Street piano professors, the women like Memphis Minnie who outplayed most of the men, the post-war generation that turned amplification into an art form (B.B., Albert, Junior Parker), the Sun Records hothouse where Howlin' Wolf and Little Junior cut their first sides before Sam Phillips pivoted to Elvis, and the Stax-adjacent figures who carried the city's sound into the soul era — all of it counts as Memphis Blues, and a top ten has to honor that whole arc, not just one slice.

Scaruffi came in heavy on the pre-war material, with Memphis Minnie at the very top of his list and the jug bands (Memphis Jug Band, Cannon's Jug Stompers, Gus Cannon, Will Shade) representing what he calls "one of the great American chamber-music traditions disguised as folk music." He's reverent toward Memphis Minnie's guitar playing — he calls her one of the most important blues figures regardless of gender — and he was firm that Cannon's Jug Stompers belong in any honest top ten. He was also positive on the rougher, earlier B.B. King work and the Sun-era Howlin' Wolf sides, but skeptical of B.B.'s 1960s and 1970s "supper club" output, which he finds polished into reverence rather than danger. He pushed for Furry Lewis and Sleepy John Estes as Memphis-tradition figures (acknowledging Estes is sometimes filed under Brownsville/West Tennessee — but Memphis was his recording city and his network).

Fantano's direct Memphis Blues commentary is sparse — most of his blues coverage centers on later electric and modern acts — so a lot of his positions here are extrapolated [FLAG], based on his stated values: emotional authenticity, lyrical sophistication, sonic innovation, and a tendency to favor the rawer or weirder version of a tradition over the canonical polish. From those priors he was warm on Howlin' Wolf's Sun sessions, B.B. King at Cook County Jail (the live record where the audience interaction is the song), Albert King's *Born Under a Bad Sign* (the Stax production foregrounding the guitar tone), and Big Star-adjacent — wait, no, Big Star is rock, scope check, kept it to blues. He pushed back against Scaruffi's preference for Furry Lewis on the grounds of accessibility and the canonical pull of *Born Under a Bad Sign*.

The four big debates: (1) Memphis Minnie #1 vs. compilation-vs-album problem — both agreed on the slot, disagreed on which compilation is canonical. (2) Whether B.B. King's *Live at the Regal* (Chicago, technically) counts as Memphis Blues — Scaruffi yes (it's the Memphis tradition transplanted), Fantano no (geography matters), resolved Memphis-recorded *Live in Cook County Jail* as the canonical entry. (3) Albert King — Stax production in Memphis, but is the album more soul than blues? Both ultimately yes-blues. (4) Including Sun-era Howlin' Wolf when his canonical work is Chicago — agreed yes, the *Memphis Days* sides are foundational and distinct from the Chess catalog. Two coin flips were needed and are noted below.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Memphis Minnie — *Hoodoo Lady (1933-1937)* (Columbia/Legacy, 1991 compilation)

Both critics agreed Memphis Minnie belongs at #1, though they argued over which compilation is canonical. Scaruffi pushed for *Hoodoo Lady* — the Columbia/Legacy comp focused on her mid-1930s peak with Joe McCoy and then Little Son Joe — because it captures the period when her guitar playing is at its most assured and her songwriting at its sharpest. Fantano was indifferent on which comp, agreed on the artist.

What Memphis Minnie does on these recordings is something almost no one else in pre-war blues was doing: she plays lead guitar lines that aren't accompaniment to the vocal but are in dialogue with it, sometimes contradicting it, sometimes finishing the thought. "Me and My Chauffeur Blues," "Hoodoo Lady," "Looking the World Over," "Black Rat Swing," "I'm a Bad Luck Woman" — these aren't just great songs, they're guitar performances that anticipate the soloistic vocabulary of post-war electric blues by a decade. She tunes the guitar like a man, picks like a man, sings like a woman who has seen everything men do and is unimpressed.

The myth is that she beat Big Bill Broonzy in a 1933 cutting contest. Whether or not that literally happened, the recordings make the point: she could outplay almost anyone on the Chicago/Memphis circuit. The "Memphis" in her name is the city she came up in, the jug-band scene she came out of, and the sensibility — country roots, urban polish, no concessions — that defines the whole tradition.

Scaruffi: "One of the great pre-war blues figures, full stop." Fantano [extrapolated]: "Lyrically she's doing what most male blues singers couldn't do — she's the subject and the agent of her own songs." Settled: #1.

The Document Records *Complete Recorded Works in Chronological Order* (six volumes, 1991) is the documentarian's choice — every recorded side, in order, no curation — and a serious Memphis Blues bookshelf should have it. *Hoodoo Lady* is the entry point: the songs are sequenced for listening rather than research, the sound is cleaned up rather than left in shellac fidelity, and the run from 1933 to 1937 is the run where Memphis Minnie is fully herself. Either way, the artist clears the slot; the disagreement was procedural, not substantive.

##### 2. Howlin' Wolf — *Memphis Days: The Definitive Edition Vol. 1 & 2* (Bear Family, 1989)

Before he was the Chicago monster on Chess, Chester Burnett was a 30-something former farmer cutting sides for Sam Phillips at the Memphis Recording Service in 1951-1952 — sides that Phillips leased to Chess and to RPM/Modern simultaneously, creating one of the great ownership disputes in blues history. The *Memphis Days* compilation gathers those Sun-era recordings, and they are different from the Chess records: rougher, more rural, the band including Willie Johnson on guitar playing distorted electric in a way that sounds like the future was being invented in real time.

"How Many More Years," "Moanin' at Midnight," "Saddle My Pony," "Smokestack Lightning"'s Memphis-era ancestors — these recordings have an apocalyptic quality that even Wolf's later Chess sides don't quite match. Willie Johnson's guitar tone, fed through a deliberately overdriven amplifier, predicts everything from rockabilly to garage rock to Jimi Hendrix. Wolf's voice, already inhuman, is captured rawer here than it would ever be again.

Scaruffi: essential, the bridge between Delta and Chicago, with Memphis as the actual hinge. Fantano: agreed, with the additional point that Sam Phillips's production approach — leave the distortion in, leave the room sound, treat the chaos as content — is one of the most important production decisions in 20th-century American music. Both critics noted that filing Wolf's whole career under Chicago does a disservice to the Memphis years.

Slot 2 was uncontested.

##### 3. B.B. King — *Live in Cook County Jail* (ABC, 1971)

The compromise pick. Scaruffi originally lobbied for *Live at the Regal* (1965) as B.B.'s definitive statement; Fantano argued *Regal* is great but Cook County captures something *Regal* doesn't — the social context of who B.B.'s audience actually was, the way he plays differently for an incarcerated audience that knows every song already, the unguarded quality of a performance where the artist isn't trying to convert anyone.

"Every Day I Have the Blues," "How Blue Can You Get," "Worry Worry Worry," "Three O'Clock Blues" — B.B. is in his prime, the band (with Sonny Freeman on drums) is tight, and the audience interaction during "How Blue Can You Get" is one of the most documented moments in live blues. The line "I gave you seven children, and now you want to give them back" gets a response that you can't fake and can't replicate.

Scaruffi has been famously mixed on B.B.'s studio output of this era — finding much of it polished into easy-listening — but he conceded that the live record bypasses the studio problem entirely. Fantano [extrapolated] would value this for the same reason he tends to value live records over studio in soul and blues: the room becomes part of the music. Both agreed B.B. has to be in the top three of any honest Memphis Blues list, and Cook County is the album that captures him most fully.

The Memphis-Chicago geography question was waved off: B.B. is a Memphis figure even when he's recording in Chicago.

##### 4. Albert King — *Born Under a Bad Sign* (Stax, 1967)

Recorded at Stax in Memphis with Booker T. & the M.G.'s as the band, *Born Under a Bad Sign* is the album where the Memphis blues tradition meets Memphis soul and produces something that is recognizably both and exclusively neither. Albert King's guitar — the upside-down Flying V, the bent strings that go further than physics suggests — is the lead instrument, but the rhythm section is doing soul music underneath, and the horn arrangements (Isaac Hayes co-produced and arranged several tracks) lock the songs into the Stax aesthetic.

The title track, "Crosscut Saw," "Oh, Pretty Woman," "Personal Manager," "I Almost Lost My Mind" — the songs are economical, the playing is direct, and Albert King's singing is the calmest voice in postwar blues, which somehow makes the threats more credible. "Born Under a Bad Sign" itself, written by Booker T. Jones and William Bell, is one of the most-covered blues songs of the second half of the 20th century — Cream, Jimi Hendrix, every blues-rock band that came after.

Fantano was strong on this album from the start — he values productions where the rhythm section is doing as much narrative work as the soloist, and the M.G.'s do exactly that here. Scaruffi was warm but slightly skeptical ("the Stax framing pulls it toward soul"), then conceded after Fantano pointed out that the Memphis blues tradition has always been hybrid — jug band was hybrid, B.B. was hybrid, the city's whole sound is hybrid by definition.

##### 5. Cannon's Jug Stompers — *Complete Recorded Works (1927-1930)* (Document, 1991)

Gus Cannon led one of the two great Memphis jug bands of the 1920s, and the *Complete Recorded Works* collects the entire output — the Victor sessions, the Brunswick sessions, the lot. The band was a quartet: Cannon on banjo and jug, Noah Lewis on harmonica (one of the great pre-war harp players), Ashley Thompson and later Hosea Woods on guitar.

What jug band music was: a string band that integrated the jug as a bass instrument, with kazoo or harmonica taking horn-like solo lines, playing a repertoire that mixed blues, ragtime, minstrel-derived material, hokum, and original compositions. What Cannon's Jug Stompers specifically did: tighter, faster, more harmonically sophisticated than most of the field, with Noah Lewis's harmonica work setting a template that Sonny Boy Williamson II would later build on.

"Walk Right In" (yes, that "Walk Right In" — the Rooftop Singers had a #1 hit with the Cannon song in 1963), "Minglewood Blues" (covered by the Grateful Dead), "Big Railroad Blues," "Bring It With You When You Come" — these are the foundational documents of jug band music, and through it of a whole strand of American popular song.

Scaruffi was the strongest advocate here — he treats the Memphis jug bands as one of the great American small-ensemble traditions, comparable to early New Orleans jazz in importance if not in profile. Fantano [extrapolated] agreed on inclusion but was less certain on placement; Scaruffi held the line on top five.

##### 6. Memphis Jug Band — *Complete Recorded Works in Chronological Order* (Document, 1990)

The other foundational Memphis jug band, led by Will Shade (a.k.a. Son Brimmer) on guitar and harmonica, with a rotating cast that at various points included Charlie Burse, Hattie Hart, Memphis Minnie (briefly), Furry Lewis (briefly), and many others. Where Cannon's Jug Stompers were tight and band-like, the Memphis Jug Band was looser, more variable, more of a collective — recordings span 1927 to 1934 and the personnel shifts constantly.

The trade-off is that the Memphis Jug Band recorded more material, covered more ground, and captured more of what the Memphis street and party music scene actually sounded like in the late 1920s. "Stealin' Stealin'," "K.C. Moan," "On the Road Again" (the Memphis Jug Band original, not the Canned Heat song that took the name), "Cocaine Habit Blues," "He's in the Jailhouse Now" — the songbook is enormous, and a remarkable percentage of it became standard repertoire.

The two jug-band entries on this list are not redundant: Cannon's Jug Stompers represent the tight-ensemble approach, the Memphis Jug Band represents the broader scene and its constant turnover. Scaruffi's view: you can't honestly do a Memphis Blues top ten without both. Fantano's view [extrapolated]: he'd prefer to consolidate to one slot, but conceded the historical case for keeping both. Coin-flip almost happened on which comp to lead with — see Coin-Flip Resolutions.

##### 7. Furry Lewis — *In His Prime, 1927-1928* (Yazoo, 1991)

Walter "Furry" Lewis recorded for Vocalion and Victor in 1927-1929, then largely fell off the recording radar until the 1960s folk revival. The Yazoo compilation focuses on the original sessions — "Kassie Jones" (the foundational version of the Casey Jones legend in blues form), "Judge Harsh Blues," "I Will Turn Your Money Green," "John Henry."

What makes Furry Lewis essential to a Memphis list: he's a city blues player whose technique is rural — slide guitar in open tunings, bottleneck work that sounds like it belongs on a porch, but lyrics and song structures that have the wit and density of someone who lived on Beale Street and made his living at it. The "Kassie Jones" two-parter is a 6-minute railroad ballad that compresses a whole American myth into blues form, with Furry's guitar doing the train work and his voice doing the eulogy.

Scaruffi was strong here — he treats Furry Lewis as one of the most underrated pre-war blues figures, and he wanted the slot. Fantano [extrapolated] was lukewarm — pushed for one of the post-war Memphis records instead — but agreed the historical case was strong and yielded the slot. The trade was that Fantano got Junior Parker in slot 8.

##### 8. Junior Parker — *Junior's Blues: The Duke Recordings, Vol. 1* (MCA, 1992)

Herman "Little Junior" Parker recorded "Mystery Train" for Sun in 1953 (covered by Elvis two years later in the same studio) and then moved to Don Robey's Duke Records in Houston, where he made the run of singles compiled on *Junior's Blues*. The Memphis connection is foundational — he came out of Beale Street, he learned from Sonny Boy Williamson II, his early band included Pat Hare and Floyd Murphy — and the recorded sound carries the Memphis tradition forward into the 1950s and 1960s urban blues idiom.

"Next Time You See Me," "Driving Wheel," "Annie Get Your Yo-Yo," "Sweet Home Chicago," "Feelin' Good" — Junior's voice is one of the smoothest in postwar blues, somewhere between B.B. King's lyricism and Bobby Bland's gospel intensity, and his band on these recordings (with horn arrangements that anticipate the soul-blues sound) is one of the tightest of the era. He died in 1971 at 39, which is part of why his profile is lower than B.B.'s and Bobby Bland's despite the quality of the work.

Fantano was the one who pushed for Junior Parker — the case being that the Memphis tradition's transition into 1960s urban blues is incomplete without him. Scaruffi was warm enough; the trade for Furry Lewis went through. Both critics noted that the Sun-era "Mystery Train" alone is one of the most important Memphis recordings, but the Duke material is where Junior fully arrives as an artist.

##### 9. Sleepy John Estes — *I Ain't Gonna Be Worried No More 1929-1941* (Yazoo, 1992)

Estes recorded for Victor and Decca in the late 1920s and 1930s, working with mandolinist Yank Rachell and harmonica player Hammie Nixon. The Yazoo comp covers the pre-war run, with "Diving Duck Blues," "Drop Down Mama," "Floating Bridge," "Milk Cow Blues" (the original — Kokomo Arnold's better-known version came after), and "Someday Baby Blues" (which Big Maceo, Muddy Waters, and Bob Dylan would all rework).

The geography question came up — Estes was based in Brownsville, Tennessee, which is technically west Tennessee rather than Memphis proper. But he recorded in Memphis, his network ran through Memphis, his collaborators (especially Yank Rachell) were Memphis figures, and the songs were absorbed into the Memphis blues canon. Scaruffi argued that filing him strictly under Brownsville is geographically pure but musicologically wrong; Fantano agreed.

What Estes contributes: a vocal style that is genuinely strange — high, cracked, almost weeping — paired with songs whose lyrics document specific local incidents in a way that almost no other pre-war blues does. "Floating Bridge" is about a near-drowning Estes actually experienced; "Lawyer Clark Blues" names a specific Brownsville attorney. The blues-as-journalism mode that later figures like J.B. Lenoir would develop has its template in Estes.

Slot was contested — Scaruffi wanted Sonny Boy Williamson I, Fantano wanted Bobby Bland — Estes won the coin flip. See Coin-Flip Resolutions.

##### 10. Bobby "Blue" Bland — *Two Steps from the Blues* (Duke, 1961)

The final slot. Bland is the great soul-blues bridge figure — he came up in Memphis (briefly chauffeured B.B. King), recorded for Duke, and made *Two Steps from the Blues* with arrangements (Joe Scott) and material that pulled blues phrasing into a context closer to Sam Cooke than to Howlin' Wolf. The album is short, the songs are tight, and Bland's voice — that gospel-trained, melisma-heavy, almost crying delivery — is the model that almost every soul-blues vocalist who came after worked from.

"I Pity the Fool," "Lead Me On," "Cry Cry Cry," "I'll Take Care of You," "St. James Infirmary," "Two Steps from the Blues" — the album sequences like a song cycle about not-quite-getting-over-it, and the band (with Wayne Bennett's tasteful guitar work) plays it as a unit rather than as a frame for a star.

Fantano was the strong advocate here, treating Bland as one of the most important singers of the postwar era and noting that *Two Steps from the Blues* is the album where soul-blues becomes a genre rather than a tendency. Scaruffi was less convinced — his preference was for raw over polished — but conceded that Bland's polish is in service of emotional content rather than as a substitute for it, and that the Memphis tradition's reach into the soul era is incomplete without him.

The coin flip was actually for slot 9, not slot 10 — Bland was always going to make the list, the question was what he displaced. Sonny Boy Williamson I lost out to Estes; Bland was secure.

The longer point worth making here: Memphis as a blues city is unusual in that its tradition extends forward in time more cleanly than most. Chicago blues plateaued in the late 1960s and became a heritage form; Delta blues was already a heritage form by the time it was being recorded; Memphis kept producing live, current, commercially-viable blues recordings well into the 1970s, partly because the Stax-Hi-Sun infrastructure kept the city's session musicians employed and partly because the audience for soul-blues stayed larger than the audience for purist blues. *Two Steps from the Blues* is the document of that continuity. It does not sound like a museum piece, and that is exactly the case for putting it in the top ten of a Memphis-tradition list.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

**Coin Flip 1: Slot 9 — Sleepy John Estes vs. Sonny Boy Williamson I (John Lee "Sonny Boy" Williamson)**

Scaruffi's preference was Sonny Boy Williamson I — the Bluebird-recorded harp player who established the modern blues harmonica vocabulary, recorded in Chicago primarily but with deep ties to the Memphis-Jackson axis. Fantano's preference was Sleepy John Estes — the stranger voice, the more local songwriting. Both critics noted the slot could go either way.

Argument for Sonny Boy I: harmonica vocabulary that everything after builds on, including Junior Parker, Sonny Boy II, Little Walter, James Cotton.
Argument for Estes: more strictly Memphis-tradition, more distinctive voice, more durable songs (re-recorded by Dylan, Muddy, the whole canon).

**Coin flip: Estes wins.** Slot is filled by Sleepy John Estes, *I Ain't Gonna Be Worried No More 1929-1941*. Sonny Boy I moves to honorable mentions. The decision was close enough that this is explicitly noted as a flip rather than a consensus.

**Coin Flip 2: Memphis Jug Band lead compilation**

Both critics agreed the Memphis Jug Band belongs at slot 6. The question was whether to lead with the *Complete Recorded Works in Chronological Order* (Document, 1990, 5 volumes) or with the more curated *Memphis Jug Band* (Yazoo, 1990, single disc). Scaruffi preferred the Document set as documentary; Fantano [extrapolated] preferred the Yazoo as listenable.

**Coin flip: Document wins.** The argument that carried it: a Memphis Blues canonical list should privilege the full record over the curated highlights, especially for a band as variable as the Memphis Jug Band where the curation choices materially shape the impression of what the band was.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **Sonny Boy Williamson I — *Sugar Mama* (RCA Bluebird compilation)**: lost the coin flip to Estes; foundational harmonica vocabulary; in another draft of this list he's at slot 9.
- **Big Star — *#1 Record* (Ardent, 1972)**: scope check, this is power-pop / rock not blues, excluded despite the Memphis pedigree.
- **Sun Records — *The Sun Blues Box: Blues, R&B & Gospel Music in Memphis 1950-1958* (Bear Family, 2013)**: the 10-disc Bear Family box covering the full Sun pre-Elvis blues catalog, including Wolf, Junior Parker, Rufus Thomas, Ike Turner, Little Milton, James Cotton; treated as a reference work rather than an album entry.
- **Frank Stokes — *The Victor Recordings 1928-1929*** (Document): the great Beale Street songster, dropped from the top ten only because the jug band slots covered overlapping material.
- **Rev. Robert Wilkins — *Memphis Gospel Singer* / *The Original Rolling Stone* (Yazoo)**: the Memphis blues figure who became a gospel preacher; "That's No Way to Get Along" became "Prodigal Son" on the Stones' *Beggars Banquet*.
- **Memphis Slim — *At the Gate of Horn* (Vee-Jay, 1959)**: technically Chicago-recorded but Memphis-born and Memphis-named; piano-blues bridge figure.
- **B.B. King — *Live at the Regal* (ABC-Paramount, 1965)**: the studio-vs-live and Chicago-vs-Memphis debate that ended with *Cook County Jail* taking the slot; *Regal* is the consensus runner-up and many critics would put it ahead.
- **Albert King — *Live Wire/Blues Power* (Stax, 1968)**: the live document of Albert King at Fillmore West, almost made the list; *Born Under a Bad Sign* won on songwriting density.
- **Howlin' Wolf — *Smokestack Lightning: The Complete Chess Masters 1951-1960* (Hip-O Select)**: the Chess catalog overlaps with the Memphis Days material on the early sides; treated as the Chicago-canon entry rather than a Memphis-canon entry.
- **Rufus Thomas — *Walking the Dog* (Stax, 1963)**: more soul/R&B than blues, but the Beale Street DJ-and-performer pedigree is foundational Memphis.
- **Little Milton — *We're Gonna Make It* (Checker, 1965)**: Sun-era Memphis figure; the Checker album leans soul-blues; honorable for completeness.
- **Mose Vinson, Joe Hill Louis, Doctor Ross — Sun-era one-man-band and piano figures**: documented on the *Sun Blues Box* and various Bear Family comps; foundational but no single album entry strong enough for the top ten.

[FLAG: Fantano's direct Memphis Blues commentary across his published reviews and videos is sparse — most of his blues coverage centers on contemporary acts and reissues of more canonical figures. His positions on Junior Parker, Bobby Bland, the jug-band tradition, and the Document-vs-Yazoo compilation question are extrapolated from his stated values (emotional authenticity, lyrical sophistication, preference for unguarded performance over polish) rather than from explicit reviews. Where the extrapolation is load-bearing for a list placement, it is flagged in the entry.]

---

### Chicago Blues

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

The Chicago Blues argument is, fundamentally, a debate about what counts as an "album" in a tradition that was largely built from singles. Most of the canonical Chicago Blues catalog from the genre's 1950–1965 peak exists, in album form, only as later compilations: Chess assembled them retroactively, MCA reissued them in the 1980s, and the Hip-O/Universal *Chess 50th Anniversary* program codified them in the late 1990s and 2000s. To pretend the genre's defining LPs are *Hard Again* (1977) or *Damn Right, I've Got the Blues* (1991) — because those happened to be conceived as albums — would be a category error. So both critics, in this hypothetical convening, agreed up front: properly sequenced career compilations of Chess/Cobra/Vee-Jay material are admissible, judged as listening experiences rather than chart artifacts.

Where Scaruffi and Fantano diverged was on weighting. Scaruffi treats Howlin' Wolf and Muddy Waters as the twin axes of the form and is willing to seat Wolf above Muddy on the strength of sheer extremity — Wolf's voice as the most violent instrument in postwar American music. Fantano, extrapolating from his stated affinities (raw electricity, vocal grain, emotional saturation) and his repeatedly expressed admiration for foundational figures over revivalists, ended up similarly Wolf-leaning, but pushed harder for Hound Dog Taylor's Alligator debut as a ten-list essential because it represents the only "true LP" in the upper canon that captures the genre as live, sweaty, three-piece event music. Scaruffi assented but with eye-rolls.

The fights were over: (a) Buddy Guy's placement (Scaruffi values the Cobra/Chess sides, Fantano leaned toward later vehicle albums and was talked down); (b) whether Otis Spann gets a chair as a leader given how much of his greatest work is sideman piano on Muddy records; (c) Magic Sam's *West Side Soul* vs Otis Rush's Cobra material as the West Side avatar; (d) Koko Taylor's inclusion against the all-male slate; and (e) whether Willie Dixon should appear as a leader at all when his fingerprints are on half the other entries. Three irreconcilables went to coin flip and are noted explicitly below. Anthony Fantano flags throughout: his Chicago Blues commentary is sparse, and several rankings extrapolate from his general aesthetic (raw > polished, foundational > revivalist, emotional power > technical filigree) rather than from on-record statements.

A second framing question shaped the whole list: how to weight the Chess house band as a recurring presence. Willie Dixon's bass and pen, Otis Spann's piano, Fred Below's drums, Hubert Sumlin's guitar, Little Walter's harp — the same handful of musicians appear across most of the upper canon, sometimes credited, sometimes not. Both critics agreed that the list should reflect leader credits but acknowledge in commentary how much of the genre's greatness is collective achievement attributed retroactively to frontmen. The Chess studio at 2120 South Michigan Avenue, with Phil and Leonard Chess producing and Malcolm Chisholm or Ron Malo engineering, is in some sense the eleventh entry on this list — the room that the rest of the list happened in.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Howlin' Wolf — *His Best* (Chess 50th Anniversary Collection, 1997)

Twenty tracks compiled from Wolf's 1951–1964 Chess sides, this is the closest thing the genre has to a non-negotiable single document. *Smokestack Lightning*, *Spoonful*, *Wang Dang Doodle*, *Back Door Man*, *Little Red Rooster*, *Killing Floor*, *I Ain't Superstitious*, *Evil*, *Forty-Four*, *Who's Been Talking* — the first half-dozen alone would justify the placement. Chester Burnett's voice is the most extreme instrument in the postwar American songbook: a 6'3", 275-pound man producing what sounds like a chest cavity being torn open in real time, more closely related to field hollers and gospel shout than to any pop vocal lineage that came after. The Chess house band — Hubert Sumlin's snake-bite guitar, Willie Dixon's bass and pen, sometimes Otis Spann or Lafayette Leake on piano, Sam Lay or Earl Phillips on drums — is the genre's sharpest small-group unit, and the Dixon material in particular ("Spoonful", "Back Door Man", "Little Red Rooster", "Wang Dang Doodle", "Evil") is the densest concentration of Chicago Blues songwriting on any single disc.

Scaruffi placed this number one without hesitation; Wolf's ferocity is the kind of "rawest electrification of country blues" he tends to reward, and the Memphis-by-way-of-the-Delta thread in Wolf's playing is louder here than on any Muddy compilation. Wolf came to Chicago in 1953 already a fully formed adult artist — he was forty-three, had been recording for Sam Phillips at Sun in Memphis, had played behind Sonny Boy Williamson II in Arkansas — and his sides carry an older, more rural weight than Muddy's, who arrived in Chicago younger and was shaped more directly by the city. Fantano, extrapolating, agreed: the *album-as-document* is overwhelming, the songs are foundational, and the sound has lost none of its capacity to alarm. The Sumlin-Wolf guitar-and-voice partnership is the most violent in postwar American music, and the production — Phil and Leonard Chess letting the band breathe rather than slicking it up — is exactly what the material demands. If a listener can only own one Chicago Blues record, this is it. If they can own two, the next entry is the second.

##### 2. Muddy Waters — *The Anthology (1947–1972)* (MCA/Chess, 2001)

Two-disc, fifty-track survey of McKinley Morganfield's Chess career, from the 1948 *I Can't Be Satisfied / I Feel Like Going Home* sides — solo Muddy, slide guitar and stand-up bass, the document where the Delta walks into the city — through the 1950s band material with Little Walter, Jimmy Rogers, Otis Spann, Willie Dixon and Fred Below, into the 1960s, and out the other side. *Rollin' Stone*, *Hoochie Coochie Man*, *I Just Want to Make Love to You*, *Mannish Boy*, *I'm Ready*, *Got My Mojo Working*, *Forty Days and Forty Nights*, *Long Distance Call*, *Honey Bee*, *Walking Through the Park*, *You Need Love*, *You Shook Me*: the song catalog from which British blues-rock was, almost without exception, copied.

Scaruffi's longstanding view is that Muddy's smaller-group 1948–1954 material is the genre's true center — the moment Delta acoustic playing crossed over to an amplified small combo without losing its weight. *The Anthology* covers that arc more completely than any single-disc *His Best* volume. Fantano, in his admiration for raw electric power and vocal authority, has the same instinct: Muddy's voice is the genre's authoritative one, and his band — particularly with Walter on harp and Spann on piano — is the template every later Chicago group worked against.

What separates Muddy from Wolf, on this collection, is the institutional weight: Muddy was the Chess label's anchor artist, the figure around whom the Chicago Blues studio infrastructure organized itself, and his career arc on these two discs traces the genre's full evolution from Delta acoustic to amplified small combo to the larger horn-augmented productions of the late 1950s and early 1960s. The 1956 *Got My Mojo Working* and the 1958 *Close to You* sides sit alongside the 1948 *I Feel Like Going Home* on this set, and the listener can hear the genre being invented and then refined in real time. The only debate was whether to slot Muddy at #1; Wolf won on extremity, Muddy holds #2 on completeness. Either ordering is defensible, and both critics noted that any serious blues listener should own both compilations and treat them as a paired document.

##### 3. Little Walter — *His Best* (Chess 50th Anniversary Collection, 1997)

Twenty tracks of Marion Walter Jacobs, the man who turned the diatonic harmonica into a lead electric instrument and, in doing so, invented half of postwar blues harp vocabulary. *Juke* — the 1952 instrumental that hit #1 R&B and stayed there for eight weeks — is the foundational document; Walter cupping a bullet mic against an amp, overdriving the tubes, treating the harmonica like a saxophone. *My Babe* (Dixon, rewriting "This Train"), *Blues with a Feeling*, *Mean Old World*, *Off the Wall*, *Sad Hours*, *Last Night*, *Key to the Highway*, *You're So Fine*: the vocabulary every Chicago harp player after him — James Cotton, Junior Wells, Big Walter Horton, Carey Bell, Paul Butterfield, Rod Piazza — was, knowingly or not, transcribing.

Scaruffi treats Walter as one of the genre's three or four indispensable individual musicians (alongside Wolf, Muddy, and Hubert Sumlin). Fantano's read, extrapolated: any time a single player invents a sound that becomes the default for the next seventy years, the catalog from that player is canon. Walter played on most of Muddy's classic 1950s sides as a sideman before launching his own career, and that double duty — sideman and leader, sometimes on the same Chess sessions — gives his catalog a peculiar density: the listener encounters the same player as foundational support and as the named star, often within months of each other. Walter's recording career was tragically short — he died in 1968 at thirty-seven from injuries sustained in a street fight — but the playing on this disc is so totalizing that the brevity feels like part of the architecture. Of all the *His Best* compilations, this is the one whose every track sounds like a primary source. Both critics agreed the placement is unimpeachable; the only argument was whether the harmonica's centrality to Chicago Blues warranted bumping Walter above Muddy. It does not, but the question is reasonable.

##### 4. Howlin' Wolf — *Howlin' Wolf* ("The Rocking Chair Album", Chess, 1962)

The rare proper album from the genre's classical period, sequenced as an LP rather than a singles assemblage. Cover: an empty rocking chair, electric guitar leaning against it. Inside: *Shake for Me*, *The Red Rooster*, *You'll Be Mine*, *Who's Been Talking*, *Wang Dang Doodle*, *Little Baby*, *Spoonful*, *Going Down Slow*, *Down in the Bottom*, *Back Door Man*, *Howlin' for My Darling*, *Tell Me*. Almost every track became part of the British blues-rock liturgy within five years — the Stones cut "The Red Rooster", Cream cut "Spoonful", the Doors cut "Back Door Man", Led Zeppelin cribbed bits of several.

Scaruffi prizes this as the moment where the Wolf-Dixon-Sumlin troika is captured in something like album form, with Sumlin's playing — angular, unsettled, the prototype for everything Keith Richards would do — at its sharpest. Fantano, reading the Pitchfork-and-after consensus he'd inherit, would treat *Rocking Chair* as the entry-point Wolf record because it's the one where "real album" listeners can engage without excavating compilations. The argument for placing it above #4 is real (Scaruffi briefly tried; Fantano tabled it on the grounds that the *His Best* compilation contains a superset of this material plus *Smokestack Lightning*). The unique value of *Rocking Chair* against the compilations is the sequencing — the way "Shake for Me" and "The Red Rooster" open the side, the way "Spoonful" lands at the dead center, the way "Back Door Man" and "Howlin' for My Darling" close it — which gives the material an album shape that no compilation can replicate. For the listener who wants Chicago Blues as an LP experience rather than a hits collection, this is the first record to reach for.

##### 5. Hound Dog Taylor and the HouseRockers — *Hound Dog Taylor and the HouseRockers* (Alligator, 1971)

Bruce Iglauer founded Alligator Records to release this album because no other label would. Theodore Roosevelt Taylor — six-fingered Mississippian, Chicago South Side fixture, Sears Silvertone solid-body guitar, no bass player — recorded this in two nights for $900 with second guitarist Brewer Phillips and drummer Ted Harvey. The result is the rawest LP in this canon: distorted slide, no overdubs, no tuning between takes, the band audibly drinking. *She's Gone*, *Walking the Ceiling*, *Held My Baby Last Night*, *Taylor's Rock*, *It's Alright*, *Phillip's Theme*, *Wild About You Baby*: boogie blues stripped to studs.

This is Fantano's strongest pick on the list, and the one he most aggressively advocated for. His read, extrapolated: this is the only entry in the upper canon that exists fully as an *album experience*, recorded as a band, sequenced as an LP, no singles-comp scaffolding. It also captures the genre's Maxwell Street / Florence's Lounge club reality more honestly than the Chess studio sides — three guys, two guitars and drums, playing for tips. Scaruffi was unconvinced at first (he tends to slot revivalist or post-1970 Chicago material lower) but conceded that the rawness here is closer to the Wolf principle than most of the polished 1970s catalog. A coin-flip kept it above *West Side Soul*.

##### 6. Magic Sam — *West Side Soul* (Delmark, 1967)

Samuel Maghett, dead at thirty-two of a heart attack in 1969, left two studio albums; this is the one. Recorded in two sessions for Bob Koester's Delmark Records, *West Side Soul* is the document of Chicago's "West Side" sound — younger than Muddy and Wolf, more soul-inflected, B.B. King's single-string attack rerouted through a Fender Twin and a tremolo arm. *That's All I Need*, *I Need You So Bad*, *I Feel So Good (I Wanna Boogie)*, *All of Your Love*, *I Don't Want No Woman*, *Sweet Home Chicago* (the version that became, retroactively, the song's standard reading), *My Love Will Never Die*, *Lookin' Good*, *Mama Mama Talk to Your Daughter*, *Every Night and Every Day*.

Scaruffi treats *West Side Soul* as one of the genre's great proper LPs — a coherent statement rather than a compilation — and rates Magic Sam's vibrato as the most expressive on any Chicago guitar record. Fantano, extrapolated, would respond to the emotional saturation: Sam sings exhausted, like a man who knows he's running out of time, and the playing has a vulnerability that the older generation's records, for all their power, generally don't. The placement above Otis Rush is contested; both critics flagged it as a coin-flip (resolved below).

##### 7. Buddy Guy — *The Complete Chess Studio Recordings* (Chess, 1992)

Two discs covering Buddy Guy's 1960–1967 Chess sides, including the legendary 1965 Junior Wells / Buddy Guy material and Guy's solo Chess output. The Chess years are the ones where Leonard Chess, by Guy's own account, kept telling him to "play it more like Muddy, less like that crazy stuff" — and where Guy's "crazy stuff", suppressed on the released sides, leaked through anyway in the form of the most aggressive guitar tone of the early 1960s. *First Time I Met the Blues*, *Stone Crazy*, *Let Me Love You Baby*, *My Time After Awhile*, *I Got My Eyes On You*, *Leave My Girl Alone*, *No Lie*: the material that, smuggled through a Marshall stack, became Hendrix and Stevie Ray Vaughan's grammar.

Scaruffi privileges the Cobra/Chess Buddy Guy decisively over the later, slicker Silvertone-era records (*Damn Right, I've Got the Blues* and after), arguing that the young Guy — pulled between Leonard Chess's conservatism and his own future-leaning attack — produces the more interesting tension. Fantano, extrapolated, lands in the same place: the rawer, the better. Guy's later Grammy-winning vehicle albums are professionally made, but the version of Buddy Guy that mattered to American guitar history is the one on these Chess sides. The sideman work on this set is also extraordinary — Guy plays guitar behind Muddy on the 1964 *Folk Singer* sessions, behind Junior Wells on the 1965 *Hoodoo Man Blues* sessions (credited as "Friendly Chap" for contractual reasons), and behind Koko Taylor on her Chess sides — and the breadth of that sideman footprint, combined with the leader work collected here, makes Guy the most consequential Chicago Blues guitarist of the 1960s by a clear margin.

##### 8. Otis Rush — *Cobra Recordings 1956–1958* (Paula/Flyright/Fuel, multiple reissues)

Otis Rush cut eight singles for Eli Toscano's Cobra Records on Chicago's West Side between 1956 and 1958, and those sixteen sides — collected on various reissues, most reliably the Fuel 2000 *His Cobra Recordings 1956–1958* and the Paula *Cobra Sessions* — are the foundational documents of West Side Chicago Blues. *I Can't Quit You Baby*, *My Love Will Never Die*, *Double Trouble*, *All Your Love (I Miss Loving)*, *Groaning the Blues*, *Three Times a Fool*, *It Takes Time*, *Checking on My Baby*: a body of work that, before Rush turned twenty-five, established the harmonic and emotional vocabulary that Mike Bloomfield, Peter Green, Eric Clapton, and Stevie Ray Vaughan would all later mine.

Rush's left-handed, upside-down guitar attack and his extraordinary gospel-soaked tenor make these sides the most emotionally torqued in the Chicago canon outside Wolf. Willie Dixon produced and wrote much of the material; Ike Turner played guitar on some of the sessions. Scaruffi rates the Cobra material with Rush's strongest later work (the Atlantic *Mourning in the Morning*, the Delmark *So Many Roads*) but treats the 1956–1958 sides as the irreducible core. Fantano, extrapolating, would respond to the unmediated quality of the singing — Rush's voice is the one in this canon that most resembles a soul singer's rather than a bluesman's, and the records hit accordingly.

##### 9. Junior Wells — *Hoodoo Man Blues* (Delmark, 1965)

Recorded in a single afternoon at Sound Studios in Chicago, *Hoodoo Man Blues* was, by some accounts, the first proper full-length blues *album* — conceived and sequenced as an LP rather than a singles package — recorded by a working Chicago band. Junior Wells (vocals, harmonica), "Friendly Chap" (Buddy Guy, contractually disguised) on guitar, Jack Myers on bass, Bill Warren on drums. *Snatch It Back and Hold It*, *Ships on the Ocean*, *Good Morning Schoolgirl*, *Hound Dog*, *In the Wee Wee Hours*, *Hey Lawdy Mama*, *Hoodoo Man Blues*, *Early in the Morning*, *We're Ready*, *You Don't Love Me*, *Chitlin Con Carne*, *Yonder Wall*.

This is the album that taught the post-1965 blues world what a Chicago Blues *LP* could be: not a singles compilation, not a live record, but a proper long-form document of a working club band. Wells's harp playing is the post-Walter standard, less acrobatic than Walter's but with deeper pocket and grit; Buddy Guy's guitar work is freer here than on his contemporaneous Chess sides because Leonard Chess wasn't in the room. Both critics agreed without much debate: *Hoodoo Man Blues* belongs in the top ten on its album-form merits alone. Scaruffi flagged that it's also one of the few entries here recorded post-1960 that he ranks alongside the 1950s material.

##### 10. Koko Taylor — *I Got What It Takes* (Alligator, 1975)

Cora Walton, the Memphis-born, Chicago-raised Queen of the Blues, recorded for Chess from 1964 (cutting Willie Dixon's "Wang Dang Doodle" — a #4 R&B hit) through 1970, but her Chess material was never assembled into a definitive LP during her tenure. *I Got What It Takes*, her 1975 Alligator debut after Bruce Iglauer signed her, is the album where her voice — coarser than any Chicago Blues vocalist except Wolf, with a similar capacity for sheer physical violence — was finally captured at full length and properly sequenced. Backing band: Mighty Joe Young on guitar, with the kind of small Alligator combo that would define the label.

The case for Koko Taylor at #10 was the longest argument in the convening. Scaruffi tends to undercount her relative to the male canon; Fantano, extrapolating, pushed to include her on grounds that her voice is one of the few in postwar Chicago Blues that can stand next to Wolf without flinching, and that excluding the only major woman in a tradition that included Memphis Minnie, Big Mama Thornton, and Etta James would distort the record. Scaruffi conceded. The album itself is a strong, if not transcendent, document — the live records and the later Alligator material (*The Earthshaker*, 1978) are arguably equal — but *I Got What It Takes* is the cleanest single-LP entry into her catalog, and the inclusion is correct. Taylor's career as a Chess artist is also worth flagging: her 1966 *Wang Dang Doodle* single, with Willie Dixon producing and Buddy Guy on guitar, was the last major hit Chess Blues produced before the label's late-1960s decline, and her Alligator transition in the 1970s mirrored the broader migration of Chicago Blues from Chess's institutional infrastructure to Bruce Iglauer's leaner, artist-friendlier independent. The Alligator-era Koko is, in that sense, both the Chess tradition's continuation and its critique.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Three placements went to coin flip after debate hit a wall. Noted explicitly so the reader can see where the canon is genuinely contested rather than settled:

1. **Hound Dog Taylor (#5) vs Magic Sam (#6).** Scaruffi argued Magic Sam's *West Side Soul* is the more sophisticated record and should sit higher. Fantano countered that *Hound Dog Taylor and the HouseRockers* better represents the genre as live-event raw material and the broader Alligator-era continuation. Coin flip: Taylor above Sam. Honest reading: these can swap without anyone losing the argument.

2. **Otis Rush Cobra sides (#8) vs Buddy Guy Chess (#7).** Both critics rated these tightly. Scaruffi leaned slightly toward Rush (the singing); Fantano leaned slightly toward Guy (the guitar's downstream influence on rock). Coin flip: Guy above Rush. Either order is defensible; both would be consensus top-ten in any serious Chicago Blues canon.

3. **Koko Taylor (#10) vs Otis Spann.** The displaced entry was Otis Spann's *The Blues Is Where It's At* (BluesWay, 1966) or *The Bottom of the Blues* (BluesWay, 1968), both strong Spann-as-leader records. Argument against Spann at the leader level: his greatest work is the piano on *every other Chicago Blues record in this list*, particularly the Muddy and Wolf sides. Argument for Koko: representational completeness and vocal force. Coin flip: Taylor in, Spann out at the leader level — but Spann is, in a real sense, present on half the entries above. Worth flagging as the closest call on the list.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **Otis Spann — *The Blues Is Where It's At* (BluesWay, 1966).** Spann at the keyboard with Muddy's band in support. The piano-led Chicago Blues LP. Scaruffi's preferred Spann-as-leader record.
- **Otis Spann — *Otis Spann Is the Blues* (Candid, 1960).** Duo session with Robert Lockwood Jr.; spare, intimate, the rare quiet record in this catalog.
- **Buddy Guy & Junior Wells — *Hoodoo Man Blues* live counterpart / *Buddy and the Juniors* (Blue Thumb, 1970).** Acoustic side of the Wells/Guy partnership. Worth seeking.
- **James Cotton — *100% Cotton* (Buddah, 1974).** The strongest LP from the post-Walter Chicago harp generation.
- **Willie Dixon — *I Am the Blues* (Columbia, 1970).** Dixon performing his own catalog — "Hoochie Coochie Man", "Spoonful", "Back Door Man", "I Can't Quit You Baby", "Little Red Rooster" — across a single LP. Documentary value enormous; performance value real if not transcendent.
- **Howlin' Wolf — *Moanin' in the Moonlight* (Chess, 1959).** The first Wolf LP. Slimmer than the *Rocking Chair Album* but historically primary; "Smokestack Lightning" lives here.
- **Muddy Waters — *Hard Again* (Blue Sky, 1977).** Johnny Winter–produced, James Cotton on harp, Muddy late but undimmed. The strongest of his 1970s comeback records; both critics noted it but kept the canonical Muddy slot for *The Anthology*.
- **Muddy Waters — *Folk Singer* (Chess, 1964).** Acoustic Muddy with young Buddy Guy on second guitar. The genre's great quiet record. Scaruffi-favored; only excluded from the top ten because the electric Muddy material is more historically central.
- **Paul Butterfield Blues Band — *The Paul Butterfield Blues Band* (Elektra, 1965).** Integrated Chicago Blues band, Mike Bloomfield on guitar, Sam Lay (Wolf's drummer) on drums. Sometimes excluded from "Chicago Blues" canon on demographic grounds; admitted here as honorable mention because the playing and the lineage are unimpeachable.
- **Hound Dog Taylor — *Natural Boogie* (Alligator, 1973).** The follow-up to the debut; equally raw, slightly less essential.
- **Magic Sam — *Black Magic* (Delmark, 1968).** Sam's second and final studio album; the equal of *West Side Soul* on a different day.
- **Otis Rush — *Mourning in the Morning* (Cotillion, 1969).** Rush at Muscle Shoals with Duane Allman in attendance. Scaruffi-respected, lost the cut to the Cobra material on primary-source grounds.
- **Various Artists — *Chess Blues* (MCA box set, 1992).** Four-disc institutional document of the entire Chess Blues catalog. Not an "album" in the conventional sense but the genre's most comprehensive single object.

---

*Compiled per assignment workflow: independent top-15 lists, four-round debate, three coin-flip resolutions noted explicitly. Fantano commentary is extrapolated where direct on-record statements are sparse and flagged in-line. All listed albums and compilations are real releases verifiable against Chess/Alligator/Delmark/Cobra/BluesWay catalog records.*

---

### Texas Blues

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Texas Blues is a difficult tradition to canonize because it lives uneasily across three eras that don't share a common aesthetic. There is the pre-war country blues lineage of Blind Lemon Jefferson and his successor Lightnin' Hopkins; the post-war electric/jump-blues invention of T-Bone Walker, who arguably did more to shape the modern electric guitar than any other single figure; and the late-20th-century blues-rock revival represented by Johnny Winter, Stevie Ray Vaughan, and the Fabulous Thunderbirds. Any honest top ten has to negotiate all three without pretending they're equivalent.

Scaruffi opened with the position that the entire idiom is essentially the long shadow of T-Bone Walker. In his framework, Walker's late-1940s Black & White and Imperial sides are the foundational document — every Texas guitarist after him is a footnote to *Call It Stormy Monday*. He places Lightnin' Hopkins second in the canon, valuing the Folkways and Aladdin material over the more polished later Prestige sessions, and he is openly suspicious of the SRV/Winter axis, which he considers a regression toward white blues-rock orthodoxy rather than a genuine extension of the form. He pushed hard for Mance Lipscomb and was reluctant to let Stevie Ray Vaughan above the cut line at all.

Fantano came in from the opposite direction. He takes the foundational Walker/Hopkins material as given but argued that a Texas Blues canon that buries SRV is performing a kind of historical purity test that the listening public has rejected for forty years. He pushed *Texas Flood* as not merely a virtuoso showcase but a genuinely re-energizing record for the form, and he made a strong case for Albert Collins and Freddie King as the sub-genre's most underrated figures. He was, however, willing to concede that *In Step* (1989) over-produces SRV, and that the Johnny Winter blues-rock catalog is closer to "guitar-hero schlock" than the Texas tradition proper.

The four flashpoints were:

1. **How many T-Bone Walker compilations belong on a top-10?** Scaruffi wanted two; Fantano said one is enough and you're stacking the deck.
2. **SRV's ranking.** Scaruffi had him at #9 or off; Fantano had him at #4.
3. **Albert Collins vs Freddie King.** Both critics like both, but disagreed about which best record represents each.
4. **Lightnin' Hopkins — which compilation?** Scaruffi's preferred *The Complete Aladdin Recordings* vs Fantano's preference for *Lightnin' Hopkins* (Smithsonian Folkways, 1959).

Two issues went to the coin: the second T-Bone slot (Scaruffi won), and the SRV ranking (compromise at #5 rather than #4 or #9).

A useful frame for the disagreement: Scaruffi reads Texas Blues as a tradition that effectively peaked between 1947 and 1962 — Walker's Black & White and Imperial sides, Hopkins's Aladdin and Folkways sessions, Gatemouth's Peacock material, Freddie King's instrumental King Records run, Lipscomb's Arhoolie debut. Everything after 1962 is, in his framing, either a continuation (the early Albert Collins instrumentals) or a regression (most blues-rock). Fantano reads the tradition as continuous through the 1980s revival — SRV, *Showdown!*, the late-career Albert Collins and Gatemouth Brown records — and resists the implication that the form ossified after 1962. The compromise canon below leans toward Scaruffi's view chronologically (six of ten records are pre-1965) but accepts Fantano's argument that the 1980s revival belongs (three of ten records are 1983–1985). The single 1969 record (*Second Winter*) is the only one neither critic was fully comfortable with, and made the cut on a specific argument about *Memory Pain* rather than on Winter's catalog as a whole.

A note on what's deliberately excluded. Buddy Guy is sometimes claimed for Texas via the southern-electric-blues affinity, but he is more accurately Louisiana-to-Chicago and lives in the Chicago Blues file. Albert King is Mississippi-to-Memphis and likewise excluded despite his enormous influence on SRV. ZZ Top, despite the band name and the Texas residency, is genre-coded as boogie-rock and lives in the blues-rock file. Big Mama Thornton recorded extensively in Houston for Peacock but is better understood as a national R&B figure. The intent is to keep this file focused on the specifically Texas-rooted tradition rather than the broader southern electric blues continuum, which is the subject of the electric-blues file in this volume.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. T-Bone Walker — *The Complete Imperial Recordings, 1950–1954* (1991, Imperial/EMI)

Both critics agreed without argument: this is the bedrock document of post-war Texas Blues, and arguably of the entire electric blues guitar tradition. The Imperial sides catch Walker at the moment his innovations had stopped being novelties and become a vocabulary — the single-string lead lines, the ninth-chord comping, the relaxed horn-driven swing, the sung-spoken vocal phrasing that made the guitar feel like a second voice in dialogue with the first. Scaruffi notes that B.B. King, Chuck Berry, and Albert Collins all built their styles on top of moves Walker made on these recordings, and Fantano agrees that any honest accounting of how rock guitar got to where it is in 1965 has to start here rather than at Robert Johnson.

What's striking about the Imperial material specifically — versus the earlier Black & White sides — is the maturity. *Strollin' with Bones*, *Blue Mood*, *Cold Cold Feeling*, *Party Girl* — Walker is no longer trying to prove he can play electric guitar; he's using it as an expressive instrument with the relaxed authority of a first-rank jazz horn player. The arrangements feature sympathetic small horn sections that frame the guitar without competing with it, and Walker's vocals are warmer and more confident than on the more famous earlier *Stormy Monday* take. Scaruffi calls Walker "the Charlie Christian of the blues," and on the Imperial sides that comparison is most defensible — there's a fluency and an architectural sense of solo construction that almost no blues guitarist before or since has matched. If you had to teach a student where electric blues comes from with a single record, this is the record.

##### 2. Lightnin' Hopkins — *Lightnin' Hopkins* (1959, Smithsonian Folkways)

Recorded by Sam Charters in a Houston rooming house in early 1959 with a single microphone, this is the first widely circulated document of Hopkins as a folk-blues figure rather than a regional R&B journeyman, and both critics agreed it belongs near the top. Scaruffi was emphatic: this is closer in spirit to Blind Lemon Jefferson than to any of the post-war electric Texas players, and it captures Hopkins's most distinctive quality — the way he treats meter as a suggestion rather than a constraint. Songs end when the thought ends. Verses are however long they need to be. The guitar accompaniment is improvised in real time around the vocal, not the other way around.

Fantano, somewhat surprisingly, pushed back on Scaruffi's preference for this record over *The Complete Aladdin Recordings* and conceded only after replaying it. The argument that won him over was the directness of the document — there's no band, no producer, no arrangement, just a man with a guitar in a room talking to a microphone for an hour. The political-economic content of *Penitentiary Blues* and *Bad Luck and Trouble* is unmediated, and the playing has the casual virtuosity of someone who has been doing this for thirty years and has nothing to prove. The Smithsonian Folkways recording is also crucial historically — it was the record that brought Hopkins into the early-1960s folk revival circuit, which in turn led to the Prestige sessions, the Carnegie Hall appearance, and the late-career re-canonization. Without this album the folk-blues archive looks substantially different.

##### 3. T-Bone Walker — *The Complete Recordings of T-Bone Walker, 1940–1954* (1990, Mosaic)

This is the album that Scaruffi won on coin-flip — Fantano argued one Walker comp was sufficient, but Scaruffi insisted that the pre-Imperial Black & White material (1946–1948, including the original *Call It Stormy Monday (But Tuesday Is Just as Bad)*) is too foundational to relegate to honorable mention. After flipping for it, both critics agreed the inclusion was correct.

The Mosaic box (six discs in its original configuration) is the only way to hear the Black & White sides in proper sequence, including the late-1947 session that produced *Stormy Monday* — the song that essentially established the slow-blues ballad as a genre and that the Allman Brothers, B.B. King, and a hundred others would later cover. What's audible in the Black & White material that's less audible later is the connection to the swing-era big bands Walker came up in: the time feel is jump-blues time, not the heavier shuffle time that became standard later. The guitar is more Charlie Christian and less B.B. King. For the historian, the Mosaic box is irreplaceable; for the casual listener, the overlap with the Imperial set is real but the Black & White material sounds materially different and earns its place. Scaruffi's argument was that without two Walker entries the canon misrepresents how dominant his shadow is, and Fantano accepted that framing post-coin.

##### 4. Stevie Ray Vaughan and Double Trouble — *Texas Flood* (1983, Epic)

Fantano's pick, and the album that — over Scaruffi's reservations — re-anchored Texas Blues as a living tradition for a generation that had largely forgotten it. *Texas Flood* was recorded in two days at Jackson Browne's studio for almost no money, and it sounds like it: live, unmixed in any aggressive sense, room-tone audible, the band playing as a band rather than as a constructed studio object. Vaughan was 28, had been gigging in Austin clubs for a decade, and had just been signed by John Hammond after his Montreux appearance.

Scaruffi's objection — that this is essentially an Albert King and Jimi Hendrix synthesis with a Texas accent rather than a true continuation of the Walker/Hopkins line — has merit, but Fantano's counter is also correct: the synthesis itself is the Texas tradition. T-Bone Walker synthesized Charlie Christian and Lonnie Johnson; Freddie King synthesized Walker and Robert Junior Lockwood; SRV synthesized King and Hendrix and Albert King and added something of his own — the tone, the attack, and the rhythmic specificity. *Pride and Joy*, *Texas Flood*, *Lenny*, and *Rude Mood* are not just guitar showcases; they're songs with shape, dynamics, and emotional content. The instrumental *Lenny* in particular is one of the most beautiful guitar ballads ever recorded by a blues player, and the title track's slow-blues phrasing is genuinely Walker-derived. Both critics ultimately agreed: removing this record from the top ten is purity-test thinking.

##### 5. Freddie King — *Let's Hide Away and Dance Away with Freddy King* (1961, King Records)

An all-instrumental album, and one of the most influential blues guitar records ever made — Eric Clapton has said repeatedly that he learned to play by copying *Hide Away* and *San-Ho-Zay* note for note. Both critics had this in their top five, and the only debate was whether to include it instead of or alongside Albert Collins's *Truckin' with Albert Collins*. They settled on King here and Collins below.

Freddie King was Texas-born (Gilmer, 1934) and Chicago-trained, and this record sits at the exact point of synthesis. The repertoire is built around boogie shuffles and slow blues, with a tight quartet arrangement that frames King's distinctive snap-and-release picking — he played with a plastic thumb pick and a metal fingerpick, which gave the leads a sharp attack that no other major Texas player had. *Hide Away* itself is the most-covered blues instrumental of the post-war era, structured as a medley of riffs from earlier Chicago tunes that King welded into a single composition. *The Stumble*, *Sen-Sa-Shun*, and *Side Tracked* round out a record that has no weak tracks and almost no vocals — just thirty-odd minutes of demonstration of how a Texas-rooted player handles the Chicago electric idiom. Fantano called it "the cleanest, most teachable blues guitar record ever cut," and Scaruffi conceded that for an instrumental record it has unusual compositional weight — these aren't jams, they're songs.

##### 6. Albert Collins, Robert Cray, Johnny Copeland — *Showdown!* (1985, Alligator)

Collins's signature record is contested — *Truckin' with Albert Collins* (1969) has the famous instrumental work, *Ice Pickin'* (1978) is the late-career comeback statement, but *Showdown!* is the album that won the Grammy and that both critics ultimately preferred for the canon because it sets Collins in dialogue with two younger players (Robert Cray) and a peer (Johnny Copeland) without diluting his identity. The production is clean, the band is tight, and Collins's "cool sound" — open F-minor tuning, capo high on the neck, fingerpicked stinging single-note lines — is captured better here than on the earlier raw recordings.
