The case Fantano made for this record over the more historically pure Collins material was that *Showdown!* shows what Texas Blues sounds like when it's healthy — three players from three generations, two from Texas (Collins and Copeland) and one Texas-influenced (Cray, from Georgia/Washington but unmistakably indebted to the Texas line), playing with each other rather than at each other. The original compositions and the covers (*The Moon Is Full*, *T-Bone Shuffle*) are arranged for ensemble interplay rather than guitar showcase. Scaruffi was less enthusiastic — he prefers the rougher *Truckin'* — but accepted this as the correct top-10 entry on the grounds that it's the Collins record with the most replay value and the clearest documentary evidence of how the tradition propagates between generations. *T-Bone Shuffle* in particular is a beautifully chosen tribute to Walker that closes the loop.

##### 7. Johnny Winter — *Second Winter* (1969, Columbia)

The Johnny Winter inclusion was the longest debate in the session. Scaruffi's view is that Winter's catalog is mostly blues-rock guitar-hero material that doesn't extend the Texas tradition meaningfully, and Fantano largely agreed — but both eventually accepted *Second Winter* on the grounds that it is the one Winter record where the playing transcends the genre. The album is famously a three-sided double LP (the fourth side is blank), recorded in Nashville in 1969 with brother Edgar on keys and saxophone. It contains *Highway 61 Revisited* and *Johnny B. Goode* covers that aren't easy choices, but also *Memory Pain*, *I Hate Everybody*, *I Love Everybody*, and a *Slippin' and Slidin'* that re-frames Little Richard as a Texas blues artist.

What's defensible about *Second Winter* in a Texas Blues canon — versus the more famous self-titled debut — is the looseness. Winter is not trying to be a guitar god here. The arrangements are loose, the vocals are unaffected, and the slide playing on *Memory Pain* in particular is genuinely Texas-rooted in a way that owes more to Hopkins than to British blues-rock. Fantano's argument that won Scaruffi over was that the *Memory Pain* performance alone — the slow, drawled phrasing, the unhurried slide work, the relaxed band feel — does what *Texas Flood* does fifteen years earlier and arguably with less production polish. It's the Winter album that doesn't sound dated, and the one that earns the Texas Blues label rather than the blues-rock label. Both critics flagged that this was a generous inclusion but agreed it was the right one if Winter belongs in the canon at all.

##### 8. Mance Lipscomb — *Texas Songster* (1960, Arhoolie)

Scaruffi's most strongly held minor pick. Lipscomb was a Navasota, Texas farmer who recorded for the first time at age 65, and the Arhoolie debut — produced by Mack McCormick and Chris Strachwitz — is one of the foundational documents of the early-1960s blues revival. Lipscomb called himself a "songster" rather than a bluesman, and the repertoire reflects that: country blues, ragtime, ballads, spirituals, and dance pieces sit alongside more conventional twelve-bar material. The fingerpicked guitar style is closer to Mississippi John Hurt or Gary Davis than to Lightnin' Hopkins, but the geographic and cultural context is unmistakably Texas.

Fantano's initial objection was that this is more accurately a country-blues or pre-blues record and belongs in the Volume 1 country-blues file rather than the Texas Blues file. Scaruffi's counter, which won the argument, was that any Texas Blues canon that excludes the songster tradition is mistaking the post-war electric idiom for the whole story. T-Bone Walker came out of the same Dallas-Houston cultural matrix that produced Lipscomb; treating Walker as Texas Blues and Lipscomb as something else is a category error. The record itself is gentle, conversational, and unhurried — songs like *Sugar Babe* and *Mama Don't 'Low* are as much social documents as musical ones, and Lipscomb's voice has the patient authority of someone who has lived the songs rather than learned them. It's the most "rural" record in this canon and the most necessary corrective to the electric-guitar-hero version of Texas Blues history.

##### 9. Clarence "Gatemouth" Brown — *The Original Peacock Recordings* (1983, Rounder)

A late-arriving compilation of the 1949–1959 Peacock sides, and the entry that both critics agreed needed to be in the top ten because Gatemouth is the missing link between T-Bone Walker's jump blues and the later Texas R&B sound that fed into soul-blues. Brown was Walker's contemporary and rival — there's a famous story about him grabbing Walker's guitar at a 1947 Houston club date and playing *Gatemouth Boogie* on the spot — and the Peacock material catches him at his most distinctive: a guitarist who could also play fiddle, a bandleader who treated Texas Blues, swing, country, and Cajun as a single continuous tradition.

Songs like *Okie Dokie Stomp*, *Boogie Rambler*, *Just Before Dawn*, and *Dirty Work at the Crossroads* feature the trademark Brown approach: tight horn arrangements, fast shuffles, and guitar work that's more clipped and percussive than Walker's more legato style. Fantano made the case that Brown's catalog is the most under-appreciated in the canon — partly because his later genre-jumping (he won a country Grammy in 1981) confused the marketplace, and partly because the Peacock sides were out of print for decades. Scaruffi agreed and added that Brown is essential to understanding how the Texas tradition stayed alive between Walker's commercial peak in the late 1940s and the Freddie King breakthrough in the early 1960s — without him there's a gap. The Rounder compilation is the right document; the more recent reissues have similar material but worse sequencing.

##### 10. Stevie Ray Vaughan and Double Trouble — *Couldn't Stand the Weather* (1984, Epic)

The closing slot was contested, with Scaruffi pushing for ZZ Hill's *Down Home* (1982, Malaco) — the soul-blues crossover that anchored Malaco's run — and Fantano arguing for the second SRV record. The compromise resolution was that *Down Home* is more accurately a soul-blues record (it lives in the soul-blues file in this volume) and that *Couldn't Stand the Weather* deserves the slot because it shows SRV consolidating rather than repeating the *Texas Flood* breakthrough.

The album is more ambitious than its predecessor: the title track is a slow blues with an almost orchestral build, *Tin Pan Alley* is a nine-minute slow blues that's arguably Vaughan's deepest single performance, the Hendrix cover (*Voodoo Child (Slight Return)*) is the rare cover that survives the comparison, and *Scuttle Buttin'* is two minutes of pure technique that justify themselves as a kind of statement. The production by Jim Capaldi, Jack Bonus, and the band is warmer and slightly more layered than *Texas Flood* without losing the live feel. Fantano's argument that won the slot was that *Tin Pan Alley* alone is one of the great slow blues performances of the post-Walker era, and that hearing it adjacent to the actual Walker recordings on this list demonstrates the continuity of the Texas slow-blues tradition across forty years. Scaruffi remained skeptical of the SRV-heavy weighting but conceded the second entry on the strength of *Tin Pan Alley* specifically. The ZZ Hill record was moved to soul-blues.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Two flashpoints went to the coin after extended debate failed to resolve them:

1. **The second T-Bone Walker slot.** Scaruffi insisted on two Walker entries on the grounds that the pre-Imperial Black & White material (Mosaic box, *Stormy Monday*, etc.) is so foundational that excluding it would misrepresent the tradition. Fantano's position was that one Walker record is enough and that doubling up on a single artist starves other deserving figures (Albert King, Buddy Guy from Texas affinity, ZZ Hill). Coin went to Scaruffi; the Mosaic compilation took the #3 slot. Note: this directly cost Buddy Guy his marginal slot — Guy is sometimes claimed for Texas Blues via affinity but is more accurately a Louisiana-to-Chicago figure, and Fantano accepted that the omission is defensible regardless of the coin.

2. **SRV's ranking.** Scaruffi had Vaughan at #9 or off; Fantano at #4. Neither was willing to flip on rank itself, so the coin determined whether SRV would land in the top half (Fantano position) or bottom half (Scaruffi position). The coin resolved to top-half, but as a compromise *Texas Flood* landed at #5 rather than #4 — both critics accepted this as a fair midpoint. *Couldn't Stand the Weather* at #10 was a separate decision, not coin-driven.

A third near-coin issue — Albert Collins's representative record — was resolved by debate rather than coin. Scaruffi initially preferred *Truckin' with Albert Collins* (1969); Fantano preferred *Ice Pickin'* (1978) or *Showdown!* (1985). The argument that *Showdown!* both showcases Collins and documents the inter-generational health of the tradition won without flipping.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **Albert Collins — *Ice Pickin'* (1978, Alligator)** — The Bruce Iglauer-produced comeback that re-established Collins after a fallow decade. Came close to displacing *Showdown!* and remains the single best document of Collins as a frontman rather than as a co-leader.

- **Albert Collins — *Truckin' with Albert Collins* (1969, Blue Thumb)** — The instrumental record with *Frosty* and *Sno-Cone*. Scaruffi's preferred Collins record; lost to *Showdown!* on ensemble argument.

- **Johnny Winter — *Johnny Winter* (1969, Columbia)** — The self-titled debut. More famous than *Second Winter*, but more clearly blues-rock and less clearly Texas. Both critics agreed it belongs in the blues-rock file rather than this one.

- **Lightnin' Hopkins — *The Complete Aladdin Recordings* (1991, EMI)** — The 1946–1948 electric Houston sides. Fantano's preferred Hopkins record; lost to the Folkways record on the unmediated-document argument. Essential listening regardless.

- **Freddie King — *Getting Ready...* (1971, Shelter)** — The Leon Russell-produced comeback, with *Going Down*. Closer to blues-rock than the King Records material; placed in honorable mention by genre rather than by quality.

- **ZZ Hill — *Down Home* (1982, Malaco)** — Reassigned to the soul-blues file. *Down Home Blues* is one of the most important blues recordings of the 1980s but the album as a whole is more accurately soul-blues.

- **The Fabulous Thunderbirds — *Tuff Enuff* (1986, CBS Associated)** — Jimmie Vaughan's band; the title-track hit makes it a marginal Texas Blues entry, but the record is more roots-rock than blues. Mentioned for completeness.

- **Big Mama Thornton — *Hound Dog: The Peacock Recordings* (1992 comp, MCA)** — Texas-affiliated via the Houston Peacock sessions, but Thornton is more accurately a national R&B/blues figure. Considered, declined.

- **Blind Lemon Jefferson — *King of the Country Blues* (1985 comp, Yazoo)** — The pre-war Dallas figure who is the deep root of the entire tradition. Both critics agreed Jefferson belongs in the country-blues file rather than this one, though his shadow is on every entry above.

- **Lightnin' Hopkins — *Mojo Hand* (1962, Fire)** — The famous title-track session. A strong second-tier Hopkins record; lost to the Folkways debut.

- **Stevie Ray Vaughan — *In Step* (1989, Epic)** — The post-rehab record with *Crossfire*. Fantano flagged the production as over-polished; both critics agreed it's the weakest top-tier SRV record.

- **Pee Wee Crayton — *Things I Used to Do* (1971, Vanguard)** — Texas-born Walker disciple whose 1948 *Blues After Hours* is essential as a single but whose albums are uneven. Mentioned as a Walker-lineage footnote.

- **Long John Hunter — *Border Town Legend* (1996, Alligator)** — The El Paso/Juárez Lobby Bar veteran whose late-career Alligator records preserve a regional Texas-Mexico border blues sound that almost no other figure on this list represents. Considered seriously, declined on the grounds that the studio output is thin.

- **Lonnie Brooks — *Bayou Lightning* (1979, Alligator)** — Texas-Louisiana hybrid figure (born in Dubuisson, LA, came up on the Texas circuit). Strong record; classified as Louisiana/swamp-blues adjacent rather than core Texas.

- **Stevie Ray Vaughan — *Soul to Soul* (1985, Epic)** — The third SRV studio album, with *Look at Little Sister* and *Change It*. Not as strong as the first two; included for completeness in the SRV discography but not for the canon.

- **Various Artists — *Texas Blues: The Gold Star Sessions* (1990 comp, Arhoolie)** — Late-1940s Houston sides featuring Lightnin' Hopkins, Lil Son Jackson, and others recorded by Bill Quinn for the Gold Star label. A historically essential compilation; both critics flagged it as required listening, but it's a multi-artist anthology rather than an album proper.

- **T-Bone Walker — *T-Bone Blues* (1959, Atlantic)** — The late-1950s Atlantic record with smaller-group arrangements. Sometimes listed as Walker's "comeback" record before the European tours of the 1960s. A fine record, considered briefly, lost on the grounds that the Imperial and Black & White compilations already represent Walker's best work.

#### Closing Note

Texas Blues is in some respects a category invented retroactively — it gathers musicians who happened to share a state and a broad cultural matrix but who often had less in common with each other than they did with players from elsewhere. T-Bone Walker has more in common with Charlie Christian than with Mance Lipscomb. Lightnin' Hopkins has more in common with Mississippi John Hurt than with Stevie Ray Vaughan. Freddie King has more in common with the Chicago players he recorded alongside than with Gatemouth Brown.

What ties this list together is not a sound but a transmission lineage — a chain of players who learned from each other, played each other's clubs, and carried the same body of repertoire forward across seventy years. T-Bone Walker taught Gatemouth Brown by example and Albert Collins by direct contact. Collins taught the young SRV at Antone's in Austin. Hopkins played the same Houston circuits as Walker and influenced Mance Lipscomb's late-career profile through shared producers. Freddie King's *Hide Away* became the universal blues guitarist's etude, learned by both Texas players and British players in the same five-year window. The canon above is, more than anything, a record of that transmission — which is why both critics agreed it was worth fighting over.

---

### Electric Blues

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

This list treats Electric Blues as the post-WWII tradition of amplified blues considered as a unified continuum — wherever that electrification happened. Because Chicago Blues and Texas Blues each get their own dedicated files in this volume, the brief here was to prioritize records that bridge regional traditions or that resist clean regional categorization: Detroit-based migrants like John Lee Hooker, Mississippi-to-Chicago electric slide players like Elmore James, Louisiana swamp blues like Slim Harpo, the Memphis-but-transcendent BB King, the West Side Chicago hybridists like Magic Sam, and the cross-regional stylists Albert King and Jimmy Reed. A handful of canonical Chicago records are still permitted where they are simply the foundational documents of what "electric blues" means as a genre — Howlin' Wolf and Muddy Waters being the obvious cases — but each entry is justified on cross-regional grounds rather than as Chicago-canon placeholders. Treat this, then, as a "best regional bridges and canonical electric blues records" list rather than a strict rejection of any record set in Chicago or Texas.

The two critic profiles diverge on what the canon is for. Scaruffi sees electric blues primarily as proto-rock — the moment the blues was forced into the harmonic and rhythmic vocabulary that everything from the British Invasion to garage to hard rock was built on. He privileges raw electrification, hypnotic boogie, and singer-as-totem, which puts John Lee Hooker, Howlin' Wolf, and the Live At The Regal era of BB King high on his list. Fantano, whose direct commentary on this material is sparse, has been extrapolated and flagged where necessary; his general critical priors — emotional power, formal innovation, sequence integrity, and a soft spot for Hooker — were used to weight his picks. Where Fantano's preferences had to be inferred from his framework rather than explicit ranked statements, the entry flags it.

The debate ran six rounds. Most of the top six were uncontested. The middle of the list (entries 5 through 8) was contested across two regional axes: how much weight to give Louisiana swamp (Slim Harpo, Lightnin' Slim) versus West Coast jump-derived electric (T-Bone Walker, Lowell Fulson), and whether Albert King's *Born Under A Bad Sign* is a soul record that can't be claimed for blues. Three irreconcilables went to a coin flip, noted explicitly below. Honorable mentions catch the records that lost a tight final cut.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. B.B. King — *Live At The Regal* (1965)

Recorded November 21, 1964 at the Regal Theater on Chicago's South Side and released the following year on ABC-Paramount, this is the record both critics put first without argument. Scaruffi treats Live At The Regal as the canonical BB King document and as one of the foundational records of electric blues period — the moment the genre's most articulate guitar voice met an audience who could finish his phrases and a band tight enough to disappear under him. Fantano's framework values live records that capture a performer at the apex of audience contract; this fits exactly.

The structural argument is that Live At The Regal solves a problem electric blues records had been struggling with through the early 60s — the studio polish that had crept into post-Kent BB sessions was draining the air out of the music. The Regal date puts him back in front of a crowd that knows the lyrics, and the eight-song program sequences his strongest material to that point. "Every Day I Have The Blues" opens at a clip; "Sweet Little Angel" and "It's My Own Fault" sit in the middle as showcases for the bend-and-vibrato vocabulary that became his signature; "How Blue Can You Get" lands the comic monologue and the slow burn payoff that every imitator since has tried to reproduce.

What makes it cross-regional rather than pure Memphis or pure Chicago is exactly that synthesis. King grew up in Mississippi, came through Memphis radio, and by 1964 was working a national chitlin circuit that flattened regional accent into a single electric vernacular. The album is the document of that vernacular crystallizing. Eric Clapton has called it the record that taught him what guitar phrasing actually was, and that line of influence — through Mike Bloomfield, Peter Green, Albert Collins, every blues-rock guitarist of consequence — runs through this LP. Both critics: 1.

##### 2. Howlin' Wolf — *Moanin' In The Moonlight* (1959)

Wolf's debut LP on Chess collects singles cut between 1951 and 1958, which technically makes it a compilation, but in 1959 the LP was where the electric blues canon was being assembled in real time and Moanin' is the record that established Wolf as the genre's most physically intimidating presence. Scaruffi puts Wolf among the founding fathers of the entire post-WWII tradition; this is the record where that case is made.

The argument for placing Wolf above Muddy Waters in an electric blues list — even as a Chicago-canon record — is that Wolf is more of a regional bridge than Waters is. He cut his teeth in West Memphis with Sam Phillips at Sun before Chess re-recorded him in Chicago, so the early Memphis sides ("How Many More Years," "Moanin' At Midnight," the title track) carry a Mississippi-Memphis-to-Chicago migration arc inside the record itself. The voice — that famous "the moan from the mountain" — is the Delta arriving in the city without losing the holler.

Hubert Sumlin's guitar work, which fully emerges on the later sides here ("Smokestack Lightnin'" being the obvious one), invents a register that neither Muddy's band nor BB's band had been operating in — a stinging, single-note West Side approach that Magic Sam and Otis Rush would later take further. "Smokestack Lightnin'" itself is the record's anchor, a one-chord vamp that does what a one-chord vamp is supposed to do — stop time. Fantano has been extrapolated to second on Wolf based on his general priors around vocal power and rhythmic hypnosis. Both critics agreed on placement; this is 2 by acclamation.

##### 3. John Lee Hooker — *The Real Folk Blues* (1966)

Detroit-based, Mississippi-born, and the figure Scaruffi most consistently elevates as one of the founding fathers of rock as well as electric blues. The Real Folk Blues is the Chess compilation that gathers Hooker's mid-60s sides for the label and makes the case that his single-chord boogie — guitar, foot, voice, no chord changes in the conventional sense — is a self-sufficient musical universe rather than a primitive predecessor to the 12-bar mainstream.

The record matters because it shows Hooker working on a label other than the small labels he came up on (Modern, Vee-Jay, Riverside) and consolidating a style that had been spread across dozens of singles into something that scans as an album statement. "I'm In The Mood" and "Boom Boom" — both featured here in their canonical Vee-Jay-derived forms across this and the companion compilations — are the songs that Eric Burdon, the Stones, the Animals, and ZZ Top built careers on top of. Scaruffi's framing is that Hooker is what happens when you electrify the field holler directly without routing it through the jump-blues band format that BB and T-Bone Walker were using; the result is closer to trance music than to song form.

Fantano has multiple positive references to Hooker across his back catalog and treats him as a foundational voice, so this placement is well-supported on his side too. The album is the cleanest single-disc entry point to the Hooker recorded universe, which is otherwise spread across so many labels that newcomers tend to bounce off it. If you want one Hooker record on a desert island, this is the one most commonly named, with The Healer (1989) as the late-career comeback alternative — that record is on the honorable mentions list. Both critics: 3.

##### 4. Muddy Waters — *The Best Of Muddy Waters* (1958)

Chess 1427, released in 1958, the first proper Muddy Waters LP and the document that established the format of "the blues album" as a thing that existed. Twelve sides cut between 1948 and 1954 — "I Just Want To Make Love To You," "Hoochie Coochie Man," "I'm Ready," "I Can't Be Satisfied," "Rollin' Stone," "Honey Bee" — sequenced as if it were a studio LP rather than a singles round-up.

The case for including this on an Electric Blues bridge list rather than confining it to the Chicago Blues file is that this record is what "electric blues" means to most listeners as a category — the full-band arrangement (Little Walter on harp, Otis Spann on piano, Jimmy Rogers on second guitar, Willie Dixon on bass, Elgin Evans or Fred Below on drums) that became the template every regional scene either adopted or pushed against. The Texas players reacted to it. The British players copied it. The West Coast players simplified it. You can't write a unified history of post-WWII electrified blues without this record as a fixed point.

Scaruffi treats Muddy as a top-tier figure in his canon, though typically slightly below Hooker and Wolf in his ranking — partly because Muddy's mid-50s sides settled into formula faster than the other two. Fantano has not commented directly; his framework values genre-defining records highly, which puts this here. The placement at 4 is a compromise: Scaruffi wanted it at 3, the Hooker-favoring reading wanted it at 5, the median is here. Both critics agreed on the top-five status; the exact ordinal is the result of the round 2 horse-trade.

##### 5. Elmore James — *The Sky Is Crying* (1965)

A posthumous Sphere Sound compilation drawing on James's Fire/Fury sides from 1959-1962 and the closest thing to a definitive Elmore single-disc. The argument for a high placement is that Elmore is the single most influential slide guitarist of the electric era and a perfect example of the cross-regional bridge this list is supposed to highlight: Mississippi roots, Chicago and Memphis recording sessions, a New Orleans rhythm section on some of the late Fire sides, and a sound that fed directly into British blues-rock (Brian Jones, Jeremy Spencer, Duane Allman) and into Southern rock without much modification.

The opening lick of "Dust My Broom" — itself a re-recording and amplification of Robert Johnson's 1936 figure — is the most-quoted slide phrase in popular music. The Sky Is Crying gathers the Fire-era version of that figure plus "The Sky Is Crying," "Shake Your Moneymaker," "It Hurts Me Too," and the slow-blues meditations that show James was more than a one-lick player. The voice is the under-discussed element: a strained, near-cracking tenor that anticipates the high lonesome sound the British blues singers would chase a decade later.

Scaruffi has Elmore in his top tier of post-war bluesmen and treats this collection as the single best document. Fantano has been extrapolated; emotional power and innovation are both clearly present. The slot at 5 reflects a slight haggle — Scaruffi pushed for 4, the Muddy-favoring reading wanted Elmore at 6, this is the median. Both critics: 5.

##### 6. Slim Harpo — *Raining In My Heart* (1961)

Excello LP-8003, the album that codified Louisiana swamp blues as an actual sub-tradition rather than a regional novelty. Slim Harpo (James Moore) recorded for J.D. Miller in Crowley, Louisiana, with a band whose tempos sat below Chicago's and whose textures emphasized space, treble, and a particular kind of nasal vocal delivery that no other regional electric blues style produced.

The reason this is on the list and not on a hypothetical "swamp blues" file (which doesn't exist in this volume) is that Slim Harpo is the load-bearing example of how electric blues didn't homogenize — it speciated. The same instruments, the same basic 12-bar form, the same post-WWII amplification, and Crowley produced something that sounds nothing like Chicago. "Rainin' In My Heart" itself is the title track and a near-ballad. "I'm A King Bee" is the side the Stones covered on their first LP and Pink Floyd echoed at the Marquee; Van Morrison cut "Don't Start Cryin' Now" a few years later; the Yardbirds and the Kinks both lifted from this catalog.

Scaruffi's writing on swamp blues is brief but treats Harpo as the genre's clearest stylist. Fantano has not commented directly; the case for inclusion on his side rests on the regional-speciation argument and on the album's compositional density (more original material than the typical 1961 blues LP). Placement at 6 was contested — see Coin-Flip Resolutions below for the Harpo-vs-Lightnin' Slim debate that resolved here.

##### 7. Magic Sam — *West Side Soul* (1967)

Delmark DL-615, released in 1967, recorded in Chicago in two July sessions with a band built around Mighty Joe Young on rhythm guitar and Stockholm Slim on piano. The reason West Side Soul belongs on a cross-regional electric blues list rather than a Chicago-only list is that Sam Maghett invented — or co-invented with Otis Rush and Buddy Guy — the West Side Chicago style, which was itself a hybrid of Chicago's South Side electric blues, Memphis soul, and New Orleans R&B. The album is the West Side sound's clearest single document.

What's distinctive: the tremolo-heavy clean guitar tone (a deliberate move away from the Muddy-Wolf distortion aesthetic), the soul-derived chord vocabulary on tracks like "I Need You So Bad," and the willingness to cover Bobby "Blue" Bland and Jimmy Reed and Junior Parker on the same record without flattening their differences. "All Your Love" reworks Otis Rush's signature into Sam's looser groove. "Sweet Home Chicago" is the version most people now hear when they think of the song — Robert Johnson's original is now mediated through this performance.

Sam died in December 1969 at 32, less than two and a half years after this record came out, which gives West Side Soul the weight of being most of a career compressed into one LP. Scaruffi rates it highly as a hybrid record. Fantano has been extrapolated; the production is clean enough and the songwriting tight enough that it would survive his typical scrutiny. Both critics: 7.

##### 8. Albert King — *Born Under A Bad Sign* (1967)

Stax LP-723, released August 1967, recorded with Booker T. and the MGs at Stax Studios in Memphis. The record is technically a singles compilation (the sides were cut between March 1966 and June 1967), assembled into LP form to capitalize on the title-track single's success. It is the clearest example of the soul-blues hybrid the Memphis Stax sound produced and the record that taught a generation of rock guitarists what bend-against-the-band guitar phrasing was supposed to sound like.

The argument that this is a soul record and not a blues record was the central debate around its inclusion. Booker T. Jones produces. The MGs play. Isaac Hayes wrote the title track with William Bell. The horn charts are pure Stax. But the lead instrument is Albert King's left-handed, upside-down, single-string-bend electric guitar in a style that is unambiguously blues — and the song forms (12-bar throughout, with the partial exception of "The Hunter") are blues forms. The hybrid is the point. This is what happened when electrified blues collided with the Memphis soul rhythm section in 1966-1967 and the result is a third thing.

Eric Clapton's Cream-era guitar style is essentially Albert King's vibrato and bending vocabulary transposed into a power-trio context. Stevie Ray Vaughan is downstream of this record. So is half of Free, Allman Brothers, and ZZ Top. Scaruffi rates the LP highly. Fantano has been extrapolated; the production-as-statement angle is clearly Fantano-friendly and the sequencing is among the tightest of any blues LP of the era. Placement at 8 reflects a slight discount for the soul-hybrid hesitation. Both critics: 8.

##### 9. Jimmy Reed — *Rockin' With Reed* (1959)

Vee-Jay VJ-LP-1008, Jimmy Reed's second LP, released in 1959, collecting Vee-Jay singles from 1957-1959 plus a few previously unreleased sides. The case for Reed on a top-10 electric blues list is that he was, by mid-decade sales metrics, the most commercially successful electric bluesman of the 1950s — more singles charted than Muddy, Wolf, or BB King — and his style was the template that almost every white American blues-rock band of the early 60s started by imitating.

The Reed sound is misleadingly simple. Two guitars (Reed plus Eddie Taylor playing the bass figures and turnarounds Reed couldn't), a rack-mounted harmonica played in a high register, a slow shuffle, and a vocal delivery that sounds half-asleep — but the arrangements are tighter than they sound, and the songs ("Honest I Do," "Baby What You Want Me To Do," "Going To New York," "You Don't Have To Go") have a melodic durability most of his contemporaries didn't match. The Stones cut "Honest I Do" on their first album. Elvis covered "Baby What You Want Me To Do" on the 1968 Comeback Special. Bob Dylan covered Reed three separate times across his career.

The cross-regional bridge argument: Reed was Mississippi-born, Chicago-based on Vee-Jay, but his style traveled outside Chicago more cleanly than Muddy's did because the band format was minimal enough to be reproduced in a juke joint or a college dorm without losing the essence. Scaruffi treats Reed as a major figure but typically below the top tier. Fantano has not commented directly. Placement at 9 is a Scaruffi-led compromise; the Fantano-extrapolated reading wanted Reed lower.

##### 10. T-Bone Walker — *T-Bone Blues* (1959)

Atlantic SD-8020, released January 1960, recorded in three sessions across 1955-1957 in New York and Los Angeles. T-Bone Walker (Aaron Thibeaux Walker) is the player who, more than any other figure, invented the vocabulary of the electric blues guitar solo — the single-note jump-style line, the use of ninth chords as comping vocabulary, the showmanship of the held-out-front Gibson archtop. Without Walker there is no BB King, no Chuck Berry's solo style, no rockabilly lead guitar, and no jazz-blues-rock crossover.

T-Bone Blues is the album that captures him at the end of his peak studio period, with sessions featuring Junior Wells on harp, Jimmy Rogers on second guitar, and various jazz-leaning sidemen on the New York dates. "Stormy Monday" appears here in a re-recorded version — the original 1947 Black & White recording is the more famous one, but this version has the band performance Walker preferred. "Two Bones And A Pick" and "Why Not" are showcases for the comping-and-soloing duality that defined his approach.

The placement at 10 — rather than higher, given Walker's foundational status — reflects two things. First, Walker's most influential work is on singles from 1947-1953 that aren't gathered on a single canonical LP; T-Bone Blues is the best LP-format document but it's not the whole story. Second, much of what Walker invented was absorbed and extended by BB King to the point that BB has become the canonical reference, fairly or not. Scaruffi rates Walker highly as a foundational figure. Fantano has not commented directly. Walker is on this list rather than the Texas Blues list because his style traveled to LA, NYC, and Chicago and shaped the national vocabulary of electric blues more than it stayed pinned to Texas regionalism.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Three irreconcilable disagreements were resolved by coin flip after round six of the debate.

**Coin-flip 1: Slim Harpo's *Raining In My Heart* (1961) vs Lightnin' Slim's *Rooster Blues* (1961) at slot 6.** Both are Excello swamp-blues LPs cut at J.D. Miller's Crowley studio with overlapping personnel. Scaruffi marginally preferred Lightnin' Slim for the rawer guitar tone; Fantano was extrapolated as preferring Harpo for the songwriting and the mainstream cultural footprint (the Stones, Pink Floyd, Van Morrison covers). Coin flip resolved to Harpo at 6, with Lightnin' Slim moving to honorable mentions.

**Coin-flip 2: Magic Sam's *West Side Soul* (1967) vs Otis Rush's *Mourning In The Morning* (1969) at slot 7.** The case for Rush was that he is arguably the more influential West Side stylist (Eric Clapton has called Otis Rush's "All Your Love" the single most important record in his development) and that the Cotillion LP, recorded at Muscle Shoals with Duane Allman on second guitar, is the more ambitious cross-regional document. The case for Sam was the death-young-with-one-LP argument and the cleaner West Side Chicago thesis. Coin flip resolved to Sam at 7, with Rush moving to honorable mentions.

**Coin-flip 3: T-Bone Walker's *T-Bone Blues* (1959) vs Lowell Fulson's *Hung Down Head* (1970) at slot 10.** The case for Fulson was that he's more of a true regional bridge — Texas-born, California-based, Oklahoma blues circuit — and that Hung Down Head captures him in his late-Kent-Records peak with material that hadn't been collected on LP before. The case for Walker was the foundational-vocabulary argument. Coin flip resolved to Walker at 10, with Fulson moving to honorable mentions.

#### Honorable Mentions

**Lightnin' Slim — *Rooster Blues* (1961, Excello).** The grittier swamp counterpart to Slim Harpo. Lost the coin flip at slot 6 but is the Excello record that documents the harder edge of Crowley.

**Otis Rush — *Mourning In The Morning* (1969, Cotillion).** Recorded at Muscle Shoals with Duane Allman. The most ambitious West Side document; lost the coin flip at slot 7 to West Side Soul.

**Lowell Fulson — *Hung Down Head* (1970, Kent).** California-via-Texas-via-Oklahoma electric blues, the cleanest example of the West Coast jump-blues tradition's late period.

**John Lee Hooker — *The Healer* (1989, Chameleon).** The late-career comeback record with guest spots from Carlos Santana, Bonnie Raitt, Robert Cray, Los Lobos, and George Thorogood. Scaruffi rates the duets unevenly but the title track is a peak Hooker performance.

**B.B. King — *Live In Cook County Jail* (1971, ABC).** The other canonical BB live record. Some critics prefer it to Live At The Regal for the audience response and the looser late-night feel.

**Howlin' Wolf — *The Howlin' Wolf Album* (1969, Cadet Concept).** The psychedelic-blues misfire that Wolf himself hated and Scaruffi treats as a fascinating failure rather than a recommended listen. Included here for completeness rather than as an actual top-level recommendation.

**Junior Wells — *Hoodoo Man Blues* (1965, Delmark).** Buddy Guy on guitar (credited as "Friendly Chap" for contractual reasons). The LP that put the Chicago South Side harmonica-led ensemble onto a proper studio document.

**Buddy Guy — *A Man And The Blues* (1968, Vanguard).** Guy's first proper LP under his own name. The bridge between Chicago electric and the post-Hendrix lead-guitar style Guy would later be canonized for.

**Freddie King — *Getting Ready...* (1971, Shelter).** Produced by Leon Russell in Tulsa. The Texas/Chicago/Oklahoma three-corner record that almost made the top 10 on bridge-credentials alone.

**Albert Collins — *Ice Pickin'* (1978, Alligator).** The late-70s Texas-into-electric-blues-revival record that almost claimed slot 10 from T-Bone. Lost on the recording-era argument — too late to count as part of the post-WWII founding canon.

**Little Walter — *The Best Of Little Walter* (1958, Chess).** The harmonica record that established the instrument as a legitimate lead voice in the electric ensemble — "Juke," "My Babe," "Mean Old World," "Sad Hours." Lost the cut on the grounds that it overlaps too heavily with the Muddy Waters context where Walter cut most of his sideman work; including both was treated as double-counting. Walter is the unsung architect behind the Chess sound, and a strict reading of "electric blues" ought to give his solo records space, but the bridge-mandate of this list pushed him just below the cut.

**Bo Diddley — *Bo Diddley* (1958, Checker).** The self-titled debut on Chess's Checker subsidiary. Argued for inclusion as an electric blues record by way of the "shave and a haircut" beat being a direct mutation of the African-derived patterns that ran through the Mississippi tradition. Lost the cut on the grounds that Diddley is more proto-rock-and-roll than electric blues proper; he belongs on a different file in this volume. Listed here because the case was made and lost in round 4 of the debate.

**Z.Z. Hill — *Down Home* (1982, Malaco).** The Mississippi-based soul-blues LP that revitalized the chitlin-circuit market for electric blues in the early 80s and bridged into the modern southern soul tradition. Lost the cut on era grounds — too late for the post-WWII founding canon but listed here because the bridge argument is real: Z.Z. Hill is a load-bearing example of how electric blues survived as a regional Black-audience genre after the rock-blues crossover absorbed most of the white listenership.

---

### Jump Blues

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Jump blues is the genre that most stubbornly resists the album-canon project. It was a singles music, made for jukeboxes, V-Discs, and the territorial circuit. The "albums" we argue about are almost entirely posthumous compilations — Decca masters licensed to MCA, then to Verve; Aladdin sides reissued by EMI; King catalog handled by Rhino, then Ace, then Bear Family. To rank them at all is to rank archival projects: which curators picked the right sides, in the right order, with the right transfers.

Scaruffi's approach to jump blues is consistent with his broader R&B writing. He fixes Louis Jordan and the Tympany Five at the structural origin of postwar Black popular music — the band whose shuffle, horn voicings, and comedic vocal phrasing produce both rock'n'roll and the entire R&B vocabulary that follows. He is also unusually warm on Big Joe Turner as a Kansas City shouter whose bridge from territory swing into rhythm and blues he treats as historically load-bearing. Wynonie Harris, Roy Brown, Amos Milburn, and Charles Brown enter his canon as the second tier — important, but read primarily through what they enabled.

Fantano is a problem case here and I am flagging it explicitly: he has effectively no public commentary on jump blues as a genre. There are no Needle Drop reviews of Louis Jordan compilations, no retrospective videos on Wynonie Harris, no rankings of late-1940s Aladdin sides. Everything attributed to him below is **EXTRAPOLATED** from his stated values: reverence for genre originators, distaste for sanitized retrospective packaging, preference for the rawest available document over the slickest one, and a generally Fantano-shaped suspicion of "best of" packaging that buries B-sides. Treat his positions as a model of how he would likely argue, not as quotations.

The two critics converge surprisingly fast on the top tier. Louis Jordan is unanimous at #1. Big Joe Turner's Atlantic-era compilation is unanimous in the top three. Wynonie Harris and Roy Brown both make both lists. The fights are over: (1) how much weight to give Charles Brown, who is a borderline case between jump blues and the smoother West Coast club blues that follows it; (2) whether Amos Milburn deserves a single or double placement; (3) the Tiny Bradshaw question, where Scaruffi argues for inclusion on architectural grounds and Fantano (extrapolated) prefers Bull Moose Jackson's grit; (4) whether Jimmy Liggins or his brother Joe Liggins better represents the West Coast jump school.

Coin flips were used twice, both noted in their own section.

A note on the compilation problem. The honest version of any jump blues canon is that nine of the ten entries below are curated singles collections. Jordan, Turner, Harris, Brown, Milburn, Charles Brown, Jackson, Bradshaw, and Joe Liggins did not make albums in the conceptual sense; they made 78s, then 45s, and the LP era arrived after most of them had stopped making their best work. Even Turner's *Boss of the Blues* (1956), the closest thing to a "real" jump blues album, is essentially a studio reunion staged by an Atlantic A&R department after the genre was already historical. To rank these compilations is to rank the work of MCA's, EMI's, Rhino's, and Bear Family's reissue producers as much as the work of the artists. Both critics accepted this constraint explicitly rather than papering over it.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Louis Jordan — *The Best of Louis Jordan* (MCA, 1975)

The unanimous #1, and not seriously contested by either critic. This is the standard MCA double-LP distillation of the Decca masters Jordan cut with the Tympany Five between roughly 1942 and 1951 — "Caldonia," "Choo Choo Ch'Boogie," "Saturday Night Fish Fry," "Ain't Nobody Here But Us Chickens," "Let the Good Times Roll," "Beware Brother Beware," "Open the Door, Richard," "Buzz Me," "Is You Is or Is You Ain't My Baby." On the singles charts of the 1940s these were not novelty hits — they were the dominant Black popular music of the decade, and they crossed over more cleanly than anything before Nat Cole or Chuck Berry.

Scaruffi's case is structural: the Tympany Five's small-band shuffle, with its boogie bass figure, riffing horn section, and conversational vocal, is the template from which both rhythm and blues and rock'n'roll are physically built. Bill Haley copied this band. Chuck Berry copied this band. The entire Atlantic R&B house style under Jesse Stone is a refinement of what Jordan and Milt Gabler had already engineered at Decca. Scaruffi's older writing on rock prehistory routinely points at Jordan as the actual fulcrum, more so than at boogie pianists or jump shouters individually.

Fantano's extrapolated case is simpler and more aesthetic: the records are funny, swung, lyrically dense, and almost shockingly modern in their phrasing. The vocal cadence on "Saturday Night Fish Fry" is essentially a rap cadence in 1949. Even stripped of historical claim, the tracks survive on craft.

The MCA *Best Of* is not the most exhaustive Jordan compilation — the Bear Family and JSP boxes go deeper — but it is the canonical object, the one that put Jordan back in print for the rock-historical audience in the 1970s and that almost every writer of either critic's generation actually heard. Both lists keep it as the entry point.

A point that did not need debating: Jordan is the only jump blues artist whose primacy is not contested even by critics outside the genre. Robert Christgau, Greil Marcus, Peter Guralnick, and Nelson George have all written some version of the same sentence — that without Jordan, postwar Black popular music looks unrecognizable. The Tympany Five recordings predate Atlantic, predate Chess in its R&B phase, and predate the entire concept of "rhythm and blues" as a Billboard category. They are the source water.

##### 2. Big Joe Turner — *Big, Bad & Blue: The Big Joe Turner Anthology* (Rhino, 1994)

Scaruffi places Turner near the top of any postwar blues canon, and the Rhino three-disc anthology is the most defensible single object representing him. It tracks Turner from his Kansas City sides with Pete Johnson in the late 1930s — boogie-woogie with a shouter on top, "Roll 'Em Pete," "Cherry Red" — through the National and Aladdin years, into the Atlantic run that produced "Chains of Love," "Honey Hush," "Flip, Flop and Fly," and the original "Shake, Rattle and Roll." It closes with the late Pablo small-group jazz dates that confirm Turner as a swing singer of permanent rank.

This is the album that resolves a long argument about whether Turner is jump blues, classic blues shouting, or swing. He is all three, and his records refuse to be subdivided. Scaruffi treats this Atlantic-centered Turner as a load-bearing figure for the bridge between territory bands and rock'n'roll: he is louder than Jordan, more declarative, less comedic, and the band behind him on the Atlantic sides is essentially the Jesse Stone studio unit that defines the early Atlantic sound.

Fantano (extrapolated) would be drawn to Turner for the same reason he tends to favor unfiltered, room-filling vocalists — there is no studio polish hiding the voice; the takes are documentary. He would likely argue against the Rhino set's length on the grounds that any 70-track survey dilutes, but neither critic has a better single-package alternative. The shorter Atlantic-only collections lose the Kansas City sides and the late jazz dates, both of which are essential to understanding what Turner actually was.

The argument for Turner over Harris at #2 — which both critics arrived at independently — turns on durability. Harris's records are tighter and more immediate, but Turner's catalog spans three distinct eras of Black popular music (Kansas City swing, postwar Atlantic R&B, late-period mainstream jazz) and he is convincing in all three. Turner is the figure who proves jump blues was not a sealed genre but a stage in a longer continuous tradition.

##### 3. Wynonie Harris — *Bloodshot Eyes: The Best of Wynonie Harris* (Rhino, 1994)

The case for Harris in the top three is the case that "Good Rocking Tonight" — written by Roy Brown, covered by Harris in 1948, then by Elvis Presley in 1954 — is the most consequential single transmission point between jump blues and rock'n'roll. Harris's version is harder, drier, and more sexual than Brown's, and the King studio band gives it the snap that Sun would later try to reproduce.

The Rhino compilation collects the King and Aladdin sides — "Bloodshot Eyes," "Lovin' Machine," "All She Wants to Do Is Rock," "Mr. Blues Is Coming to Town," "Quiet Whiskey." Across these tracks Harris establishes a vocal persona — leering, comic, swaggering — that becomes a permanent template; you can hear it filtered through Bo Diddley, through Howlin' Wolf's more upbeat sides, and eventually through the entire white rockabilly tradition.

Scaruffi rates Harris highly on influence grounds and is comfortable with him in the top three. Fantano's extrapolated position is more enthusiastic — Harris is the kind of figure (raw, blunt, slightly disreputable, historically downstream-determinative) that he has tended to elevate when he writes about earlier genres. Both critics agreed without negotiation.

The compilation itself is uneven — Harris's later sides drift into novelty — but the core run from roughly 1947 to 1952 is as strong a singles run as anyone in the genre produced. Henry Glover's productions for King during this window are also worth noting: the studio band, the riffs, the way the horns punch on the upbeat — this is the immediate prehistory of the Sun Records sound, and Sam Phillips clearly heard it. When Phillips put Elvis Presley in front of a microphone and asked him to cut "Good Rocking Tonight" in 1954, he was asking him to make a Wynonie Harris record.

##### 4. Roy Brown — *Good Rocking Tonight: The Best of Roy Brown* (Rhino, 1994)

Brown wrote "Good Rocking Tonight," and if you accept the standard rock-historical claim that this is the song where rhythm and blues becomes rock'n'roll, then Brown is the author of the inflection point. His own version, cut for DeLuxe in 1947, is more gospel-shaped than Harris's — Brown trained on Bing Crosby and on Black church singing, and his vocal style is melismatic where Harris's is barked. This is consequential: Brown is the line between jump blues and what becomes soul singing.

The Rhino compilation gathers the DeLuxe and King sides — "Good Rocking Tonight," "Hard Luck Blues," "Boogie at Midnight," "Cadillac Baby," "Long About Midnight." The arrangements are fully realized small-band jump blues, with horn riffs, walking bass, and the sort of two-beat shuffle that was about to be appropriated wholesale by white rockabilly producers.

Scaruffi gives Brown structural credit but tends to rate him slightly below Harris on raw aesthetic grounds — Harris's records swing harder. Fantano's extrapolated position is the inverse: Brown's voice is the more interesting instrument, and his gospel inflection points forward to Ray Charles and to the entire 1950s soul tradition in a way Harris's barking does not. The two critics ended up at the same placement for opposite reasons, which is a clean compromise.

A small note on what Brown is not. He is sometimes filed alongside Tampa Red or Big Bill Broonzy as a "blues" artist, but his band on the King sides is a tight horn-driven jump unit, not a Chicago small group, and his chord vocabulary owes more to gospel and pop than to country blues. Treating him as jump blues rather than as straight blues is the more honest classification.

##### 5. Amos Milburn — *Down the Road Apiece: The Best of Amos Milburn* (EMI, 1994)

Milburn is the West Coast jump school's most complete artist — pianist, singer, bandleader — and the Aladdin sides he cut between 1946 and 1954 are the cleanest demonstration of how Texas-via-Houston blues piano fused with Los Angeles small-band jump arranging. "Chicken Shack Boogie," "Bewildered," "Hold Me Baby," "Bad, Bad Whiskey," "One Scotch, One Bourbon, One Beer," "Down the Road Apiece" — the run of charting singles is one of the densest in the genre.

The drinking songs in particular ("Bad, Bad Whiskey," "One Scotch, One Bourbon, One Beer," "Let Me Go Home, Whiskey," "Vicious, Vicious Vodka") form a coherent body of work that pre-dates the country-music drinking song tradition and that is, on inspection, more sophisticated than most of what followed. Milburn's piano playing is the most interesting in jump blues after Charles Brown's — left hand boogie-strict, right hand more harmonically curious than the genre usually allowed.

Scaruffi has consistently rated Milburn as a major figure of the late 1940s. Fantano's extrapolated position would emphasize the songwriting — these are records with structures, hooks, and recurring lyrical concerns rather than just dance riffs. Both critics agreed on inclusion; the only fight was placement, with Scaruffi pushing for higher (top four) and the extrapolated Fantano pulling lower. Fifth is the compromise.

##### 6. Charles Brown — *Driftin' Blues: The Best of Charles Brown* (EMI, 1992)

Charles Brown is the genre boundary case. With Johnny Moore's Three Blazers he cut "Driftin' Blues" in 1945, and that record — slow, urbane, piano-trio arranged, sung in a smoke-room baritone — is closer to Nat Cole than to Wynonie Harris. The argument for including him in jump blues at all rests on his Aladdin solo years (1948–1952), where the arrangements pick up tempo and where "Trouble Blues," "Black Night," and "Hard Times" anchor a body of work that is jump-blues-adjacent even when it is not strictly jump blues.

The EMI compilation gathers the Aladdin material plus key Three Blazers sides. As a single object it is the best representation of Brown's significance. His phrasing influence is enormous: Ray Charles has acknowledged him directly, and the entire West Coast cocktail blues tradition that runs through Percy Mayfield to Lowell Fulson is downstream of Brown's vocal style.

Scaruffi rates Brown high but is uncertain whether to count him as jump blues. Fantano (extrapolated) treats him as a clear inclusion — the genre boundary is fuzzy, the influence is real, and the records are good. The compromise placement at #6 reflects the borderline status: he is in, but not above the more straightforwardly uptempo figures above him.

Brown's later career — the holiday ballad "Merry Christmas Baby" (which he co-wrote with Lou Baxter and which Otis Redding, Bruce Springsteen, and Christina Aguilera have all subsequently recorded), the Bonnie Raitt-produced 1990s comeback records — sits outside this canon but confirms that his vocal style was durable enough to survive every shift in Black popular music between 1945 and 1995. Few jump blues figures can make that claim.

##### 7. Various Artists — *Jumpin' Like Mad: Cool Cats & Hip Chicks Non-Stop Dancin'* (Capitol Blues Collection, 1997)

This is the only various-artists compilation that survived the negotiation, and it is included on a specific argument: jump blues at the secondary level — the great-but-not-quite-canonical artists like Joe Turner's late Capitol sides, Jimmy Liggins, Jack McVea, Helen Humes, Camille Howard, T-Bone Walker's earliest jump material — is best heard as a body, not as twelve separate compilations. The Capitol Blues Collection survey, compiled by Billy Vera across two discs and 51 tracks drawn from Capitol's own jump-blues catalog plus the affiliated Aladdin and Imperial holdings, gathers the sides that don't quite earn standalone slots but that constitute the connective tissue of the genre.

Scaruffi accepted this on the grounds that his canon for the second tier is real but diffuse — he wants Jimmy Liggins's "Drunk," T-Bone Walker's earliest sides, and the territorial-circuit female vocalists (Ella Mae Morse, Kay Starr in jump-tempo mode, Helen Humes) represented somewhere, and a multi-artist set is the honest way to do it without inflating any single secondary compilation to a top-ten slot. Fantano (extrapolated) was initially skeptical of compilations-of-compilations on principle but conceded that the alternative — ten individual best-ofs of marginal artists — was worse.

The set's 1942–1956 window catches the genre across its full commercial arc, and the curatorial sequencing — danceable, tightly programmed, audibly intended to play continuously rather than to be sampled track-by-track — gives the compilation an internal energy most archival projects of this period lack. Vera's annotation work and the Capitol vault transfers are also notably good. It is the closest jump blues has to a proper genre-survey album, and it earns its slot honestly.

##### 8. Bull Moose Jackson — *Badman Jackson, That's Me* (Charly, 1991)

The Charly Jackson collection is the one standalone compilation of a "second-tier" jump artist that both critics agreed earned its own slot. Jackson, a saxophonist and singer who came up through Lucky Millinder's band before going solo on King in 1947, recorded one of the most distinctive runs of double-entendre jump sides in the genre — "Big Ten Inch Record," "I Want a Bowlegged Woman," "I Love You, Yes I Do" (a #1 R&B hit and one of the genre's biggest crossover ballads), "Nosey Joe."

What separates Jackson from the other secondary artists is range. He could sing a straight ballad ("I Love You, Yes I Do") with enough sincerity to chart, and he could sing a filthy comic blues ("Big Ten Inch") with enough straight-faced commitment that the joke survives 75 years later. The Charly set — 22 tracks pulling from the King catalog window — documents both modes properly. Charly's UK reissue program through the late 1980s and early 1990s did substantial archival work on the King masters, and this collection is one of the better-sequenced products of that effort.

Scaruffi's interest in Jackson is moderate; he treats him as a representative figure rather than a major one. Fantano's extrapolated interest is higher — the comic-blues tradition Jackson exemplifies (sex jokes delivered with tight band arrangements and total deadpan) is exactly the kind of weird, durable craft Fantano tends to elevate retroactively. The compromise was inclusion at #8, with the Charly set as the canonical object; Bear Family's later *Greatest Hits* and *Big Fat Mamas 1945-56* sets are the deeper-archival alternatives and sit in honorable mentions.

##### 9. Tiny Bradshaw — *Breaking Up the House* (Charly, 1985)

Bradshaw is the architectural figure Scaruffi insisted on. As a bandleader he had been working since the 1930s, but his King sides from 1949 to 1955 — recorded with a band that at various points included Red Prysock, Sil Austin, and Rufus Gore on tenor — are among the hardest-swinging small-group records of the genre's late phase. "Well Oh Well," "Soft," and especially "The Train Kept A-Rollin'" (1951) constitute a significant late-jump statement.

"The Train Kept A-Rollin'" is the load-bearing track. It was covered by Johnny Burnette in 1956, by the Yardbirds in 1965, and by Aerosmith in 1974, and the Bradshaw original is identifiably the source of all of them — same riff, same rhythmic structure, only the production thinner. Scaruffi argues that this single transmission is enough to place Bradshaw in the top ten on its own.

Fantano (extrapolated) was less convinced — he tends to discount lineage arguments when the original record is less interesting than its descendants — but conceded that Bradshaw's instrumental arrangements are stronger than most jump blues of the period, and that the overall body of King work justifies the placement.

The Charly compilation is the most accessible single Bradshaw object, though the Bear Family box is more complete. Either is defensible; we kept the Charly because both critics had actually heard it.

##### 10. Joe Liggins — *Joe Liggins & The Honeydrippers* (Specialty / Ace, 1989)

The final slot was the most contested. The candidates were Joe Liggins, Jimmy Liggins (Joe's brother), Jack McVea, Helen Humes, Camille Howard, and a Roy Milton compilation. Joe Liggins won on the strength of "The Honeydripper" — which spent 18 weeks at #1 on the Billboard R&B chart in 1945, tied with Louis Jordan's "Choo Choo Ch'Boogie" for the longest run at the top in that chart's history — plus "Pink Champagne," "Got a Right to Cry," and "I've Got a Right to Cry."

The Ace compilation gathers Joe Liggins's Specialty material from 1950 onward plus key earlier sides. Liggins's piano-led small-band arrangements are slightly slower and more relaxed than Milburn's or Jordan's, occupying a tempo space between jump blues proper and the smoother West Coast club blues style — close to Charles Brown but with more horn. "The Honeydripper" itself is a structurally strange record: a long modal-ish vamp over which Liggins sings in a conversational drawl, much closer to a 1950s R&B groove than to a 1940s shuffle.

Scaruffi accepted the slot but had wanted Roy Milton instead. Fantano (extrapolated) preferred Jimmy Liggins on raw-grit grounds — Jimmy's "Drunk" and "Cadillac Boogie" are louder, dirtier records than anything of Joe's. The disagreement went to a coin flip; Joe Liggins won. (Note: this is the second of the two coin flips, both documented below.)

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

**Coin flip 1: Tiny Bradshaw vs. Roy Milton (for slot #9).** Scaruffi argued Bradshaw on architectural grounds (the "Train Kept A-Rollin'" transmission to rockabilly and rock). Extrapolated Fantano argued Milton on band grounds (the Solid Senders were a genuinely great unit, and "R.M. Blues" is a foundational record). Outcome: Bradshaw. Note: the case for Milton is real, and a Specialty Roy Milton compilation should be considered effectively co-equal with the Bradshaw slot. It is listed in honorable mentions accordingly.

**Coin flip 2: Joe Liggins vs. Jimmy Liggins (for slot #10).** Scaruffi argued Joe on chart-history grounds ("The Honeydripper" as the longest-running R&B #1). Extrapolated Fantano argued Jimmy on raw aesthetic grounds — his sides are more direct and less arranged. Outcome: Joe Liggins. Note: Jimmy Liggins remains a defensible substitute for the slot, and his Specialty compilation is in the honorable mentions.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **Roy Milton — *Roy Milton & His Solid Senders* (Specialty / Ace).** Lost the coin flip for #9 to Bradshaw. "R.M. Blues," "Hop, Skip and Jump," "Information Blues" — the Solid Senders were one of the genre's tightest units. Scaruffi privately rated him higher than the final list reflects.
- **Jimmy Liggins — *I Can't Stop It: The Aladdin Recordings, Vol. 2* (Ace).** Lost the coin flip for #10 to his brother Joe. "Drunk," "Cadillac Boogie," "Tear Drop Blues." Rougher and more electric than Joe's sides; defensible substitute.
- **Jack McVea — *Two Timin' Baby* (Jukebox Lil).** "Open the Door, Richard" was a #1 R&B hit in 1947 and a cultural moment in its own right; McVea's tenor playing is among the most distinctive in jump blues. Cut from the top ten only because his standalone catalog is thinner than the artists who made it.
- **Helen Humes — *Be-Baba-Leba* (Whiskey, Women, And...).** The genre's strongest female vocalist after Big Maybelle. "Be-Baba-Leba" (1945) is one of the foundational jump blues records by any artist, but Humes's recorded output spans too many genres for any single compilation to fully claim her for jump blues.
- **Camille Howard — *Rock 'n' Roll Boogie* (Specialty).** Boogie-woogie pianist with Roy Milton's Solid Senders who cut a remarkable run of solo sides for Specialty. More piano-instrumental than vocal, which is why she didn't make the main list.
- **Louis Prima — *Just a Gigolo: The Capitol Recordings* (Capitol).** Borderline case — Prima's late-1950s Las Vegas band material is jump-blues-adjacent but technically post-genre and racially distinct from the core canon. Both critics agreed to leave it off.
- **Various Artists — *Atlantic Honkers: A Rhythm & Blues Saxophone Anthology* (Atlantic / Rhino).** Strong companion to the Capitol *Jumpin' Like Mad* set, focused specifically on the tenor-honker tradition (Big Jay McNeely, Sam "The Man" Taylor, Red Prysock, Willis "Gator" Jackson). Cut on overlap grounds.
- **Louis Jordan — *Let the Good Times Roll: The Anthology 1938–1953* (MCA, 1999).** The deeper Jordan compilation. Not chosen over the 1975 *Best Of* only because both critics had heard the *Best Of* and both treated it as canonical.
- **Big Joe Turner — *Boss of the Blues* (Atlantic, 1956).** A studio reunion album with Pete Johnson, often cited as the first proper jump-blues "album." Cut because Turner's compilation slot already represents him; *Boss of the Blues* is essential in any deeper Turner survey.
- **Wynonie Harris — *Mr. Blues Is Coming to Town* (Bear Family).** Deeper Harris collection than the Rhino *Bloodshot Eyes*. Strong defensible substitute.
- **Amos Milburn — *The Aladdin Recordings* (Mosaic, 1994).** The completist's Milburn — a five-disc Mosaic box that supersedes the EMI single-disc on archival grounds but loses the entry-level accessibility that put EMI's *Down the Road Apiece* in the main list.
- **Saunders King — *What's Your Story Morning Glory?* (Ace).** West Coast guitar-and-vocal jump blues from a figure Carlos Santana has cited as foundational. Cut on visibility grounds; the catalog is real but the recordings are scarce.

---

### Soul Blues

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Soul Blues is a critical orphan. It is the music that happened after the urban blues bandstands of the 1950s absorbed the horn arrangements, gospel-derived vocal melisma, and 16-bar pop forms of mid-1960s soul, and then kept going long after rock criticism stopped paying attention. It is the music of the Chitlin' Circuit through the 1970s, of the Malaco label in Jackson, Mississippi through the 1980s and 1990s, of the Stax B-tier and the Hi Records B-tier and the Duke/Peacock catalog. It is, on most days, more popular among working Black audiences than any other surviving form of blues — and almost completely absent from the canonical critical lists that this project is in dialogue with.

That gap is the debate. Piero Scaruffi's blues sympathies sit firmly in the country and electric Chicago traditions; when Soul Blues enters his frame at all, it does so via Stax (Albert King's *Born Under a Bad Sign* gets a strong notice in his rock history) and via Bobby "Blue" Bland's Duke run, which he treats as a peak of urban blues vocal craft. He is much colder on the Malaco era — to him the late 1970s and 1980s recordings smooth the edges, replace combo bands with synth-pad arrangements, and trade the genre's Saturday night threat for a kind of grown-folks consolation music. Anthony Fantano has essentially no recorded position on the genre. We are extrapolating from his preferences in adjacent territory: soul (he is on record loving D'Angelo, Sharon Jones, Charles Bradley), blues-as-vocal-vehicle (his enthusiasm for Mavis Staples), and contemporary Southern soul revivalists (his praise of the Daptone family). On those grounds we project him toward the vocal-forward, less-produced records and away from the slickest Malaco product, while flagging that the projection is doing real work.

The independent top-15s diverged sharply. Scaruffi's list tilted toward 1961-1973, with three Bobby Bland records, two Albert Kings, and O.V. Wright high. Fantano's projected list weighted O.V. Wright, the Stax-era Albert King, William Bell, and one Bobby Rush, with more openness to later Z.Z. Hill and Johnnie Taylor than Scaruffi allowed. Five rounds of debate produced strong agreement on the top four; after that the argument was about how much Malaco-era material to admit and which Bobby Bland record represents him best. Two genuine ties were resolved by coin flip and are flagged below.

A second structural problem deserves naming up front. Soul Blues is a singles genre that we are evaluating on the album-statement criterion, which is the dominant criterion of post-1967 rock criticism but a poor fit for any music whose primary commercial unit was the 7-inch and whose primary live unit was the package show. Bobby Bland's 1961-1965 Duke catalog is fundamentally a singles catalog that happens to have been compiled into LPs that listen unusually well as albums. Z.Z. Hill's *Down Home* is structured around its title track to a degree that an album-as-statement frame slightly distorts. The list below reflects this honestly: every entry is being judged both as an album in itself and as a representation of an artist's work, and where those criteria diverge — most sharply at Johnnie Taylor's slot — the choice is flagged. The third issue, equally worth naming, is that the genre's geography is not a single line but a triangle: Memphis (Stax, Duke/Peacock recording at American and Royal), Mississippi/Jackson (Malaco), and Chicago (Chess subsidiaries Checker and Cadet, and Dakar). The list distributes representation across all three, with Memphis dominant — which is not a critical choice so much as a description of where the genre's records actually came from.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Bobby "Blue" Bland — *Two Steps from the Blues* (Duke, 1961)

Both critics agreed almost immediately: this is the album where Soul Blues becomes a coherent idea rather than a happy accident. Bland was not a guitarist, not a harp player, not a bandleader in the Muddy Waters mold — he was a vocalist working with arranger and trumpeter Joe Scott, building something closer to a jazz-soul small-orchestra sound than to anything Chess was releasing. *Two Steps from the Blues* is half a singles compilation and half a unified statement, and it absorbs the gospel melisma of mid-1950s Ray Charles, the horn-section sophistication of Memphis R&B, and the loose 12-bar architecture of Texas blues into a single voice — which is, finally, the voice of the album: Bland's. He uses what would later become his signature "squall," that throat-clearing growl borrowed from a Reverend C.L. Franklin sermon, sparingly here. The restraint matters. Tracks like "I Pity the Fool," "Cry, Cry, Cry" and "Lead Me On" do not announce themselves as fusions; they sound like a fully formed adult vocal music that simply assumes you know the blues and the church and the supper club are no longer separate rooms. Scaruffi, who is generally cool on vocalist-led blues records, calls this performance the work of "the most refined blues singer of his generation," and parks it near the top of his soul ratings. Fantano has not reviewed it, but his repeated praise elsewhere of the Sam Cooke-to-Otis Redding axis projects directly onto Bland — the sentimental education behind Bland's phrasing is the same education Cooke had, applied to harder material. The agreement is structural. If Soul Blues has a charter document, this is it.

##### 2. O.V. Wright — *A Nickel and a Nail and Ace of Spades* (Back Beat, 1971)

The closest thing this list has to a sleeper consensus pick. Wright is the genre's great lost voice — a gospel quartet veteran (the Sunset Travelers, the Highway QCs) who came to secular music with no apparent interest in crossover pop, working primarily with producer Willie Mitchell at Royal Studios in Memphis around the same sessions that built Al Green's run. *A Nickel and a Nail and Ace of Spades* is the moment his recording catches up with what live audiences already knew. Mitchell's rhythm section — the Hi Rhythm Section, with Howard Grimes on drums and Leroy Hodges on bass — provides a softer, more lived-in pocket than Stax's, and the horns are arranged for grain rather than punch. Over that bed Wright sings as if he has personal information about every person in the room. The title track and "Eight Men, Four Women" are the canonical entries, but the sustained cumulative pressure across the album is the real argument. Scaruffi, who keeps O.V. Wright at the edge of his soul list, conceded in debate that this is "the deep-soul record that is also a blues record" — exactly the hybrid the genre claims as its territory. Fantano's projected enthusiasm is high: Wright is the kind of unpolished, vocally-driven Southern soul artist he tends to elevate when he encounters reissues (cf. his coverage of Daptone-era material), and the absence of slick crossover production removes his usual objection to later Soul Blues. Both critics agreed: if the casual listener only takes one O.V. Wright record, this should probably be it, ahead even of the slightly more famous *If It's Only for Tonight*.

##### 3. Albert King — *Born Under a Bad Sign* (Stax, 1967)

The Stax record that Scaruffi explicitly endorses in his rock history, and the one piece of Soul Blues territory where Fantano's sensibilities and Scaruffi's overlap without much extrapolation. *Born Under a Bad Sign* is a compilation in form — most of its tracks were originally singles cut over 1966 and 1967 — but a coherent album in feel, because it is essentially one band (Booker T. & the M.G.'s, the Memphis Horns, with King out front) playing one idea: blues as Stax soul, not as a separate genre to be visited. Steve Cropper's rhythm guitar and Donald "Duck" Dunn's bass lines are doing as much work as King's leads, and that is the point. King plays a Gibson Flying V tuned to an open minor and bends strings the way most guitarists pull them; the resulting tone, dry and vocal, is one of the most-imitated sounds in twentieth-century guitar. The title track, written by Booker T. Jones and William Bell, is the one everyone knows; "Crosscut Saw," "Personal Manager," and "Laundromat Blues" make the case that the album is not built around its single. Scaruffi's blurb effectively positions this as the moment electric blues stopped being a Chicago tradition and became a Southern one. Fantano's projected take leans on the rhythm section and the production: this is the cleanest, hardest-hitting iteration of the Stax house sound applied to a blues guitarist, and that combination — vocal/instrumental immediacy, no symphonic-soul gestures, no MOR drift — is squarely in his comfort zone. It is the rare Soul Blues record both critics would actively recommend without caveats.

##### 4. Bobby "Blue" Bland — *Here's the Man!!!* (Duke, 1962)

The follow-up to *Two Steps from the Blues*, and the record where Scaruffi pushed back hardest against the temptation to consolidate Bland's career into a single canonical entry. His argument: *Two Steps* defines the genre, but *Here's the Man!!!* is where Bland and Joe Scott push the format hardest, with bigger arrangements, more ambitious horn voicings, and more explicit references to cocktail-jazz vocal phrasing. "Turn On Your Love Light" is the radio hit, and it is structurally more soul than blues; "Yield Not to Temptation" is the record's emotional center, and it is closer to a sanctified pulpit performance than to a jukebox single. Fantano, projected onto this record, is a less certain enthusiast — the sentimentalized strings that occasionally enter the Duke arrangements are the thing he tends to score down in soul records. But Bland's vocal performance is so technically remarkable that it survives the framing; the squall is more developed here than on *Two Steps*, and he is starting to use it as a punctuation device rather than as a constant texture. Both critics ultimately accepted it at #4, with the understanding that several of the live and compilation Bland records (*Ain't Nothing You Can Do*, the *Live* set on Duke) make a strong adjacent case. The decision to admit two Bland studio albums into the top 10, while excluding any second album from O.V. Wright or Albert King, is the single biggest concession Fantano's projected vote made to Scaruffi's preference for the early-1960s Duke aesthetic.

##### 5. Z.Z. Hill — *Down Home* (Malaco, 1982)

The Malaco-era record both critics agreed had to make the list, and the one that defines the genre for a generation of listeners who came to it after the Stax/Duke peak had passed. Z.Z. Hill (Arzell Hill, no relation to Lauryn) had been recording since the late 1960s without breaking through; *Down Home* — and specifically "Down Home Blues," written by George Jackson — became the longest-charting blues album on Billboard's R&B chart in the 1980s, and effectively single-handedly kept the Malaco sound commercially viable through the decade. Scaruffi's reservations are real and he stated them flatly in debate: the production is glossier than the Stax-era records, the rhythm section leans on programmed elements, and the songwriting occasionally trades blues structure for a generic mid-tempo R&B groove. But he conceded that the title track is one of the most important Soul Blues recordings ever made on its own terms — a song explicitly about the music's audience listening to itself, and the closest the genre ever came to producing its own self-mythologizing standard. Fantano's projected position is more sympathetic: the production sounds dated, but in a way that reads to a younger listener as character rather than compromise, and Hill's vocal delivery is unpolished enough to survive the smoother arrangements. The album also contains "Cheatin' in the Next Room" and "Someone Else Is Steppin' In," both subsequently Soul Blues standards. *Down Home* is the moment the genre stops trying to cross over to the rock audience and starts speaking entirely to its own.

##### 6. Little Milton — *We're Gonna Make It* (Checker, 1965)

The Chess subsidiary record that anchors the mid-decade Chicago end of the Soul Blues axis — a counterweight on this list to the Memphis/Mississippi gravity of the other entries. Little Milton (James Milton Campbell Jr.) is the genre's most under-discussed major figure: a B.B. King-derived guitarist with a baritone closer to Bobby Bland's than to King's, working with arranger and producer Billy Davis on a body of Chess material that bridges urban blues and the Northern soul sound. The title track is structurally a soul song, not a blues — a 16-bar form, civil rights-adjacent uplift lyric, with a stop-time horn arrangement borrowed straight from the Motown/Chicago soul book. The album's deeper cuts — "Who's Cheating Who," "Blind Man" — sit closer to the blues, and Milton's guitar playing across the record is consistently one of the strongest cases for the soul/blues hybrid as a guitarist's tradition rather than purely a vocalist's one. Scaruffi gave this slot up reluctantly to Z.Z. Hill at #5 but insisted on Milton's inclusion; his read is that Milton is the artist who proves the genre is not just a Memphis phenomenon. Fantano's projected position is harder to nail down — there is no obvious analogue in his coverage — but the rhythm-section punch and the lack of overt sentimental arrangement should put him on the favorable side. Both critics noted that Milton's later Stax and Malaco records (*Annie Mae's Cafe*, 1987) are also strong, but agreed that the Checker work is the peak.

##### 7. Johnnie Taylor — *Taylored in Silk* (Stax, 1973)

The pick that required the most argument. Johnnie Taylor's career has three distinct phases — gospel quartet (the Highway QCs and the Soul Stirrers, the latter as Sam Cooke's replacement), Stax soul/blues (1968-1975), and the post-Stax disco-and-blues period (the Columbia "Disco Lady" hit, then the long Malaco run). The question is which phase produces his best album, and the consensus at #7 lands on the Stax middle period. *Taylored in Silk* is built around "I Believe in You (You Believe in Me)," a soul ballad rather than a blues, but the album as a whole sits firmly in the Soul Blues territory: deep-soul ballads, mid-tempo blues-structured cuts ("Cheaper to Keep Her" appeared on a different Stax LP but the framework is the same), and the kind of grown-folks lyrical material that defines the genre. The Muscle Shoals rhythm section and Don Davis's production give the record a more urbane sound than Stax's earlier blues work, which is the source of Scaruffi's hesitation — he prefers the rougher Stax aesthetic of *Born Under a Bad Sign*.
