Fantano's projected position is more enthusiastic: the production is varied without being slick, Taylor's vocal is one of the most technically accomplished of the era, and the ballads avoid the string-section excesses that mar later Soul Blues records. Both critics noted that Taylor's earlier *Who's Making Love* (Stax, 1968) is a stronger pure singles collection but a weaker album-as-album; *Taylored in Silk* wins on the album-statement criterion this list is using.

##### 8. Albert King — *I'll Play the Blues for You* (Stax, 1972)

The second Albert King record on the list, and the one Fantano's projected vote pushed harder for than Scaruffi's. By 1972 King had moved past the Booker T. & the M.G.'s rhythm section into the Bar-Kays / Memphis Horns / Movement-era Stax sound, and *I'll Play the Blues for You* sits at the intersection of blues and the Curtis Mayfield/Isaac Hayes-influenced socially conscious soul of the early 1970s. The title track is a slow blues with a spoken introduction over an Allen Jones arrangement; "Answer to the Laundromat Blues" reframes a 1966 single into the new aesthetic; "Don't Burn Down the Bridge" and "Breaking Up Somebody's Home" sit in the deeper pocket of the era's grown-up R&B. Scaruffi's preference is clearly for the leaner *Born Under a Bad Sign*, and he argued for cutting this in favor of a second O.V. Wright. The compromise was procedural: keeping a second King record allows the list to represent the early-70s Stax expansion, which is otherwise underrepresented after Bland's Duke entries dominate the early 1960s slots. Fantano's projected affection for this record is rooted in the rhythm section; the playing is looser and funkier than on the earlier Stax sides, and the production breathes in a way that the Malaco-era records do not. The record is also the case-in-chief for King as more than a guitarist — his vocal performance on the title track is one of the strongest of his career, and on this album it is the lead instrument. Coin flip note below: this slot's runner-up was O.V. Wright's *If It's Only for Tonight*.

##### 9. Bobby Rush — *Folkfunk* (Deep Rush, 2003)

The list's most contemporary entry and its most aesthetically anomalous. Bobby Rush's career stretches from the late 1960s ("Chicken Heads," 1971) through to the present, but his recorded output before the 2000s was scattered across small labels and inconsistent in production quality. *Folkfunk* is the album where Rush finally produces a fully realized statement on his own terms — recorded acoustically, mostly solo, deliberately stripped of the synth-pad Soul Blues production that had defined his and the wider genre's 1980s and 1990s output. The aesthetic move is not unrelated to what Solomon Burke and Mavis Staples were doing in the same period (the Joe Henry-produced records, the Anti- label aesthetic). For Fantano's projected vote this is the clearest hit on the list outside the obvious Stax/Duke canon: the production is austere, the vocals are foregrounded, and the song selection has the curatorial character he tends to reward in late-career artists. Scaruffi was more skeptical — his concern is that the record is a self-conscious gesture toward a folk/blues tradition Rush did not actually come from, and that it is a better Bobby Rush record than it is a Soul Blues record. He was eventually persuaded that the genre's late period needs a representative, and that *Folkfunk* is more interesting than the Malaco-era Bobby Rush albums it implicitly critiques. The pick is also a hedge against the list's center of gravity falling entirely between 1961 and 1982. Genres that are still alive deserve at least one entry from the period in which they remained alive.

##### 10. Denise LaSalle — *Trapped by a Thing Called Love* (Westbound, 1971)

The list's only entry by a woman, which is itself a critical failure of the genre's documentation rather than of the genre's reality — Soul Blues is in practice as much a women's music as a men's music, and the live circuit through the 1980s and 1990s was sustained as much by LaSalle, Shirley Brown, Mavis Staples, Ann Peebles, Etta James, and later Shemekia Copeland as by the male names that dominate critical writing. *Trapped by a Thing Called Love* is the strongest album-statement among the available candidates: produced by Willie Mitchell at Royal Studios with the Hi Rhythm Section (the same band as the O.V. Wright record at #2), built around the title track (a #1 R&B hit in 1971), and with deeper cuts that establish LaSalle's signature voice — funny, sexually direct, cutting in a way that anticipates the assertive female Soul Blues persona that Z.Z. Hill's "Cheatin' in the Next Room" answers from the male side. Scaruffi was the holdout here; his initial vote went to Etta James's *Tell Mama* (Cadet, 1968), which is a stronger album in the abstract but functions more as a soul record with blues moments than as a Soul Blues album proper. Fantano's projected vote leaned heavily toward LaSalle on the same logic that elevated O.V. Wright: Mitchell's production, Hi Rhythm's playing, the absence of the orchestrated soul gestures that mark Etta James's Chess work. The decision to seat LaSalle at #10 rather than push her higher is an honest assessment of the album's depth (the back half is weaker than the front half) rather than a comment on her status in the genre, which is canonical.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Two genuine ties emerged that neither critic could resolve on argument:

**Slot #8 — Albert King's *I'll Play the Blues for You* (1972) vs. O.V. Wright's *If It's Only for Tonight* (Back Beat, 1965).** Scaruffi's vote went to Wright; the projected Fantano vote went to King. Both are defensible: Wright represents an earlier and rawer iteration of the genre's deep-soul axis, King represents the genre's late-Stax expansion into socially conscious arrangement. Coin-flip resolved in favor of King. The Wright record moves to honorable mentions. Note that the list still contains an O.V. Wright entry at #2; if it did not, the coin flip would have been overridden in his favor.

**Bobby Bland representation — *Here's the Man!!!* (1962) vs. *Call on Me / That's the Way Love Is* (Duke, 1963).** Both are credible second-Bland choices. Scaruffi held for *Here's the Man!!!*; the projected Fantano vote was a wash. Coin-flip resolved in favor of *Here's the Man!!!*, which is now at #4. The 1963 record is a strong honorable mention.

A third near-tie — Z.Z. Hill's *Down Home* (1982) vs. Hill's *The Rhythm and the Blues* (Malaco, 1983) — was not coin-flipped because both critics agreed the cultural impact of *Down Home* (and specifically of the "Down Home Blues" single) made it the only defensible Hill entry, even if *The Rhythm and the Blues* is arguably a more even album.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **O.V. Wright — *If It's Only for Tonight* (Back Beat, 1965).** The runner-up at slot #8. Earlier and rawer than *A Nickel and a Nail*; would be #1 on a Wright-only list for some listeners.
- **Bobby "Blue" Bland — *Call on Me / That's the Way Love Is* (Duke, 1963).** The third Bland Duke album. Would be the genre's #2 record on most days; lost to coin flip.
- **Etta James — *Tell Mama* (Cadet, 1968).** The Muscle Shoals record. Excluded as more soul than Soul Blues, but a strong adjacent case.
- **William Bell — *The Soul of a Bell* (Stax, 1967).** Bell as performer (rather than as Albert King's co-writer); one of the most underrated Stax debut LPs.
- **Tyrone Davis — *Turn Back the Hands of Time* (Dakar, 1970).** Chicago Soul Blues with Willie Henderson production; "Can I Change My Mind" and the title track.
- **Latimore — *Latimore III* (Glades, 1974).** "Let's Straighten It Out." The most underrepresented major Soul Blues vocalist.
- **Marvin Sease — *Marvin Sease* (London, 1987).** "Candy Licker." A genre extreme: the unfiltered Chitlin' Circuit aesthetic, deliberately unsuitable for crossover. Scaruffi opposed inclusion in the top 10 on craft grounds; agreed the record is a necessary reference point.
- **Little Milton — *Annie Mae's Cafe* (Malaco, 1987).** The late-period Milton record; runner-up to *We're Gonna Make It*.
- **Shirley Brown — *Woman to Woman* (Truth/Stax, 1974).** The title track is canonical; album as a whole slightly thinner than LaSalle's *Trapped by a Thing Called Love*.
- **Johnnie Taylor — *Who's Making Love* (Stax, 1968).** Stronger singles, weaker album-statement than *Taylored in Silk*.
- **Mavis Staples — *You Are Not Alone* (Anti-, 2010).** The Jeff Tweedy-produced record. Included as a flag that the genre's late period is more interesting than the canonical Malaco lens admits, and as Fantano's most likely entry point into the genre.
- **Bobby Rush — *Sitting on Top of the Blues* (Deep Rush, 2019).** The Grammy-recognized late-career record. Stronger production than *Folkfunk*; the pick at #9 went to *Folkfunk* on aesthetic-statement grounds.

A note on the projection. The Fantano vote in this exercise is largely extrapolated; he has not, to public knowledge, written extensively about Soul Blues as a category. The extrapolations rest on his coverage of adjacent vocal-soul material (Sharon Jones, Charles Bradley, Mavis Staples, D'Angelo) and on his general production preferences (favorable to lean rhythm-section recordings, skeptical of orchestrated soul, sympathetic to late-career stripped-down statements). Where his projected vote pulled the list — toward O.V. Wright, toward the Stax-era Albert King, toward Bobby Rush's *Folkfunk* — those are the picks most exposed to revision if his actual position turns out to differ. Where the list reflects Scaruffi's strong opinions — the two Bland Duke records, the elevation of Little Milton's *We're Gonna Make It* — it rests on more solid ground.

---

### Blues Rock

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

*Cross-listed with Volume 4 (Rock I — Foundations); this is the anchor list.*

#### Debate Summary

This was the most contested list in the Roots volume. The fundamental disagreement is structural: Scaruffi treats blues rock as a transitional sub-form whose interesting moments are the ones that warp blues into something stranger (Beefheart, late-Cream studio collage, Hendrix at his most untethered), and whose later instances — SRV, Black Keys, Joe Bonamassa, the entire revival apparatus — are largely redundant exercises in idiom maintenance. Fantano works the opposite axis: he privileges emotional directness and craft over revolution, has documented enthusiasm for Jack White's roots-reverence, and is willing to credit Stevie Ray Vaughan's *Texas Flood* as a complete artistic statement even if it broke no new ground.

Round 1 produced overlapping cores — Cream's *Disraeli Gears*, Led Zeppelin *I*, *Truth* by Jeff Beck, *At Fillmore East* — and immediate divergence everywhere else. Scaruffi's Top 15 included *Trout Mask Replica* (Beefheart, 1969), *Safe as Milk* (Beefheart, 1967), *Blues from Laurel Canyon* (John Mayall, 1968), *Boogie with Canned Heat* (1968), and *Wheels of Fire* (Cream, 1968). Fantano's Top 15 leaned toward *Texas Flood* (SRV, 1983), *Brothers* (Black Keys, 2010), *Elephant* (White Stripes, 2003), *Still Got the Blues* (Gary Moore, 1990), and *Eliminator* (ZZ Top, 1983).

Round 2 fought over Beefheart. Fantano's position: *Trout Mask* belongs in Avant-Garde or Outsider, not Blues Rock — putting it here distorts both lists. Scaruffi conceded the cross-listing point but held that *Safe as Milk* is unambiguously blues rock and belongs. Round 3 fought over Cream — both wanted them but disagreed on which record. *Disraeli Gears* won on quality consensus; *Wheels of Fire* was relegated to Honorable Mentions despite Scaruffi's preference for its sprawl.

Round 4 fought over the modern era. Scaruffi wanted zero post-1980 entries except possibly White Stripes. Fantano insisted on at least three: SRV, White Stripes, Black Keys. Compromise: SRV and White Stripes in, Black Keys to coin flip against Rory Gallagher's *Irish Tour '74*. Coin flip went to Gallagher — see Coin-Flip Resolutions below.

Round 5 settled the Zeppelin question. Both critics agree *Led Zeppelin I* is the most blues-saturated of the catalog and belongs here; *II* is borderline (heavier, more original-credit-disputed). *I* in, *II* to Honorable Mentions, with the explicit note that Zeppelin's fuller catalog lives in Volume 4.

Round 6 closed the back end. Allman Brothers *At Fillmore East* and Hendrix's *Are You Experienced* were the last two unanimous additions. *Truth* by Jeff Beck Group rounded the list as the bridge between British blues purism and the heavier sound that would become hard rock.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Cream — *Disraeli Gears* (1967)

The album where British blues rock stopped pretending it was American and started being itself. Eric Clapton, Jack Bruce, and Ginger Baker had already proven they could play the blues straight on *Fresh Cream*; on *Disraeli Gears* they ran the form through fuzz pedals, Felix Pappalardi's pop-savvy production, and Pete Brown's surrealist lyrics, and what came out was a new template — the power trio as psychedelic blues machine. "Sunshine of Your Love" is the obvious touchstone, its descending riff borrowed structurally from delta blues but voiced through Marshall stacks until it sounds like nothing that came before. "Strange Brew" is twelve-bar architecture with Clapton's wah-wah turning Albert King's vocabulary into something hallucinatory. "Tales of Brave Ulysses" pairs Clapton's first commercially recorded wah-wah solo with mock-Homeric lyrics; "SWLABR" is psych-blues as cubist collage.

What separates this from later blues rock isn't authenticity — Cream were three white Englishmen in a recording studio — it's compositional ambition. Bruce and Brown's songs aren't blues structures with rock decoration; they're rock structures with blues DNA, which is the actual definition of the sub-genre once you strip away the mythology. Baker's drumming, jazz-trained and polyrhythmic, refuses the backbeat that lazier blues rock would default to; Bruce's bass sits melodically forward in a way that became the secret structural template for everyone from Geezer Butler to Lemmy. Scaruffi rates this as Cream's best studio statement and the British blues rock peak; Fantano credits it as the moment where the genre's commercial template and its artistic peak briefly coincided. Unanimous #1, never seriously contested.

##### 2. The Allman Brothers Band — *At Fillmore East* (1971)

The argument for *At Fillmore East* as the greatest American blues rock record is structural, not nostalgic. Duane Allman and Dickey Betts built a twin-guitar conversation that owed more to John Coltrane and Miles Davis's modal frameworks than to any Chicago blues template — "In Memory of Elizabeth Reed" is essentially a jazz piece with Betts's "Joe" Williams-meets-Charlie-Christian phrasing locked against Duane's slide. But the blues-rock backbone is non-negotiable: the twenty-three-minute "Whipping Post" is twelve-bar at heart, and "Stormy Monday" and "Statesboro Blues" are direct readings of T-Bone Walker and Blind Willie McTell.

What makes the live document definitive — and why both critics put it top-three — is that the studio versions of these songs on *Idlewild South* and the debut sound diminished by comparison. Berry Oakley's bass and the Jaimoe/Butch Trucks double-drum kit produce a low-end conversation that the studio of 1970 couldn't capture; Tom Dowd's recording of the March 1971 Fillmore residency was the first time anyone had successfully transcribed jam-band telepathy to tape. Duane's slide on "Statesboro" remains the single most-imitated tone in American rock guitar.

The sub-genre argument: Allman Brothers proved blues rock could sustain long-form improvisation without sacrificing emotional weight. Scaruffi acknowledged this even while noting his usual skepticism toward jam aesthetics; Fantano placed it second on his independent list. The album also functions as an elegy in retrospect — Duane was dead within seven months of recording — and that contextual weight is real, but the music would still be top-three without it. The bar against which every American blues rock live record is measured.

##### 3. Led Zeppelin — *Led Zeppelin* (1969)

The debut is where Zeppelin belongs in this volume; everything after sprawls into hard rock, prog, folk, and the proto-metal that owns Volume 4. *Led Zeppelin I* is the band at their most blues-saturated: "You Shook Me" and "I Can't Quit You Baby" are direct Willie Dixon covers (with the Dixon credit dispute that would haunt the band for decades); "Babe I'm Gonna Leave You" reframes a Joan Baez folk reading; "How Many More Times" is a Howlin' Wolf medley extended into a nine-minute suite. Even the originals — "Good Times Bad Times," "Dazed and Confused" (itself adapted from Jake Holmes) — sit firmly inside blues vocabulary.

Page's production is the sub-genre's most influential technical achievement after *Disraeli Gears*: the room sound on Bonham's drums, the close-mic'd amp distortion on the slide work, and the layered acoustic-electric textures on "Black Mountain Side" set the engineering template that 1970s blues rock would follow. Plant's vocal is still in its imitative Robert-Plant-doing-Robert-Johnson phase; the more original Plant comes later. Bonham is already fully formed.

Scaruffi's verdict — that *I* and *II* are "solid blues-rock" while the wider catalog is overrated — is the position taken here. Fantano largely agreed, noting that *I* is the album where Zeppelin's sources are most legible and the band's interpretive choices most defensible. The credit-dispute material is a real artistic and ethical issue but doesn't disqualify the record; it does mean any listener should also seek out the Dixon, Holmes, and Memphis Minnie originals. Critical consensus #3, no coin flip required.

##### 4. The Jimi Hendrix Experience — *Are You Experienced* (1967)

The placement is contested only because Hendrix sits on the boundary between blues rock, psych, and the proto-metal of Volume 4 — but the blues content of the debut is undeniable. "Red House" is a straight twelve-bar in B, played with the most expressive vibrato in recorded American guitar. "Hey Joe" is a folk-blues murder ballad given the Experience's full power-trio treatment. "Manic Depression" is a blues feeling refracted through 3/4 time. Even the more psychedelic material — "Purple Haze," "The Wind Cries Mary," the title track — is harmonically rooted in dominant-seventh blues vocabulary, just stretched and feedback-treated past the point where the framework is obvious.

What separates Hendrix from the British blues purists is the source material. Hendrix had played the chitlin' circuit with Little Richard and the Isley Brothers; his blues vocabulary was a working musician's, not a record-collector's. The Experience format — Mitch Mitchell's jazz-fusion drumming, Noel Redding's economical bass — gave him room to operate as a soloist in ways that Cream's denser arrangements didn't permit. Chas Chandler's production keeps the songs tight (no track exceeds five minutes) while letting the guitar tone breathe.

Both critics agreed on inclusion; the only debate was placement. Scaruffi argued for #2, treating Hendrix as the genre's single most important American figure. Fantano argued for #5, on the grounds that *Are You Experienced* is more often discussed as psych-rock canon than blues-rock canon and that the blues content, while present, is not the album's organizing principle. Compromise at #4. The Hendrix catalog continues into Volume 4 (psych and acid rock); his blues-rock anchor is here.

##### 5. The Jeff Beck Group — *Truth* (1968)

The forgotten pivot album. Released months before *Led Zeppelin I*, *Truth* established the template — heavy, slowed-down blues covers played by a power quartet with an explosive frontman — that Page and Plant would more famously execute. Beck's lineup was Rod Stewart on vocals, Ronnie Wood on bass, and Micky Waller on drums; the sound is rougher and less produced than Zeppelin's debut but the architecture is the same. "You Shook Me" appears here too, six months before Zeppelin's version, with Beck's slide work as physical and Stewart's vocal more idiosyncratic than Plant's. "I Ain't Superstitious" is a Howlin' Wolf reading that Beck turns into a wah-pedal showcase.

The reason *Truth* matters historically — and the reason both critics insisted it stay top-five — is that it documents the exact moment British blues rock shed its purist Mayall/Bluesbreakers DNA and became something heavier and more rock-oriented. Beck had played the Yardbirds slot between Clapton and Page; his solo debut is the sound of all three guitarists' approaches synthesized before Page would take credit for the synthesis. Stewart's vocal performance is the proof-of-concept for everything the early Faces and his solo work would do.

Scaruffi rates this above *Led Zeppelin I* and argued for #3 placement; Fantano accepted top-six but pushed back on top-three on the grounds that Stewart's vocal is the album's strongest element and the songwriting (mostly covers) is thinner than Cream's or Zeppelin's. Settled at #5. The most underrated record on this list and the one most likely to be unfamiliar to readers; the case for it is structural and historical, not nostalgic.

##### 6. Captain Beefheart and His Magic Band — *Safe as Milk* (1967)

The list's most contested entry, and the one whose inclusion most clearly reflects Scaruffi's hand. *Safe as Milk* is Beefheart before *Trout Mask Replica*, which means it's still legibly blues rock — the Magic Band lineup includes a young Ry Cooder on slide, the songs follow recognizable forms, and Beefheart's Howlin'-Wolf-on-amphetamines vocal is operating inside R&B vocabulary rather than against it. "Sure 'Nuff 'n' Yes I Do" is a delta blues with theremin; "Zig Zag Wanderer" is garage-blues with an unreliable narrator; "Electricity" pairs slide guitar with theremin and a vocal that anticipates everything Tom Waits would later do.

The argument against inclusion (Fantano's position): this record's primary listenership and critical placement is in psych-rock and avant-garde canons, not blues rock; including it distorts both the Blues Rock list and downstream Avant-Garde lists. The argument for (Scaruffi's position, which prevailed): every track on *Safe as Milk* is structurally a blues song, and the album's importance to the sub-genre is precisely that it shows what happens when a virtuoso blues vocalist refuses to defer to the British purist template. Beefheart heard the same source records as Clapton and Mayall and went somewhere else with them.

The compromise: include *Safe as Milk* but explicitly cross-list it (it will also appear in Avant-Garde when Volume 9 is built). *Trout Mask Replica*, by contrast, is too far past the blues form to count as blues rock and was kept off entirely — Scaruffi conceded this point in Round 3. Settled at #6. The most likely entry to surprise general readers and the one most defensible on close listening.

The historical placement matters too: *Safe as Milk* came out the same year as *Disraeli Gears* and *Are You Experienced*, which means 1967 produced three of the top six records on this list. That clustering is not coincidence — it is the year the British blues purist tradition (Mayall, early Cream) collided with American psychedelia and West Coast garage to produce blues rock as a distinct sub-genre rather than a regional dialect of either parent. *Safe as Milk* sits on the American side of that collision; Cream and Hendrix on the bridge; Zeppelin on the British side a year later. The architecture of the top ten is essentially the architecture of 1967-69, with Allman Brothers extending the timeline into 1971 and the post-1980 entries (Vaughan, White Stripes) standing as the genre's two legitimate later peaks.

##### 7. The White Stripes — *Elephant* (2003)

The only post-1980 entry both critics agreed on without coin flip. Jack White's project with Meg White was always closer to blues rock than to garage rock proper — the catalog from *De Stijl* onward is built on Son House and Blind Willie McTell readings filtered through the most aggressive minimalism in 21st-century rock. *Elephant* is the band at their most fully realized. "Seven Nation Army" is a riff so dominant it has eaten its own context, but the album also contains "Ball and Biscuit" — seven minutes of slow blues built on three chords and an extended Jack White solo that is the most legitimate post-Hendrix electric blues guitar performance recorded in the 2000s.

The case for inclusion is craft, not innovation. Jack White was not introducing new formal ideas; he was demonstrating that the blues rock tradition could still produce emotionally direct work in an era when Stevie Ray Vaughan revivalism had reduced the genre to museum-piece status. Meg White's drumming — much-mocked at the time, increasingly defended in retrospect — is the structural reason the songs work; the deliberate looseness gives Jack room to operate in ways a metronomic drummer wouldn't permit.

Fantano's reviews of subsequent White solo work and his consistent retrospective enthusiasm for *Elephant* made this a strong push from his side; Scaruffi was lukewarm but conceded that no other 2000s blues rock record had a better claim. Both agreed *Elephant* outperforms *White Blood Cells* and *De Stijl* on completeness even if the earlier records are more conceptually pure. The proof that blues rock did not die in 1985; the only record on this list a listener under 30 is likely to recognize unprompted.

##### 8. Stevie Ray Vaughan and Double Trouble — *Texas Flood* (1983)

The Texas blues revival's foundational document and the record that single-handedly proved blues rock had commercial life past the 1970s. Vaughan's debut is a four-day live-in-studio session recorded at Jackson Browne's studio with no overdubs; what you hear is the Double Trouble trio (Tommy Shannon on bass, Chris Layton on drums) playing the way they played Austin clubs. "Pride and Joy" is the obvious single, its shuffle template owing more to Lonnie Mack and Albert King than to any British source. "Texas Flood" itself is a Larry Davis cover that Vaughan turned into the slow-blues showcase by which all subsequent slow-blues guitarists are measured. "Lenny" is an instrumental ballad whose melodic vocabulary anticipates everything Derek Trucks would later do.

The case against (Scaruffi's standing position): Vaughan was a virtuoso traditionalist who advanced the form's vocabulary by zero millimeters; *Texas Flood* is a museum piece even as it's a great museum piece. The case for (Fantano's): the emotional directness and the sheer level of guitar craft are non-negotiable, and any list that excludes Vaughan in favor of avant-blues fails the basic descriptive function of canon-making. Fantano's position prevailed at #8.

The historical importance is also real: *Texas Flood* opened the door for the entire 1980s blues rock revival (Robert Cray, Jeff Healey, the late-period Allman Brothers reformation), without which the modern Bonamassa industry would not exist. Vaughan died young (helicopter crash, 1990) and the catalog is shorter than it should be; *Texas Flood* and *Couldn't Stand the Weather* are the essential pair, with *Texas Flood* the more focused statement.

##### 9. Rory Gallagher — *Irish Tour '74* (1974)

The coin-flip winner over The Black Keys' *Brothers* — see Coin-Flip Resolutions for the procedural note. Gallagher's status as the great underrated figure of European blues rock was a point both critics actually agreed on; the disagreement was only whether his peak record belonged in the top ten or honorable mentions. *Irish Tour '74* is a live document recorded across Belfast, Dublin, and Cork during the Troubles — Gallagher was the only major rock act still touring Northern Ireland in 1974, which is the contextual weight the record carries.

Musically: Gallagher's trio (Gerry McAvoy on bass, Rod de'Ath on drums, with Lou Martin on keys) play a tight, country-blues-inflected rock that owes more to the British blues purist tradition (Mayall, Peter Green-era Fleetwood Mac) than to the heavier American template Allman Brothers had established. "Tattoo'd Lady" is the strongest original; the cover of Tony Joe White's "As the Crow Flies" is the album's slow-blues showcase. Gallagher's tone — Stratocaster through a Vox AC30, no pedals, no studio polish — is the inverse of the gear-obsessive blues rock industry that followed.

Fantano accepted Gallagher as the more historically necessary inclusion; Scaruffi pushed for *Irish Tour* over Gallagher's studio records on the grounds that the live setting is where the blues rock idiom is most legitimately tested. The Black Keys' *Brothers*, which lost the coin flip, would have been a defensible #9 — Fantano made the case for it on production craft (Danger Mouse) and the "Tighten Up" / "Howlin' for You" pop-blues template — but Gallagher's historical weight and the closer fit to the sub-genre's purist core won out.

There is a secondary argument for Gallagher that came up in Round 4 and is worth surfacing: the Belfast / Dublin / Cork residency was recorded in venues other major British and Irish acts had stopped playing entirely because of the Troubles. The political weight is not the reason the record makes the list, but it is the reason the live performances have the urgency they do — Gallagher and the trio are playing for audiences who had no other rock acts to see. The result is a document of blues rock as functional working-class music rather than as commodity, which is the framing the sub-genre's mythology claims for itself but rarely earns. The list's most likely "wait, who?" entry for younger readers and the strongest argument for spending an evening with Gallagher's full catalog (start here, then *Tattoo*, then the self-titled debut).

##### 10. Canned Heat — *Boogie with Canned Heat* (1968)

The closing slot was the longest debate of the round. Candidates: *Boogie with Canned Heat*, *Fresh Cream* (kept off because *Disraeli Gears* is already at #1), *John Mayall's Blues Breakers with Eric Clapton* (the "Beano" album, lost on the grounds that it's more British blues than blues rock and properly belongs in Volume 1's Blues anchor list), Johnny Winter's *Second Winter*, and *ZZ Top's First Album* / *Tres Hombres*.

*Boogie with Canned Heat* won because it is the most coherent realization of the boogie-blues template that Canned Heat invented and that ZZ Top, George Thorogood, and the entire Texas roadhouse tradition would build on. "On the Road Again" and "Going Up the Country" were both top-40 hits — the second based on a Henry Thomas quill-pipe melody — but the deeper material is the long-form boogies ("Fried Hockey Boogie," "Whiskey Headed Woman No. 2") where Bob Hite's harmonica and Henry Vestine's guitar work establish the slow-burn approach to John Lee Hooker's vocabulary that the band would refine on *Hallelujah* and *Living the Blues*.

Scaruffi rated this above several more famous records on the grounds that Canned Heat were the only late-60s American blues rock band whose source-engagement was scholarly rather than imitative — Al "Blind Owl" Wilson was a working musicologist who had recorded with Son House and helped reconstruct Skip James's repertoire. Fantano accepted the inclusion as the strongest available choice for the slot, though he would have preferred *Tres Hombres* by ZZ Top on accessibility grounds. *Tres Hombres* moves to honorable mentions. The least famous record in the top ten and the one that does the most to legitimize the sub-genre as scholarly rather than merely pastiche.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

**Position #9 — Rory Gallagher *Irish Tour '74* vs. The Black Keys *Brothers* (2010).** Both critics agreed one of the two should be at #9; neither would yield. Scaruffi pushed Gallagher (historical weight, purist credentials, live-album definitiveness); Fantano pushed *Brothers* (production craft, modern relevance, the "Tighten Up" pop-blues template). Coin flip resolved in favor of Gallagher. *Brothers* moves to Honorable Mentions. The procedural note: this is the only coin flip in the Top 10. If Fantano's preference had been weighted more heavily on the modern-era axis, *Brothers* would be at #9 and Gallagher at #11.

**Beefheart placement — *Safe as Milk* vs. exclude entirely.** Not a coin flip but worth noting as a contested decision. Fantano's position was that Beefheart distorts the genre boundary; Scaruffi's was that excluding *Safe as Milk* falsely narrows the canon. Resolved by inclusion at #6 with explicit cross-list flag for future Avant-Garde volume.

**Cream second-album question — *Wheels of Fire* (1968) vs. *Disraeli Gears*.** Scaruffi preferred *Wheels of Fire* (the studio half, especially "White Room" and "Sitting on Top of the World"); Fantano preferred *Disraeli Gears*. Resolved on quality consensus rather than coin flip — *Disraeli Gears* is the more compositionally complete record and the one whose influence is more legible. *Wheels of Fire* moves to Honorable Mentions.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **Led Zeppelin — *Led Zeppelin II* (1969)**. Borderline blues rock, more often classified as proto-hard-rock; properly anchored in Volume 4. The Dixon credit disputes are heaviest here.
- **Cream — *Wheels of Fire* (1968)**. The double album with the live "Crossroads" — Scaruffi's preferred Cream record but lost to *Disraeli Gears* on consensus.
- **The Black Keys — *Brothers* (2010)**. Coin-flip loss to Rory Gallagher at #9; Fantano's strongest modern-era pick.
- **ZZ Top — *Tres Hombres* (1973)**. The boogie-blues template at its most commercially refined; "La Grange" is the canonical John Lee Hooker rewrite.
- **John Mayall's Bluesbreakers — *Blues Breakers with Eric Clapton* (1966)** ("the Beano album"). Properly belongs in the Blues anchor list rather than Blues Rock; the dividing-line record between the two sub-genres.
- **Johnny Winter — *Second Winter* (1969)**. The Texas blues virtuoso's strongest studio statement before Vaughan owned the territory.
- **Fleetwood Mac — *Then Play On* (1969)**. The Peter Green lineup at its blues-rock peak; "Oh Well" and "Rattlesnake Shake" are non-negotiable.
- **Free — *Fire and Water* (1970)**. Paul Rodgers's vocal and Paul Kossoff's guitar; "All Right Now" overshadows deeper blues material.
- **Gary Moore — *Still Got the Blues* (1990)**. The strongest of the post-1980 European blues rock revival records.
- **George Thorogood and the Destroyers — *Move It On Over* (1978)**. The roadhouse-blues template that kept the sub-genre alive through the late 1970s.
- **The Jeff Beck Group — *Beck-Ola* (1969)**. Less canonical than *Truth* but with some of Beck's strongest playing.
- **Eric Clapton — *Layla and Other Assorted Love Songs* (1970, as Derek and the Dominos)**. Borderline; the Duane Allman cameos pull it toward Allman Brothers territory.
- **Captain Beefheart — *Trout Mask Replica* (1969)**. Excluded from Blues Rock proper; too far past the form. Will appear in Volume 9 (Avant-Garde).

---

### British Blues

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

British Blues is the genre where two critics with very different relationships to the music end up closer than expected, but for almost opposite reasons. Piero Scaruffi treats the British blues movement as a transitional phenomenon — a laboratory in which a small generation of young white English musicians took the recordings of Muddy Waters, Howlin' Wolf, Otis Rush, Freddie King, and Elmore James, accelerated them, and exported the results back to America with enough distortion and amplification to make hard rock viable. He grants that the lab produced a handful of genuine masterpieces, mostly clustered in 1966–1969, and that beyond those peaks the records function more as historical documents than as standalone artistic statements. Anthony Fantano has almost no direct on-record commentary about the British blues canon — he came up reviewing 21st-century music, and his engagement with this scene is mostly inferred from his Peter Green appreciation, his general respect for emotionally direct guitar players, and his stated love of unpretentious blues phrasing. Wherever Fantano's position is extrapolated rather than documented, it is flagged inline.

Round one was a Top 15 from each side. Both lists opened with the same two records — Mayall's *Blues Breakers with Eric Clapton* (the Beano album) and Fleetwood Mac's *Then Play On* — and both included *Mr. Wonderful*, *English Rose*, *A Hard Road*, the first Ten Years After live record, and the debut Savoy Brown. Scaruffi's list was heavier on Peter Green-era Mac (he rates four of those records highly) and on the proto-blues-rock margins of the Yardbirds; Fantano's extrapolated list leaned harder on the records that age best as listening experiences rather than as historical artifacts — meaning more weight on Mac's *Then Play On* and Mayall's *Blues from Laurel Canyon*, less on the more reverent Chicago-blues recreations.

Rounds two through five were spent arguing four real fault lines. First: how much Yardbirds material counts as British blues proper versus proto-psychedelia and proto-hard-rock. Scaruffi wanted *Five Live Yardbirds* in; Fantano (extrapolated) saw it as more of a beat-group artifact and pushed back. Second: whether Cream's *Fresh Cream* belongs in this canon at all. Scaruffi argued that the British blues movement effectively ends when Cream begins, and that *Fresh Cream* is the hinge record where the genre breaks open into something else; Fantano (extrapolated) treated it more as a Cream record than a British blues record, but conceded the hinge argument. Third: Ten Years After's *Undead* versus *Ssssh* — the live document versus the studio peak. Both critics ultimately agreed *Undead* is the more essential British blues artifact because *Ssssh* is already pivoting toward boogie-rock. Fourth: how to handle Chicken Shack and Christine Perfect's contribution, which is undersung in most British blues canons but matters for the scene's actual texture.

Three irreconcilables went to coin flip and are noted explicitly in the Coin-Flip Resolutions section: the inclusion of Cream's *Fresh Cream*, the choice between Savoy Brown's *Blue Matter* and *A Step Further*, and whether Alexis Korner's *R&B from the Marquee* deserves a Top 10 slot on historical grounds despite being, by both critics' admission, a record more important to talk about than to listen to.

The final list leans heavily on 1966–1969, the genre's documented golden window, and weights Peter Green's Fleetwood Mac at three slots because both critics independently arrived at that number. It is not a "guitar hero" list — both critics actively pushed back on the tendency to reduce British blues to a Clapton-Green-Beck-Page tournament — but it is unavoidably a list shaped by what those four players, plus Mick Taylor and Alvin Lee, captured on tape in a four-year stretch.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. John Mayall & the Bluesbreakers — *Blues Breakers with Eric Clapton* (1966)

The Beano album. Both critics put this at number one and barely argued about it. Released in July 1966 on Decca, the record catches Clapton at the moment he had just walked out of the Yardbirds because he believed they were abandoning the blues, and before he formed Cream and abandoned the blues himself. The window is roughly seven months long. Mayall, who was already in his thirties and had been running a working blues outfit in London since the early sixties, gave Clapton exactly the band he needed — John McVie on bass, Hughie Flint on drums, Mayall himself on keys and harp — and turned him loose on a setlist of Otis Rush, Freddie King, Robert Johnson, Ray Charles, and Mose Allison covers, plus a few Mayall originals.

What makes the record sit at the top of the canon is the Les Paul-into-Marshall combination Clapton dialed in for these sessions. The "Hideaway" instrumental and "All Your Love" set the template for every blues-rock guitar sound of the next decade — sustained, vocal, slightly overdriven without being fuzzy, with a singing midrange that Clapton himself never quite recaptured after Cream got loud. Scaruffi's read is that the Beano is the moment when British musicians finally stop sounding like English students and start sounding like themselves playing the blues — a distinct dialect rather than a translation. Fantano's appreciation, extrapolated from his general comments about emotional directness in guitar playing, would emphasize the way Clapton phrases against Mayall's vocal, never showboating, leaving space.

The record also matters because it documents a band, not a soloist. Flint's shuffle on "Steppin' Out" is one of the great unsung British blues drum performances. McVie's bass, almost subliminal in the mix, anchors the entire thing. The Beano works as a Top 10 album of all time within the genre because it is also a Top 1 album, and nothing else in the British blues canon is even arguably better.

##### 2. Fleetwood Mac — *Then Play On* (1969)

Peter Green's last Fleetwood Mac record, and the one that pushed the band past pure blues into something stranger and harder to categorize. Both critics had this at number two with no debate. By 1969 Green had added Danny Kirwan as a third guitarist alongside himself and Jeremy Spencer, and the band had stopped being a strict twelve-bar outfit. *Then Play On* still sits inside the British blues tradition — "Rattlesnake Shake," "Coming Your Way," "Searching for Madge" — but it also has the modal, raga-adjacent guitar workouts and the long instrumental passages that point toward what blues-rock would become in the seventies.

Scaruffi rates this as Green's masterpiece and one of the few British blues records that holds up as a complete artistic statement rather than a song collection. His argument is that Green, more than any other British blues player, internalized the music to the point where he could write originals that sound traditional without copying anyone — "Oh Well" (added to later pressings), "Albatross" (released as a single around the same period), and the more ambitious instrumental passages on *Then Play On* itself. Fantano's extrapolated position would emphasize Green's restraint — a guitarist who could play very few notes very well, in a decade when the temptation was to play all the notes at maximum volume.

The record is also the one where Mick Fleetwood and John McVie become a great rhythm section rather than a competent one. The grooves on "Rattlesnake Shake" and "Searching for Madge" are loose in the right way — recognizably American blues feel filtered through musicians who had played together for years. Both critics agreed that the album's only weakness is the Jeremy Spencer interludes, which are charming as palate cleansers but slow the record down. The Top 2 status is unanimous.

##### 3. Fleetwood Mac — *Mr. Wonderful* (1968)

The second Fleetwood Mac album, the most traditional of the Peter Green-era records, and the one that documents the original lineup at peak Chicago-blues purity. Both critics had this in their Top 5; Scaruffi placed it slightly higher than Fantano (extrapolated) because of his preference for the documentary value of the early Mac records. Released in August 1968 on Blue Horizon, the record was cut in a few days with Christine Perfect (then of Chicken Shack, later Christine McVie) on piano and the Duster Bennett horn arrangements giving the whole thing a Chess Records feel.

What separates *Mr. Wonderful* from the other "British band plays Chicago blues" records of the period is Green's vocal. He sings the slow blues — "Need Your Love Tonight," "Stop Messin' Round," "Trying So Hard to Forget" — without affecting an American accent and without trying to sound older or more weathered than he is. The result is a record that doesn't apologize for being made by a 21-year-old Englishman, and is somehow more authentic for that. Spencer's slide work on the Elmore James material is functional rather than transcendent — both critics agreed Spencer is the band's weak link on this record — but Green's playing on the originals is some of the most economical blues guitar ever recorded.

The album is shorter than its reputation suggests (under 40 minutes) and doesn't have a single obvious hit, which is partly why it's underrated relative to *Then Play On*. Scaruffi's case for the higher placement is that *Mr. Wonderful* is the British blues record most willing to just be a blues record — no ambition toward rock, no extended solos, no production tricks. Fantano (extrapolated) would likely respect that restraint.

##### 4. John Mayall & the Bluesbreakers — *A Hard Road* (1967)

The follow-up to the Beano album, with Peter Green replacing Clapton on lead guitar. Both critics had this in their Top 5 with no debate. The record is often discussed as the lesser of the two Bluesbreakers Decca albums, but both Scaruffi and Fantano (extrapolated) pushed back on that conventional wisdom. Where the Beano is the Clapton record, *A Hard Road* is where Green announces himself — "The Supernatural" instrumental is the moment British blues guitar finds a voice that isn't a Chicago import.

The band on this record is John McVie on bass (still), Aynsley Dunbar on drums (replacing Hughie Flint), and Mayall doing more of the heavy lifting on harp and keyboard. The setlist is more varied than the Beano: a Freddie King instrumental, an Elmore James cover, several Mayall originals, and a couple of slow blues that Green absolutely demolishes. The title track is one of the great British blues vocal performances — Mayall, who is not a great singer in the conventional sense, delivers it with the right kind of weariness.

Scaruffi's framing is that *A Hard Road* is where British blues stops being purely derivative. Green's "The Supernatural" doesn't sound like any American blues record because it isn't trying to. Fantano (extrapolated) would likely focus on Green's tone, which is darker and more vocal than Clapton's Beano tone, and on the way Dunbar's drumming sits behind the beat rather than driving it. The fourth slot reflects the fact that this is the second-best Bluesbreakers record and one of the four or five most important British blues records ever cut, but it is unavoidably overshadowed by what came before and what its lead guitarist would do next.

##### 5. Fleetwood Mac — *English Rose* (1969)

The U.S. version of *Mr. Wonderful*, with several tracks swapped out — most importantly, the addition of "Albatross," "Black Magic Woman," and "Stop Messin' Round" — making it a different record in practice and arguably the more essential one. Both critics included this on their lists, with Scaruffi noting that he treats *English Rose* and *Mr. Wonderful* as effectively two halves of the same body of work. The compilation argument is that *English Rose* shows the Peter Green band at the moment of its commercial peak, with the instrumentals that defined the band for non-blues listeners.

"Albatross" is the obvious centerpiece — a Hawaiian-flavored, almost ambient instrumental that has nothing to do with American blues but everything to do with what Green's guitar could communicate when he wasn't playing twelve bars. "Black Magic Woman" is the original, before Santana made it a Latin-rock standard, and Green's vocal is more anguished than Santana's confident swagger; the lyric reads differently when you know what would happen to Green's mental health within two years. "Need Your Love So Bad," the Little Willie John cover, is one of the great slow blues vocals of the British scene, and the strings (added by Mick Fleetwood's brother-in-law) somehow don't sink the performance.

Both critics noted that placing *English Rose* this high is partially a workaround for the discography problem — the U.S. and U.K. versions of the early Mac catalog are messy, and *English Rose* effectively functions as a "best of the early Mac" record without being marketed as one. Scaruffi's preference is to count it as a separate record because the song selection is different enough to change the listening experience. Fantano (extrapolated) would likely agree on the strength of "Albatross" and "Need Your Love So Bad" alone.

##### 6. Cream — *Fresh Cream* (1966)

Coin-flip resolution; see notes below. Both critics agreed that whether *Fresh Cream* belongs in a British blues Top 10 is the single most contested question in this genre. Scaruffi's argument for inclusion is that the record was made in the same months as the Beano album, by the guitarist who played on the Beano album, and is essentially the British blues movement's last pure-blues statement before Cream pivots into psychedelic blues-rock with *Disraeli Gears*. Fantano's extrapolated position was more skeptical — Cream is its own story, and including *Fresh Cream* here crowds out a record that is more clearly British blues proper.

The coin flip went in favor of inclusion, on Scaruffi's argument that the genre's history makes no sense if you exclude the Clapton-Bruce-Baker trio's first record. *Fresh Cream* is mostly covers — Skip James's "I'm So Glad," Robert Johnson's "Four Until Late," Muddy Waters's "Rollin' and Tumblin'" — plus a few originals ("N.S.U.," "Sweet Wine") that point toward what Cream would become. The covers are the British blues part. Clapton's playing here is more aggressive than on the Beano album, partly because Bruce and Baker are pushing him, and partly because the band hadn't yet figured out how loud to be.

The record is uneven — both critics agreed *Fresh Cream* is the weakest album in this Top 10 — but it documents a transitional moment that no other record captures. Bruce's vocals are more soulful than they would be on later Cream records. Baker's drumming is jazzier. The whole thing sounds like three musicians who haven't decided yet whether they're a blues band or a rock band, and that ambiguity is what makes the record historically essential. Both critics noted they would not defend its inclusion at number 6 specifically — the coin flip determined inclusion, not ranking.

##### 7. Ten Years After — *Undead* (1968)

The live record cut at Klooks Kleek in London in May 1968, and the document of Alvin Lee's reputation as the fastest blues guitarist in Britain. Both critics had this on their lists, with Fantano (extrapolated) placing it slightly higher than Scaruffi because of the directness of the live setting. *Undead* is, on paper, a four-track album — the original release contained "I May Be Wrong But I Won't Be Wrong Always," "Woodchopper's Ball," "Spider in My Web," and "Summertime/Shantung Cabbage" — and it works because Ten Years After at this point is a band that lives on stage and barely exists in the studio.

Lee's playing is the obvious selling point. He could play more notes per second than anyone else on the British blues scene, and he is sometimes dismissed for that reason as a technician without feeling. *Undead* is the counterargument: the long "I May Be Wrong" workout is paced, dynamically aware, and uses the speed as one tool among several. The "Woodchopper's Ball" instrumental is a Woody Herman cover done as a vehicle for Lee's hot guitar, and it works because the rhythm section (Leo Lyons on bass, Ric Lee on drums, Chick Churchill on keys) understands when to push and when to lay back.

Scaruffi's framing is that *Undead* documents the British blues scene's relationship with American jazz — Ten Years After were jazz-blues hybridists in a way that Cream and the Bluesbreakers were not, and the swing on "Woodchopper's Ball" comes directly from that hybrid. Fantano (extrapolated) would likely emphasize the live energy and the way Lee never seems to lose the audience even during the longest instrumental passages. The record is in the Top 10 because it is the best live document of the British blues scene at its 1968 peak.

##### 8. John Mayall — *Blues from Laurel Canyon* (1968)

The first Mayall solo album made entirely without a Bluesbreakers framing — though Mick Taylor (later of the Stones) is on lead guitar — and the first Mayall record where the songwriting outpaces the playing. Both critics included this, with Scaruffi noting that *Laurel Canyon* is the British blues record that most clearly anticipates the singer-songwriter movement of the early seventies. Recorded after Mayall spent time in Los Angeles in 1968, the album is structured as a continuous song cycle about California — a concept record about a British bluesman in America, which is more interesting than it sounds.

Mick Taylor's playing here is more melodic than aggressive — closer to Peter Green's restraint than to Clapton's Beano-era blues-rock. Mayall's harmonica work is some of the best of his career. The album's quietest moments ("The Bear," "Long Gone Midnight") are almost folk-blues, and they work because Mayall's voice — which has always been more affecting than skilled — is given room to carry a lyric rather than a riff.

Scaruffi's case for the high placement is that *Laurel Canyon* is the moment when British blues develops a literary dimension — the songs are about something specific (Canned Heat, Frank Zappa, the California scene) rather than being abstract blues laments. Fantano's extrapolated position would emphasize the production restraint and the way the album holds together as an album rather than a collection of cuts. The eighth slot reflects the fact that this is the most underrated record in the British blues canon — both critics agreed it is more often respected than listened to, which is exactly backwards.

##### 9. Savoy Brown — *Blue Matter* (1969)

Coin-flip resolution; see notes below. Both critics agreed Savoy Brown deserved one slot in the Top 10 but disagreed on which record. Scaruffi favored *A Step Further* (1969) for its live half and Chris Youlden vocals; Fantano (extrapolated) favored *Blue Matter* for the song "Train to Nowhere" and the band's tighter studio arrangements. The coin flip went to *Blue Matter*. The record is Savoy Brown's third album and the one where Kim Simmonds's guitar finally finds a voice distinct from the Clapton-Green tradition — more boogie, more swing, less reverence.

"Train to Nowhere" is the obvious anchor — a Youlden vocal that pushes against the band's swing and a Simmonds solo that knows when to stop. "She's Got a Ring in His Nose and a Ring on Her Hand" is one of the better British blues novelty cuts, in the sense that it manages humor without becoming a parody. The studio half is tight; the live half (yes, the album is half-studio half-live, prefiguring *A Step Further*) catches the band at a 1968 London show that documents Savoy Brown as a working unit rather than a recording project.

Scaruffi's framing for Savoy Brown more generally is that they are the British blues band most willing to just be a working band — no concept records, no arty ambitions, just blues played professionally on a Chicago-Memphis-Texas axis. *Blue Matter* documents that ethos. Fantano (extrapolated) would likely respect the lack of pretension and the way Simmonds's playing prioritizes feel over technique. Both critics noted that Savoy Brown is the band that suffers most from being adjacent to more famous outfits — Mac, Bluesbreakers, Cream — and that *Blue Matter* deserves the Top 10 slot on its own merits, not as a "best of the also-rans" pick.

##### 10. Chicken Shack — *Forty Blue Fingers, Freshly Packed and Ready to Serve* (1968)

The Chicken Shack debut, with Stan Webb on guitar and vocals and Christine Perfect on piano and second vocals. Both critics had this in their Top 15; the Top 10 placement was decided by Scaruffi's argument that Chicken Shack is the most underrepresented band in standard British blues canons and that Christine Perfect's contribution to the scene needs to be marked, not as a footnote but as a pillar. Fantano (extrapolated) agreed on the underrepresentation point.

Released in 1968 on Blue Horizon (the same Mike Vernon label that put out the early Fleetwood Mac records), *Forty Blue Fingers* is the most Chicago-blues-faithful album in this Top 10. The arrangements are tight, Webb's slide work is functional rather than flashy, and Perfect's piano is the secret weapon — her playing has a Memphis-soul looseness that the more straight-laced British blues records lack. Her vocal on "When the Train Comes Back" is one of the great underrated British blues vocal performances.

The record's weakness, both critics agreed, is that it doesn't really assert a band identity beyond being a competent Chicago-blues recreation outfit. Where Fleetwood Mac's *Mr. Wonderful* (made by the same producer, on the same label, the same year) finds a distinct voice, *Forty Blue Fingers* settles for being good at what it's doing. The Top 10 placement is partly a corrective — both critics felt that excluding Chicken Shack from the British blues canon, as many lists do, makes the genre's history less coherent than it actually is. The tenth slot is also where the list ends, and both critics agreed that there is no eleventh album they felt strongly about including; the Top 10 line falls naturally here.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Three irreconcilable disagreements went to coin flip. They are noted explicitly so the resolution is transparent.

**Cream — *Fresh Cream* (slot 6 or excluded entirely):** Scaruffi argued the record is structurally part of the British blues movement and excluding it makes the genre's chronology incoherent. Fantano (extrapolated) argued it is more meaningfully a Cream record than a British blues record, and that including it crowds out a stronger British blues proper album (specifically, Mac's *Kiln House*, which neither critic strongly defended in the end). Coin flip: include. The placement at slot 6 reflects Scaruffi's mid-table preference; Fantano (extrapolated) would have placed it lower if forced to include it.

**Savoy Brown — *Blue Matter* vs *A Step Further* (slot 9):** Scaruffi favored *A Step Further* (1969) for the Chris Youlden live performances and the "Savoy Brown Boogie" medley. Fantano (extrapolated) favored *Blue Matter* for the studio cuts and "Train to Nowhere." Coin flip: *Blue Matter*. Both critics noted that either record could fairly hold the slot and that the choice doesn't change the overall shape of the Top 10.

**Alexis Korner — *R&B from the Marquee* (Blues Incorporated, 1962) — included or excluded:** Scaruffi argued the album is the foundational document of the British blues scene — Korner's Blues Incorporated was the band that incubated everyone from Mick Jagger to Jack Bruce — and that excluding it from a British blues Top 10 is historically incoherent. Fantano (extrapolated) argued the record is more important to talk about than to listen to, and that the Top 10 should be the ten albums one would actually return to, not the ten most historically significant. Coin flip: exclude. *R&B from the Marquee* is acknowledged in Honorable Mentions instead. Both critics noted this was the hardest call on the list.

#### Honorable Mentions

**Alexis Korner & Blues Incorporated — *R&B from the Marquee* (1962):** The foundational document. Excluded from the Top 10 by coin flip but acknowledged here as the record without which the British blues movement does not happen. Recorded at the Marquee Club in London, with Jack Bruce on bass and a young Charlie Watts on drums (briefly). The historical weight is enormous; the listening experience is more difficult than the records that followed.

**The Yardbirds — *Five Live Yardbirds* (1964):** The early Yardbirds with Eric Clapton, recorded at the Marquee. More R&B than blues, and arguably outside the strict British blues canon, but indispensable as the document of Clapton's pre-Bluesbreakers playing. Scaruffi pushed for inclusion in the Top 10; Fantano (extrapolated) saw it as more of a beat-group artifact. It sits here as the strongest Yardbirds case.

**John Mayall & the Bluesbreakers — *Crusade* (1967):** The third Bluesbreakers Decca record, with Mick Taylor on lead guitar before he joined the Stones. Often dismissed as the lesser of the Mayall Decca trilogy, but Taylor's playing on "The Death of J.B. Lenoir" and the title track is some of his most affecting pre-Stones work. Both critics agreed it was a Top 15 record that just missed the Top 10.

**Fleetwood Mac — *Fleetwood Mac* (1968):** The self-titled debut. Excluded from the Top 10 because *Mr. Wonderful* and *English Rose* together cover the same ground more completely. The "I Loved Another Woman" instrumental is a Peter Green showcase that any other band would have built an album around.

**Ten Years After — *Ssssh* (1969):** The Ten Years After studio peak, with "Good Morning Little Schoolgirl" and "I Don't Know That You Don't Know My Name." Excluded from the Top 10 because *Undead* is the more essential Ten Years After document and *Ssssh* is already pivoting toward boogie-rock proper.

**John Mayall & the Bluesbreakers — *The Turning Point* (1969):** The drumless Mayall lineup, with Jon Mark and Johnny Almond, leaning toward acoustic blues and jazz. A genuine pivot record and the moment the Bluesbreakers stopped being a Bluesbreakers record in any normal sense. Important but more of a coda than a Top 10 entry.

**Chicken Shack — *O.K. Ken?* (1969):** The second Chicken Shack record, with Christine Perfect's vocal on "I'd Rather Go Blind" — a cover that arguably surpasses the Etta James original in pure vulnerability. Both critics agreed it nearly made the Top 10 on the strength of that one performance.

**Peter Green — *The End of the Game* (1970):** Green's first solo album after leaving Fleetwood Mac. An almost free-jazz blues record that documents Green's mental state at a difficult moment. Not a great record by conventional measures, but a necessary listen for anyone serious about the British blues story.

**Cyril Davies All-Stars — singles and EPs (1962–63):** Davies died in 1964 before recording a proper full-length album, but his work with Korner in the early Blues Incorporated and his later All-Stars singles are foundational. Acknowledged here because excluding Davies entirely would misrepresent the scene's harmonica tradition.

---

## Part Two — Folk

Folk is the harder of the four roots traditions to canonize, because folk's own self-conception is anti-canonical. The whole point of the form, as it constituted itself in the twentieth-century revival, was the recovery of communal song — the Child ballads, the Lomax field recordings, the Harry Smith *Anthology of American Folk Music* — material that explicitly resisted authorship, that belonged in principle to whoever sang it next. To pick "best albums" of a tradition that distrusted the auteur is already a betrayal of the tradition's own values. Both critics in this section understand that, and both work around it differently.

The deeper substrate is older than any of its recorded forms. **Anglo-American Traditional / Pre-War Folk** captures the field-recording era — Carter Family, Woody Guthrie, Lead Belly, the Almanac Singers, the Library of Congress recordings, the Folkways and Stinson catalogs as they accumulated through the 1930s and 1940s — when American folk music was still primarily an ethnographic project. Alan Lomax and his father John Lomax did most of the canonical work; Moses Asch's Folkways Records (founded 1948) was the institutional home for the recordings as they became commercially available. The Carter Family's 1927 Bristol sessions for Ralph Peer (Victor Records) — recorded the same week as Jimmie Rodgers' first sessions, in the same Tennessee storefront, by the same producer — are the foundational document; the Lomax field recordings of Lead Belly, Muddy Waters (then unknown, recorded at Stovall Plantation in 1941), and the Mississippi prison farms are the deeper archive that the 1960s revival would draw on.

**British / Celtic Folk** is the parallel tradition on the other side of the Atlantic, and arguably the deeper of the two — the source archive that the American tradition was always partly drawing from. The Anglo-American ballad canon (the Child ballads, Cecil Sharp's collecting, the various regional song collections from Scotland, Ireland, and England) is the well that figures from Joan Baez to the Pentangle and Fairport Convention generation drew on. The 1960s British folk revival — Bert Jansch, John Renbourn, Anne Briggs, Shirley Collins, the Watersons, Davy Graham, the whole Topic Records axis — is its own canon, often more harmonically adventurous and instrumentally sophisticated than the American revival happening contemporaneously, and the cross-listings with the more widely-known American figures are extensive.

The genre's pivotal moment is the **1960s Folk Revival**, which is the section's largest and most contested sub-genre file. Between roughly 1958 (the Kingston Trio's "Tom Dooley" goes to #1 on the *Billboard* Hot 100, against everyone's expectations) and 1965 (Dylan plugs in at Newport, electrifying half the audience and alienating the rest), American folk music briefly becomes the central popular form. Greenwich Village is its capital, but the geography is wider: Berkeley, Cambridge, Chicago's Old Town, the Newport Folk Festival as the annual convocation. The revival's politics are leftist and integrationist; its musical project is the recovery of pre-war material; its commercial spine runs through Peter, Paul and Mary, Joan Baez, the Kingston Trio, and Dylan in his pre-electric phase. The brief on this list is to keep the focus on records that are centrally *about* the revival's group identity and re-engagement with traditional song; pure singer-songwriter and pure protest records get filed separately, even when the same artists appear in multiple files.

**Singer-Songwriter** is the inheritor sub-genre that the revival produced and was then eaten by. Around 1965-67, the revival's most ambitious figures (Dylan first, then Tim Hardin, Tim Buckley, Fred Neil, Leonard Cohen, Joni Mitchell, James Taylor, Carole King, Jackson Browne, Neil Young as a solo act) drop the traditional-source repertoire and start writing their own material, usually as solo voice and acoustic guitar but with broader harmonic and lyrical scope than the revival had allowed. The Laurel Canyon scene of 1968-72 is the form's commercial high-water mark; the cross-listings with the Rock volume (especially Folk Rock and Country Rock) are dense and decided per-album.

**Protest / Topical Folk** is the political wing of the revival, made coherent enough to canonize on its own. The lineage runs from the Almanac Singers and Pete Seeger through Phil Ochs, early Dylan, Joan Baez's protest-era catalog, Buffy Sainte-Marie, Joan Armatrading's first records, and forward into the late-60s and 70s Black topical-folk tradition (Gil Scott-Heron, the Last Poets — the latter cross-listed with hip-hop / spoken word in later volumes). The brief separates protest from revival on the grounds that the topical writers' aesthetic ambitions and audience were genuinely different — Ochs was reading the *New York Times* every morning and writing songs from it; Baez was trying to recover the Child ballads. They overlapped socially but were doing different artistic work.

**Folk Rock** is the form's electrified branch, which begins in earnest with the Byrds' "Mr. Tambourine Man" (June 1965) and Dylan's *Bringing It All Back Home* / *Highway 61 Revisited* / *Blonde on Blonde* sequence, and extends through the British wing (Fairport Convention, the Pentangle's electric records, Steeleye Span) and the American Bay Area (Jefferson Airplane's earlier records, Buffalo Springfield, the CSNY axis). The cross-listings with Rock are extensive — many of the records on this list are also on the Rock volume's relevant sub-genre files — and the anchor placements were decided album by album based on which tradition the record was more *centrally about*.

**Contemporary Folk** is the post-revival, post-singer-songwriter continuation: the figures who continued working in acoustic-guitar-and-voice formats from the late 70s through the present, often outside the major-label apparatus. Townes Van Zandt, John Prine, Guy Clark, Lucinda Williams in her early phase, Iris DeMent, Gillian Welch, Jason Isbell — many of these figures cross-list with alt-country, since the genre boundary between contemporary folk and Americana is essentially conventional rather than formal.

**Anti-Folk** is the mid-80s downtown New York reaction against the polished singer-songwriter aesthetic — Roger Manning, Lach (who founded the Antifolk Festival at the Sidewalk Café in 1985), Cindy Lee Berryhill, later Beck on his earliest cassettes, Daniel Johnston as honorary patron, the Moldy Peaches and Regina Spektor as the second-generation breakthrough. The aesthetic is deliberately rough: untrained voices, untuned guitars, willful amateurism as a critique of revival-era earnestness. The sub-genre is small but it has produced some of the most original folk records of the last forty years, and the file argues for it accordingly.

**Freak Folk / Wyrd Folk** is the early-2000s recovery of the deeper, weirder British folk tradition (the Vashti Bunyan / Comus / Incredible String Band axis) plus the American hill-country / occult / outsider lineage (Karen Dalton, Linda Perhacs, the Anthology of American Folk Music as living document). The canonical period is roughly 2002-2010 and the central figures are Joanna Newsom, Devendra Banhart, Sufjan Stevens (in his folkier mode), Espers, Vashti Bunyan in her late-career second act, Animal Collective on *Sung Tongs*. Fantano's strongest folk-related coverage is in this sub-genre — most of the canonical records were released during his active reviewing period — and his positions here are mostly direct rather than extrapolated.

**Indie Folk** is the broadest and most contemporary file. The Bon Iver / Fleet Foxes / Sufjan / Iron and Wine / Mountain Goats / Phoebe Bridgers axis, plus the British-folk-revival adjacent acts (Laura Marling, the entire Mumford and Sons commercial wave, briefly), plus the bedroom-pop-adjacent acoustic indie (Big Thief, Soccer Mommy in her acoustic mode, Adrianne Lenker solo). The commercial scale is much larger than any of the other folk sub-genres in this section; the artistic returns are uneven but the high points are real, and the file makes the case.

Ten sub-genres. Folk's structural problem, across all of them, is that the form's original ethical project — the recovery of communal song — keeps being pulled against its commercial reality, where the records that get heard are the ones with named authors and identifiable artistic personalities. The tradition's most durable artistic legacy may, paradoxically, be the figures who used the scene as a launchpad to leave it: Dylan, Hardin, Neil, Mitchell, Cohen, Newsom. The figures who stayed inside the traditional-ballad framework and refused to evolve past it are mostly the ones whose records have not aged as well, and the lists reflect that pattern with some discomfort. The folk-revival files in particular have honorable-mentions sections that name the discomfort directly.

---

### Anglo-American Traditional / Pre-War Folk
*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

This is the deepest, weirdest pool in the whole Volume 1 project: a body of music that wasn't made as "albums" at all, but as 78 rpm sides cut by record-company scouts (Ralph Peer, Frank Walker, Art Satherley) into hotel rooms and warehouses across the South between 1923 and 1942. The "albums" we rank are therefore mostly compilations — the LP-era and CD-era reissue projects (Folkways, Rounder, Yazoo, County, Document, Bear Family) that gave the music its second life. We treat these as albums on the strength of their curatorial vision, just as Scaruffi does.

**Scaruffi's posture is clear and well-documented.** He treats Harry Smith's *Anthology of American Folk Music* as a foundational artifact of American art, and he reserves his highest praise for the eerie, modal, "weird" pole of the tradition: Dock Boggs, Clarence Ashley, Roscoe Holcomb, the holler-singers and banjo-droners whose music sounds like it's coming from inside a well. He is less excited by the smoothness of the Carter Family or the showmanship of Charlie Poole, though he respects both.

**Fantano never reviews this material directly.** He was born in 1985; his on-record engagement with pre-war American music is essentially nil. To represent him at all we have to extrapolate from adjacent positions — his enthusiasm for raw vocal expression (the way he writes about Daniel Johnston, early blues, Jandek), his stated love of dense lyric work, his openness to outsider/idiosyncratic figures, and his general bias toward "documents" over "products." This is **flagged explicitly throughout**: every Fantano position below is a reasoned extrapolation, not a citation. Where he is unlikely to have an opinion, we say so.

The debate ran six rounds. The two critics agreed almost immediately on the top of the list (the Smith Anthology, Carter Family, Dock Boggs), agreed on most of the middle, and split on three questions: (1) how much shelf space to give the Carter Family vs. the haunted soloists; (2) whether Charlie Poole's string-band virtuosity counts as essential or merely good; (3) whether late-arriving compilations like Yazoo's *American Primitive* sets belong on a pre-war list. Two of those went to coin flip and are noted as such.

A note on scope: we included canonical reissue compilations of pre-war material (the Smith Anthology, Bear Family's Carter Family box, Revenant's Dock Boggs set, Yazoo curations) because that is how the music became culturally legible. We excluded Doc Watson, who was a child during the pre-war era; his early-1960s Folkways recordings belong on the 1960s revival list, not here.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Various Artists — *Anthology of American Folk Music* (Folkways, 1952; Smithsonian Folkways reissue 1997)

The single most consequential reissue in American music. Harry Smith — painter, occultist, filmmaker, junkie, autodidact — selected 84 sides from his personal collection of commercial 78s recorded between 1927 and 1932, arranged them across three double-LP volumes (Ballads, Social Music, Songs), and wrote cryptic, hand-lettered liner notes whose typography and tone are themselves part of the work. Folkways released it in 1952. It became the primary source text for the entire 1960s folk revival; Dylan, Baez, the New Lost City Ramblers, and dozens of others learned this music here.

Both critics put this at #1 and there was no debate. **Scaruffi** treats Smith as an authentic American visionary on the level of Charles Ives or Joseph Cornell — a curator whose act of arrangement is itself an art object, and whose taste runs precisely toward the haunted, the modal, the strange. His three-volume sequence, with its alchemical color-coding (blue/red/green) and its juxtaposition of ballad, sacred song, and dance tune, makes a sustained argument that the disparate strands of southern vernacular music share a single uncanny voice.

**Fantano** has not reviewed this directly, but the artifact's status as a curated, document-style release with idiosyncratic packaging — the kind of thing he champions in other contexts — makes him an obvious enthusiast. *Extrapolated, flagged.*

The 1997 Smithsonian reissue with John Fahey's, Greil Marcus's, and Luc Sante's essays is the canonical edition. The later Volume 4, assembled from Smith's notes and released posthumously by Revenant in 2000, is a worthwhile coda but not part of the core set. If you own one record from this entire list, this is it. Everything below is, in some sense, a footnote to it.

What makes the Anthology indispensable rather than merely historical is that Smith was not trying to document a tradition; he was trying to summon one. The track sequence, the arbitrary-feeling categorical splits ("Ballads," "Social Music," "Songs"), the refusal to separate black from white performers, the deadpan wire-service summaries Smith wrote for each track ("WIFE TURNS INTO OWL"), all push the listener toward a flat plane on which Cajun fiddle, Memphis jug band, sacred-harp choir, and Kentucky ballad singer share a single weather. Greil Marcus's *Invisible Republic* (later retitled *The Old, Weird America*) made this argument book-length; the Anthology makes it in six discs of sound. There is no second album in this genre that does the same work.

##### 2. The Carter Family — *In the Shadow of Clinch Mountain* (Bear Family, 2000) / *The Original Carter Family from 1927–1934* (various issues)

The first family of country music — A.P., Sara, and Maybelle — recorded over 250 sides for Victor and ARC between their 1927 Bristol sessions and the 1941 Border Radio years. The Bear Family box is the maximalist canonical edition (12 CDs). For most listeners the better entry is JSP's 5-CD set or the Smithsonian Folkways *Anchored in Love* compilation, but on a "best album" list we have to give the nod to the complete documentation: it is one of the foundational acts of American song, and the Bristol sessions of August 1927 are arguably the moment country music became recordable.

Maybelle's "Carter scratch" — melody on bass strings, rhythm on treble — invents a guitar style that the entire country and folk tradition then borrows. Sara's lead vocal is plainspoken to the point of severity; A.P.'s song-collecting (much of it credited to him whether he wrote it or not) gathered "Wildwood Flower," "Keep on the Sunny Side," "Single Girl, Married Girl," "Bury Me Beneath the Willow," "Wabash Cannonball" into a single corpus.

**Scaruffi** rates them highly but slightly below the haunted soloists; he hears them as the tradition's classicists, not its visionaries. **Fantano** would likely respect them as foundational without claiming them as personal favorites — *extrapolated.* Both agreed on top-three placement.

The Carter Family's importance is partly that they normalize the strangeness around them. Where Boggs sounds like a man possessed and Holcomb sounds like a man alone with God, the Carters sound like a family on a porch. That domesticity is itself a deep formal achievement: the songs are sung as if everyone already knows them, which is how folk songs are supposed to be sung. The harmonies — Sara's plain lead, A.P.'s wandering bass interjections, Maybelle's high tenor — invent the country-vocal architecture that the Louvin Brothers, the Stanley Brothers, and eventually Emmylou Harris and Gillian Welch all build on. Sara Carter's "Single Girl, Married Girl," recorded at Bristol with A.P. absent, may be the single most important early female lead vocal in American recorded music.

##### 3. Dock Boggs — *Country Blues: Complete Early Recordings (1927–1929)* (Revenant, 1997)

Twelve sides cut for Brunswick and Lonesome Ace between 1927 and 1929 by a Virginia coal miner who played banjo with a thumb-and-two-fingers attack borrowed from black musicians he heard on his way to the mines. "Sugar Baby," "Pretty Polly," "Country Blues," "Down South Blues" — Boggs sings in a flat, fatalistic voice that sounds like it has already accepted death. The banjo drones, modal, almost Hindustani in its tonal stasis.
