"Waiting Around to Die" is exactly what its title says — a first-person account of a life that has run out of forward motion — and Van Zandt sings it with a flatness that refuses the pity the song would otherwise generate. "Lungs" treats spiritual suffocation as a literal condition. "I'll Be Here in the Morning" is the rare love song from a writer who clearly does not believe morning will come.

Scaruffi places Van Zandt with Cohen as the genre's two great pessimists, and argues this album is the cleanest distillation of the writing. Fantano values the directness — Van Zandt does not have Cohen's literary apparatus or Mitchell's harmonic vocabulary, but his songs do something neither of theirs do: they refuse to perform their own seriousness. The plainness is the technique.

Steve Earle's later claim that Van Zandt is "the best songwriter in the whole world" is the sort of statement that gets quoted for its hyperbole, but on the evidence of this record it is not obviously wrong.

##### 6. Elliott Smith — *Either/Or* (1997)

Fantano's pick over *XO* and the eponymous record, and the entry that anchors the modern half of the list. *Either/Or* is Smith's third album and the last he made before signing to DreamWorks; it was recorded mostly alone, on home equipment, in Portland and at Jackpot! Recording. The fingerpicked acoustic foundation is unmistakably descended from *Songs of Leonard Cohen* and Nick Drake, but Smith's harmonic vocabulary — closer to the Beatles than to anyone in the folk tradition — gives the songs a melodic density the genre had largely abandoned by 1997.

The record's emotional register is the Cohen / Van Zandt tradition processed through nineties depression: clinical, specific, refusing the gestures of grandeur. "Between the Bars" is a love song to alcohol disguised as a love song. "Say Yes" is the rare Smith song with a clean major-key resolution, and the record places it last for reasons that feel structural — having ended on hope, the album earns the despair of everything before it.

Scaruffi's reservation about Smith is that he treats the form as essentially preserved rather than extended; nothing in *Either/Or* is technically beyond what Cohen or Drake had done. Fantano's response is that preservation at this level is its own achievement — the songs are written and sung and recorded with such precision that they reveal what the form can still do thirty years after Mitchell and Cohen finished demonstrating it.

The album's afterlife is unique: "Miss Misery," recorded after *Either/Or*'s release, drove Smith to the Oscars stage in a white suit and into a fame he was visibly unequipped to handle. *Either/Or* is the record before all of that, and remains the cleanest statement of his project.

##### 7. Sufjan Stevens — *Illinois* (2005)

Fantano's modern-canon pick, and the album that did the most to argue the singer-songwriter could still produce events in the post-Napster era. *Illinois* is the second and last entry in Stevens's abandoned fifty-states project; the first, *Michigan*, is more reserved, and the project never made it to the third record. *Illinois* compensates for the abandonment by attempting to be everything at once — orchestral suite, banjo-and-voice confessional, indie-pop record, and biographical novel about a state.

The arrangements are dense to the point of comedy: Stevens plays most of the instruments himself, layering oboes, vibraphones, sleigh bells, banjo, piano, and wordless choral voices into songs that nominally take the singer-songwriter form. "Casimir Pulaski Day" sits at the album's center and is its anchor: a fingerpicked acoustic song about a teenage friend dying of bone cancer, sung in Stevens's quietest voice, which the rest of the record's maximalism orbits without disturbing.

Fantano's reading: this is the album where the genre absorbs the post-rock and chamber-pop developments of the early 2000s without losing its core. The confession is intact, the song is intact, but the sonic palette has been expanded in a way that feels generative rather than ornamental.

Scaruffi's reservation, registered in debate: the project's conceit — songs about a state, with serial-killer biography ("John Wayne Gacy, Jr.") sitting next to UFO sighting and Sears Tower — risks treating the singer-songwriter form as a costume rather than a vocation. But the songs answer the objection. "Chicago" and "Casimir Pulaski Day" survive on their own without the apparatus, which is the test the album has to pass and does.

##### 8. Karen Dalton — *In My Own Time* (1971)

Scaruffi's long-standing position is that Dalton is one of the genre's great unrecognized voices, and *In My Own Time* — her second and final album — is the case for it. Dalton wrote almost none of the material; the record is built from interpretations of Dino Valenti, Richard Manuel, Paul Butterfield, and traditional songs. What makes it a singer-songwriter record despite that is the radical inhabitation of the material: Dalton recasts every song as a vehicle for a voice that sounds nothing like anyone else's.

The voice itself is the central fact. Dalton sings in a cracked, blues-derived contralto often compared to Billie Holiday's, but the comparison undersells what is actually happening — Dalton bends pitches, rephrases lines mid-syllable, and treats the melody as raw material rather than script. Bob Dylan, who had played with her in the Village in the early sixties, called her his favorite singer in *Chronicles*; the testimony reads less like a compliment than a recognition of category.

The arrangements, produced by Harvey Brooks at Bearsville, are unusually full for the period — banjo, fiddle, electric instruments, horns on "When a Man Loves a Woman" — but Dalton's vocal pulls everything down into an intimate register. Her version of "Something on Your Mind" (Dino Valenti) is the canonical reading; the song belongs to her now in the way "Hallelujah" belongs to Cohen.

Fantano's reservation, granted in debate: the writer-versus-interpreter line is real, and a top-10 weighted toward writers like Mitchell and Cohen has to make a special argument for an interpretive album. The argument is the voice. Nobody else sang like this; the record is unrepeatable; and the genre has always made room for the great interpreters (Cash, Cale, Anohni) when their reading constitutes a kind of writing.

##### 9. Judee Sill — *Heart Food* (1973)

Scaruffi's pick over Sill's debut, and the album he uses to argue she is the genre's most underrated composer. *Heart Food* is Sill's second and last record before her death in 1979; she produced it herself, arranged the strings, and played piano and acoustic guitar throughout. The result is a singer-songwriter record that sounds almost nothing like its peers — closer to Bach chorale than to Laurel Canyon, with chord motion that moves through key centers the way a fugue subject does.

The closing "The Donor" is the album's centerpiece and the case for Sill's distinction: a seven-minute setting of the Kyrie eleison through Sill's overdubbed multi-part vocal, building from solo voice to a full counterpoint texture and resolving on a sustained vowel that lasts almost twenty seconds. There is nothing else in the genre that attempts what this track attempts; the closest reference points are the English choral tradition and Brian Wilson's *Smile*.

The other songs work in a more conventional song-form, but Sill's harmonic vocabulary remains unusual — "Soldier of the Heart" moves through chromatic mediants in a way that recalls Schubert more than any songwriter contemporary. Her writing is theological without being doctrinal; she had been raised in conflict with religion and had returned to it on her own peculiar terms, and the songs treat Christian imagery as live material rather than borrowed iconography.

Fantano's reservation, granted in debate: the album sold almost nothing and Sill's life ended badly (heroin overdose, 1979), and the canonization since 2000 has sometimes treated the biography as the work. The work survives the biography. *Heart Food* is one of the four or five most ambitious compositions any singer-songwriter has written.

##### 10. Neil Young — *On the Beach* (1974)

The coin-flip slot, won over Randy Newman's *Sail Away*. *On the Beach* is the second of Young's so-called Ditch Trilogy — *Time Fades Away*, *On the Beach*, *Tonight's the Night* — and the one most often picked as the strongest. Young recorded it with the Stray Gators and Crazy Horse personnel during a period of personal collapse following the deaths of Crazy Horse guitarist Danny Whitten and roadie Bruce Berry, and the record carries the weight of that period without performing it.

The album splits cleanly. Side one contains "Walk On," "See the Sky About to Rain," "Revolution Blues," and "For the Turnstiles" — relatively compact songs, several with rock arrangements, including the Manson-haunted "Revolution Blues" with David Crosby on rhythm guitar. Side two is the album's argument: three long songs ("On the Beach," "Motion Pictures," "Ambulance Blues") that move at a tempo bordering on suspended, with Young's voice mixed close and the band playing as if waiting for something.

"Ambulance Blues" — eight and a half minutes — is the genre's great closing track. Young writes about Toronto, about the music press, about Patty Hearst, about himself, in a verse structure borrowed from Bert Jansch's "Needle of Death" and a vocal delivery that refuses any of the consolations of folk-rock. It is the song Scaruffi cites when arguing Young belongs in this conversation rather than with the country-rock figures he is sometimes grouped with.

Fantano: the album's pacing — one of the most slow-burning sequences in the genre — is the technique. Young trusts the listener to sit inside the dread. *On the Beach* was out of print for fifteen years and has only been correctly assessed since the 2003 reissue; both critics agree it now belongs in any serious singer-songwriter top 10.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

**Slot 10: Neil Young *On the Beach* vs Randy Newman *Sail Away* (1972)**. The most contested single decision of the debate. Scaruffi advocated for Newman as the genre's great ironist — a writer who used the singer-songwriter form to ventriloquize racists, slave traders, and small-minded narrators in a way nobody else attempted, and *Sail Away*'s title track (a slave-ship recruitment pitch sung as a Stephen Foster pastiche) and "Political Science" remain among the genre's most disturbing pieces of writing. Fantano argued for Young as the more direct emotional document and the album whose late-period rediscovery has changed the canon. Neither would yield. Coin flip: heads for Young, tails for Newman. Came up heads. *Sail Away* moves to honorable mentions, and is the first album any reader should reach for after the top 10.

**Slot 8: Karen Dalton *In My Own Time* vs Nick Drake *Pink Moon* (1972)**. Resolved without a flip — both critics agreed *Pink Moon* belongs in the British folk volume rather than singer-songwriter, on Scaruffi's reading that Drake is a different tradition. Fantano initially objected and then conceded the genre boundary. Drake will appear in the British/Celtic folk file.

**Slot 9: Judee Sill *Heart Food* vs Joni Mitchell *Court and Spark* (1974)**. Difficult call. *Court and Spark* is Mitchell's commercial peak and her cleanest fusion of folk and jazz; both critics admire it. Resolved in Sill's favor on grounds of representation — putting three Mitchell albums in a top 10 misrepresents the genre's breadth, while Sill's exclusion would leave one of the form's most distinctive composers off entirely. *Court and Spark* is the strongest honorable mention.

**Slot 6: Elliott Smith *Either/Or* vs Smith *XO* (1998)**. Settled cleanly. *Either/Or* is the lo-fi peak, *XO* is the major-label expansion; Fantano argued *Either/Or* is the more representative singer-songwriter record because the home-recorded sparseness is closer to the genre's core, and Scaruffi agreed.

#### Honorable Mentions

**Randy Newman — *Sail Away* (1972)**. Coin-flip loser for slot 10. The genre's most accomplished work of ventriloquism; the title track is one of the form's most ambitious pieces of writing. *Good Old Boys* (1974) is also essential and makes a comparable case.

**Joni Mitchell — *Court and Spark* (1974)**. Mitchell's commercial peak, with Tom Scott's L.A. Express on most tracks. "Free Man in Paris," "Help Me," "Down to You." Excluded only because three Mitchell albums in a top 10 felt like overweighting.

**Joni Mitchell — *The Hissing of Summer Lawns* (1975)**. Scaruffi's other favorite Mitchell. The transition record between *Court and Spark* and *Hejira*; "The Jungle Line" prefigures world music fusion by half a decade. Could displace *Hejira* in some moods.

**Bill Callahan — *Apocalypse* (2011)**. Fantano's modern-canon pick that didn't make the cut. Callahan's post-Smog baritone over open-tuned acoustic; "America!" is the genre's strongest contemporary state-of-the-nation song. *Sometimes I Wish We Were an Eagle* (2009) is comparably essential.

**Mark Kozelek — *Benji* (2014, as Sun Kil Moon)**. The album Fantano fought hardest for and the strongest argument that the genre still produces canonical work. Long-form spoken-narrative songs about death, family, the Newtown shooting; no choruses; almost no melody. The pre-collapse Kozelek record.

**Phoebe Bridgers — *Punisher* (2020)**. The genre's most successful 21st-century mainstream entry. "Moon Song" and "I Know the End" carry the record. Lost slot 6 to Smith and slot in honorable-mention territory to Kozelek.

**Bill Fay — *Time of the Last Persecution* (1971)**. The lost masterpiece — out of print for thirty years until Wilco's Jeff Tweedy resurrected Fay's reputation. Theological singer-songwriter writing closer to Cohen than to Cat Stevens, with a backing band that occasionally sounds like Spirit.

**Vashti Bunyan — *Just Another Diamond Day* (1970)**. The pastoral-English entry. Bunyan recorded it with Joe Boyd, abandoned music for thirty years after it sold nothing, and was rediscovered in the 2000s by Devendra Banhart. A different case for the form than the confessional mainstream.

**John Prine — *John Prine* (1971)**. The country-leaning singer-songwriter peak. "Sam Stone," "Hello in There," "Angel from Montgomery." Fantano made a case here; Scaruffi was cooler. Excluded in the final cut but unimpeachable.

**Laura Nyro — *New York Tendaberry* (1969)**. The piano-driven, gospel-and-Brill-Building-derived alternative to the Mitchell line. Nyro's compositions covered by The 5th Dimension, Three Dog Night, Blood Sweat & Tears, but her own performances are the canonical readings.

**Cat Stevens — *Tea for the Tillerman* (1970)**. The mass-market peak of the early-70s singer-songwriter wave. Both critics granted its quality; both agreed it sits a step below the canonical 10.

**Rodriguez — *Cold Fact* (1970)**. The lost-and-found Detroit singer-songwriter, rediscovered through *Searching for Sugar Man*. "Sugar Man" and "Crucify Your Mind" are the record's anchors.

**Tim Hardin — *Tim Hardin 1* (1966)**. The earliest singer-songwriter document on the honorable-mention list, and the record from which "If I Were a Carpenter" and "Reason to Believe" come.

**Sufjan Stevens — *Carrie & Lowell* (2015)**. The stripped-down counter to *Illinois*. A grief record about Stevens's late mother, recorded close-mic'd and almost solo. Some listeners (and some critics in earlier rounds of this debate) prefer it to *Illinois*.

**Jackson Browne — *Late for the Sky* (1974)**. The Laurel Canyon entry that came closest to making the list. "Fountain of Sorrow," "For a Dancer," the title track. Scaruffi's hostility to the L.A. mainstream kept it off; Fantano made a case.

**Paul Simon — *Graceland* (1986)**. The mid-career reinvention, included for completeness. Both critics agreed the record sits on the boundary of the genre rather than at its center.

**James Taylor — *Sweet Baby James* (1970)**. The album Scaruffi most actively dislikes among the canonical entries. Listed here because excluding it entirely from a singer-songwriter document would be revisionist — *Sweet Baby James* is the commercial peak of the soft-rock branch of the genre, regardless of its critical standing.

---

### Protest / Topical Folk

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Protest folk is the genre where the question "is the politics primary?" must be answered "yes" before the album qualifies. Both critics started from this premise but applied it differently.

**Scaruffi's opening Top 15** leans on the historical canon and the lyric-as-document tradition. He treats Phil Ochs as the single greatest pure protest songwriter, a position consistent with his published writing. His list: Ochs *I Ain't Marching Anymore*, Ochs *Pleasures of the Harbor*, Buffy Sainte-Marie *It's My Way!*, Dylan *The Times They Are a-Changin'*, Dylan *The Freewheelin'*, Woody Guthrie *Dust Bowl Ballads*, Gil Scott-Heron *Pieces of a Man*, Pete Seeger *We Shall Overcome*, Joan Baez *Joan Baez/5*, Billy Bragg *Talking with the Taxman About Poetry*, Steve Earle *The Revolution Starts… Now*, Ani DiFranco *Living in Clip*, Holly Near *Hang in There*, Si Kahn *New Wood*, Utah Phillips & Ani DiFranco *The Past Didn't Go Anywhere*.

**Fantano's opening Top 15** (extrapolated from his lyrical-density / political-clarity priorities, FLAGGED — he has not reviewed most of these) tilts modern and rapper-adjacent: Gil Scott-Heron *Pieces of a Man*, Gil Scott-Heron *Winter in America*, Dylan *The Times They Are a-Changin'*, Phil Ochs *I Ain't Marching Anymore*, Buffy Sainte-Marie *It's My Way!*, Billy Bragg *Talking with the Taxman About Poetry*, Ani DiFranco *Not a Pretty Girl*, Steve Earle *Jerusalem*, Woody Guthrie *Dust Bowl Ballads*, Phil Ochs *Pleasures of the Harbor*, Public Enemy — STRUCK (hip-hop, ineligible per assignment), replaced by Bragg/Wilco *Mermaid Avenue*, Dylan *The Freewheelin'*, Joan Baez *Joan Baez/5*, Rage Against the Machine — STRUCK (rock, ineligible), replaced by Ochs *Tape from California*, Gil Scott-Heron *Small Talk at 125th and Lenox*, Pete Seeger *We Shall Overcome*.

**Rounds of debate.** Round 1 settled the obvious: both kept Ochs *I Ain't Marching Anymore*, Buffy *It's My Way!*, Dylan *Times*, Guthrie *Dust Bowl Ballads*, Scott-Heron *Pieces of a Man*, Bragg *Taxman*, Pete Seeger's Carnegie Hall live document. Round 2 was the Dylan question — *Times* vs *Freewheelin'*. Scaruffi argued *Times* is more politically organized (less love-song dilution); Fantano agreed — *Freewheeli­n'* gets bumped to honorable mention because "Blowin'" and "Hard Rain" share an album with "Don't Think Twice" and "Girl from the North Country." Politics not primary enough. Round 3 was the Scott-Heron question — *Pieces of a Man* vs *Winter in America*. Fantano pushed *Winter*; Scaruffi held *Pieces*; we kept *Pieces* and put *Winter* at #6. Round 4 was the Ani DiFranco entry — Scaruffi wanted *Living in Clip*, Fantano *Not a Pretty Girl*; coin flip went to *Not a Pretty Girl*. Round 5 was Steve Earle — *Jerusalem* (post-9/11, "John Walker's Blues") vs *The Revolution Starts… Now*; Fantano's *Jerusalem* won on the politics-primary test. Round 6 was the closing slot — Holly Near vs Utah Phillips/Ani *The Past Didn't Go Anywhere*; the Phillips/DiFranco record got it for being a more singular document.

**The genre boundary problem.** Both critics agreed early that the assignment's "politics primary as organizing principle" rule does most of the work of building this list, and that the work it does is mostly exclusion. Public Enemy is hip-hop, struck. Rage Against the Machine is rock, struck. Marvin Gaye's *What's Going On* is soul, belongs on a different list. Bruce Springsteen's *The Ghost of Tom Joad* is folk-leaning singer-songwriter and was discussed at length — its protest content is real and topical (Mexican border, deindustrialization, the Joad lineage from Guthrie) but the album reads as Springsteen-doing-folk rather than as a primary protest record, and got pushed to a future Singer-Songwriter list. Mavis Staples's *We'll Never Turn Back* (Ry Cooder-produced civil rights re-readings) was discussed and tabled to a Gospel/Soul Protest list. Rage's exclusion was the only contested struck — Fantano (extrapolated) wanted a slot; Scaruffi held the line on instrumentation. The genre boundary held.

**The era-balance question.** Scaruffi's list was 60s-heavy; Fantano's spread further into the 80s/90s/00s. The final list is 1940 (Guthrie) → 2002 (Earle), with the 60s well-represented (Ochs, Dylan, Buffy, Seeger present in honorable mentions) but not dominant. Both critics agreed late that a protest folk list weighted entirely on the Vietnam moment would be a falsification of the genre's actual continuity. The Wobblies-to-DiFranco arc is the genre, not the 1962-1968 window inside it.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Phil Ochs — *I Ain't Marching Anymore* (1965)

The genre's purest statement. Ochs called himself a "singing journalist," and *I Ain't Marching Anymore* is what that phrase means in practice — every track is reportage with a melody. The title song is a first-person catalog of every American war from 1812 forward, refusing each one in the same voice; "Draft Dodger Rag" is a comic monologue that makes Vietnam evasion sound like patriotic duty; "Here's to the State of Mississippi" is a stanza-by-stanza demolition of a US state, line by line indicting cops, judges, churches, schools, until the listener realizes Ochs has just told an entire population to secede; "Iron Lady" is an abolition argument against the electric chair; "Talking Birmingham Jam" is the Sunday school bombing. The album works because Ochs is a real melodist — the tunes are catchy enough that the politics enter sideways through the chorus before the listener has decided whether to agree.

Scaruffi treats Ochs as Dylan's superior in the protest mode specifically — more rigorous, less ironic, more committed. The criticism — that Ochs's clarity is also his ceiling, that he never writes a song that means two things at once — is a feature here. This is an album about specific events and specific positions, and the demand for ambiguity is the demand that the songwriter pretend not to know what he knows. Ochs refuses. The record's reputation has been damaged by its very datedness — songs about Mississippi 1964 and draft cards do not "transcend their moment" — but that misreads what topical means. The point is to be of the moment, completely, and trust that future moments will rhyme. They have. Both critics had this at #1 or #2; we put it #1 because no other album in the genre is this disciplined about its own premise.

##### 2. Bob Dylan — *The Times They Are a-Changin'* (1964)

The album where Dylan is most fully a protest singer and least anything else. *Freewheelin'* has the bigger protest songs ("Blowin' in the Wind," "Masters of War," "A Hard Rain's A-Gonna Fall") but it also has love songs and talking blues and a side that is essentially personal. *Times* commits. The title song is the generational manifesto everyone knows, but the heart of the record is the long ballad sequence — "The Lonesome Death of Hattie Carroll" (a Black hotel worker killed by a young white aristocrat who got six months), "Only a Pawn in Their Game" (the Medgar Evers assassination, structurally arguing that the trigger man is a class victim), "North Country Blues" (an iron-range mining town's slow death told by a woman in first person), "Ballad of Hollis Brown" (a South Dakota farmer who shoots his family). These are short stories with melody. The journalism is detailed enough that the songs name actual people and dates.

Scaruffi is broadly Dylan-skeptical but acknowledges this period as Dylan's strongest claim to genius. Fantano (extrapolated, FLAGGED) treats Dylan-as-protest-songwriter as a baseline historical fact. Both keep it. The argument against — that Dylan's protest phase was a costume he discarded eighteen months later for *Bringing It All Back Home*, and that the songs are therefore insincere — does not survive contact with the songs. They are too specific to be costume. Dylan's later embarrassment about being called the voice of a generation is a separate fact from how good the writing is on this record. *Times* is what protest folk sounds like at the top of its commercial powers without conceding the politics.

##### 3. Woody Guthrie — *Dust Bowl Ballads* (1940)

The historical floor of the genre. Guthrie recorded these for Victor in two sessions, a cycle of songs about Okies migrating from the Dust Bowl to California — "Tom Joad" (a song-length retelling of *Grapes of Wrath*, which Guthrie wrote because he did not have time to read the book and wanted to know how it ended), "Do Re Mi" (the California border patrol turning back migrants for lack of money), "Dust Bowl Refugee," "Dust Pneumonia Blues," "Vigilante Man," "I Ain't Got No Home in This World Anymore." This is where the whole American protest folk tradition either starts or finds its most useful ancestor — the labor song tradition predates Guthrie (Joe Hill, the Wobblies, Aunt Molly Jackson) but Guthrie is the first to make a full *album*'s worth of topical narrative songs as a coherent statement.

Scaruffi reveres Guthrie as historical foundation. The voice is plain to the point of being affectless; the guitar is functional. The aesthetic argument is that the form is correct for the content — songs about people who have nothing should not sound expensive. Every later protest folk record on this list is in some way arguing with or extending this template. Bragg's *Mermaid Avenue* with Wilco is a literal extension (Guthrie lyrics, new music). Earle's hard-country protest is unimaginable without it. Ochs called Guthrie his model. The ranking at #3 rather than #1 reflects only that the recording is almost a field document — the album as artistic object is still being invented around Guthrie. As a moral object it is unsurpassed in the genre.

##### 4. Buffy Sainte-Marie — *It's My Way!* (1964)

The debut that introduced "Universal Soldier" (an antiwar argument structurally — every soldier is the precondition for every war, refuse to be one) and "Now That the Buffalo's Gone" (a Cree woman naming the genocide of Indigenous peoples in North America in 1964, when the genocide was not a thing American culture acknowledged). Sainte-Marie is doing two things no one else on this list is doing: writing as an Indigenous woman about Indigenous history in a moment when no one is, and doing it with a vibrato and a vocal attack that is genuinely strange — not folk-pretty, not blues, something of her own. "Cod'ine" is one of the great songs about addiction. "The Incest Song" is on a 1964 folk album. The record's range is the argument — the politics are not just antiwar/civil rights, they include who is allowed to be a subject of a song.

Scaruffi rates Sainte-Marie highly and specifically points to *It's My Way!* as a lost canonical record. Fantano's catalog is silent on her; FLAG that this placement reflects extrapolation from his stated political-clarity priorities and from Scaruffi's strong advocacy. The album's relative obscurity in mainstream rock criticism is itself a data point about whose protest counts as canon. "Universal Soldier" became a Donovan hit and was decoupled from its author; "Now That the Buffalo's Gone" was decoupled from its politics. Returning the songs to the album they came from, with Sainte-Marie's voice, is part of what putting it at #4 is for.

##### 5. Gil Scott-Heron — *Pieces of a Man* (1971)

The album where Scott-Heron stops being a spoken-word poet (his 1970 debut *Small Talk at 125th and Lenox* is closer to that) and starts being a songwriter, while keeping the political content. "The Revolution Will Not Be Televised" appears here in its full-band version — bass, flute, drums, Brian Jackson on Rhodes — and it is a better recording than the spoken debut version because the groove makes the joke land harder. "Lady Day and John Coltrane," "Save the Children," "Home Is Where the Hatred Is" (one of the most clear-eyed songs ever written about heroin from inside the addiction), and the title track — a man slowly losing his mind, told through his daughter's eyes — are not all topical in the narrow sense, but the album reads as a Black political document about the cost of American life on Black interior life.

The genre question — is Scott-Heron folk? — is answered by the assignment's own framing (folk-leaning protest spoken word). The answer is yes for the purposes of this list, in the same way that Guthrie's talking blues are folk despite the form not being song-shaped. Scaruffi is a major Scott-Heron advocate; Fantano (FLAGGED, extrapolated) is presumed sympathetic given Scott-Heron's general critical reputation and the political content. Both critics had this top five. Putting it at #5 rather than higher reflects that *Pieces of a Man* is also an album about heroin and family, not exclusively political — but the political register is the organizing one, which is the test.

##### 6. Gil Scott-Heron & Brian Jackson — *Winter in America* (1974)

The Scott-Heron record where the politics get bleaker and the music gets more inward. "H2OGate Blues" is the Watergate song — a long talking blues that names every Nixon administration crime in order, treats the impeachment as inadequate, and locates the actual problem in American empire rather than in this president. "The Bottle" is a community-level alcoholism song that does not moralize. "Peace Go with You, Brother" is a goodbye to a dead movement. The title song is the thesis: the 1960s did not end, they were drained — the energy is gone, the institutions captured the dissent, the season is winter. As a 1974 document this is the post-mortem of the protest era while the era is technically still ongoing.

Fantano's catalog (FLAGGED — no specific *Winter in America* review found in his published work) supports this kind of post-movement realism on political grounds — the album refuses the consolation that the struggle was won. Scaruffi treats Scott-Heron's mid-70s Strata-East period as a creative peak. The pairing of *Pieces of a Man* and *Winter in America* at #5 and #6 is intentional. They are companion records — one is the genre arriving at its full form; the other is the genre asking what happens after the form's moment passes. Both are still asking.

##### 7. Billy Bragg — *Talking with the Taxman About Poetry* (1986)

Mid-Thatcher Britain. The title is from Mayakovsky. Bragg's third album is the one where the love songs and the protest songs are no longer two different songs — "Greetings to the New Brunette," "Levi Stubbs' Tears," "There Is Power in a Union" (a literal labor anthem rewritten for 1986), "Help Save the Youth of America" (a protest song addressed to Americans by a Brit, reversing the usual direction), "The Marriage." The argument is Marxist humanism — capital deforms intimate life and intimate life is the field where capital is fought — and the songs are short, tuneful, and funny. Bragg's electric guitar is the only instrument on most tracks; the production is almost confrontationally lean.

Scaruffi rates this as Bragg's best, against the broader claim that *Workers Playtime* is the more polished record. The "more polished" point is the case against — *Taxman* sounds like a busker in a recording studio because Bragg essentially is one. Fantano (FLAGGED) is presumed sympathetic to Bragg's political clarity. The album sits at #7 because it does what protest folk had stopped doing by 1986 in the US — taking actual material conditions (Thatcher's miners' strike, the wage relation, the family) and writing songs that name the conditions without pretending the names are also metaphors. Bragg is the bridge between Guthrie and DiFranco; without him, the 90s revival of explicitly political folk is harder to imagine.

##### 8. Steve Earle — *Jerusalem* (2002)

Released a year after 9/11, into a country that did not want this album. "John Walker's Blues" is sung from the perspective of John Walker Lindh, the American captured fighting for the Taliban, and the song does not exonerate him but refuses to dehumanize him — which in 2002 was the same thing. The record was protested before it was heard. The rest of the album is in that register: "Amerika v. 6.0 (The Best We Can Do)," "Conspiracy Theory," "Ashes to Ashes," "The Truth," "Jerusalem" (the title track, which ends the record with a vision of peace that Earle does not quite believe but is going to assert anyway). Earle is country-leaning hard, but the album is folk in the protest-folk sense — the politics are the album's organizing principle, the writing is plainspoken, the targets are specific.

Fantano (FLAGGED — no published review of this specific Earle record found, but his sensibility favors clear-eyed post-9/11 dissent) prevailed over Scaruffi's preference for *The Revolution Starts… Now* in part because *Jerusalem* is more singular — *Revolution* is also a protest record, but *Jerusalem* is the one that took the actual professional risk. The Dixie Chicks paid for less. Earle made this album knowing what would happen, which is the test the genre exists to apply. The songs are good enough that they survive the controversy that was the point. The Guthrie inheritance is direct — Earle has been explicit that he sees himself in that lineage, and the album's plain-talk argumentative mode (state your position, name the case, sing it through) is Guthrie's mode. What Earle adds is a 2002-specific paranoia — surveillance, executive overreach, the manufacture of consent — that Guthrie did not need a vocabulary for. The album's commercial fate (modest sales, hostile press, no radio) confirmed the assumption: in the post-9/11 window, the country was not interested in being told it was wrong, and the album that told it anyway is the one worth keeping.

##### 9. Ani DiFranco — *Not a Pretty Girl* (1995)

Sixth album, peak of DiFranco's mid-90s run on her own label (Righteous Babe — itself a political fact). The title song is a feminist refusal of the music-industry expectation; "Tiptoe" is about abortion access, told sideways; "Crime for Crime" is about capital punishment with a specific case in mind; "Asking Too Much" is a relationship song that is also a politics-of-relationships song; "32 Flavors" is the queer manifesto disguised as a girl-power song. DiFranco's guitar style — percussive, open-tuned, a one-woman band attack — is part of the politics: she is the entire production, no major label, no men in the room.

Scaruffi originally pushed for *Living in Clip* (the live double from 1997, which is the better introduction); Fantano pushed for *Not a Pretty Girl* on coin-flip grounds — politics-primary, single-album coherence, written-not-collected. The flip went to *Not a Pretty Girl*. The placement at #9 is also a statement that the 90s-and-after protest folk lineage is real. Folk-as-political-tradition did not end in 1975 with Vietnam; it carried into the Reagan/Thatcher 80s through Bragg, into the third-wave 90s through DiFranco, into the post-9/11 00s through Earle. DiFranco is the connective tissue. The record holds up because the songwriting is genuinely strong — the politics are not the only thing that makes the album work, but they are the thing that makes it this album. Righteous Babe Records as institution is part of the argument: DiFranco built a label so she did not have to negotiate her politics with anyone, and the label outlasted most of the majors that would have wanted to sign and tame her. The album therefore exists as both song-cycle and proof of concept — that an explicitly feminist, explicitly queer, explicitly anti-corporate folk album can be made on its own terms, in 1995, and reach an audience without intermediation. The genre's labor-union DNA (own your means of production) shows up here as an actual business decision.

##### 10. Utah Phillips & Ani DiFranco — *The Past Didn't Go Anywhere* (1996)

Phillips was a Wobbly, an itinerant labor organizer, a railroad worker, and a folk performer in the storytelling tradition rather than the song-as-pop-form tradition. *The Past Didn't Go Anywhere* is built from his recorded monologues — labor history, hobo culture, anarchist autobiography — set by DiFranco to spare musical beds (samples, loops, guitar, percussion). The result is one of the strangest documents in the genre: an oral-history record that is also a 1996 production. "Anarchy" lays out the political position; "Bridges" is a meditation on infrastructure and class; "Korea" is a Phillips-as-young-veteran story; "Natural Resources" is a labor song without a chorus; "The Most Dangerous Woman" is about Mother Jones.

The closing slot was contested with Holly Near's *Hang in There* (a strong feminist/antiwar record, late-70s Redwood Records canonical) and Pete Seeger's Carnegie Hall *We Shall Overcome*. Seeger lost on the grounds that he is everywhere on this list as a presence and the specific album is not his strongest aesthetic statement. Near lost on the singular-document test — *The Past Didn't Go Anywhere* exists nowhere else. The choice is partly political: a list of protest folk that does not include the labor-history strand (Wobblies, IWW, Joe Hill tradition) is missing the genre's spine. Phillips is that strand made personal, and DiFranco's production keeps the record from being a museum piece. Scaruffi originally listed it as a curiosity; in debate it moved up because nothing else does what it does.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Three slots came down to coin-flips after debate could not resolve them.

**DiFranco album (Round 4):** *Not a Pretty Girl* (1995) vs *Living in Clip* (1997). Scaruffi argued *Living in Clip*'s scope (double live, career-spanning, the better single introduction). Fantano (extrapolated, FLAGGED) argued *Not a Pretty Girl* on the politics-primary test — it is more concentrated as a political document. Coin-flip went to *Not a Pretty Girl*. *Living in Clip* moves to honorable mentions.

**Earle album (Round 5):** *Jerusalem* (2002) vs *The Revolution Starts… Now* (2004). Scaruffi favored *Revolution Starts… Now* (more conventionally song-shaped, won the Best Contemporary Folk Grammy, more digestible as folk). Fantano favored *Jerusalem* as the more singular, more risk-taking, post-9/11 document. Coin-flip went to *Jerusalem*. The argument that won the flip: *Jerusalem* could only have been made in 2002, by Earle, after that specific event, and getting the song "John Walker's Blues" onto a major-label record was itself the political act. *Revolution* would have happened anyway. *Jerusalem* required someone to make it.

**Closing slot (Round 6):** *The Past Didn't Go Anywhere* (Utah Phillips & Ani DiFranco) vs *Hang in There* (Holly Near, 1977). Scaruffi initially favored Near for being the canonical women's-music-movement / Redwood Records statement. Fantano (extrapolated, FLAGGED) had no strong position. Coin-flip went to Phillips/DiFranco on the grounds that the record is structurally singular — there is no other album in the genre that fuses oral labor history with mid-90s production — and that Near's catalog is better represented by her overall body of work than by any single record. Near moves to honorable mentions.

A fourth potential coin-flip — Scott-Heron *Pieces of a Man* vs *Winter in America* — was avoided by including both at #5 and #6.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **Bob Dylan — *The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan* (1963).** Bigger protest songs ("Blowin' in the Wind," "Masters of War," "A Hard Rain's A-Gonna Fall," "Talkin' World War III Blues") but politics not primary at the album level — too many love songs. Lost the Dylan slot to *Times*.
- **Phil Ochs — *Pleasures of the Harbor* (1967).** The orchestral Ochs, more ambitious as production, less disciplined as protest. "Outside of a Small Circle of Friends" is a top-five protest song. Album is more pop-art than topical folk.
- **Pete Seeger — *We Shall Overcome: The Complete Carnegie Hall Concert, June 8, 1963* (1963).** Canonical, foundational, the night Seeger debuted the song's now-standard arrangement. Ranked just outside; a list cannot leave Seeger out and almost did.
- **Joan Baez — *Joan Baez/5* (1964).** "Birmingham Sunday," "There but for Fortune" (Ochs cover). Baez as protest interpreter rather than writer; the slot went to writers.
- **Ani DiFranco — *Living in Clip* (1997).** See coin-flip notes.
- **Steve Earle — *The Revolution Starts… Now* (2004).** See coin-flip notes.
- **Holly Near — *Hang in There* (1977).** See coin-flip notes. Canonical women's-movement protest folk, Redwood Records era.
- **Bragg & Wilco — *Mermaid Avenue* (1998).** Guthrie lyrics, new music. Excluded as collaborative-archival project rather than original-statement album, but essential listening.
- **Si Kahn — *New Wood* (1975).** Labor organizer's debut; "Aragon Mill," "Lawrence Jones." Canonical Southern labor folk; just outside.
- **Anne Feeney — *Have You Been to Jail for Justice?* (2001).** Picket-line songbook from a working organizer. Outside the top 10 on aesthetic-ambition grounds, inside the genre's heart.
- **Gil Scott-Heron — *Small Talk at 125th and Lenox* (1970).** The spoken-word debut. Lost to *Pieces of a Man* on song-form grounds; the original "Revolution Will Not Be Televised" is here.
- **Woody Guthrie — *Struggle* (recorded 1940s, released 1976).** The labor-song-cycle Guthrie record. Lost to *Dust Bowl Ballads* on coherence and recording quality.

---

### Contemporary Folk

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

> **Note on Fantano:** Anthony Fantano (b. 1985) began publishing as TheNeedleDrop around 2007–2009. He has substantive on-record reviews of Gillian Welch (*The Harrow & The Harvest*), Bonnie "Prince" Billy adjacent material, and the Adrianne Lenker / Big Thief catalog, but for most 1980s/90s contemporary folk records the assessments here are **extrapolations** from his demonstrated taste (sparse arrangement, vocal-forward composition, traditionalist craft, suspicion of slick AAA production). Where extrapolation is doing heavy lifting, it is flagged inline.

---

#### Debate Summary

The two critics arrived at this sub-genre from sharply opposed directions. Scaruffi treats contemporary folk as a continuation of the American songbook tradition, weighting records by compositional density, lyrical economy, and the ability to evoke a deeper continuity with old-time, gospel, and Appalachian sources. He is openly hostile to what he calls "coffee-house" folk — the AAA radio-friendly, soft-acoustic, mid-tempo strain that dominated the late-80s and early-90s commercial folk revival. He has explicit on-site enthusiasm for Iris DeMent, Gillian Welch, and Lucinda Williams, and grudging respect for Townes Van Zandt and Steve Earle's acoustic period.

Fantano (extrapolated from his on-record reviews of *The Harrow & The Harvest*, *Two Hands*, *Songs: Ohia*, and his general framework) tends to reward intimacy of recording, lyrical idiosyncrasy, and a certain kind of haunted austerity. He is more receptive to what Scaruffi dismisses as commercial coffee-house — Tracy Chapman's debut, for instance — when the songwriting carries genuine political weight. He is also more willing than Scaruffi to consider 21st-century records (Adrianne Lenker, Mount Eerie) as legitimate contemporary folk.

The debate ran six rounds. Round 1 was lopsided: both lists agreed on Gillian Welch's *Time (The Revelator)*, Lucinda Williams' *Car Wheels on a Gravel Road*, and Iris DeMent's *Infamous Angel*. Round 2 produced friction over Tracy Chapman's debut — Scaruffi conceded its songwriting strength but pushed back on its production sheen; Fantano held firm. Round 3 hit Steve Earle's *Train a Comin'* versus Townes Van Zandt's *No Deeper Blue*: Scaruffi favored Townes; Fantano favored Earle for the higher floor of song-by-song quality. Round 4 was a fight over Suzanne Vega — Scaruffi argued her self-titled debut belongs in singer-songwriter canon, not folk; Fantano countered that the acoustic-traditional framing of *Suzanne Vega* (1985) places it firmly in this volume. Round 5 negotiated the inclusion of Adrianne Lenker's solo work — Fantano insisted, Scaruffi was skeptical of the recency. Round 6 closed out the long tail (Greg Brown, Nanci Griffith, Dar Williams, John Gorka, Eliza Carthy, Shawn Colvin, Ani DiFranco).

Three irreconcilables went to coin flip. Two of those flips Scaruffi won, one Fantano won.

---

#### Top 15 — Scaruffi

1. Gillian Welch — *Time (The Revelator)* (2001)
2. Iris DeMent — *Infamous Angel* (1992)
3. Lucinda Williams — *Lucinda Williams* (1988)
4. Gillian Welch — *Revival* (1996)
5. Iris DeMent — *My Life* (1994)
6. Townes Van Zandt — *No Deeper Blue* (1994)
7. Lucinda Williams — *Car Wheels on a Gravel Road* (1998)
8. Steve Earle — *Train a Comin'* (1995)
9. Greg Brown — *Dream Café* (1992)
10. Gillian Welch — *The Harrow & The Harvest* (2011)
11. Nanci Griffith — *Once in a Very Blue Moon* (1985)
12. Iris DeMent — *The Way I Should* (1996)
13. Eliza Carthy — *Red Rice* (1998)
14. Townes Van Zandt — *At My Window* (1987)
15. Lucinda Williams — *Sweet Old World* (1992)

#### Top 15 — Fantano (extrapolated where flagged)

1. Gillian Welch — *Time (The Revelator)* (2001)
2. Gillian Welch — *The Harrow & The Harvest* (2011)
3. Lucinda Williams — *Car Wheels on a Gravel Road* (1998)
4. Tracy Chapman — *Tracy Chapman* (1988) [extrapolated]
5. Iris DeMent — *Infamous Angel* (1992) [extrapolated]
6. Adrianne Lenker — *songs* (2020)
7. Suzanne Vega — *Suzanne Vega* (1985) [extrapolated]
8. Gillian Welch — *Revival* (1996)
9. Steve Earle — *Train a Comin'* (1995) [extrapolated]
10. Lucinda Williams — *Lucinda Williams* (1988) [extrapolated]
11. Ani DiFranco — *Dilate* (1996) [extrapolated]
12. Dar Williams — *The Honesty Room* (1993) [extrapolated]
13. Shawn Colvin — *Steady On* (1989) [extrapolated]
14. John Gorka — *Land of the Bottom Line* (1990) [extrapolated]
15. Iris DeMent — *My Life* (1994) [extrapolated]

---

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Gillian Welch — *Time (The Revelator)* (2001)

The unanimous number one. Welch and David Rawlings record an entire LP on two acoustic guitars and two voices, and the result feels less like a folk album than a sustained meditation on what folk music is *for*. The production — sparse, dry, minimally overdubbed — strips the form back to its essential geometry: two strings, one harmony, one room. *Revelator* opens with the title track's slow descent and never raises its voice; the ten songs run a deliberate, unbroken arc from the spectral country lament of "My First Lover" through the historical murk of "Ruination Day Part 2" to the fourteen-minute slow-burn closer "I Dream A Highway."

Scaruffi treats this record as the culmination of the post-O Brother folk revival's seriousness — an album that takes the Anthology of American Folk Music as a starting point rather than a costume. He singles out the interlocking guitar work between Welch and Rawlings as one of the great instrumental dialogues of the era, and the vocal blending as a study in restraint: Welch never showing off, Rawlings never overplaying, both yielding to the song. The historical layering — Casey at the Bat, the McKinley assassination, Elvis on television — turns the record into a kind of folk-archaeological exercise that never reads as academic.

Fantano's review of the follow-up (*The Harrow & The Harvest*) explicitly cites *Revelator* as the high-water mark of the duo's catalog, praising the way the album refuses the consolations of melody and form. He hears in "Everything Is Free" a quietly devastating piece of music criticism — written in response to Napster-era anxieties about the value of recorded music — and treats the closing track as a sustained dare to the listener's patience that pays off. Both critics agree the record is the rare contemporary folk album that earns its length, its silences, and its lack of ornament. It is the only album in this list both placed at #1 independently.

##### 2. Lucinda Williams — *Car Wheels on a Gravel Road* (1998)

The most argued-about record on both lists, and the eventual #2 by mutual concession. *Car Wheels* is the record where Williams' sixteen-year apprenticeship in Texas blues, Louisiana country, and Greenwich Village folk finally collapsed into a single voice — a Southern-gothic landscape painting that uses electric guitars and full-band arrangements but never abandons the folk-tradition core. Scaruffi resisted the inclusion at first, arguing that the rock-leaning production pushes the album into Americana proper, but conceded after relistening to "Lake Charles," "Concrete and Barbed Wire," and the title track that the songcraft is folk-traditional even when the arrangements aren't.

The album took six years to make and went through three producers; the final version was sequenced by Williams and Steve Earle and Roy Bittan and bears the marks of every fight along the way. Fantano flagged its production as the strongest argument against pure-acoustic dogmatism in this list — the record demonstrates that contemporary folk can absorb full-band arrangement without losing its folk identity, provided the songwriting is dense enough to carry it. He cites "Drunken Angel" (the elegy for Blaze Foley) and "Greenville" as among the best-written songs of the 90s.

What both critics finally agreed on is that *Car Wheels* is the rare folk record that maps a specific geography — the I-10 corridor from Lake Charles to Macon — without ever sounding like regional kitsch. The detail is observational, not nostalgic. Williams' voice — the famous Louisiana drawl, the controlled rasp — does what folk voices are supposed to do: carry the lyric without ornament, let the listener trust what is being said. The fact that Steve Earle co-produced parts of it ties it directly into the broader contemporary folk lineage and makes its inclusion here, despite the electric instrumentation, feel correct.

##### 3. Iris DeMent — *Infamous Angel* (1992)

Scaruffi's number two pick, Fantano's number five. The compromise placement at #3 reflects how completely both critics agree on DeMent's stature — the disagreement is only about ranking, not quality. *Infamous Angel* was produced by Jim Rooney with a small acoustic band, and its sound — fiddle, mandolin, dobro, and DeMent's startling Pentecostal-trained voice — sits closer to the Carter Family than to anything else recorded in 1992. The voice itself is the centerpiece: a high lonesome Arkansas cry that splits the difference between Sara Carter and a country-gospel revival singer, with no studio polish whatsoever.

The songs are uniformly strong. "Let the Mystery Be" — DeMent's most-covered composition — is a four-minute argument against religious certainty disguised as a folk-gospel singalong. "Our Town" is a slow, devastating obituary for a dying Midwestern community. "Mama's Opry" is autobiography, naming her mother (Flora Mae) as the source of the entire DeMent style and ending with Flora Mae herself stepping in to sing the chorus. The closer, "Higher Ground," is a straight gospel hymn delivered without irony.

Scaruffi places the record at the front of his contemporary folk canon because it does what almost no other record of its decade does: it sounds like a 1930s field recording in spirit while being a fully composed 1992 album. Fantano's hypothetical reception (extrapolated from his Welch reviews and his general love of unornamented vocal-forward folk) reads as similarly enthusiastic — DeMent's refusal to modernize her sound or her voice fits exactly the austerity-as-virtue framework he applies to *The Harrow & The Harvest*. Both critics agree the record is one of the cleanest debuts in the genre's history.

##### 4. Gillian Welch — *Revival* (1996)

Welch's debut, produced by T-Bone Burnett, and the record that announced the entire contemporary-traditionalist project that would peak with *Revelator* five years later. *Revival* is more produced than what would follow — there are full-band arrangements, a more conventional country-folk palette — but the songwriting is already fully formed. "Orphan Girl," "Pass You By," "Tear My Stillhouse Down," and "Annabelle" are all on the first record; any of them would anchor a lesser album.

Scaruffi hears *Revival* as the moment when the post-O Brother revival became a serious artistic project rather than a costume drama. Welch was 28 when she made it and had no Appalachian background — she grew up in Los Angeles, the adopted daughter of two TV writers — but the songs do not read as appropriation. They read as scholarship made personal. Burnett's production keeps the band small and dry, and Rawlings' guitar work is already the second voice it would become.

Fantano's framework prizes records where a songwriter announces a complete worldview on the first try, and *Revival* qualifies. The thematic terrain — death, work, faith, displacement — is what Welch and Rawlings would spend the next twenty-five years refining. Some of the more conventional production choices ("Only One and Only" leans on a near-Nashville arrangement) keep it below *Revelator* in both lists, but the floor is high: there are no weak tracks. The argument for placing it at #4 over *The Harrow & The Harvest* (which both critics rank lower despite its critical acclaim) is that *Revival* introduced the form, while *Harrow* refined it; in this canon, the introduction matters more.

##### 5. Lucinda Williams — *Lucinda Williams* (1988)

The self-titled record on Rough Trade — recorded after Williams' folk-and-blues period on Folkways and before her Mercury major-label run — is the album where she located her voice. Produced cheaply, recorded with a small band, and originally rejected by every Nashville label, it contains "Passionate Kisses," "Changed the Locks," and "Side of the Road" — three songs that would each eventually become standards covered by other artists.

Scaruffi places it above *Car Wheels* on his personal list because the arrangements are leaner — closer to the folk-acoustic core — and the songwriting is, in his view, more concentrated. There are no five-minute tracks; every song does its work in three or four minutes and steps aside. The combination of folk acoustic guitar work, light electric coloring, and Williams' early-period vocal control makes it the most "folk" record in her catalog by a clear margin, even if *Car Wheels* is the more famous album.

Fantano (extrapolated) would likely place it lower — his framework rewards the production density of *Car Wheels* over the spareness of the 1988 record — but the album survived both lists because the songs are undeniable. "Side of the Road" in particular is one of the most quietly radical love songs of the 80s: a woman explaining to her partner that she occasionally needs to walk away from him not because of any conflict but because she needs to remember who she is alone. The lyric is Williams at her most economical and unsentimental. Both critics conceded the record's place in the canon even when its placement was contested.

##### 6. Iris DeMent — *My Life* (1994)

DeMent's second record, produced again by Jim Rooney, and the album where the voice and the songwriting both deepen into something darker. *Infamous Angel* had a public-facing warmth — singalongs, gospel closers, a sense of community. *My Life* turns inward. "No Time to Cry," the centerpiece, is a slow autobiographical confession about working through her father's death in real time, refusing the sentimental closure folk songs about parental death usually offer. "Sweet Is the Melody" and "Easy's Getting Harder Every Day" extend the same emotional register: adult-life exhaustion, the failure of inherited religious frameworks, the slow accumulation of grief.

Scaruffi rates it nearly as high as *Infamous Angel* and treats it as the more mature record of the two. Fantano (extrapolated) would likely have a similar response — his praise for *The Harrow & The Harvest* explicitly rewards records that refuse easy uplift, and *My Life* is exactly that record. The arrangements remain austere: acoustic guitar, fiddle, light pedal steel, DeMent's voice carrying everything else. The record is shorter than *Infamous Angel* (ten tracks, all originals except a single Lefty Frizzell cover) and feels more compressed.

The case for placing it at #6 — above several more famous records in the long tail — is that DeMent is the only artist on this list other than Welch with two top-ten records, and *My Life* earns the second slot on its own terms rather than as a footnote to the debut. The voice is now doing things the debut only hinted at: slower phrasing, more deliberate vibrato, the ability to hold a held note across an entire bar without strain. It is the sound of an artist who has decided not to be in a hurry.

##### 7. Tracy Chapman — *Tracy Chapman* (1988)

Fantano won this coin flip. Scaruffi conceded reluctantly: the production is the lush, late-80s acoustic-pop sheen he distrusts, and "Fast Car" became inescapable enough to overshadow what is otherwise a quietly serious record. Fantano's argument was that the songwriting is unimpeachable and the political content — "Talkin' 'bout a Revolution," "Across the Lines," "Behind the Wall" — places the record in a folk lineage closer to early Joan Baez than to coffee-house balladry. The unaccompanied a cappella delivery of "Behind the Wall" (a lyric about domestic violence) is the moment the record most clearly declares its folk-tradition allegiance.

Chapman's voice — a low, controlled contralto with no studio processing — is the production's anchor. David Kershenbaum's arrangements use light percussion, acoustic guitar, and minimal keyboard, and the few moments of fuller band sound are restrained enough that the record never crosses into pop. Scaruffi's complaint about the production polish is fair on tracks like "Baby Can I Hold You," but the floor is high enough that even those songs work as folk-tradition compositions.

The political weight of the album is what kept it on both lists. "Fast Car" — the album's biggest hit — is a four-verse short story about cyclical poverty written with Carver-like economy: the narrator's plan to escape with her partner, the brief stability they achieve, the partner's drift back into the patterns of his own father, the narrator's eventual decision to leave. Fantano cites it as one of the most-covered folk songs of the past forty years for a reason: it is structured exactly like a traditional ballad, with a clear narrator, a setting, a turn, and a final reckoning. Both critics agreed that the record's reputation as a one-hit-wonder underrates the rest of the LP, particularly "Mountains o' Things" and "She's Got Her Ticket."

##### 8. Steve Earle — *Train a Comin'* (1995)

Earle's all-acoustic comeback record, made after his prison stint and rehab, with Norman Blake, Peter Rowan, and Emmylou Harris on backing vocals. *Train a Comin'* was the record that announced Earle's shift from country-rock outlaw to folk-tradition songwriter, and it earned a Grammy nomination for Best Contemporary Folk Album in 1996. It is the most clearly "contemporary folk" record in Earle's catalog — every track is acoustic, the band is a small bluegrass-leaning ensemble, and the song selection mixes Earle originals with traditional and Townes Van Zandt covers.

Scaruffi resisted the inclusion at first — Earle's broader catalog is rock-leaning enough to push him toward alt-country — but conceded that *Train a Comin'* is a genuine folk-traditionalist exercise rather than a stylistic detour. The Townes cover ("Tecumseh Valley") and the traditional "Northern Winds" frame the record explicitly as a folk-canon document. Earle's originals — "Goodbye," "Mercenary Song," "Hometown Blues" — fit comfortably alongside the traditional material without sounding pastiche.

Fantano (extrapolated) ranked the record higher than Scaruffi did, treating it as a model of the recovery-album-as-folk-record form: spare arrangements, autobiographical lyrics that resist self-pity, a clear sense that the artist has stripped his sound down because he had to. Emmylou Harris's harmony work is the album's secret weapon — her vocal blend with Earle on "Goodbye" is one of the most quietly devastating moments in 90s folk recording. The compromise placement at #8 reflects mutual respect for the record without quite either critic putting it in their personal top five.

##### 9. Townes Van Zandt — *No Deeper Blue* (1994)

Townes' final studio album, released two years before his death, and a record that has historically been overshadowed by his 70s work. Scaruffi argued strongly for its inclusion against Fantano's preference for Adrianne Lenker — the coin flip went to Scaruffi. The case for *No Deeper Blue* is that it is the rare late-career record where a songwriter near the end of his life is still writing first-rate material rather than coasting on legacy. "Marie," "Katie Belle Yum Yum," "BW Railroad Blues," and the title track are all Townes originals on the level of his best 70s work.

The production, by Eric Paul, is dry and acoustic — closer to the *Live at the Old Quarter* aesthetic than to the heavier 70s studio records. Townes' voice is audibly worn (the years of hard living are present in every phrase), but the songwriting compensates: "Marie" is a four-verse story-song about a homeless couple that ranks with "Pancho and Lefty" as one of his most economical narrative pieces. The final verse — the protagonist losing his partner and their unborn child — is delivered without melodrama.

Fantano (extrapolated) would likely have ranked this lower in favor of more recent records, but the placement at #9 reflects the canonical weight of Townes' position in the folk tradition. He is the bridge figure between the Greenwich Village folk era and the contemporary period, and *No Deeper Blue* is the record where he explicitly hands off the tradition — the last of his major studio records, made when he could no longer tour reliably, recorded in a single short session with minimal overdubs. Scaruffi's argument prevailed because the record represents the closing of a chapter that no other album in this list represents.

##### 10. Adrianne Lenker — *songs* (2020)

Fantano's coin flip win at the end of the list. Scaruffi resisted on grounds of recency — *songs* came out in 2020, well after the 1980s–2000s window the volume nominally targets — but Fantano's argument prevailed: the record is an explicit return to the contemporary folk aesthetic of Welch and DeMent, recorded in a single cabin in western Massachusetts with one engineer and one acoustic guitar, and it represents the form's ongoing vitality rather than its terminus.

Lenker recorded the album in April 2020 in a one-room cabin with a portable rig, no overdubs, no studio polish. The eleven tracks were written and tracked in roughly a month. Fantano gave the record one of his highest scores of 2020 and explicitly framed it as a contemporary-folk document in the lineage of *Time (The Revelator)* — same instrumental austerity, same vocal-forward composition, same refusal of modern production tools. "anything," "ingydar," and "not a lot, just forever" are the highlights; the songwriting is more impressionistic than DeMent's narrative folk but operates in the same tradition.

The case for inclusion against Scaruffi's recency objection is that the volume's nominal 1980s–2000s framing is heuristic rather than absolute, and *songs* is the clearest argument that the contemporary folk form remained creatively alive into the 2020s. Lenker's voice — thin, close-miked, occasionally cracking — is closer to the field-recording aesthetic than almost anything in the Welch catalog, and the cabin recording's audible room ambience (birds, wind, distant rain) makes the folk-tradition framing literal rather than figurative. Both critics ultimately agreed the record belonged on the list; the only fight was about ranking.

---

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Three irreconcilable disagreements went to coin flip:

**1. Townes Van Zandt — *No Deeper Blue* (1994) vs. Suzanne Vega — *Suzanne Vega* (1985)**
Scaruffi won. Vega's debut moves to honorable mentions. Scaruffi argued Townes' canonical position in the contemporary folk lineage outweighs Vega's debut, and that Vega's record sits more comfortably in singer-songwriter canon. Fantano accepted but flagged that Vega's exclusion is the result of a category-line decision rather than a quality judgment.

**2. Adrianne Lenker — *songs* (2020) vs. Greg Brown — *Dream Café* (1992)**
Fantano won. Scaruffi conceded reluctantly. The argument for Lenker was that the record represents the form's continuation; the argument for Brown was that *Dream Café* is the clearest example of working-folk-songwriter craft in the 90s. Both records have strong cases; the coin flip favored recency-as-vitality over canonical completeness.

**3. Tracy Chapman — *Tracy Chapman* (1988) vs. Iris DeMent — *The Way I Should* (1996)**
Fantano won. Scaruffi conceded. The argument was about which DeMent record (if any beyond *Infamous Angel* and *My Life*) belonged in the top ten versus which non-DeMent record. Scaruffi preferred a third DeMent slot; Fantano insisted Chapman's debut had to be present. The flip favored Chapman, partly on the grounds that three DeMent records in the top ten would distort the canon's diversity.

---

#### Honorable Mentions

**Suzanne Vega — *Suzanne Vega* (1985)** — The category-line casualty. Lost the Townes coin flip; would have been #10 or #11 otherwise. The acoustic-folk debut before *Solitude Standing* moved her into more pop-leaning territory.

**Gillian Welch — *The Harrow & The Harvest* (2011)** — Surprisingly omitted from the top 10. Both critics agreed it belonged near the top of the long tail but neither pushed for it over *Revival*. Fantano's on-record review is glowing; he ultimately placed *Revelator* and *Revival* above it.

**Greg Brown — *Dream Café* (1992)** — Scaruffi's favorite working-folk-songwriter record. Lost the Lenker coin flip. The case for inclusion is the consistency of the song-by-song craft; Brown is the closest contemporary folk has to a Loudon Wainwright figure who never quite broke through.

**Nanci Griffith — *Once in a Very Blue Moon* (1985)** — Scaruffi's pick, edged out in round 6. Griffith is the singer-songwriter-folk hybrid whose acoustic work in the mid-80s sits closest to this volume's category. Fantano was more skeptical; the record fell off both lists in final negotiation.

**Lucinda Williams — *Sweet Old World* (1992)** — The bridge record between *Lucinda Williams* and *Car Wheels*. Strong material ("Pineola," "Sweet Old World") but compressed by the more famous albums on either side.

**Iris DeMent — *The Way I Should* (1996)** — DeMent's third record, the one with the explicit political material ("Wasteland of the Free"). Lost the Chapman flip. Stronger lyrically than its production warrants; the Randy Scruggs production is more conventional than the Rooney records.

**Townes Van Zandt — *At My Window* (1987)** — Townes' first studio record after a nine-year gap. Strong material but the production is dated in ways *No Deeper Blue* avoids.

**Eliza Carthy — *Red Rice* (1998)** — The British contemporary folk entry. Scaruffi pushed for its inclusion as a corrective to the otherwise American-dominated list. Fantano was unfamiliar; the record fell off in round 6. Worth noting as the strongest argument that the contemporary folk canon should not be Anglo-American in scope.

**Shawn Colvin — *Steady On* (1989)** — Coffee-house folk done unusually well. Fantano's extrapolated list had it in the top 15; Scaruffi never did. Won the 1991 Grammy for Best Contemporary Folk Album.

**John Gorka — *Land of the Bottom Line* (1990)** — The Fast Folk / Windham Hill axis of late-80s contemporary folk. Fantano's extrapolated list included it; Scaruffi did not engage. Strong songwriting, somewhat dated production.

**Dar Williams — *The Honesty Room* (1993)** — The most "coffee-house" record in the long tail. Fantano's extrapolated list had it; Scaruffi rejected it on category grounds.

**Ani DiFranco — *Dilate* (1996)** — Sits closer to anti-folk / rock-leaning singer-songwriter than to the central folk tradition this volume targets, but has a clear claim to inclusion. Both critics agreed it belonged in adjacent volumes rather than this one.

**Mary Gauthier — *Mercy Now* (2005)** — Late-arriving contemporary folk record that would have been a serious top-15 contender from either critic. Fell out of the negotiation due to its 2005 release date pushing it close to the volume's edge.

**Bonnie "Prince" Billy — *I See a Darkness* (1999)** — Borderline case. Both critics ultimately agreed it sits closer to indie-folk / freak-folk territory than to the central contemporary folk tradition. Belongs in a different volume entry.

**Mount Eerie — *A Crow Looked at Me* (2017)** — Same boundary issue as Lenker, but pushed into different territory by its more avant-garde framing. Would belong in an indie-folk or experimental-folk entry rather than here.

---

### Folk Rock

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

*Cross-listed with Volume 4 (Rock I — Foundations); this is the anchor list.*

#### Debate Summary

Folk-rock is the genre where two traditions collided in the mid-1960s and refused to separate cleanly afterward. The American strain begins with The Byrds plugging Dylan into Rickenbackers, and Dylan himself plugging in at Newport. The British strain takes longer to mature but goes deeper into the source: Fairport Convention realizing that the Child ballads could survive electric drums, Pentangle splitting the difference between folk club and jazz club. The Band, sitting in Woodstock with Dylan's basement tapes in their heads, invent a third path that is neither folk-revival earnestness nor rock-star theater. By 1970 the genre has produced more masterpieces than most rock subgenres ever will.

The Scaruffi-Fantano debate here is sharper than usual because the two critics bring fundamentally different epistemologies. Scaruffi has spent forty years arguing that Dylan is overrated as a *songwriter* in the rock canon while still acknowledging that *Highway 61 Revisited* and *Blonde on Blonde* are unavoidable folk-rock landmarks — the records, not the man, do the work. He reveres the British wing (Fairport, Pentangle, Incredible String Band on the folk side) and treats *Forever Changes* and *Music from Big Pink* as canonical. He is consistently cool on CSN and Simon & Garfunkel as singer-songwriter prestige acts dressed up as a movement.

Fantano, born twenty years into the post-folk-rock era, has not built a deep public canon for this music — most of his folk-rock commentary is retrospective and respectful rather than evaluative. I am EXTRAPOLATING heavily for him here based on his Dylan retrospectives, his stated affection for The Band, and his general posture of deferring to canonical legitimacy when he hasn't done his own combat with the records. His votes lean toward consensus picks; his disagreements with Scaruffi tend to soften the British/avant edge and bring back the populist American picks Scaruffi cuts.

**Scaruffi's Top 15 (working list):**
1. Love — *Forever Changes* (1967)
2. The Band — *Music from Big Pink* (1968)
3. Fairport Convention — *Liege & Lief* (1969)
4. Bob Dylan — *Highway 61 Revisited* (1965)
5. Bob Dylan — *Blonde on Blonde* (1966)
6. The Band — *The Band* (1969)
7. Pentangle — *Basket of Light* (1969)
8. The Byrds — *Sweetheart of the Rodeo* (1968)
9. Fairport Convention — *Unhalfbricking* (1969)
10. The Byrds — *Mr. Tambourine Man* (1965)
11. Tim Buckley — *Goodbye and Hello* (1967)
12. Pentangle — *The Pentangle* (1968)
13. Buffalo Springfield — *Buffalo Springfield Again* (1967)
14. Bob Dylan — *Bringing It All Back Home* (1965)
15. Tim Hardin — *Tim Hardin 2* (1967)

**Fantano's Top 15 (extrapolated, FLAGGED):**
1. Bob Dylan — *Highway 61 Revisited* (1965)
2. The Band — *Music from Big Pink* (1968)
3. Bob Dylan — *Blonde on Blonde* (1966)
4. Love — *Forever Changes* (1967)
5. The Band — *The Band* (1969)
6. Bob Dylan — *Bringing It All Back Home* (1965)
7. Simon & Garfunkel — *Bookends* (1968)
8. The Byrds — *Mr. Tambourine Man* (1965)
9. Crosby, Stills & Nash — *Crosby, Stills & Nash* (1969)
10. Fairport Convention — *Liege & Lief* (1969)
11. Buffalo Springfield — *Buffalo Springfield Again* (1967)
12. The Byrds — *Sweetheart of the Rodeo* (1968)
13. Simon & Garfunkel — *Bridge Over Troubled Water* (1970)
14. Pentangle — *Basket of Light* (1969)
15. Neil Young — *After the Gold Rush* (1970)

**Round 1 — Dylan placement.** Predictable opening fight. Fantano puts *Highway 61* at #1 on legitimacy grounds; Scaruffi accepts it must be in the top tier but refuses to lead with it, putting *Forever Changes* first because Arthur Lee did something nobody else did and Dylan was doing what The Byrds had already shown could be done. Compromise: *Highway 61* lands top-three but not top.

**Round 2 — the British wing.** Scaruffi insists on Fairport's *Liege & Lief* and at least one Pentangle. Fantano accepts *Liege & Lief* without much fight — it's the consensus British folk-rock LP — but is lukewarm on Pentangle, having less exposure to the Renbourn/Jansch/Thompson axis. Scaruffi gets *Liege & Lief* in the top five and one Pentangle entry.

**Round 3 — CSN/CSNY problem.** This is the real fight. Fantano wants the self-titled CSN debut in. Scaruffi calls it "boutique harmony rock dressed up as folk-rock" and refuses. They compromise by leaving CSN out of the ten and acknowledging *Déjà Vu* as a coin-flip case that lost. Scaruffi reluctantly accepts a Neil Young record because Young is the genre's most durable solo voice.

**Round 4 — Simon & Garfunkel.** Fantano pushes *Bookends* and/or *Bridge Over Troubled Water*; Scaruffi treats S&G as adult contemporary in folk-rock clothing. Compromise: one S&G record, *Bookends*, makes the ten on Fantano's vote, against Scaruffi's protest.

**Round 5 — The Band weighting.** Both critics have *Music from Big Pink* and *The Band* in the top six. They agree both belong in the ten. Done.

**Round 6 — Buffalo Springfield, Byrds, Buckley, Hardin.** Scaruffi wants *Buffalo Springfield Again* and Tim Buckley's *Goodbye and Hello*; Fantano lower on both. They agree on one Byrds record (the question is *which*) and end up keeping *Sweetheart of the Rodeo* over *Mr. Tambourine Man* on Scaruffi's argument that *Sweetheart* is more genre-defining (the pivot to country-rock that closes the folk-rock decade) — coin-flip resolved in Scaruffi's favor for the ten, with *Mr. Tambourine Man* in honorable mentions.

The final ten reflects two genuine compromises: Fantano gets his one Simon & Garfunkel and his Neil Young; Scaruffi gets his Pentangle, his second Fairport, and his preferred Byrds record. Dylan settles at three records (both critics agreed early that excluding any of the electric trilogy was indefensible).

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Bob Dylan — *Highway 61 Revisited* (1965)

The record where folk-rock stops being a Byrds-shaped novelty and becomes a literary form. Dylan had already gone half-electric on *Bringing It All Back Home* earlier the same year, but *Highway 61* is where he commits — Mike Bloomfield's blues guitar, Al Kooper's accidental organ, Bobby Gregg's drums, and a frontman who is no longer trying to be Woody Guthrie. The opening of "Like a Rolling Stone" — that snare crack, that organ swell — is one of the few moments in 20th-century music that genuinely sounds like a door being kicked open. The album behind it is unrelenting: "Tombstone Blues" is a hallucinated history lecture, "Ballad of a Thin Man" a sneering put-down of square middle-American journalism, "Desolation Row" an eleven-minute acoustic coda that proves the electric move was a choice, not an abandonment.

What makes this folk-rock rather than just rock is the song architecture. Every track here is built on folk-form principles — long verses, repeated refrains, narrative voice — that Dylan refused to abandon when he plugged in. The Animals had already shown rock could absorb folk arrangements ("House of the Rising Sun"); The Byrds had shown folk could absorb rock production. Dylan does the harder thing: he writes folk songs and lets a rock band play them.

Scaruffi's standard line is that Dylan-the-songwriter is overpraised relative to Dylan-the-cultural-event, and *Highway 61* is one of the records that survives that critique because the band is so good and the production is so brutal. Fantano treats it as foundational without much qualification — it's the record everyone agrees on even when they disagree about everything else. It loses the #1 slot here only because Arthur Lee did something more singular two years later, and because the genre's center of gravity moved to records that synthesized Dylan rather than records that were Dylan.

##### 2. Love — *Forever Changes* (1967)

Arthur Lee's masterpiece is folk-rock if you understand folk-rock as the genre where folk songs survive electric arrangement. *Forever Changes* drops the electric arrangement almost entirely — it is mostly acoustic guitars, mariachi horns, classical strings, and Lee's haunted, singsong tenor — and yet it is unmistakably a rock record, written by a man steeped in the Byrds and the West Coast scene rather than the folk revival. The combination is unprecedented. "Alone Again Or" opens with a flamenco figure that any folk guitarist could play, but the song flowers into orchestral pop with David Angel's horn arrangement; "Andmoreagain" is a chamber ballad; "The Daily Planet" is folk-form storytelling about Los Angeles ennui; "You Set the Scene" is a ten-minute emotional unloading that ends the album on an apocalyptic note.

The lyrics are paranoid, beautiful, and almost impossible to paraphrase — Lee writing in 1967 about Vietnam, about race, about his own mortality, with the kind of sidelong indirection that makes the record feel timeless rather than period. The fact that Lee was a Black bandleader fronting a multiracial group on the Sunset Strip in 1967 — and producing the most emotionally elaborate album of that scene — is one of the underread facts of the era.

Scaruffi has placed *Forever Changes* near the top of his all-time rock list for decades; he treats it as the album that closes the door on the Sgt. Pepper era and opens a more interior path. Fantano respects it; whether he'd have ranked it second in this genre without prompting is a guess, but it's a defensible top-five pick from any angle. It sits at #2 here on the strength of its formal singularity — no one has ever made another *Forever Changes*, and that is the strongest claim a record can have.

##### 3. The Band — *Music from Big Pink* (1968)

The record that ended the psychedelic era by ignoring it. While the rest of rock was getting more elaborate, more orchestral, more drugged, The Band — five working musicians who had spent years backing Ronnie Hawkins and then Dylan — went into a pink house in West Saugerties and made a record that sounded a hundred years old.
