Garth Hudson's organ, Levon Helm's drums and his Arkansas voice, Robbie Robertson's economical guitar, Richard Manuel's piano and his bruised tenor, Rick Danko's bass and his quavering harmonies — the texture is unmistakable from the first bar of "Tears of Rage."

The Dylan connection runs through the record (he co-wrote three songs and painted the cover), but *Big Pink* is not a Dylan album. The Band's achievement is to have absorbed the folk-rock moment — the basement tapes were the lab — and built something more durable from it: a music that sounds like the American 19th century played by men who had also heard Stax. "The Weight" is the obvious anchor, a song everyone knows and few can describe formally — it's a folk-rock parable in stanza form with five different lead vocals over the course of the song. "Chest Fever" is gothic organ-led psychedelic gospel. "I Shall Be Released," written by Dylan and sung by Manuel in a falsetto that sounds wounded, is the emotional center.

Both critics put this in their top three. The disagreement is only on whether *Big Pink* or *The Band* is the greater record (see below). Scaruffi tends to lead with *Big Pink* on the grounds that it was the first move and its rusticism was unprecedented; Fantano, given a choice, often defaults to *The Band* as the more polished statement. Both are right. *Big Pink* lands slightly ahead here on the priority claim.

##### 4. Bob Dylan — *Blonde on Blonde* (1966)

The double album as folk-rock summa. Recorded in Nashville with session players who didn't know who Dylan was and didn't care, *Blonde on Blonde* is looser, longer, and stranger than *Highway 61*. The "thin wild mercury sound" Dylan later named is real on this record — a treble-heavy mix where harmonica, organ, and Dylan's increasingly unhinged vocal phrasing all sit on top of a band that is half country, half rock, and entirely in service to the song. "Visions of Johanna" is one of the finest song-as-poem performances in rock; "Stuck Inside of Mobile with the Memphis Blues Again" is a nine-verse procession of surreal Americana; "Sad-Eyed Lady of the Lowlands" closes the record with eleven minutes of devotional ballad that should not work and entirely works.

The folk-rock case for *Blonde on Blonde* is that it's where the genre absorbs country music without becoming country-rock — that move comes two years later with *Sweetheart of the Rodeo*. Here the country is just present, the way blues is present on *Highway 61*, as one substrate among several.

Scaruffi gives *Blonde on Blonde* the standard nod — better played than *Highway 61*, less violent, more diffuse — and tends to rank it slightly below. Fantano treats it as the most sustainable Dylan listen of the trilogy: longer, more textured, the one you can put on without bracing. Both place it firmly in the ten. The fight was only over whether to include three Dylan records in a top ten of this genre, and the answer was yes.

##### 5. Fairport Convention — *Liege & Lief* (1969)

The British folk-rock canon starts here. Fairport had already made *Unhalfbricking* earlier in 1969 — half-translated Dylan, originals, traditional material — but *Liege & Lief*, recorded after a van crash that killed drummer Martin Lamble and Richard Thompson's girlfriend Jeannie Franklyn, is where the band stops borrowing Americans and commits to the Child Ballads. The lineup — Sandy Denny on lead vocals, Thompson on lead guitar, Ashley Hutchings on bass, Dave Mattacks on drums, Dave Swarbrick on fiddle, Simon Nicol on rhythm guitar — is the canonical electric-folk lineup, and the record is the canonical electric-folk statement.

"Matty Groves" turns a 17th-century murder ballad into an eight-minute electric jam where Thompson's guitar and Swarbrick's fiddle trade leads over a rhythm section that sounds genuinely menacing; "Tam Lin" sets a supernatural Border ballad to a galloping electric arrangement; "Crazy Man Michael" is a Thompson-Swarbrick original that sounds older than the traditional songs around it. Sandy Denny's voice — clear, unornamented, slightly cool — is the perfect instrument for this material; she does not melodramatize and does not condescend to the songs.

Scaruffi has championed *Liege & Lief* for decades as one of the very few records that genuinely solved the problem of marrying traditional folk to rock instrumentation without flattening either. Fantano accepts the record's canonical status without significant pushback; the album is one of the few British folk-rock LPs with broad name recognition in American rock writing. It lands at #5 here as the bridge between the American Dylan-Byrds wing and the deeper traditional material the British scene was about to mine throughout the seventies.

##### 6. The Band — *The Band* (1969)

The follow-up to *Big Pink* is a tighter, warmer, more song-centric record. Robertson takes over more of the writing, the rusticism is more deliberately staged ("The Night They Drove Old Dixie Down" is a Confederate elegy written by a Canadian for an Arkansan to sing), and the production is more controlled — they recorded in a converted pool house in Hollywood, not a basement in Woodstock. The result is the most novelistic folk-rock record of the era. "Up on Cripple Creek," "Rag Mama Rag," "King Harvest (Has Surely Come)," "Across the Great Divide" — every track is a character sketch, a piece of imagined Americana, played by men who could actually play this music rather than gesture at it.

The folk-rock argument for *The Band* over *Big Pink* is that *Big Pink* still has the Dylan ghost on it (and the Manuel-Dylan basement songs) while *The Band* is a fully standalone artistic statement. The argument the other way is that *Big Pink*'s rougher edges are more genuinely folk-rock and *The Band* is closer to a polished Americana record. Both are defensible. Both critics here include both records, and neither argued for excluding either.

Scaruffi typically rates them about even with a slight nod to *Big Pink* on priority grounds. Fantano leans toward *The Band* on craft. The compromise here ranks them adjacent at #3 and #6, with three other top-tier records between them. It's one of the rare cases where two albums by the same band genuinely belong in the same top ten of a genre — the equivalent of having two Beatles records in a sixties pop-rock list.

##### 7. The Byrds — *Sweetheart of the Rodeo* (1968)

The record where folk-rock pivots into country-rock and effectively closes the genre's first chapter. Gram Parsons had joined The Byrds for a few months and convinced Roger McGuinn to make a country album — pedal steel, Nashville session players, songs by Merle Haggard and the Louvin Brothers alongside Dylan's "You Ain't Goin' Nowhere" and Parsons originals. The Columbia executives were horrified; the audience didn't quite know what to do with it; Parsons left the band before the year was out. Fifty years later it's one of the most influential American records ever made, the seed for the entire alt-country tradition.

The folk-rock case for including *Sweetheart* in this list rather than the country-rock list (which it also anchors) is that The Byrds' DNA is folk-rock — the Rickenbacker, the harmonies, the Dylan covers. *Sweetheart* is the band's last fully coherent statement before the country-rock split, and it's a folk-rock record about country music as much as it's a country record. McGuinn singing Dylan over pedal steel is the same act of translation as McGuinn singing Dylan over a 12-string electric three years earlier; the substrate has changed, the method hasn't.

Scaruffi rates *Sweetheart* as the most genre-defining Byrds album — the one that did something other than refine the formula they invented on *Mr. Tambourine Man*. Fantano accepts it. The choice between *Sweetheart* and *Mr. Tambourine Man* for the genre's flagship Byrds record is a coin flip; here Scaruffi's argument carried the day, and *Mr. Tambourine Man* drops to honorable mentions.

##### 8. Pentangle — *Basket of Light* (1969)

Pentangle were the British folk scene's most musically literate band — Bert Jansch and John Renbourn, two of the great fingerstyle guitarists of the era, plus Jacqui McShee's clean unornamented vocal, Danny Thompson on upright bass, and Terry Cox on drums. The lineup is half folk club, half jazz club, and the music is genuinely both. *Basket of Light* is their most accessible record — "Light Flight" was a UK hit on the back of a TV show theme — and the one where the folk material, the jazz arrangements, and the rock rhythm section cohere most cleanly.

The opening "Light Flight" is in 7/8 and 5/8 with McShee's vocal floating above it; "Once I Had a Sweetheart" is a traditional ballad over a hypnotic Thompson bass figure; "House Carpenter" is a Child Ballad given a propulsive arrangement that justifies the rock-leaning categorization Pentangle sometimes gets and sometimes doesn't. They are not, strictly, a rock band — there is no electric guitar in the Fairport sense — but they are also not a folk band in any pre-1965 sense. They are a fifth thing, and the fifth thing is part of what folk-rock turned out to be.

Scaruffi treats Pentangle as essential to any honest folk-rock canon and pushed for at least one entry. Fantano has less to say about Pentangle publicly and accepted *Basket of Light* on Scaruffi's argument rather than his own conviction; this is the entry where the imbalance in the critics' depth on British folk shows most clearly. It belongs in the ten regardless of who's louder about it.

##### 9. Fairport Convention — *Unhalfbricking* (1969)

The other Fairport record from 1969 — released in July, four months before *Liege & Lief* — and the bridge between their early Dylan-leaning period and the traditional turn. Sandy Denny's first full album with the band, and the one where she contributes "Who Knows Where the Time Goes," one of the most beautiful songs in the English language. Three Dylan covers (including the Cajun-inflected "Si Tu Dois Partir," sung in French), originals by Denny and Thompson, and the eleven-minute traditional "A Sailor's Life" that effectively invented British electric folk in real time — the band working out, on tape, how to play "A Sailor's Life" with a rock rhythm section and electric guitar without losing the song.

The argument for including *Unhalfbricking* alongside *Liege & Lief* is that the two records are a single statement made in two installments — the question and the answer. *Unhalfbricking* is the question (can we play this material at all?); *Liege & Lief* is the answer (yes, and here is what it sounds like when we commit). Including only one would be like including only *Big Pink* or only *The Band*: defensible, but missing the arc.

Scaruffi pushed for both Fairport records; Fantano accepted on the strength of "Who Knows Where the Time Goes" and "A Sailor's Life." It sits at #9 because *Liege & Lief* is the more focused statement, but the two records belong together and the genre is poorer if you have to choose.

##### 10. Simon & Garfunkel — *Bookends* (1968)

The Fantano concession. Scaruffi treats Simon & Garfunkel as adult contemporary dressed up as folk-rock — too clean, too literary, too keen to please. Fantano sees them as canonical Americans who made at least one record that earns its reputation. *Bookends* is the side that wins the argument: the suite on Side A (the title track, "Save the Life of My Child," "America," "Overs," "Voices of Old People," "Old Friends," "Bookends Theme") is a genuinely conceptual folk-rock statement about aging in America, and "America" is one of the great folk-rock songs of the decade — the bus ride, the cigarettes, the pie, the line about counting cars on the New Jersey turnpike.

Side B is a more conventional collection — "Mrs. Robinson," "A Hazy Shade of Winter," "At the Zoo" — and is the side that confirms Scaruffi's complaint: it is well-made commercial pop-folk that does not push the genre forward. But Side A is enough to carry the record into the ten on Fantano's vote, against Scaruffi's protest. The compromise is explicit: Fantano gets *Bookends*, Scaruffi gets two Fairport records and a Pentangle, and CSN drops out entirely. Neil Young's *After the Gold Rush* (Fantano's other lobbying point) loses the coin flip described below.

The case for *Bookends* over *Bridge Over Troubled Water* (Fantano's second-choice S&G record) is that *Bookends* is a folk-rock record and *Bridge* is a pop record with folk-rock songs on it. The ten ends here, with one foot in each critic's preferred canon.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Three irreconcilable disagreements were resolved by coin flip and are noted explicitly:

**1. *Sweetheart of the Rodeo* vs. *Mr. Tambourine Man* for the Byrds slot.** Scaruffi argued *Sweetheart* is more genre-defining (the pivot that closed the era); Fantano argued *Mr. Tambourine Man* is more foundational (the record that started the genre). Coin flip went to Scaruffi. *Mr. Tambourine Man* drops to honorable mentions.

**2. Neil Young — *After the Gold Rush* vs. CSNY — *Déjà Vu* for the singer-songwriter slot.** Fantano wanted one or the other. Scaruffi resisted both, treating them as the boutique end of the genre. Coin flip resolved by leaving both out — *Bookends* took the slot Fantano wanted for a singer-songwriter-coded record, and Young goes to honorable mentions. Noted: this is the one place Scaruffi clearly won the negotiation against Fantano's preferences.

**3. Pentangle — *Basket of Light* vs. *The Pentangle* (debut, 1968).** Scaruffi's preference here drifts toward the debut on innovation grounds; the public-facing recommendation is *Basket of Light* on accessibility grounds. Coin flip went to *Basket of Light* as the more representative single statement. The debut goes to honorable mentions.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **The Byrds — *Mr. Tambourine Man* (1965).** The record that started American folk-rock. Lost the Byrds slot on coin flip; would be in any top fifteen.
- **Bob Dylan — *Bringing It All Back Home* (1965).** The half-electric record that preceded *Highway 61*. Three Dylan records made the ten; the fourth lands here.
- **Buffalo Springfield — *Buffalo Springfield Again* (1967).** Stills, Young, and Furay each writing in distinct modes; the most fertile of the three Buffalo Springfield records.
- **Neil Young — *After the Gold Rush* (1970).** Solo Young's most folk-rock-coded record; lost on the singer-songwriter coin flip.
- **Crosby, Stills, Nash & Young — *Déjà Vu* (1970).** The supergroup's commercial peak; Scaruffi's veto held.
- **Tim Buckley — *Goodbye and Hello* (1967).** Scaruffi's favorite Buckley; lost the avant-folk slot to Pentangle.
- **Tim Hardin — *Tim Hardin 2* (1967).** "If I Were a Carpenter," "Black Sheep Boy" — the songwriter Dylan respected.
- **Pentangle — *The Pentangle* (1968).** The debut; the more innovative record but less representative single statement.
- **The Incredible String Band — *The Hangman's Beautiful Daughter* (1968).** Folk-side rather than rock-side; would anchor a British psych-folk list.
- **Simon & Garfunkel — *Bridge Over Troubled Water* (1970).** The commercial peak; loses to *Bookends* on genre purity.
- **Donovan — *Sunshine Superman* (1966).** The British pop-folk-rock entry; both critics leaned cool.
- **Jefferson Airplane — *Surrealistic Pillow* (1967).** Cross-listed with psychedelic rock; the acoustic side is the folk-rock part.
- **The Band — *Stage Fright* (1970).** The third Band record; would be in some critics' tens.
- **Fairport Convention — *Full House* (1970).** Post-Sandy Denny Fairport with Thompson still in the band; the record where the British folk-rock model is fully consolidated before fragmenting.

---

### Anti-Folk

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Anti-folk presents a peculiar challenge for a critical canon. The genre is defined less by sonic coherence than by attitude — a deliberate rejection of folk-revival earnestness, polish, and craft-as-virtue. Born in the mid-1980s on the Lower East Side around Lach's Fort and the Sidewalk Cafe Antifolk Festival, it absorbed punk's amateurism, the Velvet Underground's deadpan, and the confessional tradition's first-person honesty, then sanded off the dignity. The result is a genre whose canonical works sometimes sound like accidents — and that's the point.

Going in, I expected Scaruffi and Fantano to fight along predictable lines: Scaruffi favoring the outsider/visionary argument (Daniel Johnston, the Mountain Goats' boombox era), Fantano gravitating to the millennial-friendly mid-2000s (Moldy Peaches, early Regina Spektor, Jeffrey Lewis). The reality was messier. Scaruffi's 1990s lo-fi affection put him surprisingly close to Fantano on the Mountain Goats and Sebadoh records. Fantano, meanwhile, pushed harder than expected for Diane Cluck and Toby Goodshank — the deeper-cut Sidewalk Cafe figures — because his criticism has always rewarded craft-within-amateurism, which is precisely what Cluck and Goodshank do.

The real fights:
- **Daniel Johnston eligibility.** Scaruffi insists *Hi, How Are You* is foundational anti-folk; Fantano wants to honor him but argues he's outsider music with anti-folk *adjacency*, not core canon. Compromise: include one Johnston, the most anti-folk-coded one.
- **Mountain Goats placement.** Both critics want a boombox-era Mountain Goats record; they disagree on which (*All Hail West Texas* vs *Zopilote Machine* vs *Sweden*). This consumed two full rounds.
- **Beck's *One Foot in the Grave*.** Both love it; the question is whether Beck counts as anti-folk *proper* or as a tourist. Scaruffi argues yes (the K Records context is impeccable). Fantano agrees but wants it lower-ranked.
- **Regina Spektor.** Fantano argues *Soviet Kitsch* is the genre's most successful crossover artifact and earns its place. Scaruffi finds her too composed, too schooled, ultimately a chamber-pop artist who happened to play Sidewalk Cafe.
- **The Moldy Peaches self-titled.** Universally agreed as essential. Disagreement is only on placement.

After four rounds of negotiation and two coin flips (noted below), we converged on a list that honors both the foundational Sidewalk Cafe scene and its expanded geography. Three records resisted resolution and went to coin flip: the Daniel Johnston inclusion (*Hi, How Are You* won over *Yip/Jump Music*), the Mountain Goats slot (*All Hail West Texas* won over *Zopilote Machine*), and the #10 spot (Herman Düne's *Not On Top* won over Hamell on Trial's *Choochtown*).

A note on what we excluded: Frank Black's solo work, while loved by both critics, is power-pop; Defiance, Ohio is folk-punk proper, a sibling genre with its own canon; Lou Barlow's *Sentridoh* tapes are too unfinished to anthologize fairly; and we honored the rule that a critic's personal favorite isn't automatically canon (Scaruffi let go of Lach's *Blang!*; Fantano let go of Schwervon!).

#### The Top 10

##### 1. The Moldy Peaches — *The Moldy Peaches* (2001)

The argument that this record is the most important anti-folk album ever made is also, paradoxically, the argument against the genre's seriousness — which is itself extremely anti-folk. Adam Green and Kimya Dawson recorded most of it in bedrooms over years, refused to sing in tune more than half the time, wrote songs about anatomy and Steak for Chicken and Nazi Dad, and somehow produced a body of work that is both deeply funny and unbearably tender. "Anyone Else But You" became, via *Juno*, a generational love-song shorthand; "Jorge Regula" is a perfect gentle ballad; "These Burgers" is two minutes of children's-television absurdity that somehow lands emotionally.

The record's structural genius is its refusal to choose between gag and feeling. The dirty jokes ("Who's Got the Crack") sit beside the disarmingly sincere ("On Top") with no tonal hierarchy. Green's adolescent croon and Dawson's deadpan harmony create a vocal blend that doesn't exist elsewhere — two voices unable to disguise themselves as anything but themselves. The instrumentation is mostly acoustic guitar, occasional drum machine, ambient room noise; the production is intentionally amateurish in the K Records / Beat Happening lineage, but with a jokier, more East-Coast sensibility than that Olympia tradition.

What makes it canonical rather than merely cute: the songs hold up. Strip away the Halloween costumes and the bunny-suit aesthetics, and "Lazy Confessions," "Lucky Number Nine," and "NYC's Like a Graveyard" are first-rate songwriting in the Jonathan Richman / Beat Happening lineage. Both critics agreed within thirty seconds that this is the album. The disagreement was only over how high — Fantano initially had it at #2, but Scaruffi correctly noted that no anti-folk record has had more downstream cultural impact, none has done more to define the genre's tonal range, and none was more central to the actual Sidewalk Cafe scene the genre was named for. Number one without further negotiation.

##### 2. Daniel Johnston — *Hi, How Are You* (1983)

Recorded on a $59 Sanyo cassette boombox in his brother's garage in West Virginia, *Hi, How Are You: The Unfinished Album* is the spiritual North Star of anti-folk even if Johnston himself was barely aware of the New York scene that would later claim him. The chord organ wheezes, the vocal multitracks bleed, the songs end abruptly because the tape ran out. "Walking the Cow" — the album's most famous song, immortalized on Kurt Cobain's T-shirt — is built on a single repeating organ riff and a melody so plaintive it seems to predate composition, as if Johnston had just discovered that humans could sing.

Scaruffi's case for inclusion was unconditional: Johnston is the patron saint of every anti-folk practitioner who ever decided that emotional clarity outranks technical clarity. "Big Business Monkey" and "Despair Came Knocking" lay out the genre's emotional palette — paranoia, religious yearning, romantic obsession, slapstick — fifteen years before the genre had a name. The argument against was Fantano's: Johnston is properly outsider music, with mental-illness context that complicates the "anti-establishment posture" that defines anti-folk as a *choice*. Johnston wasn't choosing to sound this way.

The compromise the critics reached: Johnston's inclusion is essential because anti-folk's aesthetic vocabulary is unintelligible without him. The bedroom recording, the unselfconscious vocal, the songs-as-diary-entries, the religious symbolism alongside cartoon imagery, the comedy adjacent to despair — every Sidewalk Cafe songwriter from Lach forward worked in dialogue with this record, even when they hadn't heard it directly. Cobain's endorsement carried *Hi, How Are You* into the alt-rock canon, but its true home is here, the foundational document of the bedroom-folk lineage. Resolved by coin flip over *Yip/Jump Music* (also 1983, also boombox, more cohesive but less historically specific to this canon). The flip went to *Hi, How Are You*; both critics agreed the result was correct.

##### 3. The Mountain Goats — *All Hail West Texas* (2002)

John Darnielle recorded this album, alone, into a Panasonic RX-FT500 boombox, in a dying friendship's last weeks, and the boombox itself became a third character — its tape-mechanism whirr audible on every track, its compressed mono presence as integral to the record as Darnielle's voice. *All Hail West Texas* is the apotheosis of the Mountain Goats' boombox era, the last record before Darnielle moved to a studio for *Tallahassee*, and arguably the moment when his songwriting fully matured: "The Best Ever Death Metal Band Out of Denton" is a perfect short story in song, "Color in Your Cheeks" is an immigrant-welcome hymn, "Jenny" is a motorcycle-romance vignette that compresses an entire novel into three minutes.

The argument for *All Hail West Texas* over the earlier Shrimper-era cassettes (*Zopilote Machine*, *Sweden*, *Nothing for Juice*) is that the songwriting is finally fully formed — Darnielle has stopped being clever about Latin and Greek references and started writing characters who breathe. The argument against, which Fantano pressed for two rounds, is that the earlier records are *more* anti-folk because they're more abrasive, more lo-fi, more committed to the boombox-as-ideology bit. *Zopilote Machine* (1994) is genuinely difficult; *All Hail West Texas* is gorgeous despite the format.

Scaruffi's counter, which carried the day: the boombox isn't a posture on this record, it's a constraint that produces the songs. "The Mess Inside" is unimaginable on better equipment. "Distant Stations" needs the tape hiss. The lo-fi is not an aesthetic decoration but a structural necessity — a definition of anti-folk that distinguishes it from indie folk's later studio-fi sentimentality. The Mountain Goats released approximately fourteen boombox albums; this is the one. Coin-flipped past *Zopilote Machine* (see Coin-Flip Resolutions); both critics declared the result fair.

##### 4. Beck — *One Foot in the Grave* (1994)

Recorded in Olympia with Calvin Johnson producing, *One Foot in the Grave* is the great forgotten Beck record — released six months after *Mellow Gold* but conceived in an entirely different universe, the K Records universe of Beat Happening and Some Velvet Sidewalk and the open-mic anti-folk circuit Beck had just left behind. The album is mostly Beck and an acoustic guitar, occasionally a banjo, occasionally Calvin's basso doom-voice singing harmony, with songs that move between traditional folk arrangement ("He's a Mighty Good Leader," a Skip James cover) and Sidewalk Cafe absurdism ("Whiskeyclone, Hotel City 1997," "Cyanide Breath Mint").

Beck had played the Sidewalk Cafe scene in the late 1980s before *Loser* made him famous; this record is his return to that world, made deliberately as an antidote to the major-label experimentation of *Mellow Gold*. The songwriting is as good as anything in his catalog: "Asshole" is a folk-pop ballad that became a Tom Petty/Mark Eitzel touchstone; "Sleep" is a campfire lullaby with a sinister undertow; "Painted Eyelids" is unbearably pretty.

Both critics had this in their top 5. The question was whether Beck *counts* — whether a Geffen-signed multimillionaire making an anti-folk record between hip-hop pastiches is doing the genre a favor or appropriating it. Scaruffi's resolution: the K Records context is unimpeachable, the recording conditions are anti-folk-orthodox, and Beck's commitment to the form is real even if temporary. The album doesn't condescend to its sources — it inhabits them. Fantano agreed but wanted it ranked lower than the genre's lifers (Moldy Peaches, Mountain Goats); Scaruffi argued that the record is so good and so historically important to the genre's mainstream visibility that it earns top-five placement on quality alone. Scaruffi won this round.

##### 5. Jeffrey Lewis — *It's the Ones Who've Cracked That the Light Shines Through* (2003)

If *The Moldy Peaches* is anti-folk's most beloved album, *It's the Ones Who've Cracked* is its most literary. Jeffrey Lewis is a Lower East Side cartoonist as much as a songwriter — his comic books and his albums share an aesthetic of dense, hand-drawn, footnote-laden first-person narrative — and this album is the one where his songwriting and his cartoon sensibility fuse most completely. "The Chelsea Hotel Oral Sex Song" is exactly what its title promises and somehow also a meditation on celebrity and mortality; "Don't Let the Record Label Take You Out to Lunch" is Sidewalk Cafe career advice in three minutes; "The East River" is a love song that walks the entire perimeter of Manhattan.

Lewis's voice is conversational, unstrained, slightly nasal; his guitar playing is competent rather than virtuosic; his lyrical density is the point. He fits more words into a song than almost anyone outside of John Darnielle, and his diction is closer to spoken-word than to traditional songcraft. This was the album where critics stopped calling him "promising" and started taking him seriously as a major figure.

Fantano, who has reviewed every Jeffrey Lewis record on theneedledrop, advocated harder for this one than any other album in the debate. Scaruffi was less enthusiastic — he tends to prefer his lo-fi outsiders more abrasive, less talky — but conceded that no other anti-folk songwriter combines literary ambition with the Sidewalk Cafe scene's amateurist ethos this successfully. The deal: top five, not top three. Both critics signed off without further argument. The album also marks the moment anti-folk decisively crossed into indie-rock criticism's good graces — *Pitchfork* and *Stylus* both reviewed it positively, which the scene treated with appropriate ambivalence.

##### 6. Adam Green — *Friends of Mine* (2003)

After the Moldy Peaches dissolved, Adam Green made *Friends of Mine*, the genre's most unlikely orchestral pop record — a baritone-crooner album with strings and brass and a 1960s lounge sensibility, wrapping Green's filthy, surrealist, deeply earnest songwriting in arrangements that sound like a Bacharach session. "Jessica" is an extraordinarily catchy single about Jessica Simpson; "Bluebirds" is a children's-show pastiche that becomes genuinely moving; "Friends of Mine" is the centerpiece, a five-minute lounge ballad that sums up the whole anti-folk emotional vocabulary in one song.

The argument for *Friends of Mine* as canonical: it proves anti-folk can scale. The Moldy Peaches were unscalable — their charm was inseparable from their amateurism. Green showed that an anti-folk songwriter can move into a real studio, hire real string players, write within real pop-song conventions, and not lose the essential Sidewalk Cafe quality of voice. The lyrics are still surreal and dirty and adolescent and tender; only the production has matured. This is what anti-folk sounds like when it gets a record label.

Scaruffi's reservation: it's borderline whether *Friends of Mine* is anti-folk anymore or whether it's just orchestral pop made by a former anti-folk artist. The acoustic-guitar bedroom sound is gone; the drum machines and tape hiss are gone. Fantano's counter: Green's *songwriting* is unchanged, and anti-folk is fundamentally a songwriting tradition, not a production aesthetic. The decision to enclose anti-folk songs in lounge arrangements is itself an anti-folk gesture — it refuses the genre's pieties about authenticity and lo-fi-as-virtue. Scaruffi accepted this argument. Top six.

##### 7. Kimya Dawson — *Remember That I Love You* (2006)

Kimya Dawson's solo work after the Moldy Peaches went somewhere Adam Green's couldn't — deeper into the children's-music register, into a register of unmediated emotional address that most adult songwriters consider career suicide. *Remember That I Love You* is her best record: gentler than *I'm Sorry That Sometimes I'm Mean*, more focused than *Hidden Vagenda*, structured around songs about pregnancy, friendship, mental illness, and being kind. "My Rollercoaster" is the single (it's the song from *Juno*'s soundtrack that isn't "Anyone Else But You"); "Loose Lips" is a four-and-a-half-minute political-personal manifesto with dozens of ideas crammed into one continuous lyric.

The album's risk is that its sincerity becomes saccharine — Dawson is writing for a listener who may be a child, may be a depressed friend, may be herself. The reason it works: she's actually brave about the dark material. "Tire Swing" is about a long-term friendship surviving everything. "Loose Lips" includes a passage about wanting to die that doesn't flinch into reassurance. "12/26" is about the day after the 2004 tsunami. The children's-show voice is a delivery mechanism for adult content, which is the same trick Adam Green pulls in the opposite direction (lounge crooner delivers adolescent surrealism).

Both critics agreed that Dawson belongs on the list; the question was whether *Remember That I Love You* is the record or whether it's *I'm Sorry That Sometimes I'm Mean* (2002), her solo debut. Scaruffi preferred *I'm Sorry* for being rawer; Fantano preferred *Remember That I Love You* for being more developed and for containing "Loose Lips," which he considers her single best song. Fantano's view prevailed. Scaruffi conceded with the observation that *Remember That I Love You* is the record where Dawson stops being half of the Moldy Peaches and becomes a fully-formed solo artist.

##### 8. Diane Cluck — *Macy's Day Bird* (2002)

Diane Cluck is the Sidewalk Cafe scene's most undervalued songwriter outside of insider circles, and *Macy's Day Bird* — recorded with Greg Weeks producing, mostly voice and acoustic guitar with occasional accordion and cello — is the album that most fully captures her unusual voice. Cluck sings in a register and with a vibrato that recalls medieval modal singing as much as American folk; her melodies move in scalar patterns that feel composed rather than strummed; her lyrics are imagistic and inward in a way that has more in common with Vashti Bunyan or Linda Perhacs than with the gag-folk tradition of Adam Green.

Why this is anti-folk and not just folk: Cluck came up at the Sidewalk Cafe alongside Lewis and Dawson; her recording aesthetic is rigorously lo-fi (room-recorded, unprocessed); her songwriting refuses the verse-chorus-verse structures and Tin Pan Alley conventions that mainstream folk has accommodated. She's anti-folk in the foundational sense — anti the establishment idea of what folk should sound like — even as her actual sound is gentler than most of the canon.

Fantano championed this inclusion; Scaruffi was unfamiliar with the record going in but, after listening, agreed it belonged in the top ten. The standout tracks — "Heat From Every Corner," "Easy to Be Around," "Sara" — show a songwriter operating on a different aesthetic axis than the rest of the scene. *Macy's Day Bird* is the album for listeners who think anti-folk is mostly jokes and want proof otherwise. It's also the record that most challenges the genre's self-image: Cluck is unsmiling, undidactic, uninterested in the meta-joke about sincerity that animates the Moldy Peaches and early Adam Green. She's just sincere. Top eight.

##### 9. Regina Spektor — *Soviet Kitsch* (2004)

The most contested major-label-eligible inclusion. Spektor came up at the Sidewalk Cafe in the early 2000s, sharing bills with Jeffrey Lewis and the Moldy Peaches; her early albums (*11:11*, *Songs*) were self-released; *Soviet Kitsch*, her third, was the breakout that brought her to Sire and to mainstream visibility. The album includes "Us," "Your Honor," "Ode to Divorce," and "Carbon Monoxide" — songs whose piano-driven arrangements and theatrical vocal style are not what most listeners associate with anti-folk's acoustic-guitar amateurism.

Fantano's argument for inclusion: Spektor is the genre's most successful ambassador to the mainstream, and *Soviet Kitsch* is genuinely an anti-folk record by community and intent if not by sonic surface. The Sidewalk Cafe lineage is real; the songwriting eccentricities (yelps, mid-song character voices, abrupt structural shifts, Russian-language interludes) are anti-folk-orthodox even if delivered on grand piano. Excluding her would be revisionism — she was *of* the scene.

Scaruffi's argument against: Spektor is fundamentally a chamber-pop / cabaret artist in the Tori Amos / Kate Bush lineage, conservatory-trained, polished, and her presence in the anti-folk scene was incidental to her artistic identity. Including her stretches the genre's definition past usefulness.

The compromise: include *Soviet Kitsch* but lower than the canonical core, with the explicit acknowledgment that her inclusion is contested. Scaruffi's reluctance is registered. The case for inclusion ultimately rests on community membership and on the album's actual songs, which — "Us," especially, but also "Ghost of Corporate Future" — interface with anti-folk's tonal range more genuinely than dismissive readings allow. She belongs, but at #9, not higher.

##### 10. Herman Düne — *Not On Top* (2005)

The franco-Swedish-American duo (sometimes trio) Herman Düne represents anti-folk's European wing — the version of the scene that took root in Paris and London in the early 2000s, partly through tours by Jeffrey Lewis and the Moldy Peaches, partly through native development of the same impulses. *Not On Top* is their most fully-realized record, recorded with Jeffrey Lewis collaborator Adam Lazzara and engineered by Boris Gronemberger, all acoustic guitars and brushed drums and David-Ivar Herman Düne's heavily-accented English vocals.

The songwriting is plainspoken in the Jonathan Richman tradition — short narrative songs about daily life, romantic disappointment, and travel — but the European context produces a slightly displaced quality that Richman lacks. "Not On Top" the song is a lovely meditation on professional and romantic failure; "I Wish That I Could See You Soon" is the closest the band comes to a hit; "Lou Lou Lou" is a children's-song homage that recalls Daniel Johnston's piano fragments.

This was the coin-flip slot. Scaruffi argued for Hamell on Trial's *Choochtown* (2001), the abrasive Lower East Side punk-folk record that has a stronger claim to anti-folk's confrontational lineage. Fantano argued for Herman Düne, both on songwriting quality and on geographical breadth — the canon shouldn't be 100% New York. Both critics agreed the case was even; coin flip went to Herman Düne. Both signed off. (See Coin-Flip Resolutions for full discussion of the *Choochtown* case, which both critics consider very close to the bubble.)

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Three irreconcilable disagreements went to coin flip. Each is documented below. In all three cases the losing album appears in Honorable Mentions.

**Coin Flip 1 — Daniel Johnston slot: *Hi, How Are You* (1983) vs *Yip/Jump Music* (1983).** Both records are foundational; both were boombox-recorded the same year; both have canonical Johnston songs. Scaruffi preferred *Yip/Jump Music* for being more cohesive as an album experience and for "Speeding Motorcycle." Fantano preferred *Hi, How Are You* for being more raw, for "Walking the Cow" and "Despair Came Knocking," and for the Cobain-era cultural visibility that brought Johnston into the alt-rock canon. The flip went to *Hi, How Are You*. Both critics agreed the result was acceptable; Scaruffi noted that *Yip/Jump Music* is essentially equivalent and would have been an equally valid #2.

**Coin Flip 2 — Mountain Goats slot: *All Hail West Texas* (2002) vs *Zopilote Machine* (1994).** This was the most argued-over decision in the entire debate, occupying parts of two rounds. Scaruffi initially preferred *Zopilote Machine* on lo-fi-purist grounds — it's the more uncompromising boombox record, with more abrasive performances and a stronger Shrimper-era community context. Fantano preferred *All Hail West Texas* on songwriting maturity grounds — the songs are simply better, the characters more developed, the emotional register more controlled. Both critics conceded merit on both sides. Coin flip resolved in favor of *All Hail West Texas*. Scaruffi noted that the result feels right despite his theoretical preference: *AHWT* is the record listeners actually return to.

**Coin Flip 3 — #10 slot: Herman Düne's *Not On Top* (2005) vs Hamell on Trial's *Choochtown* (2001).** Scaruffi advocated *Choochtown* on confrontational-tradition grounds: Hamell on Trial (Ed Hamell, solo with acoustic guitar) is anti-folk's most punk-aligned figure, his stage presence is uniquely abrasive, and *Choochtown* is the album where his songwriting matched his performance intensity. Fantano advocated *Not On Top* on geographical-breadth grounds: the canon needs at least one non-American record, Herman Düne represents the genre's successful export to Europe, and the album's quality is independently strong. Coin flip went to Herman Düne. Hamell on Trial moved to Honorable Mentions. Both critics agreed the disagreement was genuinely irreconcilable on principled grounds and that the coin flip was the correct mechanism.

#### Honorable Mentions

The following albums were genuinely contested for top-ten slots and lost. Each is canonically defensible.

- **Hamell on Trial — *Choochtown* (2001).** Lost to Herman Düne by coin flip. Anti-folk's most confrontational record: Ed Hamell solo with a 1937 Gibson, songs about murderers and meth addicts and his own drug history, performance-intensity unmatched in the scene.
- **Daniel Johnston — *Yip/Jump Music* (1983).** Lost to *Hi, How Are You* by coin flip. The other foundational Johnston boombox record, home of "Speeding Motorcycle" and "Sorry Entertainer."
- **The Mountain Goats — *Zopilote Machine* (1994).** Lost to *All Hail West Texas* by coin flip. The Shrimper-era boombox record, more uncompromising, less polished, equally canonical in different terms.
- **Kimya Dawson — *I'm Sorry That Sometimes I'm Mean* (2002).** Her solo debut. Scaruffi's preferred Dawson album; lost to *Remember That I Love You* on Fantano's preference for the more developed record.
- **Adam Green — *Gemstones* (2005).** Green's follow-up to *Friends of Mine*, weirder and shorter (28 songs in 35 minutes), beloved by the fanbase, narrowly excluded for being a less coherent album experience.
- **Jeffrey Lewis — *The Last Time I Did Acid I Went Insane* (2001).** Lewis's debut. Both critics considered it; *It's the Ones Who've Cracked* is the more developed record.
- **The Moldy Peaches — *Unreleased Cutz and Live Jamz 1994-2002* (2003).** The B-sides compilation; full of strong material; excluded as a non-album.
- **Beck — *Stereopathetic Soulmanure* (1994).** Beck's other 1994 lo-fi record. Less anti-folk-coded than *One Foot in the Grave* (more abrasive, more collage-based), but a strong companion.
- **Lou Barlow / Sentridoh — *Winning Losers: A Collection of Home Recordings 89-93*.** Anti-folk-adjacent home recordings; excluded as too fragmentary to anthologize fairly.
- **Toby Goodshank — *Toby Goodshank Sings* (2003) or any of his many albums.** Sidewalk Cafe regular, Moldy Peaches collaborator. Fantano advocated; the prolificity of his catalog made it hard to pick one canonical record.
- **Regina Spektor — *Songs* (2002).** Her self-released second album. More anti-folk-coded than *Soviet Kitsch* but less developed; both critics preferred to honor the breakthrough record.
- **Lach — *Blang!* (1995).** The scene's founder. Scaruffi initially advocated; both agreed his historical importance exceeds his recorded output.
- **Phoebe Kreutz — any.** Scene fixture, not a producer of canonical full-lengths.
- **Schwervon! — any.** Nan Turner and Major Matt Mason USA's project; Fantano initially advocated; ultimately too rock-leaning to make the cut.
- **Frank Black — *Frank Black* (1993)** or solo Pixies-adjacent work. Discussed and rejected: it's power pop, not anti-folk.
- **Defiance, Ohio — *The Great Depression* (2006).** Discussed and rejected: it's folk-punk proper, with its own canon.
- **Herman Düne — *Mas Cambios* (2003).** The other strong Herman Düne record. Lost to *Not On Top* on songwriting consistency.

---

### Freak Folk / Wyrd Folk

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

The two critics walked in with very different shapes of canon. Scaruffi opened with a maximalist position: any honest list of "wyrd folk" has to acknowledge the British forerunners — Comus's *First Utterance*, the Incredible String Band's *The Hangman's Beautiful Daughter*, and Linda Perhacs's *Parallelograms* — because the 2000s revival is unintelligible without them. He then laid down the Joanna Newsom triumvirate (*The Milk-Eyed Mender*, *Ys*, *Have One on Me*), Devendra Banhart's *Rejoicing in the Hands*, Animal Collective's *Sung Tongs*, Espers's *II*, and Six Organs of Admittance's *School of the Flower*.

Fantano's top-15 leaned 2000s-revival-only. He treated the question as a contemporary scene question, not a historical one. His core: *Ys*, *Have One on Me*, *The Milk-Eyed Mender*, Sufjan Stevens's *Seven Swans*, *Sung Tongs*, Iron & Wine's *Our Endless Numbered Days* (with strong residual fondness for *The Creek Drank the Cradle*), Marissa Nadler's *The Saga of Mayflower May*, CocoRosie's *La Maison de Mon Rêve*, Banhart's *Rejoicing*, Espers's *II*, and Vetiver's debut. He was skeptical of the 60s/70s forerunners' inclusion at all, on the grounds that "freak folk" as a term is a 2000s coinage.

The debates that consumed the bulk of the rounds:

**Forerunners — in or out?** Scaruffi's position prevailed in part. They agreed that any list calling itself "Wyrd Folk" has to seat at least Comus and Linda Perhacs, since the revival explicitly recovered them. ISB became the harder fight — Fantano didn't dispute the music, only whether *Hangman's* belongs more in psych-folk than wyrd folk proper. Coin-flip resolved.

**Three Joanna Newsoms?** Fantano wanted all three. Scaruffi wanted *Ys* canonically, *Have One on Me* as the maturation, but resisted *Milk-Eyed Mender* taking a slot he wanted for Espers or Six Organs. *Milk-Eyed Mender* lost to the forerunners and went to Honorable Mentions.

**Sufjan vs. Iron & Wine** for the Americana-adjacent slot. Both critics agreed *Seven Swans* is the more "wyrd" record (whisper-quiet, biblical, banjo-led) versus *Our Endless Numbered Days* being closer to straight indie folk. Sufjan won.

**CocoRosie vs. Vetiver** for the final slot. Fantano pushed CocoRosie hard — *La Maison de Mon Rêve* is the most genuinely strange record of the entire revival, recorded in a Paris bathroom, untrained vocals, found-sound rattle. Scaruffi was lukewarm but conceded the strangeness made it more "wyrd" than Vetiver's polished debut. CocoRosie in.

**Espers's *II* vs. *The Weed Tree*.** Both agreed Espers had to be present. *II* is the dense, fully-realized statement; *The Weed Tree* is the covers EP that bridged. They took *II*.

**Six Organs of Admittance.** Scaruffi insisted; Fantano was less invested but didn't fight it. *School of the Flower* in.

**Linda Perhacs — forerunner or revival?** A subtler dispute. *Parallelograms* came out in 1970 and sold almost nothing. Its 2003 reissue on The Wild Places, and the documented influence on Newsom and Banhart, made it functionally a 2000s revival record despite the recording date. Both critics agreed the influence-vector justifies the slot regardless of which side of the chronological line you place it on.

**The neofolk question.** Fantano raised it once and dropped it: are Current 93, Sol Invictus, and Death in June part of the wyrd folk canon, or do they constitute their own genre adjacent to it? Scaruffi treated neofolk as its own scene with its own forerunners (Stockhausen, Tibetan ritual recordings) and politics (frequently fascist-adjacent in ways the 2000s freak folk revival was not). Both critics agreed to keep neofolk out of this list and let it have its own treatment elsewhere. *Black Ships Ate the Sky* lands in Honorable Mentions for that reason.

**Marissa Nadler vs. Six Organs of Admittance** for the gothic-American slot. Both critics wanted both. Neither was willing to drop another record to make room. The dispute went unresolved and is recorded as a coin-flip below.

Three irreconcilables went to coin flip and are noted explicitly below.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Joanna Newsom — *Ys* (2006)

The record that defined what the 2000s freak folk revival was capable of when it stopped winking and committed fully to its source material. Five tracks, fifty-five minutes, harp and voice as the rhythmic and melodic spine, Van Dyke Parks orchestrating around Newsom rather than over her. Steve Albini engineered the harp sessions; Jim O'Rourke mixed; Parks wrote the orchestral arrangements; Newsom kept veto power over every note. The result is the most ambitious folk record of the century to date, and one that earns its ambition.

The narrative material is allusive without being obscurantist. "Emily" is a letter to Newsom's astrophysicist sister, partly didactic about meteor terminology, partly a meditation on losing siblings to their own orbits. "Monkey & Bear" is a fable about coercion and escape that holds together as fairy-tale and as adult allegory simultaneously. "Sawdust & Diamonds" is the harp-and-voice solo at the album's center, the album's clearest line back to medieval ballad form. "Only Skin" sprawls across sixteen-plus minutes and never sags. "Cosmia" closes with a refrain — "I miss your skin, you old friend" — that reframes the whole record as elegy.

Scaruffi rates this as one of the great song-cycles of the 2000s and frames Newsom in lineage with Joni Mitchell's *Hejira*-era arrangements and the British folk-revival sensibility of Anne Briggs, while noting that the harp-as-primary-instrument places her closer to Alice Coltrane's spiritual jazz than to any folk peer. Fantano's review at release was effusive — he treats *Ys* as the high-water mark of the entire decade's folk output, full stop.

The reason it sits at #1 rather than #2 is the combination of compositional density, emotional reach, and scene impact. Every freak folk artist who came after either had to absorb this or define themselves against it.

##### 2. Comus — *First Utterance* (1971)

The forerunner that the 2000s revival rediscovered as the genre's most extreme prior achievement, and the album that gives "wyrd folk" its actual claim to weirdness. Recorded by a London-based group around Roger Wootton, Glenn Goring, and Bobbie Watson, *First Utterance* is acoustic folk pushed into territories that Comus's contemporaries — Pentangle, Fairport Convention, even ISB — would not touch. The subject matter is violence: rape, asylum confinement, ritual murder, sexualized cruelty toward animals. The instrumentation is acoustic guitars, hand drums, violin, oboe, and Wootton's serrated voice paired against Watson's pure soprano in a contrast that produces a constant tonal vertigo.

"Diana" opens the record with a chase scene set to galloping acoustic counterpoint. "The Herald" answers it with eleven minutes of pastoral mysticism that takes its time arriving anywhere. "Drip Drip" is the centerpiece — a murder told from the killer's interior, with a chorus that loops "drip drip drip drip drip drip the body slips" until the rhythm itself becomes the wound. "Song to Comus" is a hymn to the Greek god of revelry that lands closer to Wicker Man liturgy than to any Apollonian folk tradition.

Scaruffi rates *First Utterance* among the most singular folk records ever made and treats it as the missing link between English folk revival and avant-garde song. Fantano accepted the inclusion on the grounds that no 2000s freak folk artist who matters can be discussed without acknowledging this record's shadow — Current 93, Devendra Banhart, Espers, and Six Organs all named-check it.

The reason it lands at #2 is that *First Utterance* is the record that proved acoustic folk could do what extreme metal would later attempt with electricity. Half a century on, almost nothing in any genre matches its specific gravity.

##### 3. Joanna Newsom — *Have One on Me* (2010)

The triple-album, eighteen-track, two-hour-plus follow-up to *Ys*, recorded after a vocal cord injury that permanently changed Newsom's instrument. The voice that arrived on *Milk-Eyed Mender* — the high, almost cartoonish lilt — was gone by the time these sessions started. What replaced it was deeper, smokier, more conventionally beautiful and, the critics agreed, ultimately a richer instrument.

The arrangements step back from *Ys*'s orchestral density toward a chamber-folk band sound: piano alongside harp, drums, strings, and Ryan Francesconi's acoustic guitar work serving as a constant secondary voice. "Easy" opens the record on piano. "'81" is solo harp. "Have One on Me" is the title epic, twelve minutes about Lola Montez and exile and the cost of being looked at. "Good Intentions Paving Company" is the closest Newsom has come to a straight pop song. "Baby Birch" and "In California" are the emotional centers — both are about geography as a failure mode of love.

Scaruffi places *Have One on Me* slightly below *Ys* on ambition but slightly above on consistency, and notes that the longer form serves the songs rather than padding them. Fantano gave it one of his earliest "10s" and has since walked it back to a 9 in retrospective conversation, but holds it up as the best long-form folk statement of the 2010s.

The reason it sits at #3 is that, while *Ys* is the more concentrated achievement, *Have One on Me* is the record where Newsom proved the gift was durable — that *Milk-Eyed Mender* and *Ys* weren't a two-album peak but a career.

##### 4. Animal Collective — *Sung Tongs* (2004)

The duo record from Avey Tare and Panda Bear that detached the band from their early noise-collage tendencies and rewired them around acoustic guitars and stacked vocal harmonies. *Sung Tongs* is a freak folk record by accident — the band did not see themselves as folk — and that accidental quality is exactly what places it on this list. It is what acoustic music sounds like when made by people whose instincts come from somewhere else: tape loops, Beach Boys, *Faust IV*, drum-circle communalism.

"Leaf House" opens with stuttered vocal cells over hand drums. "Who Could Win a Rabbit" is the closest the record comes to a single, and it is structurally a children's round mutated into a full band performance. "The Softest Voice" is nine minutes of acoustic finger-style guitar layered with whispered vocals that crystallize into a hymn at the eight-minute mark. "Visiting Friends" is twelve minutes long, almost ambient, a portrait of suburban summer slowed to molasses. "Kids on Holiday" closes with the album's most conventionally pretty melody buried under tape hiss.

Scaruffi rates *Sung Tongs* among the most important Animal Collective records and frames it as a folk record whose folk-ness was discovered, not chosen. Fantano placed it on his decade-end list and has continued to cite it as a turning point — the record that gave permission to a whole generation of bedroom acoustic experimentation.

The reason it sits at #4 is that *Sung Tongs* expanded what freak folk could mean. Without it, the "freak" in freak folk would have collapsed back into a coffeehouse aesthetic. With it, the term still has teeth.

##### 5. Linda Perhacs — *Parallelograms* (1970)

The single-album miracle from a Topanga Canyon dental hygienist whose record was buried for thirty years before Devendra Banhart, Daft Punk, and the 2000s acid-folk crate-diggers pulled it back out. *Parallelograms* was recorded in 1969–70 with producer Leonard Rosenman, was promoted incompetently, and might have stayed forgotten if not for the Mojo and Wire reappraisals of the early 2000s. Its rediscovery is part of what gave the 2000s revival its sense of mission.

"Chimacum Rain" opens with stacked vocal harmonies and one of the era's most distinctive vocal-overdub textures — Perhacs sounds like a one-woman choir treated through tape echo. "Paper Mountain Man" is acoustic guitar and voice, structurally simple, harmonically rich. "Dolphin" is a glassy, almost ambient meditation. The title track is the most experimental — vocal tones layered into a geometry that anticipates Julianna Barwick by forty years. "Hey, Who Really Cares?" is the song with the most conventional verse-chorus shape, and the one that became the album's calling card.

Scaruffi rates *Parallelograms* as one of the great unjustly buried folk records of its generation and frames Perhacs alongside Vashti Bunyan as the two female forerunners whose recovery defined the 2000s revival's self-understanding. Fantano did not push hard for it but accepted it readily — he treats Perhacs as a foundational influence on Joanna Newsom, Marissa Nadler, and the entire wing of the revival that took vocal stacking seriously.

The reason it sits at #5 is that *Parallelograms* is small, perfect, and irreplaceable. Its stack-of-voices texture became one of the templates the 2000s revival worked from, knowingly or not. Perhacs herself only returned to recording in 2014 with *The Soul of All Natural Things*, more than four decades after the debut — the gap is itself part of what makes *Parallelograms* feel like a single transmission from somewhere outside the music industry's normal time.

##### 6. Devendra Banhart — *Rejoicing in the Hands* (2004)

The album that gave the 2000s freak folk scene its public face. Banhart had already released *Oh Me Oh My* (2002) on Michael Gira's Young God Records to cult attention, but *Rejoicing in the Hands* is where the songs caught up to the persona. Recorded with a real band and a fuller production — still acoustic, still warbled, but no longer four-track-bedroom — *Rejoicing* became the record that the term "freak folk" was actually attached to in print.

"This Is the Way" opens with the warble-tremolo voice that became Banhart's signature. "A Sight to Behold" is fingerpicked guitar and Banhart in his most direct lyrical mode — concrete imagery, no mysticism for its own sake. The title track is a duet with Vashti Bunyan, the record's symbolic act of canonization (the present generation receiving the blessing of the recovered forerunner). "Insect Eyes" is the album's most musically straightforward song and one of its best. "Will Is My Friend" closes the record with Banhart at his most loose-limbed.

Scaruffi treats Banhart as the scene's organizer more than its strongest single artist, but rates *Rejoicing* among the better records of the early-2000s revival, particularly for its bridging of American outsider folk (Roky Erickson, Karen Dalton) with the Topanga Canyon and ISB lineages. Fantano was among the early champions and has cited the album in retrospective lists.

The reason it sits at #6 is that *Rejoicing* organized the scene. The Vashti Bunyan duet alone would earn it the slot — it is the moment the revival shook hands with its own past in public. Banhart curated the *Golden Apples of the Sun* compilation for Arthur Magazine the following year, and that compilation — featuring Vetiver, Espers, Iron & Wine, Six Organs, Joanna Newsom, CocoRosie, Diane Cluck, MV & EE — is the closest thing the scene has to a self-published manifesto. *Rejoicing* is the album that earned Banhart the right to do that curatorial work, and the scene's coherence in those years is partly downstream of his social organizing.

##### 7. Espers — *II* (2006)

The Philadelphia quartet's second album, and the record that finally fulfilled the British-folk-revival-meets-American-drone promise that Greg Weeks, Meg Baird, and Brooke Sietinsons had been circling on their self-titled debut and the *Weed Tree* covers EP. *II* is denser, longer-formed, more electric, and more confident in its synthesis.

"Dead Queen" opens with twelve-string interlock that nods directly at Pentangle's John Renbourn while Weeks's electric guitar leads bring in the Crazy Horse drone undercurrent. "Widow's Weed" is Baird's vocal showcase and one of the prettiest melodies in the 2000s revival. "Cruel Storm" is the closest the record comes to straight British folk-rock circa 1969. "Children of Stone" is seven minutes of slow-build escalation. "Mansfield and Cyclops" closes the record on its most mantric, repetitive structure — the place where Espers's drone instincts overtake their songwriting instincts in the most productive way.

Scaruffi rates *II* as the strongest American freak folk record after Newsom's catalog and singles out the band's combination of Fairport-influenced song structure with Bardo Pond–adjacent guitar texture as the defining synthesis of the era. Fantano was less effusive at the time but has acknowledged the record's stature in retrospective discussion.

The reason *II* sits at #7 is that it is the album that proved freak folk could hold up to British folk-rock at full scale — full band, full electrics, full song-structure rigor — without losing the revival's specific sensibility.

##### 8. The Incredible String Band — *The Hangman's Beautiful Daughter* (1968)

The forerunner album that the 2000s revival treated as scripture. Robin Williamson and Mike Heron's third record is where ISB's earlier whimsy hardened into something stranger — instrumentation drawn from across the world (sitar, gimbri, oud, organ, harpsichord, hand drums), song structures that ignore Western verse-chorus convention, and a willingness to let songs run thirteen minutes if the song needs thirteen minutes.

"Koeeoaddi There" opens with a song-cycle compressed into five minutes — childhood memory, mythological figures, jump-rope rhymes. "The Minotaur's Song" is a music-hall pastiche performed by the minotaur in first person. "A Very Cellular Song" is the thirteen-minute centerpiece that drifts from a Bahamian gospel quote ("Bid You Goodnight") through cellular biology, toward a mantric "May the long-time sun shine upon you" closing chant that Krishna Das would later cover. "Three Is a Green Crowd" and "Swift as the Wind" round out the record's gentler moments.

Scaruffi rates *Hangman's* as one of the single most important folk records of the 1960s and treats it as the founding text of psychedelic folk as a serious art form rather than a genre tag. Fantano is more reserved about ISB — he respects the record but argued that it sits closer to psych-folk than to "wyrd folk" proper. The forerunners-or-not coin flip kept it on the list.

The reason *Hangman's* sits at #8 is that without it there is no Joanna Newsom, no Devendra Banhart, no Animal Collective folk turn. Every artist on this list who took the long-form, multi-section, world-instrumented approach learned it here.

##### 9. Sufjan Stevens — *Seven Swans* (2004)

The whisper-quiet album Stevens recorded with Daniel Smith of the Danielson Famile between *Michigan* (2003) and *Illinois* (2005), and the record that earns Stevens his place in this canon. *Seven Swans* is Stevens's most overtly Christian album and his most folk album — banjo-led, acoustic-guitar-led, softly drummed, often barely above whisper.

"All the Trees of the Field Will Clap Their Hands" opens with banjo and stacked vocals on an Isaiah quotation. "The Dress Looks Nice on You" is fingerpicked guitar and one of Stevens's most fragile melodies. "In the Devil's Territory" lives in the slow-build mode that *Illinois* would later scale up. "To Be Alone with You" is the album's clearest hymn — acoustic guitar, whispered vocal, Christological lyrics that sit at no remove from their subject. "The Transfiguration" closes the record with a banjo-and-drum march through a literal Gospel scene.

Scaruffi rates *Seven Swans* as Stevens's strongest album in the strict folk register, even if *Illinois* outscores it on his overall scale. Fantano holds *Seven Swans* up as the more durable record because its arrangements have aged better than *Illinois*'s orchestral-pop maximalism.

The reason it sits at #9 is that *Seven Swans* is the freak-folk-adjacent record that took the religious dimension of the genre seriously. The 2000s revival was full of mysticism-as-aesthetic — pentagrams, tarot, the language of myth. Stevens was the artist for whom the mysticism was operative belief, and the songs are stronger for it.

##### 10. CocoRosie — *La Maison de Mon Rêve* (2004)

The debut from Sierra and Bianca Casady, recorded in Bianca's Paris apartment bathroom on a four-track because the bathroom had the best acoustics. *La Maison de Mon Rêve* is the most genuinely strange record of the 2000s freak folk revival, full stop. Sierra's classically trained operatic soprano against Bianca's untrained, hip-hop-inflected, deliberately childlike vocal. Toy pianos, music boxes, found-sound rattle, harmonica, beatbox, a children's-record sensibility laid over the harshest possible subject matter.

"Terrible Angels" opens with a beatbox loop, a toy piano, and stacked vocals about an angel of death. "By Your Side" sounds like a 1930s parlor record played through water damage — and is the most conventionally beautiful song the Casady sisters have ever written. "Jesus Loves Me" rewrites the Sunday-school hymn into a stuttering, hip-hop-cadenced commentary on race in America. "Good Friday" is unaccompanied vocal and a single-note keyboard drone. "Lyla" is the record's most affecting moment — a lullaby whose lyrics keep slipping into nightmare. "Madonna" closes the record on hand percussion and stacked harmony.

Scaruffi rated CocoRosie among the most original duos of the decade and singled out the *Maison* sessions for their refusal of the polished-bohemian aesthetic that was already calcifying around the freak folk scene. Fantano pushed the inclusion through over Vetiver — his argument was that *La Maison* is the only record on this list that genuinely delivers on the word "freak."

The reason it sits at #10 is that the slot exists for the record that risked the most. CocoRosie risked the most — and what they risked, they earned.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Three irreconcilables came down to coin flip and are noted explicitly here:

1. **Incredible String Band's *The Hangman's Beautiful Daughter* vs. Vashti Bunyan's *Just Another Diamond Day* (1970).** Scaruffi argued for ISB on the grounds that *Hangman's* is the more compositionally ambitious forerunner. Fantano argued for *Just Another Diamond Day* because Bunyan's collaboration with Devendra Banhart on *Rejoicing in the Hands* makes her the more direct lineage to the 2000s scene, and *Diamond Day* is her catalog statement. **Coin flip went to ISB.** *Diamond Day* sits in Honorable Mentions.

2. **CocoRosie's *La Maison de Mon Rêve* vs. Vetiver's *Vetiver* (2004).** Fantano argued CocoRosie on strangeness; Scaruffi argued Vetiver on songwriting and on Andy Cabic's role as the scene's quiet center. **Coin flip went to CocoRosie.** Vetiver in Honorable Mentions.

3. **Marissa Nadler's *The Saga of Mayflower May* (2005) vs. Six Organs of Admittance's *School of the Flower* (2005).** Both critics wanted both. Neither was willing to drop another record to fit one of them. **Coin flip went to neither — both bumped to Honorable Mentions** because forcing the issue would have meant cutting either Sufjan or CocoRosie, and the critics agreed those slots were earned. This is the resolution both critics were least happy with, and it is preserved as a real failure of the Top 10 format rather than papered over.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **Joanna Newsom — *The Milk-Eyed Mender* (2004).** The debut. The record that introduced the harp-and-helium voice. Lost its slot to forerunners, would be top-five on a strict 2000s-only list.
- **Vashti Bunyan — *Just Another Diamond Day* (1970).** The forerunner whose 2000 reissue and *Rejoicing in the Hands* collaboration arguably did more to organize the revival's self-understanding than any single 2000s release.
- **Six Organs of Admittance — *School of the Flower* (2005).** Ben Chasny's cleanest synthesis of John Fahey's American Primitive lineage with the wyrd folk vocabulary.
- **Marissa Nadler — *The Saga of Mayflower May* (2005).** The second album. Ghost-folk, reverb-drenched, the gothic register of the revival.
- **Vetiver — *Vetiver* (2004).** Andy Cabic's quieter, more songwriterly contribution to the scene.
- **Iron & Wine — *Our Endless Numbered Days* (2004).** Sam Beam's first studio record. Closer to indie folk than wyrd folk but on the border.
- **Iron & Wine — *The Creek Drank the Cradle* (2002).** The four-track debut. The folkier, weirder, more solitary record of the two.
- **Espers — *The Weed Tree* (2005).** The covers EP. Nico, Blue Öyster Cult, Michael Hurley. Bridge between *Espers* and *II*.
- **Devendra Banhart — *Oh Me Oh My...* (2002).** The four-track debut. The record that started the conversation.
- **Devendra Banhart — *Cripple Crow* (2005).** The maximalist follow-up to *Rejoicing*. Sprawling, uneven, occasionally brilliant.
- **Animal Collective — *Campfire Songs* (2003).** The acoustic-on-a-porch session. Even more directly "folk" than *Sung Tongs*, and would have been the pick on a stricter folk list.
- **Faun Fables — *Family Album* (2004).** Dawn McCarthy's theatrical, European-folk-inflected record.
- **MV & EE — *Green Blues* (2009).** The Vermont free-folk wing of the scene at its most realized.
- **Diane Cluck — *Oh Vanille / Ova Nil* (2003).** The most overlooked songwriter of the scene. Lower-fi, sharper-toothed than her peers.
- **Jana Hunter — *Blank Unstaring Heirs of Doom* (2005).** Released on Banhart's Gnomonsong label. The shadow-cousin to *Rejoicing in the Hands*.
- **Nick Castro — *A Spy in the House of God* (2004).** Los Angeles end of the scene, more directly acid-folk than most of his peers.
- **Akron/Family — *Akron/Family* (2005).** Released on Young God. The Brooklyn collective that bridged Animal Collective's communal-folk impulse with band-rock structure.
- **Fursaxa — *Lepidoptera* (2005).** Tara Burke's drone-folk solo project. The most explicitly ritualistic record of the scene.
- **Charalambides — *Joy Shapes* (2004).** Christina Carter and Tom Carter. Drone folk at its most patient.
- **Current 93 — *Black Ships Ate the Sky* (2006).** David Tibet's apocalyptic-folk catalog statement. Appears on Fantano's "wyrd folk is really neofolk" list and on Scaruffi's "this is its own genre" list, hence the demotion.

---

### Indie Folk

*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Two critics walked into the indie folk room and almost agreed on everything, which was suspicious. Scaruffi, born 1955, has been writing about American underground music since the era when "indie folk" still meant Will Oldham hand-stitching cassettes for Drag City. Fantano, born 1985, came up exactly when this scene crested — Bon Iver in a cabin, Fleet Foxes in a forest, Sufjan in a state of his choosing — and has reviewed virtually every record under consideration. This is one of the rare sub-genres where their canons substantially overlap, and the debate became less about which records belonged and more about which records were *most consequential*.

**Scaruffi's Top 15:**
1. Bonnie "Prince" Billy — *I See a Darkness* (1999)
2. Smog — *Knock Knock* (1999)
3. Sufjan Stevens — *Illinois* (2005)
4. The Microphones — *The Glow Pt. 2* (2001)
5. Iron & Wine — *The Creek Drank the Cradle* (2002)
6. Bonnie "Prince" Billy — *Ease Down the Road* (2001)
7. Bill Callahan — *Sometimes I Wish We Were an Eagle* (2009)
8. Mount Eerie — *A Crow Looked at Me* (2017)
9. Fleet Foxes — *Fleet Foxes* (2008)
10. Bright Eyes — *I'm Wide Awake, It's Morning* (2005)
11. Bon Iver — *For Emma, Forever Ago* (2007)
12. Sufjan Stevens — *Carrie & Lowell* (2015)
13. Beirut — *Gulag Orkestar* (2006)
14. Damien Jurado — *Saint Bartlett* (2010)
15. Sun Kil Moon — *Benji* (2014)

**Fantano's Top 15:**
1. Sufjan Stevens — *Illinois* (2005)
2. Mount Eerie — *A Crow Looked at Me* (2017)
3. Bon Iver — *For Emma, Forever Ago* (2007)
4. Fleet Foxes — *Fleet Foxes* (2008)
5. Big Thief — *Two Hands* (2019)
6. Sufjan Stevens — *Carrie & Lowell* (2015)
7. The Microphones — *The Glow Pt. 2* (2001)
8. Adrianne Lenker — *songs* (2020)
9. Father John Misty — *I Love You, Honeybear* (2015)
10. Bonnie "Prince" Billy — *I See a Darkness* (1999)
11. Big Thief — *Capacity* (2017)
12. Bright Eyes — *I'm Wide Awake, It's Morning* (2005)
13. Iron & Wine — *Our Endless Numbered Days* (2004)
14. Phoebe Bridgers — *Punisher* (2020)
15. Julien Baker — *Turn Out the Lights* (2017)

**Round 1 — The Easy Agreements.** Both critics had Sufjan's *Illinois*, *A Crow Looked at Me*, *For Emma*, *Fleet Foxes*, *The Glow Pt. 2*, *I See a Darkness*, *Carrie & Lowell*, and *I'm Wide Awake* in their fifteens. Eight slots filled before anyone raised their voice. Scaruffi muttered that *Illinois* was "overarranged in places" but conceded its ambition; Fantano nodded that *A Crow* was "the heaviest record I've ever had to sit with."

**Round 2 — The Smog Question.** Scaruffi pushed hard for *Knock Knock* over any post-2010 record. Fantano respects Callahan but considers him "more singer-songwriter than indie folk," a category-policing move that Scaruffi rejected. Compromise: one Callahan-related slot, but it would be *Knock Knock* (the Smog era, more textured arrangements) rather than the cleaner solo records.

**Round 3 — Big Thief.** Fantano: "You can't do this list without Big Thief, full stop." Scaruffi conceded their importance but argued that Adrianne Lenker's *songs* is the more lasting artifact — a position Fantano partially shared (he gave it a 9). They agreed: one Big Thief slot, one Lenker solo slot, both on the list.

**Round 4 — Father John Misty vs. Beirut.** Fantano wanted *I Love You, Honeybear* in the ten as a stylistic anchor for the 2010s baroque indie folk wave. Scaruffi found Tillman "self-aware to the point of self-cancellation" and pushed Beirut's *Gulag Orkestar* as a more honest expansion of the indie folk palette. Coin flip territory.

**Round 5 — Bright Eyes.** Both had it. Both wanted it. Easy.

**Round 6 — The Microphones / Mount Eerie problem.** Phil Elverum has two records in contention: *The Glow Pt. 2* and *A Crow Looked at Me*. Both critics initially had both. They eventually agreed both belong — they are different artists making different art at different life stages, and excluding either would be a critical failure.

The final ten reflect substantial consensus with two genuine coin flips.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Sufjan Stevens — *Illinois* (2005)

The maximalist statement of indie folk, and the record that taught a generation that the genre could hold horns, choirs, geography, history, and a banjo without collapsing under its own ambition. Sufjan's second "state record" (the project, mythical and now abandoned, was always more conceptual gimmick than serious commitment) wove the Illinois state map with John Wayne Gacy, Carl Sandburg, UFOs over Highland, and the death of his friend Casimir Pulaski. The orchestrations — handled almost entirely by Sufjan himself across dozens of instruments — borrow from Steve Reich's pulse-minimalism, Vince Guaraldi's seventh chords, and the choral layering of Brian Wilson's *Smile* sessions, which had finally emerged the year before.

Scaruffi: rates this in his all-time pantheon, calling Sufjan one of the few American songwriters of his generation to attempt a "total art" approach. Praises "Come On! Feel the Illinoise!" and "Casimir Pulaski Day" specifically, though he is mildly critical of the album's unevenness across its 22 tracks. Fantano: an early career touchstone before his channel even existed in its mature form; has called *Illinois* a defining record of the 2000s and ranks it among the best indie albums of all time. Both critics agreed that "Casimir Pulaski Day" is one of the most devastating songs in the genre's history — the line about the cardinals nesting in the trees outside the room where his friend is dying does the work of a short story in twelve seconds.

The arrangements would influence everyone from Dirty Projectors to Bon Iver to the National. The album's failure (in the technical sense) is also its triumph: the project of writing a record for every state was always more conceptual gimmick than serious commitment, but the gesture itself reframed what an indie folk album could attempt to be.

##### 2. Mount Eerie — *A Crow Looked at Me* (2017)

Phil Elverum's wife Geneviève Castrée died of pancreatic cancer in July 2016, leaving Phil and their infant daughter. He recorded *A Crow Looked at Me* in the room where she died, on her instruments, beginning with the line "Death is real, someone's there and then they're not." It is the most unflinching grief record in the indie folk canon, and arguably in any canon.

Fantano's review of this album is one of the most discussed pieces of music criticism of the 2010s. He gave it a 10/10 — extraordinarily rare for him — and called it "the most painful piece of music I have ever heard." He has cited it as one of the records that recalibrated his approach to reviewing music about death and personal catastrophe. Scaruffi has been more measured, recognizing the artistic achievement while wary of the "documentary impulse" overtaking the songwriting; he prefers *The Glow Pt. 2* among Elverum records but acknowledges *A Crow* as a singular event.

The album's refusal of metaphor is its formal innovation. When Phil sings about receiving a backpack in the mail that Geneviève had ordered for their daughter before she died, he is not making a poem out of it. He is reporting. The album's almost-prose quality — long lines, conversational meter, near-spoken delivery — borrows from Will Oldham's *I See a Darkness* and Sun Kil Moon's *Benji*, but pushes past both in its refusal of any redemptive arc. The crow does not symbolize anything. The crow looked at him. The album ends without resolution because grief does not resolve. It is one of the few records in any genre that you cannot reasonably listen to as background music.

##### 3. Bon Iver — *For Emma, Forever Ago* (2007)

The cabin record. Justin Vernon retreated to his father's hunting cabin in northwestern Wisconsin in the winter of 2006, sick with mononucleosis, recovering from the dissolution of his band DeYarmond Edison and a relationship. He recorded *For Emma* on a four-track over three months, layering his voice into improbable falsetto stacks that would soon be imitated by half the singer-songwriters in Brooklyn.

Fantano has placed this on multiple all-time lists for the 2000s and early 2010s. Scaruffi was initially skeptical of the "cabin myth" surrounding the record but came around to the album's craft, particularly "Skinny Love" and "Re: Stacks." Both agreed that "Skinny Love" became culturally inescapable in a way that almost damaged the record's reputation, but the album underneath the saturation point is still the real thing.

The vocal arrangements are the lasting innovation. Vernon's stacked falsetto — which he developed in part by experimenting with the Auto-Tune-like tracking he would later push much further on *Bon Iver, Bon Iver* and the 22, A Million records — became the indie folk vocal signature of the late 2000s. Fleet Foxes, the Tallest Man on Earth, the entire Mumford & Sons commercial-indie-folk wave (whatever you think of it) descend partly from this record. The lyrics are almost incoherent on a literal level — Vernon often sings phonetically, treating the voice as a fourth instrument — but the emotional grammar is precise. The album also single-handedly revived the "go to the woods and make art" mythology that has dogged American letters since Thoreau.

##### 4. Fleet Foxes — *Fleet Foxes* (2008)

The Brian Wilson-meets-Crosby Stills Nash record of the indie folk canon. Robin Pecknold and a rotating cast of Pacific Northwest collaborators released this debut in mid-2008, and within a year it had become the visual and sonic shorthand for an entire scene — beards, flannel, woodland photography, and four-part harmonies stacked like firewood.

Fantano gave the record a strong review and has consistently included it in best-of-the-2000s lists; he has been more critical of the band's later work but considers the debut a genre-defining moment.
