This is the Haggard album where Bakersfield-as-aesthetic begins to fade into Haggard-as-genre. It is included here because the songwriting is undeniable, not because it is a definitional Bakersfield record.

##### 7. Buck Owens — *Together Again / My Heart Skips a Beat* (1964)

The earlier Buck Owens album that contains the two singles that announced what the Buckaroos were going to be. "Together Again" — recorded in November 1963, released early 1964 — is one of the most-covered country songs of the second half of the twentieth century, and Tom Brumley's pedal steel solo on the original is the version every other steel player has worked from since. "My Heart Skips a Beat" is the harder rock-adjacent single that pairs with it.

The album as a whole is less consistent than *Tiger*, but its two anchor songs are arguably more important to the genre's history. Scaruffi seats it at seven on the strength of the singles and the band's emerging identity. Fantano (extrapolated, FLAGGED) would likely respond to the unguardedness of the production — this is a 1964 record made by a band that has not yet learned how famous it is about to become — and would probably rate it similarly.

##### 8. Wynn Stewart — *Wynn Stewart Sings* (1962)

The under-canonized record. Wynn Stewart was, by most accounts, the actual first Bakersfield artist — Buck Owens's early career was built playing in Stewart's band, and the Tomcats (Stewart's group) developed the Telecaster-and-pedal-steel template that the Buckaroos later codified. *Wynn Stewart Sings* (Challenge Records, 1962) is the album that documents this earlier moment.

Scaruffi has cited Stewart as historically central; Fantano (extrapolated, FLAGGED) has not commented on him at all but would likely respond to the under-recognized-prime-mover narrative. The record itself is rougher than the Buck Owens albums that followed — smaller production, less harmonic sophistication — but the rhythmic and timbral DNA of Bakersfield is fully present. "Above and Beyond" (later a Buck Owens single) is here in its original form.

The album is hard to find in clean reissue, which is part of why it does not show up on more general best-of country lists. It belongs in any honest Bakersfield top 10 on origin grounds.

##### 9. Rose Maddox — *The One Rose: The Capitol Years* (compilation, 1962-1965 sessions)

The Rose Maddox question was the longest debate. Maddox's career predates Bakersfield-as-coined-genre — she was working with the Maddox Brothers throughout the 1940s and 1950s — and her Capitol solo sides from the early-to-mid sixties are arguably hillbilly honky-tonk rather than Bakersfield proper. Scaruffi argued for inclusion on grounds that her Capitol band overlapped with the Buckaroos' personnel and that her vocal aesthetic — harder, less sentimental, less Patsy Cline — is the female counterpart to what Buck and Merle were doing.

Fantano (extrapolated, FLAGGED) is hardest to predict here. He has expressed support for under-recognized women in roots music elsewhere, but his commentary on country is so thin that any specific extrapolation is speculative. The compilation format is also unusual for a top-10 list, and we considered substituting one of her studio LPs; the consensus was that the Capitol-years compilation is the most representative artifact.

The record sits at nine on a combination of historical-importance and album-coherence grounds.

##### 10. Don Rich and the Buckaroos — *The Buck Owens Songbook* (1971)

The Buckaroos' instrumental-and-light-vocal album, fronted by Don Rich, which functions as the document of the Bakersfield instrumental vocabulary divorced from Buck Owens's songwriting. This is the closest thing the genre has to a "musicians' album" — every Telecaster lick, every pedal-steel voicing, every two-part harmony move that the Buckaroos invented over the previous decade is here in concentrated form.

The album is included over several stronger candidates (more Haggard, a second Yoakam, a Tommy Collins LP) on the grounds that no Bakersfield top 10 is honest without acknowledging that the genre's signature was as much instrumental as vocal. Don Rich died in a motorcycle accident in 1974; this record is among the last full-band documents of his playing.

Scaruffi was the stronger advocate. Fantano (extrapolated, FLAGGED) would likely engage with the band-as-craft argument. The slot survived a coin flip against Haggard's *Big City* (1981).

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Three irreconcilables were resolved by coin flip after six rounds of debate produced no consensus:

1. **Buck Owens *Tiger* vs *Carnegie Hall* for slot two.** Scaruffi: studio coherence wins. Fantano (extrapolated, FLAGGED): live-band energy wins. Coin flip went to Scaruffi. *Tiger* at two, Carnegie Hall at four. Note: if this were a Fantano-only list, the order would invert.

2. **Slot ten: Don Rich *Buckaroos* album vs Merle Haggard *Big City* (1981).** Scaruffi argued for the instrumental document; Fantano (extrapolated, FLAGGED) might have leaned toward more Haggard. Coin flip went to Scaruffi. *Big City* moves to honorable mentions.

3. **Wynn Stewart album choice.** *Wynn Stewart Sings* (1962) vs *It's Such a Pretty World Today* (1967). Both critics agreed Stewart belongs; the album-level choice was 50/50. Coin flip went to the earlier record on origin-priority grounds.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **Merle Haggard — *Big City* (1981).** Lost slot ten by coin flip. The "Big City" title track is one of the most-covered Haggard songs of the 1980s.
- **Tommy Collins — *Words and Music Country Style* (1957).** Singles artist primarily, but this Capitol LP holds together as an album. Scaruffi's flag: historically important, slightly underweight at the album level.
- **Red Simpson — *Roll Truck Roll* (1966).** The trucker-country side of Bakersfield. Important for genre breadth, hard to seat in a top 10 against Haggard and Owens.
- **Jean Shepard — *Songs of a Love Affair* (1956).** Pre-Bakersfield-proper but tonally adjacent. Country's first concept album by a woman, on some readings.
- **Dwight Yoakam — *Hillbilly Deluxe* (1987).** The follow-up to *Guitars, Cadillacs*. Slightly more polished, slightly less essential.
- **Buck Owens — *Open Up Your Heart* (1966).** Second-tier Capitol-era Buck. Strong but redundant given *Tiger* and *Together Again*.
- **Merle Haggard — *Same Train, A Different Time* (1969).** The Jimmie Rodgers tribute album. Historically interesting; tangential to Bakersfield-as-genre.

---

### Outlaw Country
*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Outlaw Country is, in many ways, an oppositional genre — a label coined to describe a handful of musicians who had decided, around 1972 or 1973, that they would no longer let Nashville producers pick their songs, hire their session players, and master their records to a polished radio gleam. Willie Nelson left RCA for Atlantic and then Columbia. Waylon Jennings renegotiated his RCA contract to retain creative control. Kris Kristofferson, who had been writing for Johnny Cash and Roger Miller, started recording his own gravelly demos. Tompall Glaser opened "Hillbilly Central" on Nineteenth Avenue South. The compilation *Wanted! The Outlaws* (1976) gave the movement a brand and a platinum certification, but the records that matter were already in the world.

Constructing a top ten between Scaruffi and a hypothetical Fantano forces a particular kind of argument. Scaruffi has actually written about this music — at length, in idiosyncratic English, with a clear hierarchy. He treats Willie Nelson's *Red Headed Stranger* as a "concept album that pioneered the country song-cycle," reveres Townes Van Zandt as a poet on the level of the great singer-songwriters, holds Waylon Jennings's mid-1970s run in high regard, and is fond of Kris Kristofferson's debut. He is less interested in honky-tonk grit for its own sake (Gary Stewart, David Allan Coe) and more interested in conceptual ambition, lyrical density, and the absorption of rock and folk vocabularies into a country frame.

Fantano has reviewed essentially zero classic outlaw country records on camera. Anything attributed to him here is **flagged as extrapolation** from his stated values: artistic autonomy, anti-corporate framing, lyrical specificity, and an ear for production that resists slickness. He has shown affinity for Sturgill Simpson, Tyler Childers, and Colter Wall — modern descendants — and has spoken warmly of Townes Van Zandt and Willie Nelson in passing. The debate below assumes he would extend that respect to the canonical outlaw records while pushing back against the more lacquered or jingoistic entries.

The debate proceeded in five rounds. Scaruffi led with *Red Headed Stranger* (1975) and *Honky Tonk Heroes* (1973) as immovable. Fantano (extrapolated, **FLAG**) agreed but pushed harder for Townes Van Zandt's *Live at the Old Quarter* (1977) over the studio records on the grounds that it captures the man at his most uncompromised. Scaruffi countered that *Townes Van Zandt* (1969) and *The Late Great Townes Van Zandt* (1972) are the artistic peaks; we cross-listed Townes and gave one slot to a studio record here. Kristofferson's debut was uncontested. Jennings's *Dreaming My Dreams* (1975) edged out *This Time* (1974) on songwriting depth. Billy Joe Shaver's *Old Five and Dimers Like Me* (1973) made it on Scaruffi's insistence that the songwriter behind *Honky Tonk Heroes* deserves his own slot. Guy Clark's *Old No. 1* (1975) was uncontested. Jessi Colter's *I'm Jessi Colter* (1975) made it on the strength of "I'm Not Lisa" and her role in the *Wanted!* compilation. The compilation itself was debated — Scaruffi tends to dismiss compilations, but its historical weight forced inclusion. Gary Stewart's *Out of Hand* (1975) and Steve Earle's *Guitar Town* (1986) were the final two slots; Earle was contested as a late entry but defended on the grounds that he closes the loop on the first wave. David Allan Coe was debated and dropped to honorable mentions; both critics found his persona more compelling than his records.

Three irreconcilable splits went to coin flip: Townes studio vs. live, Jennings *Dreaming My Dreams* vs. *Honky Tonk Heroes* primacy, and the inclusion of *Wanted! The Outlaws* over a second Willie Nelson record (*Phases and Stages*, 1974). Resolutions are noted at the bottom.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Willie Nelson — *Red Headed Stranger* (1975)

Willie Nelson had been a Nashville songwriter for over a decade — "Crazy" for Patsy Cline, "Hello Walls" for Faron Young, "Funny How Time Slips Away" for Billy Walker — when he signed with Columbia in 1975 with full creative control written into the contract. He used that control to record what label executives initially called a demo: thirteen tracks built around an old Western ballad about a preacher who shoots his unfaithful wife and her lover, then wanders the plains with her horse. The arrangements are skeletal — acoustic guitar, Mickey Raphael's harmonica, Bobbie Nelson's saloon piano, a brushed snare. There are almost no overdubs. Several songs run under two minutes. The whole record clocks in at thirty-three.

Scaruffi calls it "the album that pioneered the country song-cycle," and the description holds. *Red Headed Stranger* is not a collection of singles assembled to fill an LP; it is a continuous narrative in which "Time of the Preacher," "Blue Rock Montana," "Red Headed Stranger" itself, and Fred Rose's "Blue Eyes Crying in the Rain" function as movements rather than tracks. Nelson sings behind the beat in his characteristic phrasing, treating the melody as something to wander through rather than land on. The conceptual ambition — a Western tone poem in country drag — places it closer to Robbie Robertson's *The Last Waltz* curatorship or Randy Newman's character studies than to the Nashville machine it rebuked.

The commercial outcome confirmed the artistic gamble: "Blue Eyes Crying in the Rain" hit number one, the album went multi-platinum, and Columbia stopped second-guessing him. **Fantano (extrapolated)** would single out the production austerity — the refusal to add strings, the trust in space — as the move that makes this record durable. Scaruffi places it at the top of the outlaw canon and near the top of country music broadly. Both critics agree without amendment.

##### 2. Waylon Jennings — *Honky Tonk Heroes* (1973)

If *Red Headed Stranger* is the conceptual statement of the outlaw movement, *Honky Tonk Heroes* is its founding document. Waylon Jennings had been chafing at RCA for years, and in 1972 he renegotiated his contract to win producer choice, song selection, and his own band in the studio. The first record made under those terms was *Honky Tonk Heroes*, and nine of its ten songs were written by Billy Joe Shaver, a Texas songwriter whom Jennings had reportedly threatened to fight if he didn't get to record his catalog.

The result is a record that sounds, even now, unlike anything Nashville was producing in 1973. The rhythm section pushes; the guitars buzz with a Telecaster snarl borrowed from Bakersfield and amplified beyond it; Jennings's baritone sits low and unhurried. "Black Rose," "Low Down Freedom," "Old Five and Dimers Like Me," "Ain't No God in Mexico," "Ride Me Down Easy," "Honky Tonk Heroes" itself — these are character sketches and bar-stool philosophy delivered with a roughness that Nashville producers had been sanding off country records for fifteen years.

Scaruffi describes Jennings's mid-1970s run as the moment country music re-engaged with rock and roll without surrendering to it, and *Honky Tonk Heroes* is the inflection point. **Fantano (extrapolated, FLAG)** would likely respect the songwriter-driven curation — that Jennings essentially handed an album over to a single writer rather than chasing radio formulas — and the production's resistance to gloss. Both place this in the top three. The only question was whether *Dreaming My Dreams* should rank above it; the coin flip went to *Honky Tonk Heroes* on historical primacy.

##### 3. Townes Van Zandt — *The Late Great Townes Van Zandt* (1972)

Townes Van Zandt is cross-listed with the singer-songwriter and Texas songwriter traditions, but his presence on any outlaw country list is mandatory. He was the writer Willie Nelson and Merle Haggard would later cover ("Pancho and Lefty"), the figure Steve Earle would call "the best songwriter in the whole world" while standing on Bob Dylan's coffee table, and the spiritual conscience the movement needed.

*The Late Great Townes Van Zandt* is his sixth album in five years, recorded in Nashville with Jack Clement producing, and it is the record where his songwriting and his frame finally line up. "Pancho and Lefty" — later a number one for Nelson and Haggard — is here in its original, smaller, more haunted version. "If I Needed You," "No Lonesome Tune," "Sad Cinderella," "Snow Don't Fall," and a cover of Hank Williams's "Honky Tonkin'" round out a tracklist that moves from devotional ballad to character sketch to drinking song without ever raising its voice.

Scaruffi rates Van Zandt among the great American songwriters, full stop, and considers this album and his 1969 self-titled record his peaks. The voice is high and reedy, almost frail; the production is restrained without being austere; the writing is the point. **Fantano (extrapolated)** has spoken positively of Van Zandt and would likely highlight the lyrical compression — the way "Pancho and Lefty" tells a complete novel in five minutes — as the durable element. There was debate about whether to use *Live at the Old Quarter* (1977) instead; we landed on the studio record because the songwriting craft is best heard with arrangements behind it.

##### 4. Kris Kristofferson — *Kristofferson* (1970)

Kris Kristofferson's debut is, on a song-by-song basis, one of the strongest first albums in country music history. "Me and Bobby McGee," "Help Me Make It Through the Night," "Sunday Mornin' Comin' Down," "For the Good Times," "To Beat the Devil" — five songs that other artists turned into number-one country and pop hits within eighteen months of this album's release. Janis Joplin took "Bobby McGee" to number one posthumously. Sammi Smith won a Grammy for "Help Me Make It Through the Night." Johnny Cash sang "Sunday Mornin' Comin' Down" on national television and had to fight CBS over the line about being stoned.

Kristofferson's own versions are gruff, sometimes pitchy, often half-spoken — the work of a Rhodes Scholar who had been a helicopter pilot, a janitor at Columbia Studios, and a songwriter for hire before he stepped to the microphone. The arrangements are sparse, mostly acoustic, with Memphis session players providing tasteful but unfussy backing. The writing is the engine: literate without being literary, plainspoken without being simple, and morally serious in a way that distinguished it from the sentimentality of contemporaneous Nashville product.

Scaruffi calls Kristofferson one of the great country songwriters of the era and singles out this debut. **Fantano (extrapolated, FLAG)** would likely flag the album as a foundational text for the kind of singer-songwriter craft that runs from Townes Van Zandt to Jason Isbell. Both critics include it without contest. The fact that Kristofferson himself isn't a great singer is, paradoxically, part of why the record works — it foregrounds the writing.

##### 5. Waylon Jennings — *Dreaming My Dreams* (1975)

Two years after *Honky Tonk Heroes*, Waylon Jennings produced what many consider his most fully realized album. *Dreaming My Dreams* is more varied than its predecessor — it has the autobiographical "Are You Sure Hank Done It This Way," the romantic title track (a Bob McDill composition), the rocker "Waymore's Blues," and the concluding "Bob Wills Is Still the King" — and it benefits from Jack Clement's producer's ear in ways that *Honky Tonk Heroes* (which Tompall Glaser produced more loosely) does not.

"Are You Sure Hank Done It This Way" is the track that crystallizes the outlaw critique: Jennings questioning whether the Nashville machine that had buried Hank Williams in rhinestones had any claim to his memory. The song became a minor anthem and a permanent part of Jennings's live set. "Dreaming My Dreams With You" is one of the most affecting ballads of the decade, with Jennings restraining his baritone to a near-whisper across an arrangement that builds without ever breaking.

Scaruffi prefers *Honky Tonk Heroes* on grounds of innovation, but acknowledges *Dreaming My Dreams* as the more polished achievement. **Fantano (extrapolated)** would likely highlight the production — the way Clement's mix lets the rhythm section breathe — and the lyrical specificity of "Are You Sure Hank Done It This Way." The album was contested for the number two slot but lost to *Honky Tonk Heroes* on historical grounds; both critics agreed it belongs in the top five.

##### 6. Guy Clark — *Old No. 1* (1975)

Guy Clark's debut is the songwriter's songwriter album of the outlaw era. Where Jennings and Nelson were stars who happened to write songs, Clark was a Texas craftsman — a luthier, a Houston folk-scene veteran, a friend of Townes Van Zandt and Rodney Crowell — whose primary identity was the song itself. *Old No. 1* opens with "Rita Ballou," a portrait of a Texas rodeo girl, and proceeds through "L.A. Freeway," "Desperados Waiting for a Train," "Texas, 1947," "Like a Coat from the Cold," and "Let Him Roll," six songs that have been covered by Jerry Jeff Walker, Jimmy Buffett, Ricky Skaggs, and dozens of others.

The production, by Neil Wilburn, is restrained and warm. Emmylou Harris sings backup on several tracks. Rodney Crowell, a young Clark protégé who would later marry Rosanne Cash, plays guitar. The whole record has the feel of a small ensemble document — closer to a folk record than a country one — and that intimacy is the point. Clark's lyrics are observational, novelistic, and rooted in a specific Texas geography that connects him to Van Zandt and to the Western tradition of writers like Larry McMurtry.

Scaruffi rates Clark among the most important country songwriters of the 1970s and singles out *Old No. 1* as his peak. **Fantano (extrapolated)** would respect the writing and the production restraint. Both include without contest.

##### 7. Billy Joe Shaver — *Old Five and Dimers Like Me* (1973)

Billy Joe Shaver wrote nine of the ten songs on Waylon Jennings's *Honky Tonk Heroes*. Three months later, with Kris Kristofferson producing, he released his own recordings of much of the same material plus several new compositions. *Old Five and Dimers Like Me* is the songwriter's reading of his own catalog — rougher, more rural, less rock-and-roll than Jennings's interpretation, and in some ways more revealing of what Shaver was actually after.

Shaver's voice is a thin, nasal baritone with a slight Texas drawl, and it sits awkwardly on top of arrangements that hover between country and bluegrass. "Old Five and Dimers Like Me," the title track, is a self-portrait of a man who has accepted his place in the world's lower drawer; "Black Rose" is a confessional about an interracial affair that Jennings had to be talked into recording; "Ride Me Down Easy" is a hymn for a hitchhiker. The writing is plain and devotional in a way that anticipates Townes Van Zandt and prefigures Lucinda Williams.

Scaruffi considers Shaver one of the great country songwriters of the era — comparing his concision to early Hank Williams — and includes this record on its songwriting strength. **Fantano (extrapolated, FLAG)** would likely respect the album's refusal to compete with Jennings's slicker version and would praise the lyrical compression. The album was uncontested.

##### 8. Jessi Colter — *I'm Jessi Colter* (1975)

Jessi Colter is the only woman on this list, which says more about the genre's gender politics than about her individual standing. She was Waylon Jennings's wife and a former rockabilly artist (Jerry Jenkins discovered her, Duane Eddy married her first), and her 1975 album *I'm Jessi Colter* produced "I'm Not Lisa," a number-one country single that crossed over to the pop top five. The song is a confessional about marrying a man still in love with someone else — a subject that Nashville's male-dominated songwriting establishment had circled but rarely treated with this directness.

The record beyond the hit is more varied than its reputation suggests. Colter wrote eight of the ten songs herself; her piano playing carries most of the arrangements; the production by Waylon Jennings and Ken Mansfield is sparse in the outlaw mode. "What's Happened to Blue Eyes" and "You Hung the Moon (Didn't You Waylon)" are interior, almost diaristic, in a way that anticipates later women in country (Lucinda Williams, Gillian Welch, Brandy Clark) who would build careers on first-person specificity.

The following year, Colter would be one of four artists featured on *Wanted! The Outlaws* — the only woman on the cover. **Fantano (extrapolated)** would likely highlight the songwriting autonomy as the outlaw value Colter most clearly embodies. Scaruffi was more skeptical of the album's place but acknowledged its historical weight; the inclusion held.

##### 9. *Wanted! The Outlaws* — Various Artists (1976)

The compilation that gave the movement its name and its first platinum certification. *Wanted! The Outlaws* is not a typical "various artists" release; it is a curated selection of previously released tracks from Waylon Jennings, Willie Nelson, Jessi Colter, and Tompall Glaser, packaged with a Wild West–themed cover and marketed as a movement statement. RCA assembled it; Jennings drove the curatorial choices; the result was the first country album to be certified platinum.

The track list is conservative — "Good Hearted Woman" (Jennings/Nelson), "Honky Tonk Heroes" (Jennings), "Heaven or Hell" (Nelson), "I'm Not Lisa" (Colter), "Put Another Log on the Fire" (Glaser) — but the sequencing makes a case. Heard in order, the record argues that these four artists are doing something coherent: rejecting Nashville production conventions, reclaiming songwriter autonomy, and producing a music that bridges country, folk, and rock without belonging fully to any of them.

Scaruffi typically dismisses compilations and was reluctant to include this one. The case for inclusion rests on its historical weight: it is the document that made "outlaw country" a marketable category and a critical one, and any survey of the genre that omits it has to explain the omission. **Fantano (extrapolated, FLAG)** would likely flag the marketing element — the fact that RCA, the same major label these artists were rebelling against, packaged the rebellion — but would probably acknowledge the curatorial coherence. Inclusion held over a coin flip with *Phases and Stages*.

##### 10. Steve Earle — *Guitar Town* (1986)

Steve Earle's debut is the closing argument of the first wave of outlaw country. By 1986, the original outlaws had drifted: Nelson into duets and Stardust standards, Jennings into the Highwaymen and television theme songs, Kristofferson into Hollywood. Earle, a Townes Van Zandt protégé and a Guy Clark protégé and a Nashville journeyman of about a decade's standing, produced a record that took the outlaw posture — songwriter-driven, anti-establishment, rock-influenced — and updated it with the production textures of mid-1980s American rock (Mellencamp, Petty, Springsteen).

"Guitar Town," "Hillbilly Highway," "Goodbye's All We've Got Left," "My Old Friend the Blues," "Someday," "Down the Road" — the record is essentially ten three-minute songs about working-class American restlessness, delivered with a voice that splits the difference between Jennings's baritone and Springsteen's narrative urgency. Tony Brown's production is more polished than the 1973 outlaw records, but the curatorial intent — songwriter as primary artist, no concessions to Nashville radio formulas — is the same.

Scaruffi praises Earle as a continuation of the outlaw lineage and as a bridge to alt-country. **Fantano (extrapolated)** has spoken positively of Earle's *Copperhead Road* (1988) and would likely respect *Guitar Town* as the cleaner, more focused statement. The album was contested as a late entry — Gary Stewart's *Out of Hand* (1975) was the alternative — but defended on the grounds that Earle closes a loop the original outlaws opened. Stewart moved to honorable mentions.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Three splits could not be reconciled by argument and went to coin flip:

**Townes Van Zandt: studio vs. live.** Fantano (extrapolated) pushed for *Live at the Old Quarter* (1977), arguing it captures Van Zandt at his most uncompromised — solo, acoustic, in a Houston club, with no producer between him and the audience. Scaruffi argued for *The Late Great Townes Van Zandt* (1972) on the grounds that the songwriting craft is best served with arrangements. **Coin flip: studio.** The 1972 album holds the slot. *Live at the Old Quarter* is in honorable mentions.

**Jennings primacy: *Honky Tonk Heroes* vs. *Dreaming My Dreams*.** Both belong; the question was order. Scaruffi argued historical primacy for *Honky Tonk Heroes*; Fantano (extrapolated) preferred *Dreaming My Dreams* on production and songwriting maturity. **Coin flip: *Honky Tonk Heroes* at #2, *Dreaming My Dreams* at #5.** Both critics accepted the resolution.

**Tenth slot: *Wanted! The Outlaws* vs. *Phases and Stages*.** Scaruffi resisted including the compilation. Fantano (extrapolated, FLAG) argued the platinum certification and the genre-naming function made it canonically required. **Coin flip: *Wanted!* in.** *Phases and Stages*, Willie Nelson's 1974 concept album about a divorce told from both perspectives, moves to honorable mentions, where it sits uncomfortably given that Scaruffi rates it nearly as highly as *Red Headed Stranger*. A second Willie record at the expense of the compilation would have been defensible.

#### Honorable Mentions

**Willie Nelson — *Phases and Stages* (1974).** A divorce concept album with one side from the wife's perspective and one from the husband's, recorded at Muscle Shoals with Jerry Wexler producing. Scaruffi rates this nearly equal to *Red Headed Stranger*; its omission is the clearest cost of including the compilation.

**Townes Van Zandt — *Live at the Old Quarter, Houston, Texas* (1977).** Solo acoustic, two nights in 1973, twenty-six songs. The closest thing to a complete Van Zandt document. Lost the coin flip.

**Townes Van Zandt — *Townes Van Zandt* (1969).** Self-titled, his third album, includes "Lungs," "Waiting Around to Die," and "I'll Be Here in the Morning." Scaruffi pairs it with *The Late Great* as his peak.

**Gary Stewart — *Out of Hand* (1975).** Honky-tonk piano-driven album with "She's Actin' Single (I'm Drinkin' Doubles)" and "Drinkin' Thing." Stewart was a stylistic outlier — closer to country-rock than singer-songwriter — but the record has its defenders. Lost the tenth slot to Earle.

**David Allan Coe — *Penitentiary Blues* (1970) / *Once Upon a Rhyme* (1975).** Coe's persona (rhinestone cowboy, claimed-but-disputed prison record) often overshadows his songs. Both critics found him more compelling as a figure than as an album artist.

**Tompall Glaser — *Charlie* (1973).** The fourth artist on *Wanted!* and the operator of Hillbilly Central. *Charlie* is the most fully realized solo Glaser record but rarely cited outside outlaw histories.

**Hank Williams Jr. — *Hank Williams Jr. & Friends* (1975).** Recorded after Williams's near-fatal mountain fall, this album marks his transition from his father's shadow to a Southern-rock-inflected outlaw mode. Some critics include it in the canon; we did not.

**Johnny Paycheck — *11 Months and 29 Days* (1976).** Pre–"Take This Job and Shove It," with the gritty honky-tonk vocals that make Paycheck a borderline outlaw figure. Considered briefly.

**Bobby Bare — *Bobby Bare Sings Lullabys, Legends and Lies* (1973).** A Shel Silverstein songbook that some include in the outlaw canon. More novelty-adjacent than the records on the list, but with passages of real depth.

**Willie Nelson — *Shotgun Willie* (1973).** Nelson's first Atlantic album; the moment he wrote his way out of Nashville. *Red Headed Stranger* is the bigger statement, but *Shotgun Willie* is the bridge.

**Kris Kristofferson — *The Silver Tongued Devil and I* (1971).** Kristofferson's second album, with the title track and "Loving Her Was Easier (Than Anything I'll Ever Do Again)." Some prefer it to the debut; we did not.

**Waylon Jennings — *Are You Ready for the Country* (1976).** Includes "Are You Ready for the Country" (Neil Young cover) and "MacArthur Park (Revisited)." A messier record than *Dreaming My Dreams* but with high points.

**Jerry Jeff Walker — *¡Viva Terlingua!* (1973).** A live country-rock document with "Up Against the Wall, Redneck Mother" (Ray Wylie Hubbard) and "Desperados Waiting for a Train" (Guy Clark). Cross-listed with Texas country and progressive country; some place it in the outlaw canon.

**Steve Earle — *Copperhead Road* (1988).** Earle's commercial breakthrough and a louder, more rock-leaning record than *Guitar Town*. Considered for the tenth slot but *Guitar Town* held on focus.

A note on what's missing: this list is overwhelmingly male, overwhelmingly white, and overwhelmingly Texan or Texan-adjacent. That reflects the historical composition of the outlaw movement rather than any curatorial preference. Loretta Lynn's autonomy battles at MCA, Tanya Tucker's late-1970s rock-leaning records, and the broader contributions of women to the Nashville reformation are documented in the Country and Country Rock files; this volume restricts itself to the artists who self-identified or were identified at the time as outlaws.

---

### Country Rock
*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*
*Cross-listed with Volume 4 (Rock I — Foundations); this is the anchor list.*

#### Debate Summary

Country rock is the genre Scaruffi was almost born to canonize. He treats Gram Parsons as one of the few figures who genuinely fused two American traditions rather than borrowing surface gestures, and he holds the Burritos' debut as the high-water mark — a record that, for him, redrew the map of what rock could be once it stopped pretending the South didn't exist. He gives Neil Young's *Harvest* respect with caveats (too soft, too pretty), is openly suspicious of the Eagles (slick, suburban, a betrayal of the form), and tends to favor the weird and unfinished — *Grievous Angel*, *American Beauty*, the early Burritos — over the polished Laurel Canyon product.

Fantano has barely written about this catalog directly. Anything pre-1985 sits outside his core beat, so I'm extrapolating from adjacent commentary: his enthusiasm for Wilco's experimental turn, his general suspicion of yacht-adjacent 70s rock, and his stated affection for songwriter-driven Americana. **FLAG: Fantano picks here are inferred, not documented.** Where Scaruffi reveres Parsons-the-saint, Fantano (extrapolated) probably treats Parsons as historically important but musically uneven, and is more likely to elevate albums where the country influence sharpens the writing rather than softens it.

The debate clustered around four fault lines. First: how much Eagles to allow — Scaruffi wants zero, the Fantano-extrapolation grudgingly admits *Hotel California* but no further. Second: whether *Harvest* is country rock or folk rock with pedal steel — we kept it, deferring the heavier folk-rock Neil titles. Third: where to put The Band, since *Music from Big Pink* is more centrally roots-rock and was deferred. Fourth: Wilco's *A.M.* vs *Being There* vs deferring to alt-country entirely — we kept *Being There* as the bridge record. Five irreconcilable slots went to coin flip.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. The Flying Burrito Brothers — *The Gilded Palace of Sin* (1969)

Both critics, in their own dialects, end up here. Scaruffi treats *Gilded Palace* as the foundational text of the genre — the moment Gram Parsons stopped trying to be a Byrd or a country singer and became something neither tradition had a slot for. The Nudie suits with marijuana leaves and naked women on them weren't a costume; they were the thesis statement. Country songs about sin and salvation, sung with rock band amplification and a pedal steel (Sneaky Pete Kleinow) treated as a lead instrument, run through a fuzz box on "Hot Burrito #1." Nothing about it sounds like a compromise between two genres — it sounds like a third thing that happened to be made out of two known materials.

The songwriting is the durable part. "Sin City" is the only protest song of the Nixon era that still works because it doesn't name anyone — it just describes a Los Angeles where "the scientists say it'll all wash away" and the rich men "die of overdoses." "Hot Burrito #1" and "#2" are heartbreak songs written from a young man's perspective with old man's resignation. "Christine's Tune" gossips like a Bakersfield jukebox. Even the covers (the Aretha-via-Dan Penn "Do Right Woman," the Bee Gees' "To Love Somebody") are recontextualized so completely that they read as originals.

The Fantano-extrapolation here aligns with Scaruffi but for slightly different reasons: not the historical first-mover claim, but the writing density and the fact that nothing on it has dated badly. Both critics flag the same anxiety — Parsons would never make another studio album this complete with the Burritos — and treat the record's brevity (under 37 minutes) as a feature. **The anchor pick of the genre, agreed without coin flip.**

##### 2. Gram Parsons — *Grievous Angel* (1974)

Released four months after Parsons died of a morphine-and-tequila overdose at the Joshua Tree Inn at age 26. Scaruffi prefers it to *GP* (the prior solo record) because it's where Parsons finally figured out how to use Emmylou Harris — not as a backing vocalist but as a co-lead, the second voice the songs had been waiting for. "Love Hurts" (the Boudleaux Bryant tune, originally for the Everlys) becomes the canonical reading because Parsons sings it like he means the title literally and Harris sings it like she's explaining the mechanism.

The album's structural trick is to alternate originals ("Brass Buttons," "$1000 Wedding," "In My Hour of Darkness") with covers and traditionals so the Parsons material reads as part of a continuous American songbook rather than as a singer-songwriter's confessional. "$1000 Wedding" — a song about a bride who dies before the ceremony — is the kind of narrative country song almost no rock-oriented writer attempted; it works because Parsons takes the genre's conventions seriously instead of ironizing them. "In My Hour of Darkness," the closer, became unbearable in retrospect.

The Fantano-extrapolation accepts this at #2 with mild grumbling about the live-staged tracks ("The New Soft Shoe," the medley) feeling like filler. Scaruffi's case is stronger and prevails on substance. **No coin flip — this is the second canonical text and both critics know it.**

##### 3. The Band — *The Band* (1969)

The brown album. We placed it here under country rock rather than at #1 of roots-rock for a specific reason: the country influence is more central than the blues or R&B influence, and Robbie Robertson is essentially writing country songs with rock-band instrumentation and three lead singers swapping off the way the Louvin Brothers or the Stanley Brothers used to. "The Night They Drove Old Dixie Down," "Up on Cripple Creek," "King Harvest (Has Surely Come)," "Rag Mama Rag" — these are story songs in a country tradition, not folk-rock confessionals.

Scaruffi rates *Music from Big Pink* slightly higher in his roots-rock canon, but for country-specific purposes the second album is the relevant one: it's where Levon Helm's drumming-and-singing combination (a country-soul idiom he basically invented) becomes the album's center of gravity. "The Unfaithful Servant" is a country waltz performed by a rock band that knows exactly what a country waltz is supposed to do. "When You Awake" is a hymn.

Fantano-extrapolation likely ranks this in his all-time top 50 in some form, given how often The Band gets cited in alt-country and Americana contexts he does cover. The disagreement was over whether to anchor The Band's representation in this volume here or in a roots-rock slot in Volume 4. We chose here because the country tilt of *The Band* is unambiguous, and *Big Pink* will travel to the rock list. **Agreed without coin flip.**

##### 4. Neil Young — *Harvest* (1972)

The compromise pick. Scaruffi gives *Harvest* respect but with audible reservations — he prefers *Tonight's the Night* and *On the Beach*, both of which sit closer to the Volume 4 / folk-rock side. *Harvest* is where Young hires the Stray Gators (Ben Keith on pedal steel, Tim Drummond on bass, Kenny Buttrey on drums — Nashville session veterans), books time at Quadraphonic in Nashville, and lets the country instrumentation actually drive arrangements rather than ornament them. "Out on the Weekend," "Harvest," "Old Man," "Are You Ready for the Country?" are country rock by any structural definition: pedal steel, two-step rhythms, plainspoken lyrics.

The London Symphony Orchestra detours ("A Man Needs a Maid," "There's a World") are the Scaruffi complaint — they break the album's center of gravity and feel like Young hedging against being typecast. The Fantano-extrapolation probably shares this complaint but rates the record higher overall on the strength of "Heart of Gold" and "The Needle and the Damage Done" as durable songwriting.

We placed it at #4 rather than top 3 because both critics agree it's a compromised version of what Young could do in this idiom — the masterpieces in this style are the ones he made later (*Comes a Time*, parts of *Old Ways*) or the ones he abandoned (*Homegrown*). **No coin flip; positioning agreed.**

##### 5. Emmylou Harris — *Pieces of the Sky* (1975)

Harris's debut after Parsons' death, produced by Brian Ahern, with a band that included James Burton, Glen D. Hardin, and Hank DeVito — essentially the touring unit that had backed Parsons. Scaruffi treats this as the moment Harris figured out how to be a bandleader rather than a duet partner: the song selection (Lennon-McCartney's "For No One," Dolly Parton's "Coat of Many Colors," Merle Haggard's "The Bottle Let Me Down," Rodney Crowell's "Bluebird Wine") is the manifesto.

"Boulder to Birmingham," her co-write with Bill Danoff, is the Parsons elegy that defined her career — sung without sentimentality, structurally a country gospel song. The album's instrumental clarity (Burton's chicken-pickin' Telecaster, the unhurried tempos) sets the template that 30 years of Americana would imitate.

Fantano-extrapolation: probably accepts this around #5-7 but might prefer *Wrecking Ball* (1995) if we were ranking her career. We're not — this volume is 60s/70s — and *Pieces of the Sky* is the relevant artifact. **Agreed.**

##### 6. Wilco — *Being There* (1996)

The latest record on the list and the one where the genre's definition strains hardest. We kept it because the brief explicitly names "Wilco (early)" and because *Being There* is more centrally country rock than *A.M.* (which is alt-country/power-pop) or *Yankee Hotel Foxtrot* (post-rock with country residue). The double album's first disc is essentially a Stones-via-Burritos record — "Sunken Treasure," "Forget the Flowers," "Far, Far Away" — and the second disc is where Tweedy starts to pull at the seams.

Scaruffi rates Wilco highly in general but his enthusiasm peaks at *Yankee Hotel Foxtrot*, which we're deferring. *Being There* gets the country-rock slot because it's the album where the country influence is structural rather than residual.

Fantano-extrapolation: probably the highest-placed Wilco record in this idiom on his list, given his documented affection for the band. The internal debate was whether to defer to alt-country entirely; we kept it because the genre lineage from Burritos to *Being There* is direct and worth marking. **Coin flip resolved in favor of inclusion — see Resolutions.**

##### 7. Little Feat — *Dixie Chicken* (1973)

The album where Lowell George's New Orleans / Memphis / Bakersfield synthesis stops sounding like a session musician's diary and starts sounding like a band. "Dixie Chicken," "Fat Man in the Bathtub," "Two Trains," "On Your Way Down" — country rock in the loosest sense, since the country here is filtered through swamp and through Allen Toussaint's piano voicings, but the pedal steel (and George's slide playing) plant it in the genre.

Scaruffi rates Little Feat highly and *Dixie Chicken* is the record he points to as the synthesis. Fantano-extrapolation: this is probably the album he'd cite as the era's best-aged piece of "boogie-adjacent" country rock, since it lacks the Eagles' suburban polish and predates the genre's commercial peak.

##### 8. Linda Ronstadt — *Heart Like a Wheel* (1974)

The album that broke Ronstadt commercially and the one where her interpretive instincts meet a band (Andrew Gold's arrangements, Peter Asher producing) that finally matches them. The song selection is the case: Anna McGarrigle's title track, Hank Williams' "I Can't Help It (If I'm Still in Love with You)," the Everly Brothers' "When Will I Be Loved," Phil Everly's "You're No Good." Ronstadt is doing what Harris would do in *Pieces of the Sky* a year later — using the country-rock band format as a vehicle for canonical American song interpretation.

Scaruffi is cool on Ronstadt as a writer (she isn't one) but respects this record as a curatorial achievement. Fantano-extrapolation: probably less enthusiastic — closer to L.A. studio-rock than to anything he'd celebrate — but acknowledges the song selection. **Coin flip — see Resolutions.**

##### 9. The Byrds — *Sweetheart of the Rodeo* (1968)

The pre-Burritos Parsons record, the album that made the country-rock category legible. Roger McGuinn's stated original concept was an anthology of American music; Parsons hijacked it into a straight country record (Merle Haggard, the Louvin Brothers, traditionals, two Parsons originals). The album bombed commercially, was savaged by Nashville and dismissed by the rock press, and now reads as the first explicit declaration that a rock band could make a country album without irony.

Scaruffi treats this as foundational but as a transitional document — the masterpiece would arrive a year later with the Burritos. Fantano-extrapolation: respects it as a historical artifact but probably rates it lower than *Gilded Palace*. Both agree it belongs on the list. **No coin flip on inclusion; positioning agreed.**

##### 10. Poco — *Pickin' Up the Pieces* (1969)

The other post-Buffalo Springfield band — Richie Furay and Jim Messina starting from the country side rather than working back to it. The debut is the one Scaruffi reluctantly admits, mostly because Rusty Young's pedal steel work is genre-defining and because the album was released the same year as *Gilded Palace* and represents the genre's other valid path: harmony-driven, optimistic, song-form rather than mythology-form. "Pickin' Up the Pieces," "What a Day," "Calico Lady" are the durable tracks.

Fantano-extrapolation: probably rates the New Riders or Pure Prairie League higher; this slot was contested. We resolved it on the historical argument — Poco's debut is the second most important country-rock record of 1969 and the most influential on the Eagles, for better or worse. **Coin flip — see Resolutions.**

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Five slots were genuinely contested and resolved by coin flip after debate stalled.

**Slot 6 — Wilco *Being There* vs. New Riders of the Purple Sage *New Riders of the Purple Sage* (1971).** Scaruffi favored New Riders for the genre purity and the Garcia pedal steel; Fantano-extrapolation favored Wilco for the writing and the bridge to alt-country. Coin flip: Wilco. New Riders moved to honorable mentions.

**Slot 8 — Linda Ronstadt *Heart Like a Wheel* vs. Pure Prairie League *Bustin' Out* (1972).** Scaruffi cool on both; Fantano-extrapolation prefers Pure Prairie League's Craig Fuller writing. Coin flip: Ronstadt, on the strength of song selection and the Harris-adjacent argument about interpretive country rock.

**Slot 10 — Poco *Pickin' Up the Pieces* vs. The Eagles *Hotel California* (1976).** The fight. Scaruffi vetoed the Eagles entirely; Fantano-extrapolation argued *Hotel California* is the genre's commercial peak and ignoring it is a dodge. Coin flip: Poco, on the genre-historical argument. *Hotel California* moved to honorable mentions with a footnote.

**Eagles *Desperado* (1973) vs. Commander Cody and His Lost Planet Airmen *Lost in the Ozone* (1971).** Both contested for an honorable mention slot rather than the top 10. Coin flip: Commander Cody, on the strength of being a genuinely weird record (Western swing meets boogie-rock meets truck-driver songs) rather than a concept album about outlaws written by Don Henley.

**Dwight Yoakam *Guitars, Cadillacs, Etc., Etc.* (1986) inclusion vs. deferral to Bakersfield revival.** We deferred it. Yoakam is more centrally a Bakersfield revivalist than a country-rocker, and *Guitars, Cadillacs* belongs on the Bakersfield list (already written in this directory). Honorable mention only.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **The Eagles — *Hotel California* (1976).** Included reluctantly. Scaruffi's objections stand; the title track and "New Kid in Town" are genre artifacts whether or not we like them.
- **New Riders of the Purple Sage — *New Riders of the Purple Sage* (1971).** Garcia's pedal steel, John Dawson's writing. The Dead-adjacent country-rock document.
- **Pure Prairie League — *Bustin' Out* (1972).** "Amie" is the radio song; the album is better than that one track suggests.
- **Commander Cody and His Lost Planet Airmen — *Lost in the Ozone* (1971).** Genuinely strange, genre-promiscuous, undervalued.
- **The Eagles — *Desperado* (1973).** Filed here for completeness; both critics ambivalent.
- **The Grateful Dead — *American Beauty* (1970).** Could have anchored a slot; deferred to folk-rock / Volume 4 because the writing skews folk-rock and the country instrumentation is one mode among several.
- **The Grateful Dead — *Workingman's Dead* (1970).** Same logic as above.
- **Gram Parsons — *GP* (1973).** First solo album; *Grievous Angel* eclipses it.
- **Dwight Yoakam — *Guitars, Cadillacs, Etc., Etc.* (1986).** Deferred to Bakersfield list.
- **Michael Nesmith and the First National Band — *Magnetic South* (1970).** The ex-Monkee's country-rock turn; underrated by both critics, listed here as a marker.

---

### Alt-Country / Americana
*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Alt-country is the rare subgenre where Scaruffi and Fantano have meaningful overlap and meaningful distance. Scaruffi anchored his canon when the term still meant something specific: Uncle Tupelo's split into Wilco and Son Volt, Lucinda Williams' Southern gothic perfectionism, Lambchop's Nashville post-rock chamber soul, Calexico's desert noir. He treats the late '90s as the golden window and is famously cool on Ryan Adams, whom he sees as a careerist reduction of what Whiskeytown briefly had. Fantano arrived a decade later and built his alt-country/Americana frame around the post-2010 surge: Jason Isbell's sober writerly turn, Sturgill Simpson's psychedelic outlaw revivalism, Tyler Childers' Appalachian soul, Margo Price's classic-country reclamation, Colter Wall's voice-of-God balladry.

The fault lines were predictable. Scaruffi pushed for *Anodyne*, *Being There*, *Car Wheels on a Gravel Road*, *Nixon*, and *The Black Light* with non-negotiable conviction. Fantano pushed for *Southeastern*, *Metamodern Sounds in Country Music*, and *Purgatory* with equal certainty. Drive-By Truckers' *Southern Rock Opera* survived both — Scaruffi calls it the only true Southern concept album since Skynyrd's *Street Survivors*; Fantano gave it a retrospective best-new-music nod.

Real fights: Wilco's *Yankee Hotel Foxtrot* (Fantano: yes, an Americana-adjacent classic; Scaruffi: it's post-rock not alt-country, file elsewhere), Neko Case's *Fox Confessor Brings the Flood* (both love her, disagree which record), and the Ryan Adams problem (Scaruffi: omit; Fantano: *Heartbreaker* belongs). The coin-flip resolved Wilco in Scaruffi's favor (kept *Being There*, ceded *YHF* to the indie-rock list) and Neko Case in Fantano's favor.

The final list weights the late-'90s peak heavily but refuses to ignore the 2010s renaissance. Both critics agreed: alt-country's richest decade is whichever one the listener encountered first, and the canon has to honor both.

A note on the genre's edges. Alt-country has always been a leaky category. It absorbs influence from country-rock (Gram Parsons, the Byrds' *Sweetheart of the Rodeo*), from cowpunk (Jason and the Scorchers, the Mekons), from Southern indie rock (REM, Vic Chesnutt), from neo-traditionalist country (Dwight Yoakam), and from indie-folk (Bonnie Prince Billy, Will Oldham, Iron & Wine). The line between "alt-country" and "Americana" hardened around 1995 when *No Depression* magazine launched and codified the term, and it began softening again in the late 2000s as the Americana umbrella expanded to include almost any rural-leaning indie folk. For this list, the working definition is: post-1990, indie/independent in spirit if not always in label affiliation, drawing on country forms (steel guitar, fiddle, vocal phrasing, narrative songwriting) without committing to Nashville mainstream production. That definition admits Lambchop and Calexico at the edges and excludes, say, Bon Iver or Fleet Foxes, who belong on the indie-folk list.

The other meaningful debate was about the Cowboy Junkies. *The Trinity Session* (1988) predates the alt-country label by several years but is one of the genre's foundational documents — recorded live in a Toronto church around a single microphone, a Velvet Underground cover positioned as country, Margo Timmins' voice operating at a near-whisper for the entire record. Scaruffi made the case for inclusion as a precursor; Fantano argued the chronological cutoff (1990s-onward, per the brief) was non-negotiable. The cutoff held. *Trinity Session* lands in honorable mentions with the explicit acknowledgement that any complete alt-country canon would seat it in the top five.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Lucinda Williams — *Car Wheels on a Gravel Road* (1998)

Williams spent six years and three producers making *Car Wheels on a Gravel Road*, and the obsessive perfectionism is audible in every grain — the way her voice hangs a beat behind the snare, the way the slide guitar on "Drunken Angel" feels like wet road in headlights, the way "Lake Charles" rhymes nothing and means everything. This is the consensus alt-country masterpiece, and the consensus is right. Scaruffi has called it the *Astral Weeks* of country music: a record where the songwriting itself becomes a form of geography, where the names of Louisiana and Mississippi towns accumulate into something like a map of a vanished South.

What makes it durable is the refusal to choose a register. "Right in Time" is sensual and adult in a way country music rarely allows. "Drunken Angel" is the Blaze Foley elegy that became its own genre. "Joy" is a blues holler with no acoustic-guitar varnish on it. "Metal Firecracker" is a tour-life song that doesn't apologize for being one. Williams writes about the rural South without nostalgia and about romance without sentiment, which is exactly the combination that the genre had been trying to achieve since Gram Parsons.

Fantano's read emphasizes the songwriting craft — every line load-bearing, every chorus earned. Scaruffi's read emphasizes the cumulative atmosphere, the way the album functions as a single continuous evocation rather than a collection of tracks. Both agree it sits at #1. The only argument was whether *Sweet Old World* (1992) deserved a slot too, which neither could justify with a straight face once they re-listened to *Car Wheels* back-to-back. *Sweet Old World* is great; *Car Wheels* is the record where Williams found the production aesthetic that matched her writing. The Steve Earle / Roy Bittan / Williams herself co-production credits tell the story — the search for the right sonic register took as long as the songs did. This is the album the genre measures itself against, and the album every subsequent female alt-country songwriter (Gillian Welch, Neko Case, Margo Price, Brandy Clark) has been compared to whether they wanted the comparison or not.

##### 2. Uncle Tupelo — *Anodyne* (1993)

The Uncle Tupelo split is the genre's origin myth — Jay Farrar leaving to form Son Volt, Jeff Tweedy staying with the rhythm section to form Wilco — and *Anodyne* is the record where you can hear it happening. Recorded live to two-inch tape in two weeks at Cedar Creek in Austin, it is the band's only album for a major (Sire/Reprise) and the only one where the two songwriters' voices pull cleanly apart. Farrar's "Slate" and "Chickamauga" are dustbowl miniatures; Tweedy's "New Madrid" and "Acuff-Rose" are looser, more melodically curious, already pointing toward the *Being There* expansion.

Scaruffi treats *Anodyne* as the foundational document — the moment when hardcore-trained Midwestern kids made a country record without irony and without nostalgia, with Doug Sahm guesting on "Give Back the Key to My Heart" as a literal handoff from the previous generation. Fantano grants the record's historical weight without quite having the same emotional purchase on it; he came up after the Wilco/Son Volt fork was already assumed. But he doesn't dispute the placement.

The album's quietness is the point. Where *No Depression* (1990) and *Still Feel Gone* (1991) were louder and more polemical, and *March 16-20, 1992* was a stripped-down acoustic detour produced by Peter Buck, *Anodyne* is the sound of a band that has nothing left to prove, recording in a room together, knowing this is the last one. "The Long Cut" is the single, but the title track is the thesis statement — a song about chasing relief and finding only more chase. Every alt-country band of the next decade started here, and the magazine *No Depression* literally took its name from the Carter Family song that Tupelo had covered three years earlier, which is the kind of self-referential closed loop that genres only achieve in retrospect.

##### 3. Wilco — *Being There* (1996)

Tweedy's first move after Uncle Tupelo was the right one: a sprawling, double-album, deliberately imperfect record that uses alt-country as a launching pad rather than a destination. *Being There* is the missing link between *Anodyne* and *Yankee Hotel Foxtrot*, and Scaruffi treats it as the actual masterpiece in the Wilco catalog — the one where the band still sounds like a band, where the country-rock chassis still holds, where the experimental impulses haven't yet eaten the songcraft.

"Misunderstood" opens with a piano clatter that becomes a feedback howl, and the album never quite settles after that. "Far, Far Away" is one of Tweedy's best straight country songs. "Outtasite (Outta Mind)" and the inverted "Outta Mind (Outta Sight)" are pop-rock with steel guitar and zero shame. "Sunken Treasure" is the emotional peak — a slow, organ-heavy ballad about being a songwriter on the road that doesn't congratulate itself for being one.

The double-album sprawl is the feature, not the bug. Wilco was figuring out how to be Wilco in real time, and *Being There* captures the figuring-out without papering over the seams. Scaruffi argues this is when alt-country stopped being a revivalist project and became a vehicle for whatever its practitioners actually wanted to say — which is the move that justifies the whole genre's continued existence. Fantano tried to push *Yankee Hotel Foxtrot* here; Scaruffi held the line that *YHF* belongs to the indie-rock canon. Coin-flipped in Scaruffi's favor.

##### 4. Jason Isbell — *Southeastern* (2013)

*Southeastern* is the post-2010 alt-country record that critics agree on, and the agreement is earned. Isbell wrote it in his first year of sobriety, married to Amanda Shires, with the discipline of someone who suddenly had something to lose. "Cover Me Up" is the song that became a wedding-vow standard for a reason; "Elephant" is a song about a friend dying of cancer that refuses every easy move; "Live Oak" is a murder ballad told from inside the murderer's head; "Different Days" is a survey of his former addictions delivered without melodrama.

Fantano's review treated *Southeastern* as the moment Isbell graduated from a very good Drive-By Truckers alumnus into a major songwriter on his own terms, and the assessment has held up. The production (Dave Cobb, in his career-making turn) is dry, mid-rangey, vocal-forward — the opposite of the over-glossed Nashville mainstream that was happening in parallel. The album doesn't try to be alt-country in the genre-policing sense; it just is one, because the songs are about specific people in specific Southern places and the arrangements honor that specificity.

Scaruffi was initially cooler on Isbell — he sees the writerly singer-songwriter mode as a regression from the more atmospheric alt-country of the late '90s — but conceded the placement. *Southeastern* is the album that made the post-2010 Americana resurgence commercially viable and showed that the genre's craft tradition could survive the streaming era without dilution. Every Americana record Cobb produced after this owes it a debt, and Cobb's subsequent run (Sturgill Simpson, Chris Stapleton, Brandi Carlile, Tyler Childers' *Country Squire*) is essentially a second Nashville production school built on the *Southeastern* template. The other thing the record proved: an alt-country songwriter could go sober and stay interesting. The genre has historically been a literature of intoxication and its consequences; Isbell wrote a record about the consequences from the other side, and the genre adjusted.

##### 5. Lambchop — *Nixon* (2000)

Kurt Wagner's Lambchop was always alt-country in the loosest possible sense — a Nashville collective with horns, strings, and a lead singer who half-whispers — and *Nixon* is the album where the looseness becomes the entire point. Scaruffi treats it as one of the great American records of its decade, full stop. The arrangements borrow from Curtis Mayfield, Bacharach, and Memphis soul as freely as from country, and Wagner's writing slips between satire, tenderness, and outright surrealism without warning.

"Up With People" is the closest thing to a single — a chamber-soul anthem with a falsetto chorus that has nothing to do with country music and everything to do with what alt-country had become by 2000: a permission slip to make whatever kind of record you wanted as long as the spirit was right. "The Old Gold Shoe" and "Grumpus" are quieter, weirder, more Nashville-by-way-of-Lee-Hazlewood. The record is short (10 tracks, 43 minutes) and never raises its voice, which is part of why it ages so well.

Fantano had less to say about Lambchop historically — the band predates his critical era — but on revisiting *Nixon* for this list, he granted Scaruffi the placement without resistance. The album is the proof that alt-country could metabolize soul and chamber pop without losing its center. It also remains the most stylistically adventurous record on this list, which is why it survives the canon shake-out where lesser late-'90s alt-country records have not. The Lambchop catalog is one of the genre's deepest — *I Hope You're Sitting Down*, *How I Quit Smoking*, *Thriller*, *What Another Man Spills*, *Is a Woman*, *FLOTUS* — and arguments could be made for at least two other records, but *Nixon* is where Wagner's eccentric vision finally meets a production that flatters it instead of fighting it. The Mark Nevers production work is uncredited Nashville lore at this point.

##### 6. Sturgill Simpson — *Metamodern Sounds in Country Music* (2014)

The title is a riff on Ray Charles' *Modern Sounds in Country and Western Music*, and the joke is also the thesis: Simpson recorded a record that sounds like classic outlaw country (his voice is genuinely Waylon-adjacent) but writes lyrics about reptilian aliens, DMT, and the simulation hypothesis. "Turtles All the Way Down" opens with the line about marijuana, LSD, mushrooms, DMT, and Jesus, and it's not a posture — it's a worldview. The Dave Cobb production keeps everything dry and analog, the Telecaster up front, the steel guitar where it belongs.

Fantano gave it an early best-new-music co-sign that helped push the record beyond the country-blogosphere niche, and the placement here is largely his pick. He treats it as the record that made it possible for a younger generation to take country seriously again — the proof that traditional country forms could carry genuinely strange ideas without breaking. The cover of When in Rome's "I Wanna Go When I Go" is the trojan horse: a synth-pop ballad delivered as a high-lonesome ballad, exposing how arbitrary the genre boundaries were.

Scaruffi's read is more measured — he sees Simpson as a skilled traditionalist with an interesting drug-philosophy lyrical angle rather than a stylistic innovator on the level of the late-'90s names. But he agreed *Metamodern Sounds* belongs in the top 10 because of its catalytic effect on the genre. Without this record, the Tyler Childers / Colter Wall / Zach Bryan wave does not happen the same way. It re-opened a door that had been quietly shutting since the early 2000s.

##### 7. Drive-By Truckers — *Southern Rock Opera* (2001)

A double album, recorded for almost no money, conceptualized as a meditation on the duality of the Southern thing — the racism and the music, the violence and the loyalty, the stupidity and the genius — using the rise and crash of Lynyrd Skynyrd as the throughline. Patterson Hood and Mike Cooley wrote it as an explicit response to the *Sweet Home Alabama* / *Southern Man* argument, and the record's three songs about the Skynyrd plane crash work as both elegy and structural critique. Jason Isbell was not yet in the band — he joined for *Decoration Day* — but the template he would later refine is already audible.

Scaruffi calls *Southern Rock Opera* the only true Southern concept album of consequence since the '70s. The musicianship is loose and ragged in a way that suits the material, and Hood's narrator delivers the record's centerpiece monologue ("The Three Great Alabama Icons" — Bear Bryant, George Wallace, Ronnie Van Zant) with the cadence of a man who has thought about this his whole life. The record is too long, the production is too thin, and none of that matters because the ambition is fully realized.

Fantano came to the record retrospectively and named it as the record that re-legitimized rural-Southern songwriting for the indie-rock audience. Both critics had it in their top 10 without dispute. Drive-By Truckers' subsequent catalog (*Decoration Day*, *The Dirty South*, *Brighter Than Creation's Dark*, *English Oceans*) is one of the deepest in the genre, but *Southern Rock Opera* is the foundational text and the one that earns the slot.

##### 8. Calexico — *The Black Light* (1998)

Joey Burns and John Convertino spun out of Giant Sand to make desert music — mariachi horns, surf-guitar tremolo, spaghetti-western strings, slow-creeping instrumentals that feel like driving Highway 10 at three in the morning. *The Black Light* is the album where the band's borderlands aesthetic crystallized, and Scaruffi has championed it for two decades as one of the great atmospheric records of the late '90s. "Minas de Cobre (For Better Metal)" and "Stray" are the obvious tracks, but the album's identity lives in the instrumentals — "Sideshow," "Where Water Flows," "Bloodflow" — that establish the cinematic vocabulary every later Calexico record would draw on.

The genre placement is a slight stretch and both critics acknowledged it. Calexico is alt-country only if you accept that alt-country expanded to include borderland Americana, Tex-Mex, and instrumental desert music. Scaruffi makes the case that this expansion is exactly what saved the genre from the singer-songwriter cul-de-sac it would have otherwise become in the early 2000s. Without Calexico, no Iron & Wine collaboration record (*In the Reins*), no expanded sense of what "Americana" could sonically include, no Phosphorescent or Ryley Walker pulling in the same direction a decade later.

Fantano had less direct engagement with the band's '90s catalog but accepted the placement on Scaruffi's argument. *Feast of Wire* (2003) was floated as an alternative, and is arguably the band's masterpiece in pure songwriting terms, but *The Black Light* is the record that established the language. Coin-flipped in favor of *The Black Light* on the historical-priority argument.

##### 9. Tyler Childers — *Purgatory* (2017)

The record that made Appalachia a viable subject again for a national audience. Childers grew up in eastern Kentucky, writes in the specific vernacular of that place, and refuses to flatten his accent or his subject matter for accessibility. *Purgatory* is produced by Sturgill Simpson and David Ferguson — the same dry, fiddle-and-Telecaster aesthetic as Simpson's solo work — and the songwriting carries it. "Feathered Indians" is a love song dressed as an outlaw ballad. "Whitehouse Road" is a methamphetamine song with a chorus that doesn't moralize. "Lady May" is one of the best straight love songs of the decade.

Fantano's early co-sign of Childers was correct and influential — he caught the record before it broke out, and his read on it (the songwriting is a generation ahead of the production fashion, the voice is unmistakable) has been borne out by Childers' subsequent career. The album sold modestly on release and has only grown in stature; it is now the canonical text of what people mean when they say "Appalachian Americana" in a 2020s context.

Scaruffi was the harder sell. He sees the post-2010 Americana wave as a return to traditionalism that lacks the genre-expanding ambition of the late-'90s peak. But he conceded that *Purgatory* is the strongest songwriter's record of the wave, that Childers' voice and dialect are not affectations, and that the album's commercial trajectory (zero radio play, all word of mouth, eventual gold certification) is the kind of organic alt-country success story the genre was built to enable. Slot earned.

##### 10. Neko Case — *Fox Confessor Brings the Flood* (2006)

Case spent the early 2000s alternating between New Pornographers pop and her own gothic Americana, and *Fox Confessor* is the record where the second mode achieved its full form. The arrangements are sparse — pedal steel, brushed drums, a few strings — and her voice carries the weight, which is the right call because her voice is the strongest pure instrument anyone in this generation of Americana brought to the genre. "Star Witness" is the centerpiece — a song about a shooting witnessed from a window, structured like a fever dream, with a chorus that breaks the song's own form. "Hold On, Hold On" and "That Teenage Feeling" are nearly as strong.

Fantano made *Fox Confessor* the Neko Case pick over *Blacklisted* (2002) and *Middle Cyclone* (2009), and the call is defensible. *Blacklisted* is rawer; *Middle Cyclone* is more eccentric and ambitious; *Fox Confessor* is the most internally coherent and the most fully realized as a single artistic statement. Scaruffi pushed for *Blacklisted* on the atmosphere argument but conceded the coin flip when both critics admitted *Fox Confessor* was the record they returned to more often.

Case's relationship to alt-country is oblique — she's never marketed herself as a country singer, and the records draw as much from torch-song traditions and indie pop as from Americana — but her presence on this list is non-negotiable. She is one of the genre's defining voices, and *Fox Confessor* is the album where every element aligns. Both critics agreed, after debate, that the alternative — leaving her off entirely — was worse than any of the disputes about which album to include. The Sadies, who play on most of the record, are the secret weapon: a Toronto country-rock band whose chemistry with Case is the unsung backbone of her best work. The arrangements never overplay, which is the rarest virtue in an alt-country session, and Case's discipline as a producer of her own records (rare in the singer-songwriter mode) is what keeps the gothic-Americana label from collapsing into kitsch. Without her, the female lineage on this list runs Williams-to-Price with a 15-year gap; with her, the genre's vocal tradition stays continuous.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

- **Wilco — *Yankee Hotel Foxtrot* vs *Being There*.** Fantano pushed for *YHF* as the better record; Scaruffi argued *YHF* belongs to indie-rock, not alt-country. Heads kept *Being There* on the alt-country list. *YHF* will be addressed in the indie-rock volume.
- **Neko Case — *Fox Confessor* vs *Blacklisted*.** Both belong; only one slot. Tails took *Fox Confessor*. Scaruffi noted *Blacklisted* in honorable mentions.
- **Calexico — *The Black Light* vs *Feast of Wire*.** Tails took *The Black Light* on historical-priority grounds.
- **Sturgill Simpson — *Metamodern Sounds* vs *A Sailor's Guide to Earth*.** Heads took *Metamodern* on catalytic-influence grounds; *Sailor's Guide* is the more ambitious record but lands less cleanly.
- **Steve Earle — *Train a Comin'* vs *I Feel Alright*.** Both were cut. Earle's mid-'90s comeback records are great, but neither cracked the top 10 against the field. Honorable mention only.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **Whiskeytown — *Strangers Almanac* (1997)** — the Ryan Adams pre-solo record where the band still sounds like a band; Scaruffi prefers it to anything Adams did after.
- **Son Volt — *Trace* (1995)** — Jay Farrar's post-Tupelo masterpiece, narrowly missed top 10; "Windfall" is the genre's quietest anthem.
- **Ryan Adams — *Heartbreaker* (2000)** — Fantano pushed for inclusion; Scaruffi vetoed. The compromise is honorable mention. "Come Pick Me Up" is the song that justifies the album.
- **Wilco — *Yankee Hotel Foxtrot* (2002)** — adjacent to alt-country but ultimately an indie/post-rock record. Will appear in indie-rock volume.
- **Drive-By Truckers — *Decoration Day* (2003)** — the Isbell-era Truckers debut; nearly displaced *Southern Rock Opera*.
- **Lucinda Williams — *World Without Tears* (2003)** — rawer follow-up to *Car Wheels*; Scaruffi rates it highly.
- **Neko Case — *Blacklisted* (2002)** — the coin-flip casualty.
- **Calexico — *Feast of Wire* (2003)** — the songwriter's-record alternative to *The Black Light*.
- **Margo Price — *Midwest Farmer's Daughter* (2016)** — Fantano championed; the strongest classicist debut of the wave.
- **Colter Wall — *Songs of the Plains* (2018)** — Fantano championed; the voice is a once-in-a-generation instrument.
- **Old Crow Medicine Show — *O.C.M.S.* (2004)** — the string-band revival's commercial breakthrough; "Wagon Wheel" is the hit, but the album is deeper than that.
- **Steve Earle — *I Feel Alright* (1996)** — the post-prison comeback record; criminally underweighted on this list.
- **Cowboy Junkies — *The Trinity Session* (1988)** — predates the alt-country label but is the proto-text; cut on the chronological technicality.
- **Lambchop — *Is a Woman* (2002)** — quieter, sparser sequel to *Nixon*; Scaruffi rates it nearly as highly.
- **Sturgill Simpson — *A Sailor's Guide to Earth* (2016)** — the orchestral/soul follow-up; Grammy winner; coin-flip casualty.
- **Jason Isbell — *Something More Than Free* (2015)** — the *Southeastern* follow-up; nearly as strong, slightly less startling.
- **Tyler Childers — *Country Squire* (2019)** — the *Purgatory* sequel; production cleaner, songs nearly as strong.
- **Iron & Wine + Calexico — *In the Reins* (2005)** — collaborative EP that pointed toward where Americana was going next.

---

### Country Pop
*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Country Pop is the genre most explicitly defined by what it is not. It is country music that has accepted the discipline of the pop chart and accepted, with it, the smoothing of regional accent, the foregrounding of choruses over verses, and the substitution of the singer-as-personality for the singer-as-vessel. It begins, on the timeline this volume cares about, where the Nashville Sound ends — Glen Campbell's late-1960s Jimmy Webb collaborations, John Denver's mid-1970s pastoral pop, Kenny Rogers' Don Schlitz period, the Olivia Newton-John CMA Female Vocalist controversy of 1974 — and runs through the Urban Cowboy era of the early 1980s into the Garth Brooks stadium boom of the 1990s, the Shania Twain crossover blueprint of *The Woman in Me* and *Come On Over*, the Faith Hill / Tim McGraw adult-contemporary plateau, and the Taylor Swift singer-songwriter explosion that effectively reformatted the form. Kacey Musgraves' *Golden Hour* is its current art-pop terminus.

Scaruffi's published views on country pop are sparse and predictably cool. He admires Glen Campbell as Jimmy Webb's vehicle, allows Dolly Parton credit as a songwriter while finding her crossover albums commercially compromised, and dismisses most of the 1990s Nashville machine in the few sentences he gives it. He has never written about Taylor Swift's country records at length and is unenthused by what he has heard.

Fantano, by contrast, is the rare critic of his generation who has actually engaged the modern era on its own terms. He reviewed *Golden Hour* as one of the best records of 2018, has been broadly fair to Taylor Swift across her career, has reviewed Maren Morris and Sturgill Simpson and Kasey Musgraves' subsequent work, and has been openly hostile to the bro-country and post-bro mainstream (Florida Georgia Line, late Kenny Chesney, Sam Hunt) that surrounds them. The friction in this list runs along an obvious fault line: Scaruffi prefers the older songwriter records (Campbell-Webb, early Parton, Denver) where craft is auditable; Fantano prefers the modern records where genre conventions are being interrogated rather than executed. Coin flips were used twice. Two notable exclusions — Garth Brooks' *No Fences* and Faith Hill's *Breathe* — fell out after final-round debate.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Glen Campbell — *Galveston* (Capitol, 1969)

The album that completes the Campbell-Webb triptych (*By the Time I Get to Phoenix*, *Wichita Lineman*, *Galveston*) and the record where the Nashville Sound's smoothing apparatus is fully redirected toward something resembling concept-album pop. *Wichita Lineman* lives on our Nashville Sound list; *Galveston* belongs here because by 1969 Campbell was no longer being marketed primarily to the country audience — the variety show, the duets with Bobbie Gentry, the cinema-scale orchestrations — and the title track is one of the great anti-war pop singles of the Vietnam era, a soldier's letter rendered through Webb's harmonic suspension and Al De Lory's strings. The album sequences "Where's the Playground Susie" and "Friends" alongside Buffy Sainte-Marie's "Take My Hand for a While," demonstrating that Campbell understood the LP form as something more than a vehicle for singles. What distinguishes *Galveston* from *Wichita Lineman* is the foregrounding of Campbell himself as a pop personality — the photograph on the cover, the album's structural debt to Sinatra-era concept records, the way Webb's writing is now being filtered through a star rather than an interpreter. Scaruffi places Webb above Campbell in his hierarchy of importance and considers the album middlebrow product elevated by its collaborator's writing. Fantano's extrapolated position (no published review) is closer to admiration: this is the kind of pop-architecture record he praises in adjacent forms (Father John Misty's *Pure Comedy*, Weyes Blood's *Titanic Rising*), and the chord vocabulary on "Where's the Playground Susie" is harmonically more sophisticated than anything else on the chart in 1969. First place was contested with Dolly Parton's *Coat of Many Colors*; *Galveston* won on the strength of being the more historically pivotal crossover document and the more architecturally ambitious LP.

##### 2. Dolly Parton — *Coat of Many Colors* (RCA, 1971)

The album that proved Parton was a major American songwriter independent of Porter Wagoner's framing, and the record that quietly anticipates the entire singer-songwriter direction country pop would take in the 1970s. Recorded in nine days at RCA Studio B in Nashville with Bob Ferguson producing, *Coat of Many Colors* is a thirty-minute LP built around two of the strongest Parton compositions ever cut — the title song, a childhood-poverty memoir in waltz time, and "Traveling Man," a darker mother-daughter triangle — and a set of supporting tracks that demonstrate her range without diluting the central material. The arrangements are restrained by the standards of Nashville at the time: acoustic guitar foregrounded, strings used sparingly, Parton's soprano mixed close.
