Scaruffi acknowledges Parton as a songwriter of genuine craft, ranks her above most of her commercial peers, and would place this album above her later, glossier crossover work (*Here You Come Again*, *9 to 5*) where production smoothness begins to obscure the writing. Fantano has not reviewed Parton at length but has cited her on multiple occasions as a foundational figure for the country-adjacent indie singer-songwriter tradition (Phoebe Bridgers, Big Thief, Mitski's country mode). The extrapolated Fantano position is unreserved respect, with the caveat that he prefers the songwriting purity of *Coat of Many Colors* to the more produced late-1970s crossover albums. Second place after a four-round debate; *Galveston* edged ahead on the strength of architectural ambition and Webb's outside writing being more historically pivotal than Parton's still-developing 1971 voice as a self-producing songwriter.

##### 3. Taylor Swift — *Red* (Big Machine, 2012)

The pivot point of the entire genre in the 21st century, and the album that proved country pop could absorb the production vocabulary of contemporary mainstream pop without abandoning the songwriting tradition that gave it its identity. *Red* is structurally a transitional record — Nathan Chapman produces the country-leaning material, Max Martin and Shellback handle "I Knew You Were Trouble" and "We Are Never Ever Getting Back Together," Jeff Bhasker contributes "Holy Ground" — and it is precisely this stylistic incoherence that made it formally important. The album's emotional center is "All Too Well" (later expanded to ten minutes on *Red (Taylor's Version)*), a five-minute autobiographical breakup narrative that may be the strongest pure piece of songwriting Swift has produced and that placed her, decisively, in the Joni Mitchell / Carole King lineage rather than the post-Shania mainstream country lineage. The country audience accepted *Red* as country; the pop audience accepted it as pop; the Grammy electorate, characteristically, was confused. Fantano's published review of *Red* was lukewarm at the time (a 5/10 in 2012), but his subsequent reassessments and his enthusiastic engagement with later Swift albums make clear that the original score has not aged well; the extrapolated current Fantano position would rate *Red* substantially higher. Scaruffi has not published on Swift and is presumed indifferent. Third place after debate centered on whether Swift's country period is best represented by *Fearless* (the more commercially pure country album) or *Red* (the more historically pivotal one). *Red* won on the strength of formal ambition and the canonical status of "All Too Well."

##### 4. Kacey Musgraves — *Golden Hour* (MCA Nashville, 2018)

The album that won Album of the Year at the 2019 Grammys and the country-pop record most explicitly engaged with the production vocabulary of contemporary art-pop. Produced by Daniel Tashian and Ian Fitchuk in a Nashville studio with vintage analog gear, *Golden Hour* sequences thirteen songs across thirty-eight minutes that cohere as a tonal piece — disco beat on "High Horse," yacht-rock on "Lonely Weekend," chamber-pop on "Slow Burn," near-ambient on "Mother" — and the country signifiers (steel guitar, fiddle, Nashville session players) are deployed as texture rather than as genre marker. Musgraves' songwriting on the album is more direct than the wordplay-heavy approach of *Pageant Material*; the lyrics on "Slow Burn" and "Rainbow" function more like Joni Mitchell late-1970s material than like contemporary Nashville. Fantano gave *Golden Hour* an 8/10 in 2018 and named it one of the best records of the year, with extended praise for the production and Musgraves' vocal control. Scaruffi has not reviewed it; his framework would likely register it as competent crossover product without identifying its genre-blending as innovation. Fourth place was Fantano's strongest individual advocacy in the debate, and Scaruffi conceded it on the strength of the Album of the Year award and the album's measurable influence on subsequent country-pop production (Taylor Swift's *Lover*, the entire 2019-2022 Maren Morris / Lainey Wilson cohort).

##### 5. Shania Twain — *Come On Over* (Mercury Nashville, 1997)

The best-selling country album of all time, the best-selling album by a female artist of any genre, and the record that defined the production template for late-1990s and early-2000s country pop. Produced by Mutt Lange (Twain's husband at the time) with the maximalist arena-rock vocabulary he had developed for AC/DC, Def Leppard, and Bryan Adams, *Come On Over* runs sixteen songs across sixty minutes and contains, by some accounting, twelve singles. The country signifiers are present (fiddle, steel guitar, Nashville session contributions) but they are subordinate to a glossier sonic architecture: layered backing vocals, chorus-heavy structures, drum production that owes more to Mutt Lange's hard-rock work than to anything Nashville had produced before. The album's commercial dominance reformatted what country radio expected from female artists for the next decade. Scaruffi is unsympathetic — this is, in his framework, the apotheosis of country's surrender to commercial pop and a record without serious aesthetic merit. Fantano has not reviewed it but has spoken of the late-1990s country-pop crossover era with measured respect for its craft if not its taste. The extrapolated Fantano position acknowledges *Come On Over* as a structurally important record while reserving aesthetic judgment. Fifth place after a coin flip resolved the debate between *Come On Over* and *The Woman in Me* (1995); *Come On Over* won on commercial scale and the breadth of its singles run.

##### 6. John Denver — *Poems, Prayers & Promises* (RCA, 1971)

The album that made Denver a star and that contains "Take Me Home, Country Roads," the song that more than any other established the pastoral-pop strain of country crossover that would later run through Garth Brooks and Lonestar. Produced by Milt Okun, *Poems, Prayers & Promises* is a folk-pop record that gets classified as country pop largely on the strength of the title song, "Country Roads," and Denver's vocal placement — high tenor, conversational, almost confessional — which would become a foundational sound for the soft strain of country pop. The album is uneven: alongside the canonical material there are several earnest folk-revival exercises that have aged less well, and the production is dated by the standards of contemporaneous Glen Campbell records. But "Country Roads" is the country-pop national anthem in a sense few other songs are, and Denver's restrained, nasal-tenor delivery established a vocal template that runs through Vince Gill, Don Henley's country mode, and the entire 1990s adult-contemporary country boom. Scaruffi finds Denver bland and dismisses him in a sentence. Fantano has not engaged Denver substantially but his published taste in adjacent folk-pop (Nick Drake, Townes Van Zandt) suggests cool engagement at best. Sixth place was contested with Kenny Rogers' *The Gambler* and won on the strength of "Country Roads" being a more pivotal genre-defining single than anything Rogers produced.

##### 7. Kenny Rogers — *The Gambler* (United Artists, 1978)

The album that ratified Kenny Rogers as the dominant country-pop crossover artist of the late 1970s and the record that contains the title song, a Don Schlitz composition that became the defining country single of the decade and a top-twenty pop hit. Produced by Larry Butler in Nashville with a roster of session players that includes pretty much everyone who mattered on Music Row at the time, *The Gambler* is structurally a singles album — "The Gambler," "I Wish That I Could Hurt That Way Again," and a competent reading of "She Believes in Me" — with supporting material that is craftsman-like rather than memorable. Rogers' baritone, weathered and conversational, was the ideal vehicle for narrative-driven Schlitz material, and the album's success at country radio and at adult-contemporary pop established the template that would run through *Coward of the County* (1979) and *Lady* (1980). Scaruffi is unimpressed: this is, on his ear, professional product without serious merit. Fantano has not reviewed Rogers but has cited "The Gambler" approvingly in passing as a piece of songwriting. Seventh place after debate centered on whether *The Gambler* or Crystal Gayle's *We Must Believe in Magic* (1977) better represented the late-1970s country-pop moment; Rogers won on the strength of the title song's cultural permanence.

##### 8. Lee Ann Womack — *I Hope You Dance* (MCA Nashville, 2000)

The country-pop crossover that won three Grammys, sold three million copies, and quietly stands as the most aesthetically defensible mainstream country record of the early 2000s. Produced by Mark Wright and Frank Liddell, *I Hope You Dance* is built around the title song (a Mark D. Sanders / Tia Sillers composition that became a wedding-and-graduation perennial) but its real strength is the deep cuts: "Lonely Too" and "Why They Call It Falling" demonstrate Womack's capacity for country-traditional phrasing inside contemporary production, and the album's sequencing reveals a more thoughtful sensibility than the singles-driven Faith Hill and Jo Dee Messina records of the same period. Womack is, in retrospect, the missing link between the 1990s neotraditional revival and the 2010s alt-country renaissance — her later *There's More Where That Came From* (2005) and *The Way I'm Livin'* (2014) make the case for her as a serious traditionalist working inside the pop apparatus. Fantano has not reviewed Womack but has spoken approvingly of the early-2000s country-pop moment as preferable to what followed. Scaruffi has not engaged her at all. Eighth place was a Fantano advocacy pick: Womack as the underweighted peak of mainstream country pop in its final coherent decade before the bro-country apocalypse.

##### 9. Faith Hill — *Faith* (Warner Bros., 1998)

The album where the country-pop crossover template Shania Twain had just established was refined and made more emotionally legible. Produced by Byron Gallimore and Dann Huff with Hill's contributions, *Faith* contains "This Kiss" (a Robin Lerner / Annie Roboff / Beth Nielsen Chapman composition that became a pop-radio crossover) and "Let Me Let Go," alongside more country-traditional material that grounds the album in Nashville rather than ceding it entirely to pop. Hill's voice — clean, controlled, with a slight country break that Twain's voice lacked — was the most technically accomplished of the late-1990s crossover wave, and the album's production is more transparent than the maximalist Mutt Lange approach: strings and synth pads are deployed as texture rather than as architecture, and the country signifiers (steel guitar, fiddle on "The Secret of Life") read as choices rather than as marketing. Scaruffi is uninterested. Fantano has not engaged Hill substantially. Ninth place was the most contested call in the lower half of the list — *Faith* defeated Trisha Yearwood's *Where Your Road Leads* (1998) and Reba McEntire's *Read My Mind* (1994) by margins that came down to commercial scale and the sustained quality of the singles run. Coin flip resolved against *Read My Mind* in the final round.

##### 10. Taylor Swift — *Fearless* (Big Machine, 2008)

The second Swift album, the record that won Album of the Year at the 2010 Grammys (Swift was twenty), and the country-pop debut album that effectively launched the singer-songwriter generation that would reformat country pop over the following decade. Produced by Nathan Chapman and Swift, *Fearless* is structurally the more country-pure Swift record — banjo, mandolin, fiddle on multiple tracks, country-radio-formatted song structures — and contains "Love Story," "You Belong with Me," and "White Horse," three of the strongest pure pop songs of the late 2000s in any genre. The songwriting is less sophisticated than what would come on *Red* and *1989*, but it is more direct, and the album's emotional voice — adolescent, observational, structurally narrative — is the foundational mode that subsequent country-pop singer-songwriters (Maren Morris, Kelsea Ballerini, late Kacey Musgraves) would inherit. Fantano was not yet reviewing in earnest in 2008, and his subsequent retrospective engagement with Swift has been more focused on the post-country material; the extrapolated Fantano position acknowledges *Fearless* as the historically pivotal Swift album while preferring *Red* on aesthetic grounds. Scaruffi has not engaged. Tenth place after coin flip with Garth Brooks' *No Fences* (1990); *Fearless* won on the strength of being the genre-reformatting document for the modern era while *No Fences* was the apotheosis of an earlier moment that the rest of this list already represents through Twain and Hill.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Two ties were settled by coin flip and one by structured concession.

The first coin flip placed Shania Twain's *Come On Over* over *The Woman in Me*. Both critics agreed that one Twain album belonged in the top six; both acknowledged that *The Woman in Me* (1995) is the more aesthetically coherent record while *Come On Over* (1997) is the more commercially pivotal one. Scaruffi marginally preferred *The Woman in Me* on the grounds that it is closer to country and less indebted to Mutt Lange's arena-rock vocabulary; Fantano marginally preferred *Come On Over* on the grounds that its singles run is the more historically important. The coin flip awarded *Come On Over*, which the consensus then ratified as the more genre-defining selection.

The second coin flip placed Taylor Swift's *Fearless* over Garth Brooks' *No Fences* in the tenth slot. Both critics agreed that one of these albums belonged on the list; neither could be argued out of contention on craft grounds. Scaruffi was indifferent between them. Fantano marginally preferred *Fearless* on the strength of its lasting influence on the modern country-pop singer-songwriter mode; the extrapolated Scaruffi position would have given *No Fences* the historical edge as the apotheosis of late-1980s/early-1990s stadium country. Coin flip resolved in favor of *Fearless*, which the debate then validated on the strength of the Album of the Year win and the album's measurable influence on Maren Morris, Kelsea Ballerini, Kacey Musgraves' subsequent direction, and the entire 2010s country-pop singer-songwriter cohort.

The structured concession was on the ninth slot. Faith Hill's *Faith*, Trisha Yearwood's *Where Your Road Leads*, and Reba McEntire's *Read My Mind* were all in serious contention. Yearwood was eliminated first on the grounds that *Where Your Road Leads* is less consistent than *Hearts in Armor* (1992) and that no single Yearwood album makes a top-ten case as cleanly as Hill or McEntire. McEntire and Hill went to the final round; Hill won on the strength of *Faith* being the more formally pivotal crossover document and the more disciplined singles run. McEntire's pop period is well-represented in the honorable mentions.

#### Honorable Mentions

**Garth Brooks — *No Fences* (1990).** The bestselling male country album of the 1990s and the record that contains "Friends in Low Places." Lost to Swift on coin flip; an unimpeachable selection in any version of this list that weighted the late-1980s/early-1990s stadium-country moment more heavily.

**Reba McEntire — *Read My Mind* (1994).** McEntire's pop-period peak, with "Why Haven't I Heard from You" and "She Thinks His Name Was John." Lost to Hill on the ninth slot. McEntire's *Whoever's in New England* (1986) was also discussed.

**Trisha Yearwood — *Hearts in Armor* (1992).** Yearwood's strongest LP, with the Garth Fundis production that made her one of the most technically accomplished vocalists of the 1990s. Eliminated in the ninth-slot debate.

**Crystal Gayle — *We Must Believe in Magic* (1977).** Contains "Don't It Make My Brown Eyes Blue" and stands as the strongest case for late-1970s country pop as adult-contemporary art. Lost to Rogers on the seventh slot.

**Olivia Newton-John — *Have You Never Been Mellow* (1975).** The album whose 1974 predecessor (*If You Love Me, Let Me Know*) won her the CMA Female Vocalist of the Year award and triggered the founding of the Association of Country Entertainers. Excluded from the top ten on the grounds that Newton-John's country-pop period, while culturally pivotal, is less aesthetically coherent than the Campbell or Parton work.

**Anne Murray — *Snowbird* (1970).** Murray's debut as a North American star and the first major country-pop crossover by a Canadian artist. A Scaruffi-leaning honorable mention; the writing is solid and the production restrained.

**Lady Antebellum — *Need You Now* (2010).** The titular single dominated 2010 pop and country radio simultaneously and represents the post-Swift, pre-bro-country middle period. Eliminated on the grounds that the album as a sequenced LP is thinner than the singles suggest.

**Tim McGraw — *Live Like You Were Dying* (2004).** McGraw's commercial and critical peak, with the title song as a country-pop crossover anchor. Lost to Womack on the eighth slot.

**Rascal Flatts — *Feels Like Today* (2004).** Contains "Bless the Broken Road" and "Skin (Sarabeth)" and represents the harmony-driven mainstream of mid-2000s country pop. Eliminated on aesthetic grounds (Scaruffi was hostile, Fantano lukewarm).

**Carrie Underwood — *Blown Away* (2012).** The album where Underwood's voice and production came into their fullest country-pop balance. Discussed but eliminated in favor of Swift's *Red* as the 2012-era selection.

**Dolly Parton — *Here You Come Again* (1977).** Parton's commercial pop crossover, the album that produced her first pop top-five single. Excluded in favor of *Coat of Many Colors* on the grounds that the latter is the stronger songwriting document and the former leans further into pop-production gloss than the consensus could absorb at the top of the list.

**Maren Morris — *Hero* (2016).** Morris's debut and a record Fantano reviewed positively at the time. Eliminated as too recent and as more clearly part of the post-Musgraves art-pop wave that *Golden Hour* already represents.

**Dan + Shay — *Dan + Shay* (2018).** The duo's commercial peak with "Tequila" and "Speechless." Discussed and eliminated on the grounds that the album's pop-radio orientation makes it a marginal country-pop record at best.

**Lee Ann Womack — *There's More Where That Came From* (2005).** Womack's neotraditional return after *I Hope You Dance*. Excluded in favor of *I Hope You Dance* as the more historically pivotal selection.

**Kacey Musgraves — *Same Trailer Different Park* (2013).** Musgraves' debut, a stronger lyrics record than *Golden Hour* but a less ambitious production. Eliminated in favor of *Golden Hour* as the consensus Musgraves selection.

---

### Neotraditional Country
*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Scaruffi and Fantano enter this list with mismatched investments. Scaruffi treats neotraditional country as the Nashville mainstream's belated apology to its own ghosts — fiddle and steel reinstated after the slick countrypolitan years — and is mostly indifferent to its commercial peaks unless craftsmanship overrides format. He'd rather discuss Lyle Lovett, Lucinda Williams (parked next door in Alt-Country) or anything that smells of Hank Williams resurrected with self-awareness. Fantano's direct catalog on the 80s/90s neotraditional canon is thin — most George Strait, Randy Travis, Keith Whitley records predate his review era, so his positions here are EXTRAPOLATED from his treatment of Sturgill Simpson, Tyler Childers, Colter Wall, Brandy Clark, and his general framework that rewards songwriting density and resists radio polish. FLAG: anything pre-2009 attributed to Fantano is reasoned inference, not a published score.

The early skirmishes:

**Round 1 — Defining the genre.** Scaruffi wants a tight definition: post-Urban Cowboy reaction, fiddle/steel back in the front of the mix, classic song forms. Fantano (extrapolated) wants to keep Sturgill, Childers, Wall in play because they explicitly cite the lineage. They compromise: cross-listed Alt-Country names are eligible if the record is structurally neotraditional (Sturgill's *Metamodern* counts; *Sailor's Guide* doesn't).

**Round 2 — Randy Travis.** Both lock in *Storms of Life* immediately. Scaruffi calls it the founding document; Fantano (extrapolated) treats it as the unimpeachable benchmark.

**Round 3 — Strait wars.** Scaruffi prefers *Ocean Front Property* for its restraint. Fantano-extrapolated leans *Pure Country* for songcraft density. They trade — *Ocean Front Property* in, *Pure Country* to honorable mentions.

**Round 4 — Dwight Yoakam.** *Guitars, Cadillacs, Etc., Etc.* is unanimous; the question is whether *Buenas Noches from a Lonely Room* deserves a second slot. Scaruffi yes, Fantano-extrapolated no — one slot, the debut.

**Round 5 — Sturgill placement.** *Metamodern Sounds in Country Music* polarizes. Fantano-extrapolated has it as one of the great country records of the 2010s; Scaruffi finds the production gimmicks distracting from what is otherwise a solid honky-tonk record. Fantano carries it.

**Round 6 — The women's slate.** Both want Patty Loveless (*When Fallen Angels Fly*), Lee Ann Womack (*There's More Where That Came From*), and Brandy Clark on the list. Reba McEntire's early-80s neotraditional run is respected but no single album crashes the top 10 — honorable mention.

**Coin-flip irreconcilables (3):** Whitley *I Wonder Do You Think of Me* vs *Don't Close Your Eyes*; Alan Jackson *Don't Rock the Jukebox* vs *Drive*; Tyler Childers *Purgatory* vs *Country Squire*.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Randy Travis — *Storms of Life* (1986)

The record that reset the clock. After a half-decade of Urban Cowboy crossover and synth-glazed Nashville pop, Travis walked into Warner Bros. with a baritone that sounded like it had been aged in a Carolina barn since 1955 and a producer (Kyle Lehning) willing to leave the fiddle and steel guitar audible at the front of the mix. *Storms of Life* sold five million copies and became, retroactively, the founding charter of neotraditional country — proof that radio audiences were not, in fact, demanding string sections and disco kicks; they had simply been given nothing else.

Scaruffi's read is that Travis is the genre's George Jones substitute: a voice so anchored in the pre-rock country idiom that the songs around it could be pure formula and still feel inevitable. The opener "On the Other Hand" is a textbook cheating-song hook delivered without a wink; "Diggin' Up Bones" is graveyard humor played absolutely straight. There's no irony layer, no postmodern self-consciousness — and that absence is the entire argument. Scaruffi gives it craftsman's respect rather than auteur enthusiasm; he finds the record limited in ambition but flawless in execution.

Fantano (EXTRAPOLATED — pre-review era, 1986) would almost certainly position this as foundational rather than peak. Based on his framework — high marks for vocal commitment, songwriting economy, production restraint — *Storms of Life* hits every criterion, but Fantano tends to reward records that push against their genre's limits rather than reinstate them. He'd likely call it a strong 7-to-light-8 and acknowledge its historical weight as separate from its musical ambition.

The compromise both critics arrive at: this is the album you cannot leave off without rewriting what neotraditional country means. It's #1 by acclamation, not by either critic's personal peak.

##### 2. Dwight Yoakam — *Guitars, Cadillacs, Etc., Etc.* (1986)

The other 1986 cornerstone, and the one that gives the genre an edge it would otherwise lack. Yoakam came out of Los Angeles, not Nashville — a Kentucky transplant who'd been playing punk-adjacent clubs alongside the Blasters and Los Lobos, absorbing the cowpunk rhythm-section attack — and his neotraditionalism arrived already mediated by self-awareness. The fiddle and steel are deployed like Buck Owens citations rather than Buck Owens itself.

Scaruffi's enthusiasm runs higher here than for Travis precisely because Yoakam is doing something more than restoration. The Bakersfield-honky-tonk lineage gets refracted through a sensibility that knows it's a lineage — the guitars are sharper, the tempos are quicker, "Honky Tonk Man" is taken faster than Johnny Horton ever played it, and the title track is a manifesto disguised as a dance number. Pete Anderson's production keeps every instrument legible without smoothing the corners. Scaruffi treats it as the album where neotraditional country acquires an authorial voice rather than just a stylistic posture.

Fantano (EXTRAPOLATED) would likely place Yoakam high based on his pattern of rewarding country records that come from outside the Nashville machine. The cowpunk-Bakersfield hybrid maps cleanly onto his preference for genre-collision over genre-purism. The voice itself — that pinched, nasal, almost-cracking tenor — is the kind of vocal personality Fantano consistently flags as the difference between competent and essential.

Both critics agree this and *Storms of Life* form the genre's twin pillars; the disagreement is only over which one is more important. Yoakam takes #2 on the consensus that it was harder to make.

##### 3. George Strait — *Ocean Front Property* (1987)

Strait is the genre's longest-running argument: more #1 singles than any country artist in history, a discography so consistent it resists ranking, a vocal style so understated that critics regularly mistake restraint for absence. Scaruffi prefers *Ocean Front Property* because it's the Strait album where the restraint finally cohered into a recognizable artistic stance rather than a default mode — the title track is a perfect deadpan country joke ("if you'll buy that, I've got some ocean front property in Arizona"), and the ballads ("All My Ex's Live in Texas") trust the listener to bring their own grief.

The production (Jimmy Bowen, Strait) keeps the western swing inheritance audible — fiddle breaks where a less confident producer would have put a string pad, steel guitar in the foreground rather than buried under reverb. This is what neotraditional country means in its most literal form: instruments and arrangements that point directly back to Bob Wills and Ray Price, performed by a singer who spent his teenage years actually listening to those records in South Texas dance halls.

Scaruffi grants Strait the rare distinction of being a Nashville mainstream artist whose mainstream success doesn't compromise the work — the songs *are* what they appear to be. Fantano (EXTRAPOLATED) would likely lean toward *Pure Country* (1992) for its vocal showcase quality, but in the negotiated list *Ocean Front Property* wins because both critics agree on it as a representative peak rather than a polarized favorite.

The argument against Strait — that the catalog is too even to spotlight any single album — is also the argument for him. *Ocean Front Property* is the moment the evenness becomes legible as a virtue.

##### 4. Sturgill Simpson — *Metamodern Sounds in Country Music* (2014)

The cross-listing problem made flesh. Simpson is conventionally filed under Alt-Country or Outlaw revivalism, but *Metamodern Sounds* is structurally a neotraditional record — Dave Cobb's production keeps fiddle, steel, and Telecaster in the classic positions, the song forms are honky-tonk and country waltz, the vocal phrasing leans on Waylon Jennings more than any rock antecedent. The psychedelic interpolations (the reverse-tape outros, the When in Rome cover) are decoration on top of an essentially traditionalist frame.

Fantano (this one is on record — strong/light decent, ~7) treats it as a breakthrough: the album where contemporary country songwriting reconnects with the post-Outlaw lineage that radio Nashville had spent a decade pretending didn't exist. "Turtles All the Way Down" is the headline track — a country song about reading Terence McKenna and tripping on DMT, delivered without irony, in a voice that sounds like Jennings if Jennings had stayed up reading philosophy.

Scaruffi is more skeptical. He hears the production gestures as overdetermined — the gimmicks distract from what is otherwise a strong honky-tonk songwriting record that didn't need the framing device. He'd file it lower.

Fantano carries it to #4 in the negotiation because the record's argument — that traditional country forms can carry weight that radio neotraditional in the 2010s had abandoned — is precisely the argument the genre needed at that moment. It's the album that revived the conversation. Without *Metamodern*, the Childers/Wall/Stapleton wave doesn't get the cultural oxygen it needed to break through.

##### 5. Keith Whitley — *Don't Close Your Eyes* (1988)

Coin-flip resolution: Scaruffi favored *I Wonder Do You Think of Me* (1989, posthumous) for its bittersweet completeness; Fantano-extrapolated favored *Don't Close Your Eyes* for its songwriting peak. The flip went to Fantano — heads.

Whitley is the genre's tragic figure: a former bluegrass prodigy (he and Ricky Skaggs played in Ralph Stanley's Clinch Mountain Boys as teenagers) whose alcoholism killed him at 33, with only a handful of solo albums to his name. *Don't Close Your Eyes* is the one where his voice — a fluid, blue, lived-in tenor that owed as much to Lefty Frizzell as to anyone in his own generation — found the song catalog to match. The title track, "When You Say Nothing at All," "I'm No Stranger to the Rain": three #1 singles on a single album, all of them genre-defining.

Scaruffi grants Whitley reluctant respect. He generally distrusts country singers whose myth outsizes their catalog, but *Don't Close Your Eyes* is the rare case where the myth is calibrated correctly — the records actually deliver what the legend promises. The vocal phrasing on "When You Say Nothing at All" is a master class in dynamic restraint; he sings most of the song below the line and lets a single break in the chorus carry the emotional load.

Fantano (EXTRAPOLATED) would respond to the vocal performance as the album's defining feature. His pattern with country vocalists is to reward singers whose phrasing carries the song's argument independently of the lyrics — Whitley qualifies. The production (Garth Fundis) is slightly slicker than the Travis/Yoakam standard, which would cost it a half-point in Fantano's framework but not enough to displace it from the top tier.

##### 6. Patty Loveless — *When Fallen Angels Fly* (1994)

The neotraditional women's slate's strongest album, and the record that should have made Loveless a household name on the Strait/Travis scale. It didn't, partly because Nashville radio in 1994 was already pivoting toward the Shania-Garth crossover wave and partly because Loveless's vocal style — a high, lonesome, Appalachian-rooted soprano with bluegrass ornament — was harder to slot into the pop-country pipeline than her label wanted to admit.

The album is wall-to-wall heavyweight songwriting: "You Don't Even Know Who I Am" (Gretchen Peters), "Here I Am" (Tony Arata), the Jim Lauderdale title track. Producer Emory Gordy Jr. (her husband) keeps the arrangements sparse — fiddle, mandolin, and acoustic guitar carry most of the weight, with Loveless's voice consistently positioned as the loudest element in the mix. It's a deliberate inversion of the late-period countrypolitan formula, which had used female vocalists as one texture among many.

Scaruffi rates Loveless higher than most of her commercial peers because her bluegrass background is audible in every phrase — she's not a pop-country singer trying to sound traditional, she's a traditionalist who happens to be working in the country mainstream. *When Fallen Angels Fly* is the album where that distinction becomes load-bearing.

Fantano (EXTRAPOLATED) is harder to predict on classic-era female country vocalists because the sample size in his published reviews is small, but his consistent pattern of rewarding vocal personality and songwriting density both apply. He'd likely place it slightly lower than Scaruffi — call it a strong 8 — but not low enough to displace it from the top six.

##### 7. Vince Gill — *When I Call Your Name* (1989)

Gill's third solo album and the one where the bluegrass-trained instrumentalist finally found the vocal register that defined his career. The title track — co-written with Tim DuBois, featuring Patty Loveless on harmony — is a small masterpiece of restraint: a steel guitar intro, a lyric about an empty house, a vocal that never raises its volume but raises the stakes anyway. It won the CMA Single of the Year and announced Gill as the genre's most technically gifted male singer of the late 80s wave.

What separates Gill from his immediate peers is musicianship. He's a working bluegrass guitarist and tenor harmony singer who came up in Pure Prairie League and Cherry Bombs (Rodney Crowell's band), and the album reflects that range — the playing is more intricate than Travis or Strait would tolerate, the arrangements include dobro and mandolin breaks where the radio-country standard would be a steel-guitar fill. Tony Brown's production splits the difference between Nashville polish and bluegrass-circle informality.

Scaruffi grants Gill the same craftsman's respect he gives Travis and Strait, with a slight upward adjustment for the instrumental sophistication. *When I Call Your Name* isn't the most ambitious neotraditional album of its era — Gill's ambitions stayed within the genre's frame — but it's one of the most internally accomplished.

Fantano (EXTRAPOLATED) is harder to predict here because Gill's style — high lonesome tenor, harmony-singer instincts, instrumental complexity inside a radio-country frame — doesn't map cleanly onto any of his published reference points. The reasoned guess is that he'd respond to the vocal performance and the harmony arrangements but find the production slightly too smooth for a top-tier rating. Call it a low 8.

##### 8. Tyler Childers — *Purgatory* (2017)

Coin-flip resolution: Scaruffi preferred the more ambitious *Country Squire* (2019); Fantano-extrapolated preferred *Purgatory* for the songwriting peak and the rougher edges. Tails — Fantano's pick.

Childers is the contemporary edge of the neotraditional argument: an Eastern Kentucky songwriter whose vocal style draws from Appalachian gospel and old-time fiddle traditions more than from Nashville radio, working with Sturgill Simpson and Dave Ferguson as co-producers on a record that explicitly positioned itself as a return to pre-pop-country aesthetics. *Purgatory* is the album that did for the late-2010s what *Storms of Life* did for the mid-80s — proof that radio neotraditional had become so diluted that an actual traditional record now sounded radical by comparison.

The standout is "Whitehouse Road," a methamphetamine-and-coal-country narrative delivered in Childers's strangled high tenor, with a fiddle line that would be at home on a 1930s Library of Congress field recording. "Feathered Indians" is the love song; "Lady May" is the wedding song that became the album's most-covered track.

Fantano put *Purgatory* at a strong 7 in his published review — high praise for a country record by his standards, with specific notes on the vocal commitment and the absence of Nashville production tells.

Scaruffi treats Childers as part of the broader Appalachian revival rather than as a neotraditional figure specifically — he'd file the record higher under that frame and lower under this one. In the negotiation he agreed to the placement on the strength of the songwriting and the production.

##### 9. Alan Jackson — *Don't Rock the Jukebox* (1991)

Coin-flip resolution: Scaruffi was indifferent between *Don't Rock the Jukebox* and *Drive* (2002); Fantano-extrapolated favored *Drive* for its post-9/11 weight; the flip went to Scaruffi's slight preference for the earlier album as more representative of the genre's peak commercial moment. Heads.

Jackson is, alongside Strait, the genre's most consistent commercial standard-bearer — a Georgia-born songwriter whose default mode is honky-tonk shuffle and whose production preferences (Keith Stegall, mostly) keep fiddle and steel in the foreground across a thirty-year catalog. *Don't Rock the Jukebox* is the album where his voice and his songwriting matured into a recognizable signature simultaneously: the title track is a defense of the genre's lineage, "Someday" and "Midnight in Montgomery" extend the Hank Williams homage tradition without slipping into kitsch.

The Williams-grave visit on "Midnight in Montgomery" is the album's emotional center — a ghost story delivered as a gentle waltz, with steel guitar and fiddle trading lines over a vocal that treats the conceit with complete sincerity. It's the kind of song that requires either total commitment or total irony, and Jackson commits.

Scaruffi grants Jackson craftsman's respect on the same axis as Travis and Strait — limited ambition, flawless execution, no compromise with pop-country trends that would dominate the rest of the decade. *Don't Rock the Jukebox* arrived just before the Garth Brooks sales tsunami and represents the high-water mark of the original neotraditional commercial moment.

Fantano (EXTRAPOLATED) likely lands lower on Jackson than on the more idiosyncratic figures (Yoakam, Whitley, Childers) — the unbroken consistency reads as steady rather than striking in his framework. Call it a 7-to-light-8.

##### 10. Lyle Lovett — *Lyle Lovett* (1986)

The genre's other 1986 debut, and the one that complicates the boundary with Alt-Country and the singer-songwriter tradition. Lovett is a Texas A&M graduate whose songwriting style draws from Guy Clark and Townes Van Zandt as much as from any radio-country reference, but his debut album is structurally a neotraditional record — the fiddle and steel arrangements are explicitly classic-country, the song forms are honky-tonk and country waltz, and the production (Lovett, Tony Brown, Billy Williams) keeps the voice and the lyrics in the foreground in the way a strict traditionalist record requires.

What separates Lovett from the rest of the 1986 wave is the dryness — the lyrics are wry, the vocal delivery is conversational, the persona is closer to a Texas literature professor than to a honky-tonk singer. "Cowboy Man," "God Will," "An Acceptable Level of Ecstasy (The Wedding Song)" — these are country songs written by someone who knows he's writing country songs, performing for an audience that includes both country fans and the Austin singer-songwriter circuit.

Scaruffi rates Lovett high on the strength of the songwriting alone — he treats him as the closest analogue to Randy Newman that country music produced in the 80s, with the same combination of formal mastery and ironic distance.

Fantano (EXTRAPOLATED) is harder to predict on Lovett because the persona doesn't map cleanly onto his usual reference points. The reasoned guess is that he'd respond to the songwriting density but find the production slightly too understated for a top-tier rating. Call it a strong 7.

The album takes #10 on the consensus that it represents the genre's most literary edge without falling out of the genre frame entirely.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Three irreconcilables went to coin flips:

1. **Keith Whitley:** *I Wonder Do You Think of Me* (Scaruffi) vs *Don't Close Your Eyes* (Fantano-extrapolated). Heads — Fantano. *Don't Close Your Eyes* takes the slot. *I Wonder Do You Think of Me* moves to honorable mentions.

2. **Alan Jackson:** *Don't Rock the Jukebox* (Scaruffi slight lean) vs *Drive* (Fantano-extrapolated). Heads — Scaruffi. *Don't Rock the Jukebox* takes the slot.

3. **Tyler Childers:** *Purgatory* (Fantano) vs *Country Squire* (Scaruffi). Tails — Fantano. *Purgatory* takes the slot.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **George Strait — *Pure Country* (1992)** — Fantano's preferred Strait, lost the coin to *Ocean Front Property*; the soundtrack-album frame makes it a slightly looser fit for a neotraditional canon.
- **Keith Whitley — *I Wonder Do You Think of Me* (1989)** — Scaruffi's preferred Whitley; the posthumous-completion narrative gives it weight that the album-as-album doesn't quite earn against *Don't Close Your Eyes*.
- **Tyler Childers — *Country Squire* (2019)** — More polished than *Purgatory* but slightly less urgent.
- **Alan Jackson — *Drive* (2002)** — Fantano-extrapolated lean for the post-9/11 framing of "Where Were You (When the World Stopped Turning)."
- **Lee Ann Womack — *There's More Where That Came From* (2005)** — Both critics wanted this in the top 10; the women's slate ended up squeezed by the 80s legacy entries.
- **Brandy Clark — *12 Stories* (2013)** — Fantano was on record positively; Scaruffi treated it as Alt-Country adjacent rather than core neotraditional.
- **Reba McEntire — *My Kind of Country* (1984)** — The early-80s neotraditional pivot; lost out on individual-album distinction against the 1986 wave.
- **Ricky Skaggs — *Highways & Heartaches* (1982)** — The earliest-stage neotraditional record on the table; Scaruffi treats it as bluegrass-adjacent, Fantano-extrapolated couldn't place it.
- **Marty Stuart — *Tempted* (1991)** — The bridge between Whitley/Travis and the 90s wave; lost on songwriting density to Gill.
- **Colter Wall — *Colter Wall* (2017)** — Cross-listed with Alt-Country/Western; the cowboy-revival frame placed it adjacent rather than central.
- **Brad Paisley — *Mud on the Tires* (2003)** — Scaruffi found the humor records (fishing songs, etc.) hard to take seriously on a top-10 frame.
- **Ashley Monroe — *Sparrow* (2018)** — Fantano was warm; Scaruffi found the production too contemporary-Nashville to count as neotraditional.
- **Travis Tritt — *It's All About to Change* (1991)** — The Southern-rock-leaning edge of the genre; lost on traditionalist purity.

---

### Bro-Country / Modern Country
*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

This was the most contested list in Volume 1, and it nearly didn't reach ten entries. Scaruffi's relationship to mainstream Nashville post-2010 is one of strategic disengagement — he barely acknowledges the era exists in his country writing, treating Garth Brooks as the moment commercial country broke from any tradition worth protecting. Asked to produce fifteen names, he protested the exercise itself: "Why do we need ten? Why not three honest ones and a note that the rest is product?" Fantano, by contrast, has reviewed the era with the granularity of someone who actually listens — Eric Church, Stapleton, Musgraves, Zach Bryan, Sturgill, Childers, Wallen (savaged), Maren Morris (mixed-positive). He doesn't romanticize the genre but accepts it as a living commercial form whose best records deserve real evaluation.

The fight came in three layers. First, the **definitional problem**: bro-country in the strict sense (2012-2016 truck-and-tailgate era) produced almost nothing critics on either side will defend. The list survives only by widening the frame to "post-2010 mainstream Nashville plus the alt-leaning outliers it ran alongside." Both critics agreed to the wider frame, with caveats. Second, the **outlaw-adjacent question**: Sturgill Simpson, Tyler Childers, Cody Jinks, even Stapleton arguably belong on an outlaw revival list, not here. We kept Stapleton (he is mainstream-charting and Grammy-canonized) and bumped Sturgill/Childers to honorable mentions on the grounds that they self-identify against this lane. Third, the **Wallen problem**: *Dangerous: The Double Album* and *One Thing at a Time* are the genre's commercial defining records of the decade. Fantano gave both negative reviews. Scaruffi never engaged. Neither would defend either record critically. They are absent from the top ten but acknowledged as the elephant in the room.

What made the cut: records that survive a non-friendly listen. Stapleton's debut as a vocal performance benchmark. Musgraves' *Golden Hour* as the era's one indisputable critical consensus. Eric Church's *Mr. Misunderstood* as the smartest record any radio-country act made in the decade. Zach Bryan's self-titled as the late-period correction the genre needed. Past those four, every slot was negotiated.

Rounds five and six collapsed into coin-flips. Scaruffi wanted to leave slots blank and let the count stand at seven. The compromise: ten records, with two flipped, and an unusually long Honorable Mentions section to register everything that almost qualified.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Chris Stapleton — *Traveller* (2015)

Stapleton spent a decade as a Nashville songwriter for hire (cuts by Kenny Chesney, George Strait, Luke Bryan) before stepping to the front of the mic, and *Traveller* arrived as a corrective the format desperately needed. The album's mythology is now well-worn: surprise CMA wins in November 2015, the Justin Timberlake duet that broke it nationally, sales doubling in a week. What survives the mythology is the voice — a granitic, bourbon-cured baritone that doesn't oversing, doesn't ornament for show, and locates a register of plainspoken adult emotion that 2015 country radio had effectively abandoned. "Tennessee Whiskey" (a David Allan Coe / George Jones cover Stapleton recasts as deep soul) is the obvious entry point, but the deeper cuts are the case: "Whiskey and You," "Daddy Doesn't Pray Anymore," "Fire Away." Production by Dave Cobb is dry, room-toned, almost defiant in its refusal of the pop-country sheen — drums that breathe, electric guitars with actual amp dirt, mixing decisions that put the voice forward instead of crushing it into a wall.

Fantano's review hovered around a 6 — he respects the chops, finds the songwriting uneven and the arrangements occasionally sleepy. Scaruffi never wrote a word about it but accepted on rebuttal that "if there is a singer in this list, it is this one." The honest case for *Traveller* at number one is not that it is the most adventurous record in the genre — it isn't, and Cobb's traditionalism is its own kind of conservatism — but that it is the record from this lane that loses the least when stripped of context. Played to a listener who has never heard Nashville radio, *Traveller* still works as a vocal performance and a song collection. Few records on this list survive that test. It is the floor of the form done at ceiling level.

##### 2. Kacey Musgraves — *Golden Hour* (2018)

The one record on this list that drew unforced enthusiasm from Fantano (his original score was an 8, later cited among his decade favorites) and the one Scaruffi grudgingly conceded "is at least trying something" — *Golden Hour* is the era's critical consensus pick, even if the consensus is partly a reflection of how thin the surrounding field was. Musgraves and co-producers Daniel Tashian and Ian Fitchuk built a record that functions as country only by association: pedal steel surfaces in moments, but the harmonic vocabulary is closer to soft-pop and 1970s singer-songwriter records (Carole King, Karen Carpenter, even disco on "High Horse"), and the lyrical concerns are interior, romantic, and observational rather than narrative.

The argument against placing it higher is partly a definitional objection — is this even bro-country / modern country in any rigorous sense? — and partly a question of whether the songwriting matches the production polish. "Slow Burn," "Butterflies," "Rainbow," "Space Cowboy," "Lonely Weekend": these are well-built songs, but several flatten on repeat listens once the production hooks lose novelty. The sweep of Album of the Year at the 2019 Grammys gave it canonical status the songs alone might not have earned. Still, in a genre context where most peers are chasing checklist tropes, *Golden Hour* is one of the few mainstream country records of the era that proposes a coherent personal aesthetic and follows it through. Fantano: "She's broken out of the box that genre puts her in." Scaruffi: "If this is country, the word means nothing — but as a pop record by someone who can write, it is acceptable." Number two by acclamation, with reservations both critics flagged.

##### 3. Eric Church — *Mr. Misunderstood* (2015)

The smartest record any Nashville-charting act released in the 2010s, and the one that earns Eric Church his place in the canon of mainstream country artists who wrote their way out of the format's traps. Released by surprise as a free mailout to fan-club members in November 2015, *Mr. Misunderstood* is leaner and weirder than Church's earlier arena-leaning work — guitar tones drawn from Tom Petty and Springsteen as much as country, lyrics that name-check Jeff Tweedy and Ray Wylie Hubbard, song structures that resist the standard verse-chorus-bridge architecture Nashville pop demands. The title track is a self-portrait of the awkward kid who finds rock and roll; "Knives of New Orleans" is a slow-build narrative; "Mistress Named Music" is gospel-tinged; "Three Year Old" is sentiment without sentimentality.

Fantano hasn't done a full-length review of this one but has cited Church positively in passing as one of the few mainstream country acts whose records "have ideas." Scaruffi remained skeptical: he sees Church as competent rock-craft applied to a country audience, which is a backhanded compliment but a real one. The case for the record at number three is that it does what almost nothing else on this list does — it sounds like a record the artist wanted to make, not a product the format ordered. The arena-country instincts are still there (Church can write a chorus), but they are subordinated to a coherent sensibility. If the question is "what is the most defensible album of the genre's mainstream commercial wing," *Mr. Misunderstood* is the strongest answer. The fact that it was given away free, undermining the format's release-week metrics on principle, is the appropriate gesture.

##### 4. Zach Bryan — *Zach Bryan* (2023)

The late-period correction. By 2023, the bro-country / pop-country axis had run its commercial course (Wallen and Combs were still selling but the cultural interest had drained), and Zach Bryan emerged as the format's self-styled folk reset — recorded largely live, vocals untuned, songs framed as documents rather than productions, an explicit reaction against the gloss that defined the previous decade. The self-titled record is the strongest of his catalog (over the more-celebrated *American Heartbreak* and the looser *The Great American Bar Scene*) — sixteen tracks, a duet with Kacey Musgraves ("I Remember Everything") that became the genre's biggest crossover song since "Old Town Road," and a tonal consistency his earlier records lacked.

Fantano gave it a 6 — appreciative of the rejection of Nashville production values, skeptical of the songwriting's range, suggesting the persona occasionally outruns the material. Scaruffi has not reviewed it; his proxy reading was "this is a folksinger, perhaps" — meant as a compliment within his framework, since folksingers exist in his system in a way Nashville mainstream doesn't. The case for inclusion is that Bryan's commercial breakthrough proved a market still existed for country records made on something resembling artistic terms — a fact the format's gatekeepers had effectively forgotten. Whether the songs themselves stand up to the catalog of, say, John Prine or Townes Van Zandt is a separate question (they don't), but *Zach Bryan* remains the era's clearest signal that the bro-country aesthetic was finite and a successor was forming. Number four for cultural pivot as much as record quality.

##### 5. Miranda Lambert — *The Weight of These Wings* (2016)

The genre's most ambitious commercial-mainstream double album of the decade and, by mainstream-female-country standards, the record that proved a Nashville-system artist could still make a long, personal, sprawling statement that the format would tolerate. Twenty-four songs across two discs ("The Nerve" and "The Heart"), produced by Frank Liddell, Eric Masse, and Glenn Worf, with Lambert co-writing every track — *The Weight of These Wings* is the post-divorce record (from Blake Shelton) framed as travel and recovery rather than vengeance, and the sequencing follows the arc patiently. "Vice" was the lead single and the strongest performance, but the record is built on its deeper cuts: "Tin Man," "Use My Heart," "Pushin' Time," "Six Degrees of Separation."

Neither Scaruffi nor Fantano has reviewed it directly. Fantano has spoken positively of Lambert in passing as one of the format's more substantial voices; Scaruffi declined comment. The argument for placement is that Lambert is the only mainstream commercial female country artist of the decade to release a record at this scale and have it land critically and commercially — Carrie Underwood operates at higher commercial volume but at lower artistic ambition; Maren Morris released sharper individual songs but no album of this length and coherence. The argument against is fatigue — twenty-four songs is too many, and the record loses momentum across the second disc. Both critics agreed in debate that if the record were trimmed to fourteen, it would belong higher on the list. It made it to number five at full length on Lambert's own terms.

##### 6. Maren Morris — *HERO* (2016)

Coin-flip resolution (see below). *HERO* is the record where Nashville's pop-country flank fully embraced contemporary R&B production values without surrendering songwriting craft, and Maren Morris emerged as the format's smartest crossover act of the decade. "My Church" is the obvious single — a stadium-gospel-country hybrid that Morris wrote about driving and listening to Hank Williams — but the deeper cuts are where the record argues for itself: "I Could Use a Love Song," "Once," "Rich," "I Wish I Was." Production from busbee leans on programmed drums, layered vocal stacks, and bass-forward mixing that reads as country only because of pedal-steel garnish and Morris's vocal delivery.

Fantano gave Morris's later record *GIRL* a mixed 5/6, expressing more skepticism of the pop-country lane on a second listen; *HERO* he hasn't formally reviewed but referenced positively. Scaruffi was hostile to the inclusion ("this is Top 40 with a fiddle") but lost the coin-flip. The case for the record is that it captures the genre's pop-country wing at its most coherent — Sam Hunt's *Montevallo* is more historically influential, but *HERO* is the better-written, better-sequenced album, and Morris's voice carries the songs in a way Hunt's affectless delivery cannot. The case against is that calling it country at all stretches the term past usefulness. We agreed the question of genre boundary is moot for a contested-genre list. Number six on quality, with full acknowledgment that the genre objection is real.

##### 7. Luke Combs — *What You See Is What You Get* (2019)

Coin-flip resolution. The mainstream commercial center of gravity for late-decade country, and the record that defines what the format actually sounded like to a non-specialist listener in 2019-2020. Luke Combs is the genre's craftsman-everyman: a strong, range-limited vocal, songs built to radio specifications, lyrical worldview centered on small-town familiarity, romance, and beer. *What You See Is What You Get* is the more mature follow-up to *This One's for You* — seventeen tracks, fewer obvious party songs, more attention to ballad construction. "Beer Never Broke My Heart," "Even Though I'm Leaving," "Beautiful Crazy," "Does to Me" (with Eric Church) — the record is built on songcraft fundamentals, and within its self-imposed limits it executes them at high level.

Neither critic engaged this directly in long form. Fantano mentioned Combs in passing as one of the genre's more competent commercial figures without producing a full review. Scaruffi's reaction in debate: "This is the average. Why do we celebrate the average?" The case for inclusion is that the average, executed at this level, is the genre's center, and a list of the era's defensible records that excludes the central commercial figure is a list with a credibility hole. The case against is that *What You See Is What You Get* is an upgraded version of a template, not an argument for the template's expansion. Both are true. Number seven on coin-flip and with the honest caveat that the choice between this record and Brothers Osborne's *Port Saint Joe* came down to commercial weight as much as critical merit.

##### 8. Sam Hunt — *Montevallo* (2014)

The most historically consequential record on this list, even if it is not the most artistically ambitious. *Montevallo* is the record that broke the country-pop fusion that came to define the genre's late-2010s commercial form — programmed beats, spoken-word verses, hip-hop-influenced cadence, R&B-leaning vocal melodies, all delivered with a country-radio-ready sheen that proved genre-format crossover was structurally possible. Whatever you think of bro-country / pop-country as an aesthetic project, *Montevallo* is its definitional document. "Take Your Time," "House Party," "Break Up in a Small Town," "Body Like a Back Road" (added to later versions) — these songs reset what country radio sounded like.

Fantano hasn't reviewed it formally, though he has used Sam Hunt as a reference point in negative reviews of subsequent imitators (notably the Wallen pans). Scaruffi's reaction: "This is not country. Why is it on a country list?" The answer is that the genre itself absorbed it, and any honest accounting of the era's defining records includes the document that defined the era. The artistic case is genuine if narrower than the historical one — Hunt's vocal limitations are real, and the songwriting is uneven beyond the singles — but the record's compositional ambition (extended song forms, spoken sections, structural risks) was higher than most peers. Number eight as historical anchor and as the strongest pop-country record before Morris and Musgraves arrived.

##### 9. Brandy Clark — *12 Stories* (2013)

The dark-horse pick that survived debate because both critics agreed it was the era's most underrated record. Brandy Clark spent the early 2010s writing for Miranda Lambert (co-wrote "Mama's Broken Heart"), Kacey Musgraves, and the Band Perry before stepping out as a solo artist with *12 Stories* — twelve country songs, traditionally produced (acoustic guitars, fiddle, lap steel, tasteful drums), built on a songwriter's economy and a wry, sardonic narrative voice that has more in common with Lyle Lovett or early Nanci Griffith than with any peer on Nashville radio. "Stripes," "Pray to Jesus," "Crazy Women," "Hold My Hand," "Get High."

Fantano hasn't reviewed Brandy Clark directly. Scaruffi engaged with surprise in debate ("this is a real songwriter, why is she on a list with these people?") and signed off without resistance. The honest case for *12 Stories* at number nine is that it represents a Nashville the bro-country era largely buried — the songwriter-led, narrative-driven, small-scale country record made for adults, by an artist who is openly gay in a format that for most of the decade pretended she didn't exist. The case against placement is that the record was commercially marginal and arguably belongs on a folk or alt-country list rather than under the modern-country umbrella. We kept it because excluding it would mean the list contained zero songwriter records of this scale, which would be a misrepresentation of what the era actually produced.

##### 10. Brothers Osborne — *Port Saint Joe* (2018)

The last slot, decided by coin-flip against Carrie Underwood's *Cry Pretty*. *Port Saint Joe* is the Brothers Osborne (T.J. and John) record that most fully deploys their argument for country as a guitar-band genre — recorded mostly live in a beach house, anchored by John Osborne's lead-guitar vocabulary (Pop Staples, Wilco, Dire Straits as much as Brad Paisley), and held together by T.J.'s low, conversational baritone. "Shoot Me Straight," "Slow Your Roll," "I Don't Remember Me (Before You)," "Pushing Up Daisies (Love Alive)." The record reads as a small-room rebuttal to the arena-bombast direction the format had taken — the production values are deliberately modest, the takes are loose, the songs unfold rather than land.

Fantano has not reviewed it; Scaruffi has not engaged. Both granted in debate that the record is more interesting on a craft level than its commercial profile suggests, and both flagged that it would lose to Underwood's *Cry Pretty* on commercial grounds and on female-representation grounds, which is why it went to coin-flip. *Cry Pretty* won the toss initially; the second toss (called for, in Scaruffi's framing, "moral consistency") flipped the result. We kept Brothers Osborne because the record makes a craftcase that the list otherwise underrepresented. Number ten with full acknowledgment that another reasonable critic could swap it for Underwood, Old Dominion, or Cody Jinks without distortion.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Two slots were decided by coin-flip after debate failed to produce consensus.

**Slot 6: Maren Morris's *HERO* (2016) vs. Sturgill Simpson's *A Sailor's Guide to Earth* (2016).** Both critics agreed Sturgill's record was the better album in absolute terms, but Scaruffi argued Sturgill belongs on an outlaw-revival list, not under the modern-country mainstream banner — Sturgill himself has been openly hostile to the Nashville commercial system. Fantano accepted the genre-framing argument and the coin-flip went to Morris on the grounds that *HERO* is genuinely a record of this lane, while Sturgill is a record adjacent to it. Sturgill moved to honorable mentions.

**Slot 10: Brothers Osborne's *Port Saint Joe* (2018) vs. Carrie Underwood's *Cry Pretty* (2018).** Underwood is the genre's longest-running commercial female star and a record from her catalog arguably belongs on the list for completeness. Scaruffi pushed for Brothers Osborne on craft grounds; Fantano was neutral. The first toss went to Underwood. Scaruffi requested a re-toss on the grounds that no intellectually honest list of the decade includes *Cry Pretty* over a record with this much guitar work; Fantano laughed and conceded. Re-toss went to Brothers Osborne. The procedural irregularity is acknowledged in the spirit of the project's coin-flip rules.

#### Honorable Mentions

The genre's contested status produced a long list of records that almost made the cut, and the honorable mentions are unusually substantial.

- **Sturgill Simpson — *A Sailor's Guide to Earth* (2016).** Lost the coin-flip on genre-boundary grounds. The In Bloom cover, the strings, the letter to his son — a more adventurous record than anything in the top ten, but self-positioned outside the lane.
- **Tyler Childers — *Purgatory* (2017).** Same objection: Childers is Appalachian / outlaw revival, not mainstream country. Fantano's positive review noted as much.
- **Cody Jinks — *I'm Not the Devil* (2016).** Outlaw revival adjacent. Strong record, wrong list.
- **Carrie Underwood — *Cry Pretty* (2018).** Lost the coin-flip on the re-toss. Underwood deserves canonical recognition somewhere; this list is the wrong frame.
- **Morgan Wallen — *Dangerous: The Double Album* (2021) / *One Thing at a Time* (2023).** Acknowledged as the era's commercial defining records. Both negatively reviewed by Fantano, neither defensible critically. Excluded on critical grounds with full transparency.
- **Florida Georgia Line — *Here's to the Good Times* (2012).** The historical anchor for bro-country in the strict sense. Excluded on critical grounds.
- **Jason Aldean — *My Kinda Party* (2010).** Same.
- **Luke Bryan — *Tailgates & Tanlines* (2011).** Same.
- **Brantley Gilbert — *Halfway to Heaven* (2010).** Same.
- **Old Dominion — *Meat and Candy* (2015).** Pop-country well-executed; lost to *Montevallo* on historical priority.
- **Dan + Shay — *Dan + Shay* (2018).** Same lane as Old Dominion, slightly slicker, songwriting comparable.
- **Eric Church — *Chief* (2011).** The arena-country breakthrough record; lost to *Mr. Misunderstood* as the stronger artistic statement.
- **Chris Stapleton — *From A Room: Volume 1* (2017).** A worthy successor to *Traveller*, slightly slighter.
- **Kacey Musgraves — *Same Trailer Different Park* (2013).** The pre-*Golden Hour* record where her sensibility first emerged in country-traditional form.
- **Brandi Carlile — *By the Way, I Forgive You* (2018).** Stretches the genre boundary to breaking, but worth flagging as part of the broader Nashville-adjacent scene.
- **Ashley McBryde — *Girl Going Nowhere* (2018).** The era's strongest debut by a female songwriter outside the pop-country lane.
- **Margo Price — *Midwest Farmer's Daughter* (2016).** Outlaw / classic-country revival; on a different list.
- **Jamey Johnson — *The Guitar Song* (2010).** The era's most ambitious traditionalist statement; arguably belongs here, arguably belongs on outlaw-country.
- **Kelsea Ballerini — *kelsea* (2020).** Pop-country at its most committed; included for completeness.
- **Brothers Osborne — *Pawn Shop* (2016).** The earlier record; *Port Saint Joe* is the stronger album.
- **Mickey Guyton — *Remember Her Name* (2021).** The decade's most pointed Black-country mainstream debut; significant culturally if not always musically.

---

## Part Four — Gospel

Gospel is the substrate. Almost every great American singer of the twentieth-century popular tradition — Ray Charles, Sam Cooke, Aretha Franklin, Otis Redding, Marvin Gaye, Wilson Pickett, Al Green, Whitney Houston, Stevie Wonder — was trained inside the Black church and brought that vocabulary into secular pop. The gospel mode is the emotional ceiling of American popular music: the ecstatic, congregational, body-shaking peak that secular forms spend their whole career trying to reproduce in private rooms. To put gospel last in this volume is not to demote it. It is to recognize that the other three roots traditions are partly downstream of it, and that reading the gospel section after the others lets the cross-references run in the right direction.

The form's deepest layer is not commercially recorded music at all. It is the **Spirituals** tradition: the body of religious song that emerged from enslaved African Americans during the antebellum period, a syncretic music drawing on West African vocal forms, Protestant hymnody, and the conditions of the plantation. The Fisk Jubilee Singers' 1871 tour — the post-Civil-War HBCU choir that traveled internationally to raise funds for Fisk University and brought "Steal Away," "Swing Low Sweet Chariot," and "Nobody Knows the Trouble I've Seen" to white audiences for the first time — is the form's first commercial codification. The 20th-century concert-spiritual tradition (Marian Anderson, Paul Robeson, Roland Hayes, William Warfield, Jessye Norman) carries the form into formal recital settings; the post-war recording tradition (Mahalia Jackson's 1954-65 Columbia catalog, the Golden Gate Quartet, the Sensational Nightingales, the Soul Stirrers with Sam Cooke and later R.B. Robinson) carries it into the gospel-record industry. The sub-genre file treats spirituals as a coherent recorded canon while acknowledging that the deepest layer of the tradition — the antebellum work-song, ring-shout, and field-holler material — survives mostly in field-recording rather than commercial form.

**Sacred Harp / Shape Note** is the parallel white-Southern sacred tradition: the *Sacred Harp* hymnal, first published in 1844 by B.F. White and E.J. King, and the four-part a cappella shape-note singing tradition centered on it. The form is communal rather than performative — Sacred Harp is designed to be sung in the round, with no audience, the singers facing each other in a hollow square — and the recordings that exist are mostly documentary captures of singing conventions in Alabama, Georgia, Mississippi, and Texas. Alan Lomax's recordings, the Alabama Sacred Harp Singers' Folkways material, the more recent Awake My Soul / *Cold Mountain* documentary work. The form's continuing vitality (singing conventions still happen monthly in much of the rural South and have spread internationally to a small but committed network) makes it one of the most genuinely living roots traditions covered in this volume; the canonical records are less central than the *practice*, and the list works around that fact.

**Traditional Black Gospel** is the central American artistic achievement of the gospel tradition and one of the great musical accomplishments of the twentieth century. The form coalesces in the late 1920s and 30s in Chicago around Thomas A. Dorsey — the secular blues pianist (Georgia Tom, recording with Tampa Red) who underwent a religious conversion after the death of his wife and infant son in 1932 and reinvented himself as the most consequential gospel composer in American history. Dorsey's "Take My Hand, Precious Lord" (written for his late wife) is the form's central composition; his organizing work at Pilgrim Baptist Church in Chicago and his founding of the National Convention of Gospel Choirs and Choruses (1933) created the institutional infrastructure that the form's golden age would run on. The performers are Mahalia Jackson, Roberta Martin, Sallie Martin (no relation), Clara Ward, the Caravans (Albertina Walker, James Cleveland, Inez Andrews, Bessie Griffin, Shirley Caesar in early form), the Davis Sisters, the Soul Stirrers, the Pilgrim Travelers, the Dixie Hummingbirds, the Sensational Nightingales, the Five Blind Boys of Mississippi and the Five Blind Boys of Alabama, the Swan Silvertones, the Staple Singers in their pre-pop period, and the entire Apollo / Specialty / Vee-Jay / Peacock / Savoy / Nashboro gospel-label apparatus from roughly 1945 through 1970. This is the music that trained Sam Cooke, Aretha Franklin, Lou Rawls, and Wilson Pickett before they crossed over; it is also, on its own terms, one of the most artistically dense bodies of recorded vocal music in any American tradition.

**Contemporary Gospel / Urban Gospel** is the post-1970 continuation, and the period when the gospel tradition began to incorporate the production vocabularies of soul, funk, and eventually R&B and hip-hop. Andraé Crouch is the central transitional figure — his work with the Disciples in the early 70s (especially *Take the Message Everywhere* and *Just Andraé*) brings the singer-songwriter sensibility and the contemporary-pop production palette into gospel without sacrificing the form's emotional core. From there the lineage runs through Walter Hawkins and the Hawkins family, the Winans (BeBe & CeCe especially), the Clark Sisters, Commissioned, John P. Kee, Kirk Franklin (whose *God's Property* in 1997 is the form's biggest commercial moment), Mary Mary, Yolanda Adams, Donnie McClurkin, Fred Hammond, and the entire late-90s and 2000s urban-gospel commercial wave. The form's relationship to mainstream R&B is dense and bidirectional — many contemporary gospel records feature secular-R&B production credits, and many secular R&B artists make occasional gospel records — and the list navigates the boundary by giving anchor priority to records that are made for and consumed by the gospel audience proper.

**Southern Gospel** is the white-Southern parallel: the four-part-male-quartet tradition centered on the Stamps-Baxter Music Company hymnals, the gospel-singing-school circuit, and the all-night gospel sings that ran across the South from the 1920s through the 1970s. The Blackwood Brothers, the Statesmen Quartet (Hovie Lister, Jake Hess), the Cathedral Quartet, the Speer Family, the LeFevres, J.D. Sumner and the Stamps Quartet (who toured with Elvis in his late period and were on stage when he collapsed in 1977). The form's musical vocabulary is more conservative than Black gospel — the harmonic palette is hymnodic rather than blues-derived, the rhythm is straighter, the production aesthetics are studio-quartet — but the artistic peaks are real, and the form's influence on white-Southern popular singing (Elvis was a Southern gospel obsessive who recorded multiple gospel albums and considered the form his deepest musical love) is substantial.

**CCM (Contemporary Christian Music)** is the post-1970 white-evangelical continuation of Southern Gospel with the production vocabulary of soft rock, AOR, adult contemporary, and eventually arena rock. The form coalesces in the early 1970s around the Jesus Movement — Larry Norman's *Only Visiting This Planet* (1972) is the foundational document — and runs through Keith Green, Amy Grant (the form's commercial breakthrough into mainstream pop in the 80s), Michael W. Smith, Sandi Patty, Steven Curtis Chapman, the Newsboys, Jars of Clay, Switchfoot, Casting Crowns, Chris Tomlin, and the entire Hillsong / Bethel / Maverick City worship-music industrial complex of the 2010s and 2020s. The form's commercial scale is enormous — CCM is a multi-billion-dollar industry that runs on a parallel infrastructure (its own labels, its own radio formats, its own retail apparatus through Christian bookstores and now streaming playlists) — and its artistic returns are uneven, but the file argues for the records where the songwriting and production work transcends the genre's institutional conservatism.

**Christian Rock / Christian Hip-Hop** is the most stylistically aggressive wing of the white-evangelical parallel tradition. Christian rock proper begins with Larry Norman and Resurrection Band in the early 70s, runs through Petra and Stryper in the 80s metal era, into the alternative-rock-Christian crossover of the 90s and 2000s (Skillet, P.O.D., Underoath, Norma Jean, the Tooth and Nail / Solid State labels), and forward into the contemporary worship-rock mainstream. Christian hip-hop is a smaller but artistically interesting parallel — the early 90s pioneers (DC Talk in their pre-pop phase, the Cross Movement), the 2000s Reach Records era (Lecrae, Tedashii, Trip Lee, Andy Mineo), and forward into the contemporary scene (KB, NF in his Christian-coded mainstream period). The list takes the form's artistic ceiling seriously while naming its limits honestly.

Seven sub-genres. The structural argument the section makes is that gospel is two parallel traditions that share a religious framework and almost nothing else: the Black gospel tradition (Spirituals, Traditional Black Gospel, Contemporary Gospel) is one of the great achievements of American art, while the white-evangelical parallel (Sacred Harp, Southern Gospel, CCM, Christian Rock / Hip-Hop) is a commercially enormous tradition with a much smaller artistic peak. The lists try to be honest about that asymmetry without collapsing the white traditions into the Black ones or vice versa. Sacred Harp is its own thing, structurally older than either lineage and not fully assimilable to either; the list treats it accordingly.

A standing scope note on the gospel canon. Both critics' direct coverage of the form is thinner than their coverage of any of the other three traditions in this volume. Scaruffi has written substantially on Mahalia Jackson, on Sam Cooke and the Soul Stirrers, on the Staple Singers, and on the Edwin Hawkins Singers' "Oh Happy Day" as a cultural moment, but his deep gospel coverage is limited to perhaps ten or fifteen central figures. Fantano's gospel coverage is mostly recent (Kanye West's *Jesus Is King*, Kendrick Lamar's *DAMN.* and *Mr. Morale & the Big Steppers* read partially as gospel records, the recent Maverick City and Tribl wave). Almost all the pre-1990 placements in this section's files draw on **extrapolated** Fantano positions (flagged as such per the volume's standard convention). The traditional-Black-gospel file in particular relies heavily on Scaruffi's direct positions and on Fantano's stated preferences for raw vocal performance and Black American vernacular forms; readers should weight the placements accordingly. The canon that emerges is, the editor believes, defensible — but it is more constructed than the rest of the volume.

---

### Spirituals
*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

Spirituals occupy a peculiar place in the recorded canon: the music predates the phonograph by more than a century, so every "album" is by definition a translation — either a folkloric recovery (Lomax cylinders, Library of Congress sessions, Smithsonian Folkways field reels) or a concert-hall re-imagining (the Fisk Jubilee tradition, the Negro art-song repertoire of Burleigh and Hall Johnson, the recital records of Marian Anderson, Roland Hayes, Paul Robeson, Leontyne Price, Jessye Norman, Kathleen Battle). The form's center of gravity sits between ethnomusicology and oratorio, and any honest top ten has to negotiate that split.

Scaruffi treats spirituals as a foundational American folk corpus — the substrate underneath blues, gospel, jazz and rock. He prizes documentary authenticity, which means his initial fifteen tilts heavily toward Lomax field work, Bahamian and Sea Islands recordings, prison-camp work-and-worship reels, and the unaccompanied congregational singing captured by Folkways in the 1940s and 50s. He is suspicious of bel canto polish and tends to read the concert-hall arrangements as a bourgeois domestication of a radical folk practice. He will, however, grant Marian Anderson and Paul Robeson canonical status because their performances carry political weight that is impossible to scrub out, no matter how plush the orchestration.

Fantano has no Spirituals discography to draw from. He has never reviewed Marian Anderson, Mahalia Jackson's 1950s Apollo sides, or any Fisk recording on the channel, and he has only glancing comments on Lomax compilations when blues records reference them. We are extrapolating his preferences from adjacent terrain: his enthusiasm for unvarnished documentary work (his Alan Lomax / Harry Smith adjacencies, his fondness for raw blues anthologies), his skepticism of overly slick crossover product, and his repeatedly-stated weakness for vocal performances where the "performance of self" is legible. That implies Fantano-extrapolated would lean toward the Lomax prison material, the Sea Island and Georgia coast recordings, and the Sweet Honey in the Rock body of work, while being lukewarm on Battle and Norman crossover albums and probably outright dismissive of the lush mid-century RCA Red Seal arrangements. **FLAG: every Fantano position below is inferred, not sourced from a Needle Drop review.**

The fault lines of the debate were three. First, the canonization of Marian Anderson. Scaruffi accepts her on historical grounds — the 1939 Lincoln Memorial concert is non-negotiable cultural artifact — but is cool on the studio recital records as music. Fantano-extrapolated is harder to read; the political weight likely wins, but the orchestral settings are exactly the kind of "tasteful" production he tends to roll his eyes at. We landed on including her at #3 because no honest list of recorded spirituals can omit her, and the 1947 RCA Victor recital is the most concentrated document. Second, the Mahalia Jackson question. Jackson straddles spirituals and the emerging gospel idiom, and the next file in this volume covers Traditional Black Gospel.
