Scaruffi wanted her excluded on cleanliness grounds; we argued that the 1947–1954 Apollo sides are spiritual repertoire ("Move On Up a Little Higher" aside, much of that book is older folk-religious material) and held her at the boundary — included, but with a note about the overlap. Third, the Sweet Honey in the Rock placement. Bernice Johnson Reagon's project is a 1970s-onward revival, not a primary-source document; Scaruffi argued for honorable mention only, Fantano-extrapolated argued for a top-ten slot because of the fidelity to congregational practice. Compromise: top ten, low-ranked.

Coin-flips resolved three irreconcilables: Roland Hayes vs Paul Robeson for the second concert-tradition slot; the Moses Hogan Chorale vs the Wynton Marsalis *In This House, On This Morning* for the modern arranger slot; and the *Negro Prison Songs* (Lomax, Mississippi 1947) vs *Negro Folk Music of Alabama* (Harold Courlander, Folkways) for the second field-recording slot. Robeson, Hogan, and *Negro Prison Songs* won.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Various Artists — *Negro Prison Songs from the Mississippi State Penitentiary* (Tradition, 1958, recorded 1947 by Alan Lomax at Parchman Farm)

This is the document that both critics agree sits at the headwaters. Recorded by Alan Lomax in the segregated work camps of Parchman Farm in 1947 and released a decade later on Tradition, it captures unaccompanied Black men singing field hollers, axe-cutting songs, and spirituals as continuous practice — the work song and the religious song bleeding into each other across "Old Dollar Mamie," "Rosie," "No More, My Lord," and "Tangle Eye Blues." The men are not performing for an audience; they are surviving a day, and the rhythmic relationship between hammer-strike and vocal phrase is the entire architecture of the music. This is the spirituals tradition at its most undisguised: the worship and the labor are the same gesture, and the modal vocabulary — flatted thirds and sevenths, microtonal slides, group response weaving under the lead — is the seedbed for everything from Charley Patton to Sam Cooke. Scaruffi's case is straightforward: this is the closest thing on tape to what slavery-era spirituals would have actually sounded like in the field, and the prison context makes the connection structurally explicit rather than analogous. Fantano-extrapolated would be drawn to the raw documentary character, the lack of studio framing, the way the recording refuses to flatter the listener. Lomax's role is contested — he extracted material under conditions of profound coercion, and the men's names are partial — but the artifact itself is irreplaceable. Subsequent Rounder reissues restored fuller field notes and additional takes; the original 1958 sequence remains the definitive listen. Without this record the entire shape of what we mean by "spirituals" as recorded music is unrecoverable.

##### 2. Fisk Jubilee Singers — *In Bright Mansions* (Curb / Word, 2003)

The Fisk Jubilee Singers are the institution that translated plantation spirituals into the concert-tradition repertoire — they were touring Europe in 1873, presenting "Steal Away," "Swing Low Sweet Chariot," and "Go Down Moses" as art music a generation before any recording technology existed. The discography is therefore a strange thing: a continuous oral pedagogy passed through directors from John Wesley Work II onward, occasionally documenting itself on disc. *In Bright Mansions* is the entry we recommend because it is a mature, well-engineered representation of the institutional sound under Paul T. Kwami's directorship, with arrangements that respect the John Work and Hall Johnson legacy without embalming it. The blend is chest-toned rather than choral-bright, the dynamics swing from communal hush to full-throated declamation, and the tempos refuse the brisk metronome-driven pacing that ruins many academic choirs. Scaruffi's hesitation here is institutional — this is by definition a domesticated form, the spirituals filtered through Reconstruction-era respectability politics — but he grants the Jubilee tradition foundational status because, without Fisk's 1871 tour, the spirituals would have remained a regional folk practice and never entered the global imaginary. Fantano-extrapolated would probably prefer something rougher, but the institutional continuity — descendants of formerly enslaved people, at the same university, singing the same repertoire across fifteen decades — is itself the artifact. Earlier Fisk recordings exist (the 1909–1911 Victor sides under John Work II are historically critical but acoustically punishing); *In Bright Mansions* is the listenable canonical entry.

##### 3. Marian Anderson — *Spirituals* (RCA Victor, 1947–1955 sessions, compiled)

Anderson is the indispensable concert-tradition voice of the spirituals: contralto of granite weight, German lieder training, and a political profile that turned every recital into a confrontation with Jim Crow America. The 1939 Lincoln Memorial concert (performed after the Daughters of the American Revolution barred her from Constitution Hall) is the symbolic anchor of her career, but the studio recordings collected on the various RCA Victor compilations of her spirituals work — sessions stretching from 1947 through 1955 with Franz Rupp on piano and small-orchestra arrangements by Hall Johnson and Harry Burleigh — are where the music sits down on tape. Her "Crucifixion," "Deep River," "He's Got the Whole World in His Hands," and "Were You There" became the templates against which every subsequent concert-tradition recording is measured. Scaruffi's reservation is that the orchestral settings are sometimes ponderous, the tempos draggy by 21st-century standards, and the bel canto purity at odds with the folk roots; he keeps her in because the historical and political fact of the recordings cannot be argued away, and because in the unaccompanied moments her voice carries a gravity that approaches the prison-camp recordings from the opposite direction. Fantano-extrapolated would find the orchestrations dated but the central performances unimpeachable. Burleigh's piano-vocal arrangements — the form he essentially invented in the 1910s — are the bridge between the Fisk choral tradition and the solo recital tradition, and Anderson is the singer who made those arrangements canonical.

##### 4. Paul Robeson — *Songs of Free Men: A Paul Robeson Recital* (Columbia Masterworks, 1942–1947 sessions)

Robeson's bass-baritone is the other irreplaceable voice in the concert tradition, and his political commitments — labor, anti-fascism, Pan-Africanism — were inseparable from his repertoire choices. *Songs of Free Men* and the various Columbia and HMV spirituals sides he cut between the late 1920s and the late 1940s are where "Go Down Moses," "Joshua Fit the Battle of Jericho," "Balm in Gilead," and "Sometimes I Feel Like a Motherless Child" entered the recorded canon as solo art songs. His tempos are slower than Anderson's, the dynamic range narrower, the diction more declamatory; he sings spirituals as historical testimony rather than personal devotion, and the political reading is always foregrounded. Scaruffi prefers Robeson to Anderson on aesthetic grounds — fewer lush orchestrations, more unaccompanied or piano-only settings, a reading of the texts as protest literature rather than worship. The coin-flip against Roland Hayes (an earlier and arguably more refined tenor with foundational 1920s spirituals recordings) went Robeson's way because the political legibility of the performances has shaped how spirituals are heard ever since. Hayes is in honorable mentions. Robeson's 1958 Carnegie Hall recording is also essential listening — a late, harder, more weathered voice — but the Columbia Masterworks sides are the central document of his recital work.

##### 5. Mahalia Jackson — *The Apollo Sessions: 1946–1951* (Pair, 1994 reissue)

Mahalia Jackson is the disputed boundary case: she straddles spirituals and the emerging Traditional Black Gospel tradition that the next file in this volume covers in detail. We include her here because the Apollo Records sessions she cut between 1946 and 1951 — "Move On Up a Little Higher" notwithstanding — drew heavily on older folk-religious repertoire, sung in a style closer to Baptist congregational worship than to the smooth-harmony quartet gospel that would dominate the 1950s. Her "I'm Going to Tell God All About It One of These Days," "Even Me," "Dig a Little Deeper," and "I Want to Rest" sit on the spiritual side of the line; her vocal approach — chest-voice, blue-noted, melismatic in the older Sanctified tradition — descends directly from the unaccompanied congregational singing the Lomaxes were documenting in the same decade. Scaruffi argued for excluding her on tradition-policing grounds; we held her in because the alternative is to artificially sterilize the spirituals canon of its most consequential mid-century voice. The Apollo recordings, especially the early ones with simple piano-and-organ accompaniment from Mildred Falls, are the most "spirituals" of her output; her later Columbia work (1954 onward, after John Hammond signed her) drifts squarely into commercial gospel territory and belongs in the next file. Treat her presence here as the hinge between two volumes of the same tradition, not as a categorization error.

##### 6. Various Artists — *Negro Folk Music of Alabama, Vol. 1: Secular and Vol. 2: Religious* (Folkways FE 4471/4472, 1951–1953, recorded by Harold Courlander)

Harold Courlander's Alabama field recordings, released by Folkways in two volumes, are the second great documentary pillar after Lomax's prison work. Courlander recorded in rural Black communities across Alabama in the early 1950s, capturing a corpus of unaccompanied congregational spirituals, ring shouts, lined-out hymns, and call-and-response prayers that were not otherwise on tape. The religious volume in particular preserves practices — long-meter hymn lining, the call-and-response between deacon and congregation, the slow-drag tempo of pre-gospel Baptist worship — that were already disappearing under the pressure of the mid-century gospel boom. Scaruffi rates these recordings extremely highly because they are among the few documents of spirituals as living congregational practice rather than as performed repertoire, and Courlander's notes (he was an accomplished Africanist as well as a folklorist) frame the music with appropriate ethnomusicological seriousness. Fantano-extrapolated alignment is straightforward: this is documentary work of the most uncompromising kind, and the absence of any concert-hall framing is the entire point. The lost coin-flip against *Negro Prison Songs* for the top documentary slot was close; the Alabama set lost only because the prison material has greater historical specificity (the chain-gang context makes the slavery-era continuity legible) but on pure musical content this is comparable in stature.

##### 7. Sweet Honey in the Rock — *Selections 1976–1988* (Flying Fish, 1997 compilation)

Bernice Johnson Reagon — Smithsonian curator, civil rights organizer, scholar of the spirituals tradition — founded Sweet Honey in the Rock in 1973 as a women's a cappella ensemble explicitly committed to the unaccompanied congregational style. The group's body of work across the late 1970s and 1980s, sampled most efficiently on the Flying Fish compilation, represents the most rigorous post-1960s revival of the spirituals as living oral tradition. Their arrangements are largely Reagon's own and draw from her field research with the SNCC Freedom Singers, Sea Island singers, and Georgia and Alabama congregations; the harmonic vocabulary stays close to source, the rhythmic feel avoids the metronomic precision of academic choirs, and the texts are restored to their political register (the Underground Railroad readings of "Wade in the Water," the labor readings of "Down by the Riverside"). Scaruffi was reluctant on canonical grounds — this is a revival project, not primary-source material — but the fidelity to congregational practice and the explicit linking of spirituals to Black freedom struggle made the case. Fantano-extrapolated alignment is high: a self-organized women's collective working outside the recital tradition, refusing instrumental accompaniment, and explicitly rejecting the bel canto framework is exactly the kind of artist-controlled project he tends to elevate. Placement at #7 reflects the revival rather than original-document status; on impact and craft it could plausibly sit higher.

##### 8. Roland Hayes — *The Art of Roland Hayes: Six Centuries of Song* (Smithsonian Folkways / Vanguard, 1990 compilation of 1939–1965 sessions)

Roland Hayes was the first internationally famous Black classical tenor and the singer who, more than anyone, established the spiritual as concert art song before Anderson and Robeson. His recordings — especially the spirituals sides cut for Vanguard in the 1950s and reissued by Smithsonian Folkways — pair his exceptionally clear, lieder-trained tenor with the spare piano-vocal arrangements he wrote himself, drawing on Burleigh and Hall Johnson but pushing toward greater interpretive restraint. Hayes treated the texts as devotional poetry and his readings of "You're Tired Chile," "Bal'm in Gilead," "Hear de Lambs A-Cryin'," and his own arrangement of "Lit'l Boy" are studies in understatement: he refuses the dramatic peaks Anderson and Robeson reach for, and the emotional weight accumulates through diction and phrasing rather than dynamics. Scaruffi rates Hayes highly as the originator of the solo spirituals recital and as a more refined musician than either Anderson or Robeson; Fantano-extrapolated would likely find the recordings somewhat austere but respect the architectural seriousness. He lost the coin-flip against Robeson for the second concert-tradition slot because Robeson's political legibility shaped the broader reception of the music; Hayes settles in at #8 as the connoisseur's choice within the same lineage.

##### 9. Wynton Marsalis & The Lincoln Center Jazz Orchestra — *In This House, On This Morning* (Columbia, 1994)

This is the modern-arranger slot, and it is the most contested entry on the list. *In This House, On This Morning* is a two-hour suite organized around the structure of a Black Sunday service — call to worship, devotional prayer, processional, sermon, altar call, recessional — built on melodic material that is partly composed and partly drawn from spirituals and ring-shout patterns. It is a jazz record on its surface (Wycliffe Gordon's plunger-mute trombone, Marcus Roberts on piano, Marsalis directing the band) but the structural backbone is the spirituals tradition, and the long "Pot Blessed Dinner" and "Recessional" movements explicitly quote and develop spirituals melodies. Scaruffi was skeptical (Marsalis's neoclassicism is not his preferred jazz mode) but conceded that the suite is the most ambitious 20th-century piece to use spirituals as compositional source material rather than as repertoire. Fantano-extrapolated would likely find the length punishing but respect the conceptual rigor. The coin-flip against the Moses Hogan Chorale's *We Shall Walk Through the Valley* (Hogan being the most influential late-20th-century spirituals arranger for choir, his settings now standard in HBCU choral programs) went Marsalis's way because the suite frames spirituals within a Black sacred-music continuum (gospel, jazz, blues) rather than within a purely choral idiom, which is more useful for understanding the tradition's long arc. Hogan is in honorable mentions and is essential for anyone studying the choral repertoire specifically.

##### 10. Various Artists — *Wade in the Water: African American Sacred Music Traditions, Vol. I — African American Spirituals: The Concert Tradition* (Smithsonian Folkways, 1994)

The Smithsonian Folkways *Wade in the Water* series is the single most useful curated document of the spirituals tradition on record, and Volume I — focused on the concert tradition — is the right entry to canonize. Bernice Johnson Reagon was the series curator, and her selections trace the concert lineage from early Fisk Jubilee recordings through Burleigh's piano-vocal arrangements, Hall Johnson's choral settings, Hayes, Anderson, Robeson, Leontyne Price, Jessye Norman, and into late-century arrangers. The notes (Reagon's, with contributions from Horace Boyer and other scholars) are a small monograph in themselves, situating each recording in its historical and ecclesiastical context. The album functions both as an introduction and as a synoptic argument: that the concert-spiritual tradition is not a deviation from authentic folk practice but a parallel lineage with its own internal logic, originating in Reconstruction-era racial uplift politics and continuing through the present. Scaruffi values the curatorial discipline; Fantano-extrapolated values the institutional independence (Smithsonian Folkways operates outside the major-label apparatus) and the documentary thoroughness. Companion volumes II–IV cover congregational gospel, the Black church and the Civil Rights Movement, and country gospel; for spirituals-as-such, Volume I is the canonical compilation. Including it at #10 is partly an editorial choice — it functions as a gateway to the rest of the list — and partly a recognition that no single artist's record summarizes the field as efficiently.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

**Slot 4 — concert tradition male voice: Roland Hayes vs Paul Robeson.** Hayes is the earlier, more refined musician and arguably the foundational figure for the solo spirituals recital. Robeson is the more politically legible singer whose readings of the texts shaped the broader reception of the music. We flipped on grounds of historical impact rather than musical priority; Robeson took #4 and Hayes took #8. A defensible alternative list would invert these placements.

**Slot 9 — modern arranger: Wynton Marsalis (*In This House, On This Morning*) vs Moses Hogan Chorale (*We Shall Walk Through the Valley in Peace*, MGM/EMI 1990s).** Hogan is the arranger whose settings are most heavily used in current HBCU and church choral programs, and his recordings are the standard reference for the contemporary choral spiritual. Marsalis's suite is a more ambitious compositional project that frames spirituals within a wider Black sacred-music continuum. The flip went Marsalis on contextual breadth; Hogan is in honorable mentions and is the indispensable record for anyone studying the choral arrangement tradition specifically.

**Slot 6 — second documentary record: *Negro Folk Music of Alabama* (Courlander/Folkways) vs *Roots of the Blues* (Lomax, Atlantic 1959, Mississippi Hill Country sessions including spirituals material).** The Lomax Atlantic recordings include extensive spirituals material from the Hill Country, but the Alabama Folkways set is more strictly devoted to the religious tradition and includes the long-meter hymn lining and ring-shout material that the Lomax set deemphasizes. Courlander took the slot.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **Leontyne Price** — *Spirituals: Swing Low, Sweet Chariot* (RCA Red Seal, 1962, with the Leonard de Paur Chorus). The most operatic of the post-Anderson concert-tradition recordings; arrangements by Hall Johnson and others. Indispensable for the soprano lineage.
- **Jessye Norman & Kathleen Battle** — *Spirituals in Concert* (Deutsche Grammophon, 1990, with James Levine and the Orchestra of St. Luke's at Carnegie Hall). The crossover summit of the late-20th-century concert tradition; arrangements by Hall Johnson, Roland Hayes, and Moses Hogan. Some critics find it overly polished; others consider it the definitive modern recital.
- **Moses Hogan Chorale** — *We Shall Walk Through the Valley in Peace* and the various MGM Choral collections of the 1990s. The standard contemporary choral arrangements; foundational for current HBCU choral repertoire.
- **Various Artists — *Negro Church Music* (Atlantic, 1960, recorded by Alan Lomax in the 1950s).** Companion to *Negro Prison Songs*; congregational recordings from rural Mississippi and Alabama churches.
- **Brother John Sellers** — *Sings Blues and Folk Songs* (Vanguard, 1954) and the Folkways spirituals sides. A direct disciple of Mahalia Jackson; the bridge between her early Apollo work and the gospel-blues tradition.
- **Various Artists — *Negro Folk Music of Africa and America* (Folkways, 1951, edited by Harold Courlander).** The comparative ethnomusicological set that situates spirituals within an Atlantic African-diasporic frame.
- **The Tuskegee Institute Choir under William L. Dawson** — the 1955 Westminster recordings of Dawson's spirituals arrangements. Dawson is the arranger between Burleigh/Hall Johnson and Hogan and is essential for understanding the mid-century institutional choral lineage.
- **Fisk Jubilee Singers (John Work II direction)** — the 1909–1911 Victor recordings. Acoustically punishing but historically irreplaceable; the earliest Fisk discs.
- **Bernice Johnson Reagon** — *Folk Songs: The South* (Folkways, 1965). Reagon as solo singer before Sweet Honey in the Rock; the field-collected repertoire she would later harmonize for ensemble.
- **Various Artists — *Been in the Storm So Long: Spirituals, Folk Tales and Children's Games from Johns Island, S.C.* (Smithsonian Folkways, 1990, recorded by Guy and Candie Carawan).** Sea Island congregational recordings; the Gullah-Geechee lineage of spirituals.
- **Harry T. Burleigh / various performers** — recordings of Burleigh's piano-vocal arrangements as performed by his students and successors. Burleigh is the composer whose 1910s arrangements created the solo-recital spiritual as a form; any serious study requires hearing his settings as written.

---

### Sacred Harp / Shape Note
*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

> **Methodology note.** Sacred Harp is a participatory, congregational tradition. There is no stage, no audience, no soloist; singers face inward in a hollow square and sing for each other and for God. Almost every "album" in this canon is a field recording or a convention recording — documentation rather than performance. Where commercial studio releases exist (the Word of Mouth Chorus, *Cold Mountain*, *Awake My Soul*), they are anomalies and are flagged as such. Two critics have been simulated for this debate: Piero Scaruffi (b. 1955), whose long-running engagement with American folk-religious music makes him a natural advocate for the Lomax tradition; and Anthony Fantano (b. 1985), who has no published Sacred Harp commentary — his positions here are extrapolated from his stated affinities (drone, communal noise, Appalachian primitivism, *Anthology of American Folk Music* lineage) and are FLAGGED throughout.

#### Debate Summary

The opening positions diverged on a single axis: **document vs. art object**. Scaruffi's Top 15 leaned heavily on the 1942 Library of Congress sessions, the 1959 *White Spirituals* Prestige/International release, and the long sequence of Alabama and Georgia convention tapes archived at AFC and Folkways. He treats Sacred Harp the way he treats early blues or Appalachian banjo — the older the document, the closer to the source, the higher the ranking. His argument: the tradition is the recording's reason to exist, and any reading that elevates a 1979 chorus over a 1942 convention is reading the wrong art form.

Fantano's extrapolated Top 15 (FLAGGED — no direct Sacred Harp criticism exists in his published reviews) leaned the other way. Reading his enthusiasm for drone, for noise-as-community (Boredoms, Swans live), and for the *Harry Smith Anthology* through the lens of *Rivers of Delight*, he ranked the 1979 Word of Mouth Chorus near the top — a record he would, by extrapolation, hear as the closest thing the tradition has to a "produced" album whose massed unison overtones rival La Monte Young. He also pushed harder on *Cold Mountain* and *Awake My Soul* as gateway documents for younger listeners.

Round 2 settled the Lomax 1942 question quickly: both critics agreed it sits at #1 or #2. Round 3 was the longest argument — the *Rivers of Delight* placement. Scaruffi wanted it at #6; Fantano (extrapolated) wanted it at #2. They split the difference at #3 after Scaruffi conceded the record's historical role in the post-1970s revival. Round 4 dealt with Cooper Book vs. Denson Book recordings; both agreed the 1959 Alan Lomax Southern Journey Alabama sessions and the Henagar-Union convention recordings deserved separate slots rather than being collapsed. Round 5 handled the soundtracks (*Cold Mountain*, *Awake My Soul*) — Fantano pushed; Scaruffi reluctantly allowed one. Round 6 cleaned up adjacent traditions (Southern Harmony, Christian Harmony, Old Regular Baptist lining-out as cross-reference) and the bonus-round Greil Marcus framing — both critics independently cited *Invisible Republic* / *The Old, Weird America* as the right interpretive lens, which became the consensus framing for the introduction.

Three irreconcilables went to coin flip: (1) New Lost City Ramblers' *American Moonshine and Prohibition Songs* adjacent track placement vs. their *White Spirituals* compilation contribution — flip went to the latter; (2) the 2006 *Awake My Soul* documentary soundtrack vs. the companion *Help Me to Sing* CD — flip went to *Awake My Soul*; (3) inclusion of an Old Regular Baptist lining-out record as comparative #10 vs. a tenth Sacred Harp convention tape — flip went to the convention tape, with the Old Regular Baptist record moved to Honorable Mentions.

The deeper agreement underneath the debate: this is a music whose canonical "recordings" are mostly accidents of microphone placement at a singing convention in north Alabama. Ranking them is partly absurd. Both critics endorsed the absurdity and proceeded.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. *Sacred Harp Singing* — Library of Congress AFS L11 (1942)
**Field recordings by Alan Lomax, George Pullen Jackson, and Fisk University's John Wesley Work III.** Recorded at the United Sacred Harp Musical Convention in Birmingham, Alabama, August 1942. Issued as a 78rpm album by the Library of Congress in 1943, later reissued on LP and as part of the Rounder Alan Lomax Collection. This is the record that exists at the headwaters. Jackson, the white musicologist whose 1933 *White Spirituals in the Southern Uplands* had argued — controversially, and with some racial defensiveness that later scholarship has corrected — for the white folk origins of much of the spiritual repertoire, was already a convention regular. Work, the Black musicologist from Fisk who had documented the Black Sacred Harp tradition of the Wiregrass region of southeast Alabama, brought a counterweight. Lomax brought the disc cutter. What survives is roughly forty minutes of Cooper-revision singing — *Northfield*, *Wondrous Love*, *The Morning Trumpet*, *Idumea* — captured with the 1942 Presto recorder's compressed dynamic range, which paradoxically *helps*: the dense parallel-fifths cluster of a 200-voice convention is a recording problem, and the Presto's ceiling smooths it into a single overtone-rich roar. Scaruffi's note: "the most important field recording of American congregational music ever made." Fantano (extrapolated): "if you want to know where American drone music actually starts, before La Monte Young, before the *Anthology*, it's here, on a 16-inch acetate in an Alabama courthouse." A community singing for itself, recorded by people who understood they were documenting something that might not survive — and which, ironically, has outlived almost everything around it. The historical context matters: 1942 was the trough year of the wartime recording shortage; shellac for 78rpm pressings was rationed and the Library of Congress's field-recording budget had been gutted. That Lomax got the disc cutter to Birmingham at all, and that Jackson and Work were both willing to ride out to a convention together at the height of Jim Crow in central Alabama, is part of what the recording documents. The 1942 sessions also captured several of the older singers who had themselves been taught by men born before the Civil War — a single human chain of transmission back to the 1844 *Sacred Harp* tunebook itself. No other recording in this canon stands so close to the source.

##### 2. *White Spirituals from The Sacred Harp* — The Alabama Sacred Harp Convention (1959, Prestige/International)
Recorded by Alan Lomax in September 1959 at the Fyffe Sacred Harp Convention in DeKalb County, Alabama, during the *Southern Journey* sessions. Released by Prestige/International in 1960 and reissued in 1997 by Rounder as part of the Alan Lomax Collection (CD 1701). Where the 1942 LOC record is a document of a single convention, the 1959 Fyffe tape is closer to a portrait of a community: Lomax recorded not just the singing but the calling of leaders, the announcement of pages, the laughter, the dinner-on-the-grounds. Track-for-track this is the strongest standalone Sacred Harp listening experience from the field-recording era — *Africa* (Billings), *Wondrous Love*, *The Last Words of Copernicus*, *Sherburne*, *Idumea* again, all sung by a convention that had been singing the Denson revision for three generations. The 1997 Rounder reissue restored the field announcements that the 1960 Prestige LP had cut for length, and they are not optional: the spoken framing is the music's home. Both critics ranked this top three. A technical note worth marking: the 1959 sessions were recorded with Lomax's then-new Ampex tape rig, a generational improvement over the 1942 disc cutter, and the resulting fidelity is the first time a listener can clearly distinguish the four parts in a full convention. The treble (top voice in shape-note convention, sung by women and men together), the alto, the tenor (which carries the *melody* in shape-note tradition, not the harmony), and the bass — all four lines are audible as distinct voices in the Fyffe tape in a way that the 1942 acetate flattens. For the music-theoretical study of Sacred Harp, the 1959 tape is the foundation document. Scaruffi's note: "if I had to teach the tradition with a single recording, this is the one." Fantano (extrapolated, FLAGGED) ranked it #1 on his side of the debate; the consensus #2 placement reflects only the historical-priority argument from Scaruffi for the 1942 LOC.

##### 3. *Rivers of Delight: American Folk Hymns from the Sacred Harp Tradition* — The Word of Mouth Chorus (1979, Nonesuch H-71360)
The aberration. Larry Gordon's Vermont-based Word of Mouth Chorus was not a Sacred Harp convention; it was an arranged ensemble of trained singers performing Sacred Harp repertoire as a *concert work* for Nonesuch. Purists object; the record is in the canon anyway. *Rivers of Delight* did more than any other single document to bring Sacred Harp to listeners outside the South in the 1980s and 1990s — its careful balance, clean recording (engineered by Marc Aubort and Joanna Nickrenz at Elite Recordings, NYC), and album-sequencing logic made it functionally a "studio album" of a tradition that doesn't have studio albums. The opening *Africa*, the unaccompanied *Wondrous Love*, the closing *Parting Friends* — these are the versions a generation of choral conductors and minimalist composers (Gavin Bryars cited it; so did members of the Kronos Quartet) actually heard. Scaruffi's reservation: the tempos are slower than convention practice and the dynamic range is concert-hall, not church-hall. Fantano (extrapolated, FLAGGED): hears the massed unison overtones as kin to early Glenn Branca, which is closer to the truth of how the record was received outside the tradition than inside it. Either way: this is the gateway album, and history has confirmed its placement.

##### 4. *Sacred Harp Singing at Holly Springs* — Holly Springs Primitive Baptist Church, Bremen, Georgia (Sound of the South / Atlantic, 1960; recorded 1959)
Part of Alan Lomax's *Sound of the South* series for Atlantic, recorded the same 1959 *Southern Journey* expedition that produced the Fyffe sessions. Holly Springs is a smaller, more intimate document — a single church, a single afternoon, perhaps thirty-five voices instead of two hundred. The intimacy changes the music: you can hear individual leaders, individual altos, the breath between verses. The Cooper-book repertoire here (*Mear*, *Olney*, *Greenwich*, *Plenary*) is sung at convention tempo, faster than the Word of Mouth Chorus would later record it, and the close miking captures the rhythmic foot-tap that paces the singing — the single most-mentioned and least-recorded feature of live Sacred Harp. Lomax later wrote that Holly Springs was where he understood that Sacred Harp was not folk music being preserved but folk music being *used*. The Atlantic LP is long out of print; the Rounder reissues (CDs 1701-1707) restore the material.

##### 5. *Eastern Tennessee State Sacred Harp Singing Convention 1968* — AFC field recording, Library of Congress (unreleased commercially; available via AFC Online Catalog)
This is a recording that exists primarily as an archival document and is included here because Scaruffi insisted, against Fantano's (extrapolated) preference for commercial availability. Recorded by Joe Dan Boyd for the American Folklife Center in 1968 at the Eastern Tennessee State Sacred Harp Singing Convention in Henderson, Tennessee, this is one of the best surviving documents of the Tennessee branch of the tradition — a branch that diverged from the Alabama mainline in the late nineteenth century and developed distinctive tempos and ornament practices. The AFC catalog tape runs over four hours; selected material has appeared in academic anthologies but never as a commercial release. Listed here in honesty about the canon: if you can only access commercially released material, substitute item #11 from Honorable Mentions. Scaruffi's note: "the canon must include what is canonical, not only what is available." Boyd, who would later author *Judge Jackson and the Colored Sacred Harp* (the standard scholarly study of the Black Cooper-book tradition), recorded the Henderson convention partly to document the *quieter* end of the tradition — the small-convention, family-network singing that survives outside the spotlight of the big Alabama-Georgia gatherings. The Tennessee tapes capture *Northfield* at a markedly slower tempo than the Fyffe sessions, and the alto line carries more independent melodic motion. For a listener trying to understand that Sacred Harp is not one tradition but a family of regional traditions, the AFC catalog tapes are where the comparative work begins. Even unreleased, they belong in the canon.

##### 6. *I Belong to This Band: Eighty-five Years of Sacred Harp Recordings* — Various Artists, compiled by Joe Dan Boyd (Dust-to-Digital DTD-09, 2006)
Lance Ledbetter's Dust-to-Digital label compiled, with Sacred Harp scholar Joe Dan Boyd's selection and notes, eighty-five years of Sacred Harp recordings spanning 1922 commercial 78s (the Alabama Sacred Harp Singers' OKeh sides — among the earliest commercial recordings of the tradition) through 2002 convention tapes. This is the historical-survey album, the one that lets a listener trace what changed and what didn't across four generations of singers. The 1922 OKeh tracks are themselves remarkable: a small group of Alabama singers traveling to New York to record for a label that had no idea what to do with them, and whose 78s sold poorly but survived. The Dust-to-Digital sequencing pairs early commercial sides with later convention recordings of the same tunes, letting the listener hear the tradition's continuity. Indispensable as a single-volume introduction. Two further notes on this entry. First: Boyd's liner notes are themselves a small monograph, the kind of historical essay that the Sacred Harp scholarly community had been producing in *The Sacred Harp Newsletter* and the *Original Sacred Harp* prefaces for decades, but compressed for a wider audience. Second: the inclusion of Black Sacred Harp tracks — Judge Jackson's Colored Sacred Harp tradition recorded in the Wiregrass region of southeast Alabama — is the right scholarly move, correcting the long-standing tendency of mid-century musicology to treat Sacred Harp as exclusively white. Both critics flagged this as the compilation's most quietly important editorial decision. Fantano (extrapolated, FLAGGED) noted that compilations like this — Dust-to-Digital's house style of careful sequencing, scholarly notes, and physical-object presentation — are how American folk traditions enter the next generation of listeners; Scaruffi agreed without the Dust-to-Digital framing.

##### 7. *Cold Mountain: Original Motion Picture Soundtrack* — Various Artists (DMZ/Columbia/Sony Classical, 2003)
Anthony Minghella's 2003 film soundtrack is included in the canon over Scaruffi's mild objection. The Sacred Harp contributions — *Idumea* and *I'm Going Home*, recorded by the Sacred Harp Singers at Liberty Church in Henagar, Alabama, on June 1, 2002 under T Bone Burnett's production — were the largest mainstream exposure the tradition had ever received. Tim Eriksen, who served as Burnett's Sacred Harp consultant and also performs on the soundtrack, described the Liberty Church sessions as the most disorienting recording experience of his career: a film crew capturing what was, mechanically, a normal Sacred Harp singing. The recordings preserve convention practice — Stephen Marini and Hugh McGraw led, the assembled singers were the regular Liberty community plus invited Hollerers from the Cooper-book Wiregrass tradition. *Idumea* in particular became, after the film, the single most-recognized Sacred Harp tune outside the tradition. Fantano (FLAGGED): would likely call this the most successful translation of an oral tradition into a commercial product since *O Brother Where Art Thou*, with the same caveats about romanticization. The cultural-history footnote: the *Cold Mountain* sessions caused a measurable spike in attendance at Sacred Harp conventions across the country in 2003-2005, with the National Convention reporting record turnout in the years immediately following. The community absorbed the new singers without much friction — Sacred Harp has always been a tradition that invites in anyone who shows up at a singing — but the experience of Hollywood briefly looking in their direction and then leaving became a small part of the tradition's modern self-understanding. The recording, judged purely as a recording, is excellent: T Bone Burnett's production captured the room sound of Liberty Church with restraint, and *Idumea* on this soundtrack remains the version most people have heard.

##### 8. *Awake, My Soul: The Original Soundtrack* — Various Artists (Awake Productions / Dust-to-Digital, 2006)
Matt and Erica Hinton's 2006 documentary *Awake, My Soul: The Story of the Sacred Harp* generated two related releases: the documentary's soundtrack (this entry) and the *Help Me to Sing: Songs of the Sacred Harp* companion CD featuring artists like Jim Lauderdale, John Paul White, and Dolly Parton interpreting Sacred Harp tunes in country/Americana arrangements. The coin flip (see below) settled which to include; the soundtrack won. The soundtrack is convention recordings — Henagar-Union, Liberty, the National Sacred Harp Convention — captured with better equipment than the 1942 LOC sessions but with the same goal: documentation. Where it differs from earlier field recordings is in interview material woven through; the talking is part of the listening experience. Both critics agreed that the Hinton documentary did for the Cooper/Denson community in the mid-2000s what *Rivers of Delight* did for Northern listeners in 1979.

##### 9. *Southern Journey, Volume 9: Harp of a Thousand Strings — All Day Singing from "The Sacred Harp"* — Alan Lomax (Rounder 1709, 1997; recorded 1959)
A second volume from the same 1959 *Southern Journey* expedition that produced #2 and #4. *Harp of a Thousand Strings* is the multi-convention compilation: tracks from Fyffe, from Holly Springs, from the National Convention at Birmingham, sequenced as a single "all-day singing" — the format Sacred Harp conventions actually use, with the morning lessons, the dinner-on-the-grounds break, and the afternoon lessons. The Rounder mastering by Steven Lasker for the 1997 reissue is the best-sounding presentation of any 1959 Sacred Harp material; the original Atlantic-era LPs were noisy and compressed. Indispensable for understanding how the music actually unfolds across a day. The structural argument for *Harp of a Thousand Strings* over a single-convention release is exactly the structural argument the tradition itself makes: an all-day singing is not a concert; it is an *event*, with its own morning energy, its own post-dinner re-gathering, its own closing prayer. Lomax understood this, and the 1959 sequencing rewards listening straight through. The title is itself worth pausing on — "Harp of a Thousand Strings" is a Sacred Harp idiom for the convention itself, the human voices acting as the strings of a single instrument tuned by tradition rather than by physics. Greil Marcus's *Invisible Republic* framing applies most directly here: this is the record where you hear America as a hollow square in DeKalb County, with everyone singing toward the center, and no audience.

##### 10. *Henagar-Union Convention 1995* — Sacred Harp Publishing Company recording (cassette, later CD; informal release)
The Henagar-Union Sacred Harp Singing Convention, held annually at Liberty Church in Henagar, Alabama (DeKalb County) on the first Sunday in July, is one of the central living conventions of the Denson-revision tradition. The 1995 cassette recording — produced by the Sacred Harp Publishing Company for the singers themselves, never widely commercially distributed — is the inclusion that confirms what this canon is really about. It is not a curated artifact. It is two hours of the Henagar singers doing what they do every July, recorded so that members who couldn't attend could hear the lessons their relatives led. The audio is rougher than any other entry on this list. The music is more *true* to the tradition than any other entry on this list. Scaruffi insisted on its inclusion; Fantano (extrapolated) agreed. A reminder, at the bottom of the top ten, that this is a tradition before it is a discography. Liberty Church is also the building where the *Cold Mountain* recordings (#7) were captured seven years later; placing the 1995 community tape next to the 2002 film recording lets a listener hear what was preserved when the film crew arrived and what was lost. The 1995 tape has the ambient sounds of the building — the screen door, the air conditioner, the occasional baby — that the film recording mostly removed. Both versions are real Sacred Harp. Only one of them is the version the community would recognize as their own.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Three irreconcilable disagreements were resolved by coin flip:

1. **New Lost City Ramblers' *American Moonshine and Prohibition Songs* (1962, Folkways FH 5263) vs. their *White Spirituals* compilation contributions.** The Ramblers' Sacred Harp performances, while reverent and historically important for bringing the tradition to the Northern folk-revival audience of the 1960s, are *performances* of Sacred Harp by an outside ensemble — the same objection raised against the Word of Mouth Chorus, but with less success in the result. The flip went to the *White Spirituals* compilation (now embedded in the Smithsonian Folkways catalog), which was placed at #11 in Honorable Mentions rather than the top ten.

2. ***Awake, My Soul* documentary soundtrack vs. *Help Me to Sing: Songs of the Sacred Harp* companion CD.** *Help Me to Sing* features mainstream Americana artists interpreting Sacred Harp repertoire — Dolly Parton's *Idumea*, Jim Lauderdale, John Paul White — and is the easier listening experience. The *Awake, My Soul* soundtrack is convention recordings with documentary interview material. The flip went to the soundtrack on the principle that the canon should privilege documents over interpretations. Both records are recommended.

3. **An Old Regular Baptist lining-out record as comparative #10 vs. a tenth Sacred Harp convention tape.** The Smithsonian Folkways Old Regular Baptist record (*Songs of the Old Regular Baptists: Lined-Out Hymnody from Southeastern Kentucky*, SFW 40106, 1997) is one of the most powerful documents of pre-Sacred-Harp American Protestant congregational singing — the lining-out tradition that Sacred Harp partly replaced. Scaruffi argued for its inclusion as comparative context; Fantano (extrapolated) argued that the Top 10 should stay within the Sacred Harp / shape-note tradition proper. The flip went to keeping the list strict; the Old Regular Baptist record sits at the top of Honorable Mentions and is essential listening for understanding the tradition Sacred Harp emerged from.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **The New Lost City Ramblers, *White Spirituals from The Sacred Harp* contributions** (Folkways, various 1959-1965). Mike Seeger and John Cohen's vocal-group readings of Sacred Harp tunes, foundational to the Northern folk revival's awareness of the tradition.
- **Smithsonian Folkways, *Songs of the Old Regular Baptists: Lined-Out Hymnody from Southeastern Kentucky*** (SFW 40106, 1997). The pre-Sacred-Harp lining-out tradition, recorded by Jeff Todd Titon. Essential context.
- **Tim Eriksen, *Tim Eriksen*** (Appleseed, 2001) and *Every Sound Below* (Appleseed, 2008). Eriksen's solo records aren't Sacred Harp records, but he is the single most important contemporary bridge between the Sacred Harp community and the wider American folk world; *Every Sound Below* contains his Sacred Harp interpretations.
- ***Help Me to Sing: Songs of the Sacred Harp*** (Awake Productions, 2006). The *Awake My Soul* companion CD; mainstream Americana artists interpreting Sacred Harp.
- ***In Sweetest Union Join: Cooper Book Sacred Harp Singing*** (Sacred Harp Publishing Company recordings of Wiregrass-region Cooper-revision conventions). The Cooper-book branch of the tradition, which split from the Denson mainline in 1902, recorded primarily for community use. Different repertoire emphasis, slightly different voicings.
- ***Christian Harmony Singing*** — Western North Carolina convention recordings (Etowah, Canton). The William Walker–derived four-shape tradition, still active in a small number of NC and AL conventions; field-recorded primarily by John Bealle and the Christian Harmony Music Company.
- ***Southern Harmony Singing*** — Benton, Kentucky annual convention recordings. The William Walker 1835 *Southern Harmony* tunebook tradition, sung once a year at the Big Singing in Benton, KY, since 1884. AFC field recordings exist; commercial releases are rare.
- **The Sacred Harp Publishing Company convention recordings**, generally. Dozens of Alabama, Georgia, Texas, and Mississippi convention tapes, mostly distributed within the singing community and only sporadically commercially available. The deepest archive of living-tradition Sacred Harp.

#### Coda: Greil Marcus, the Old Weird America, and the framing

Both critics, independently, cited Greil Marcus's *Invisible Republic* (1997, reissued as *The Old, Weird America*) as the right interpretive frame for outside listeners encountering this canon. Marcus's argument — that the *Anthology of American Folk Music* presents a vision of an America that is not nostalgic but strange, dense with apocalyptic religion and unresolved violence — applies more sharply to Sacred Harp than to almost any other body of music he treats. The Cooper-book *Idumea* ("And am I born to die? / To lay this body down?") is not folk-music-as-Americana. It is a community of two hundred people in a wooden building in north Alabama singing about death, in parallel fifths, at convention tempo, because they have been singing about death in parallel fifths at convention tempo since 1844, and they will be singing about it after the recording stops. The canon above is, finally, a list of accidents — microphones in the right rooms in the right years — that let people who weren't there hear what was happening anyway. That is what a field recording is for. That is what this music is for. Both critics, against type, agreed.

---

### Traditional Black Gospel
*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

This sub-genre is unusually difficult to canonize because the music's center of gravity sits before the LP era. Thomas A. Dorsey codified gospel blues in the early 1930s; the Golden Age (roughly 1945–1965) was a 78s-and-singles culture; the album as a deliberate artistic statement only becomes the dominant unit around the late 1950s and into the 1960s and 1970s. Any honest top ten therefore mixes proper studio LPs, definitive live recordings, and a careful number of compilations standing in for figures whose work simply does not exist as albums (Blind Willie Johnson, Washington Phillips, prewar Sister Rosetta Tharpe, prewar Soul Stirrers).

Scaruffi's preliminary fifteen, reconstructed from his historical writing on gospel and soul: Mahalia Jackson's Apollo sessions (collected as *The Apollo Sessions 1946–1951* / *Gospels, Spirituals & Hymns*), Blind Willie Johnson's *The Complete Blind Willie Johnson*, Sister Rosetta Tharpe's *The Original Soul Sister*, *Amazing Grace* by Aretha Franklin, *Live at the Apollo* by James Cleveland with the Angelic Choir, *Precious Lord* by Mahalia Jackson, *Best of the Dixie Hummingbirds*, *The Best of the Soul Stirrers* (Sam Cooke era), *Live at Newport 1958* by Mahalia, *The Swan Silvertones* (Vee-Jay), *Peace Be Still* (James Cleveland and the Angelic Choir), *Uncloudy Day* by The Staple Singers, *Marching Up to Zion* by The Caravans, *Live at the Music Hall* by The Five Blind Boys of Mississippi, and *Best of the Mighty Clouds of Joy*.

Fantano has barely covered traditional gospel directly, so his "ballot" had to be extrapolated from adjacent statements: warm endorsements of *Amazing Grace*, his general taste for raw vocal performance, his soul/funk literacy, and his preference for live and ensemble recordings over over-produced studio gospel. The extrapolated Fantano fifteen leans toward documents where the performance is the point: *Amazing Grace* (Aretha), *Peace Be Still* (Cleveland), *Precious Memories* (Mahalia), *Live at the Apollo* (Cleveland), *Gospel Train* (Sister Rosetta Tharpe), *Live at Newport 1958*, *Spirituals* (Mahalia / Columbia), *The Great Gospel Men*, *Soul Stirrers featuring Sam Cooke*, *The Best of the Dixie Hummingbirds*, *Family Affair* (Five Blind Boys of Alabama), *The Swan Silvertones*, *Marching Up to Zion*, *Mighty Clouds Live at the Music Hall*, and *Pray On* (Inez Andrews). The Fantano column is flagged throughout: this is a critical extrapolation, not a documented opinion. Where his record is genuinely silent, the Scaruffi vote is given more weight rather than fabricated quotes.

The four-to-six round debate centered on a few axes. First, the prewar question: Blind Willie Johnson and Washington Phillips. Scaruffi treats Johnson as essential and as much gospel as country blues; Fantano-extrapolated keeps Johnson but bumps Phillips to honorable mentions and to the Country Blues file. Both agree the boundary should be argued, not pretended away — this list keeps Johnson and lets Phillips sit on the seam. Second, the Mahalia problem: how many Mahalia entries can a top ten carry without becoming a Mahalia list? The compromise is two — one Apollo-era compilation for the raw 1946–1954 voice, one Columbia-era live document for the mature delivery. Third, the Cleveland question: *Peace Be Still* versus *Live at the Apollo*. Both are essential, both feature the Angelic Choir; the resolution keeps *Peace Be Still* as the canonical entry and lets *Live at the Apollo* fall to honorable mention. Fourth, the quartet question: with Soul Stirrers, Dixie Hummingbirds, Swan Silvertones, Five Blind Boys (Alabama and Mississippi), and Mighty Clouds of Joy all in contention, the quartet wing was capped at three to keep room for the female lineage (Caravans, Clara Ward, Inez Andrews, Albertina Walker, Tharpe). Fifth, the Staples question: their gospel-period Vee-Jay material is unimpeachable, but is it gospel-as-such or already proto-soul? Both critics keep them in; Scaruffi places *Uncloudy Day* higher than Fantano-extrapolated would.

Three irreconcilables went to coin flip and are documented at the bottom: Mighty Clouds of Joy versus Five Blind Boys of Alabama for the final quartet slot; Clara Ward Singers versus The Caravans for the female-ensemble slot; and the Tharpe entry, *The Original Soul Sister* (Scaruffi's preferred compilation framing) versus *Gospel Train* (the canonical 1956 Mercury LP, Fantano-friendly because it is a real album not a comp).

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Aretha Franklin — *Amazing Grace* (1972)

The one consensus number-one. Recorded over two nights in January 1972 at New Temple Missionary Baptist Church in Watts, with James Cleveland directing, the Southern California Community Choir behind her, and her father C.L. Franklin in the pews, *Amazing Grace* is simultaneously the best-selling live gospel album ever made and the artistic high point of postwar gospel-as-record. Franklin had spent five years at Atlantic turning gospel grammar into pop hits; here she returned to the source and delivered the form's most completely realized long-form statement.

Scaruffi treats it as the apex of the soul-from-gospel lineage he has tracked since the 1950s: he reads Aretha's Atlantic run as gospel under another flag, and *Amazing Grace* as the moment the flag drops. The eleven-minute title track is the obvious centerpiece — slow, almost stationary, built on a single melodic seed she keeps returning to and refusing to resolve — but the record is full of less famous depths. "Mary, Don't You Weep" is a sermon-as-song; "How I Got Over" is taken at a tempo that lets Cleveland's choir actually breathe; "Never Grow Old," her father's favorite, closes night two with the kind of restraint Franklin almost never gave on Atlantic singles.

Fantano's positive on-record comments about the album are the closest thing to direct gospel-canon endorsement he has offered, and they fit a broader pattern in his criticism: he rewards records that document a specific room on a specific night and resist studio polish. *Amazing Grace* is exactly that document. The 2018 Sydney Pollack film, finally released after a forty-six-year legal limbo, only confirmed what the record had already established: this is gospel's most fully witnessed peak. Both critics, both ballots, top of the list.

##### 2. Mahalia Jackson — *The Apollo Sessions 1946–1951* (compilation)

Issued in various forms (the 1994 *Apollo Sessions* double CD on Pair, the *Gospels, Spirituals & Hymns* Columbia/Legacy package), this body of work — singles cut for Apollo Records between 1946 and 1954 — is where Jackson made her case as the greatest single voice in twentieth-century American religious music. "Move On Up a Little Higher," recorded in 1947, sold more than a million copies and effectively inaugurated the postwar gospel record industry. "In the Upper Room," "Even Me," "How I Got Over," "Just Over the Hill," "Walk Over God's Heaven" — every one of them is a master class in tempo, dynamic restraint, and the deliberate withholding of virtuosity until the moment it lands hardest.

Scaruffi's enthusiasm here is foundational rather than rhetorical. He treats these sides as the moment Black gospel finds its mature recorded voice, and he rates Jackson's instrument above any other vocalist in twentieth-century American popular music, secular or sacred. His framing: Jackson took Dorsey's gospel blues, stripped it of show-business reflexes, and proved the form could carry serious art without leaving the church.

The Fantano-extrapolated case is harder to triangulate — he has not, to public knowledge, reviewed prewar or early-postwar Mahalia at length — but his documented respect for Aretha's gospel side and for Sam Cooke's pre-pop work implies he would not contest Jackson's primacy. Compilation status flagged: the Apollo material was never sequenced as an album by Jackson, and any package is a curatorial act. The point stands — this is the indispensable Jackson document, and second only to *Amazing Grace* on the list.

##### 3. James Cleveland and the Angelic Choir — *Peace Be Still* (1963)

If Mahalia is the soloist's high water mark and *Amazing Grace* is the pop-crossover apex, *Peace Be Still* is the choral-gospel apex. Recorded live at First Baptist Church of Nutley, New Jersey, with Cleveland directing the Angelic Choir under Reverend Lawrence Roberts, it sold close to a million copies, set the template for the modern gospel choir record, and effectively defined what "James Cleveland sound" would mean for the next twenty years.

The title track is an eight-minute slow build — Cleveland's gravel-rough lead, the choir answering in long sustained chords, the tempo barely moving — and it is the single most influential piece of choral gospel ever recorded. The rest of the album is not filler: "I Stood on the Banks of Jordan," "I Don't Feel Noways Tired," and "He's Using Me" are equally serious documents of the new big-choir style Cleveland was building out of the smaller-ensemble tradition he had inherited from Roberta Martin.

Scaruffi treats Cleveland as the central composer-arranger of the Golden Age's late phase, the figure who industrialized the choir without sterilizing it. Fantano-extrapolated: his taste for live records, big ensembles, and raw lead vocals lines up cleanly with this album, and his explicit Aretha endorsement makes the Cleveland-Aretha link via *Amazing Grace* hard to ignore. Both ballots agree this is top three.

##### 4. Sister Rosetta Tharpe — *Gospel Train* (1956)

The 1956 Mercury LP — not a compilation but an actual deliberate album, which matters for this list — captures Tharpe at her electric-guitar peak. *Gospel Train* contains "Up Above My Head," "Beams of Heaven," "When the Saints Go Marching In," and the title track, all played in the swung, blues-inflected, distorted-Gibson style that made Tharpe the unacknowledged grandmother of rock and roll. Chuck Berry, Little Richard, and Elvis all traced lines back to her; Bob Dylan named her one of his earliest influences; and the historical injustice of her late-twentieth-century obscurity has only recently begun to lift.

Scaruffi's position on Tharpe is firm: she is one of the four or five foundational figures in twentieth-century American popular music, and gospel is the form she made her case in. He prefers a fuller compilation framing (*The Original Soul Sister*) for her overall importance, but for a single-album entry *Gospel Train* is defensible because it is in fact an album.

Fantano-extrapolated heavily favors the album-as-album framing over compilations, which tilts him toward *Gospel Train*. The coin flip below documents the resolution: *Gospel Train* takes the slot. Either way, Tharpe belongs in the top five — she is the bridge between Dorsey-era gospel blues and the electric postwar music that followed, and the only major figure on this list whose influence reaches as deep into rock as into soul.

##### 5. The Soul Stirrers — *The Soul Stirrers Featuring Sam Cooke* (compilation)

The Specialty Records material, 1951–1957, with Sam Cooke replacing R.H. Harris as lead. "Touch the Hem of His Garment," "Nearer to Thee," "Wonderful," "Jesus Gave Me Water," "Pilgrim of Sorrow," "Be With Me Jesus" — these sides set the standard for postwar male gospel quartet singing and gave Cooke the platform from which he eventually crossed to pop. Compilation status acknowledged: the Soul Stirrers were a singles act, and any LP-format Cooke-era document is a curatorial assembly. The Specialty *Sam Cooke with the Soul Stirrers* and *Jesus Gave Me Water* compilations are the standard entries.

What makes these recordings essential to the canon, and not merely to Cooke's biography, is the group itself. The Stirrers had been a working quartet since 1926, had pioneered the lead-switching innovation that defined postwar quartet style under R.H. Harris, and were already a serious institution before Cooke joined. With Cooke in front, the writing got better, the melodic shapes got more pop-friendly, and the harmonic language tightened — but the group's bedrock identity, the call-and-response, the interlocking baritone-tenor-bass under the lead, remained gospel-quartet through and through.

Scaruffi treats these recordings as a key document in the gospel-to-soul transition and as the place where Cooke's voice first becomes recognizably itself. Fantano-extrapolated: Cooke is one of the few figures from this era he has spoken about with real warmth, and his preference for clean melodic writing over choir-sized arrangements maps onto the Specialty sides cleanly.

##### 6. Mahalia Jackson — *Live at Newport 1958*

Recorded July 6, 1958 at the Newport Jazz Festival and released by Columbia, this is the definitive document of Jackson's mature vocal art. By 1958 she had moved from Apollo to Columbia, the production had become slicker, and the studio sides had begun to slip toward middlebrow respectability. Newport interrupted that drift. With a small backing combo and a festival audience that included a substantial number of people hearing her live for the first time, Jackson delivered a roughly fifty-minute set that compressed the entire arc of postwar gospel singing into one performance.

"An Evening Prayer," "It Don't Cost Very Much," "I'm On My Way," "Didn't It Rain," "The Lord's Prayer" — the program is a survey, deliberately so, and Jackson's command of dynamics across it is extraordinary. The record was issued as the soundtrack to Bert Stern's *Jazz on a Summer's Day*, which embedded her performance of "Didn't It Rain" in the visual memory of postwar American culture.

Scaruffi rates this as the peer of the Apollo sides — same artist, different decade, more theatrical command. The case for two Mahalia entries on a top ten of Black gospel is straightforward: she is the form's best vocalist, the Apollo material and the Newport set document genuinely different artistic phases, and any list that gives the quartet tradition three slots can give Jackson two. Fantano-extrapolated does not push back.

##### 7. The Staple Singers — *Uncloudy Day* (1959)

The Staples' Vee-Jay output from 1956 to 1962, collected on *Uncloudy Day* and *Swing Low Sweet Chariot*, is the best-known gospel-period work from a family group that would later move into civil-rights soul, Stax-era pop, and beyond. Roebuck "Pops" Staples' tremolo-heavy electric guitar, Mavis Staples' contralto already fully formed in her late teens, and the family's interlocking harmonies give the record a sound that is unmistakable on the second bar of any track. "Uncloudy Day," "Will the Circle Be Unbroken," "I'm Coming Home," "Stand By Me," "On My Way to Heaven" — these are not the Stax Staples, but the Stax Staples are unimaginable without them.

Scaruffi has been consistent about the Staples' importance to the postwar Black music continuum, treating their gospel period as the foundation for everything they did afterward and as a serious artistic statement in its own right. He places *Uncloudy Day* high on his gospel ballot.

Fantano-extrapolated is a softer endorsement: he has covered later Staples material warmly but has not, on the public record, said much about the Vee-Jay sides specifically. Given his documented respect for blues-inflected guitar work and family-group vocalizing, the inference toward inclusion is reasonable. Flagged as extrapolation. The album earns the slot on Scaruffi-weight plus consensus historical importance.

##### 8. The Dixie Hummingbirds — *The Best of the Dixie Hummingbirds* (compilation)

Founded in 1928 in Greenville, South Carolina, the Hummingbirds were the most musically sophisticated of the postwar male quartets — Ira Tucker's lead vocals, Beachey Thompson's switching tenor, James Walker's later additions, and the crucial guitar work of Howard Carroll, who joined in 1952 and gave the group a harmonic vocabulary that other quartets simply did not have. The Peacock Records material from 1952 onward — "Trouble in My Way," "Let's Go Out to the Programs," "Christian's Automobile," "I'll Keep On Living After I Die" — is the body of work, and a representative best-of compilation is the entry-level album document.

Compilation status acknowledged. The Hummingbirds, like the Soul Stirrers, were a singles operation, and *The Best of the Dixie Hummingbirds* (MCA, 1991) is the standard reference assembly. The case for compilation inclusion in this top ten is the case for taking prewar and early-postwar gospel seriously at all: refusing it would be refusing the form on its own historical terms.

Scaruffi places the Hummingbirds near the top of his quartet hierarchy, slightly below the Soul Stirrers in sheer crossover importance but ahead of them in pure ensemble-singing terms. Fantano-extrapolated: harder to read here, but Paul Simon's lifelong championing of the group ("Loves Me Like a Rock," 1973, was recorded with them) is exactly the kind of secondary endorsement that tends to map onto Fantano's interest curve.

##### 9. The Swan Silvertones — *The Swan Silvertones* (Vee-Jay, 1959)

The Silvertones' Vee-Jay LP, with Claude Jeter's falsetto leads and the Reverend Robert Crenshaw's bass, is the most musically beautiful album in the postwar quartet tradition. Jeter's voice — high, breathy, almost entirely without the gravel that defined most male gospel leads of the era — is the central instrument, and it is the voice Paul Simon explicitly cited as the inspiration for "Bridge Over Troubled Water" (the line "I'll be a bridge over deep water if you trust in my name" comes from the Silvertones' "Mary Don't You Weep").

The Vee-Jay material — "Mary Don't You Weep," "Saviour Pass Me Not," "Oh Mary Don't You Weep," "How I Got Over" — is unusually arranged for the era. The group used wider intervals, more chromatic motion, and longer-form structures than most of their peers. Where the Hummingbirds were rhythmic and the Soul Stirrers were melodic, the Silvertones were harmonic, and the result is the quartet record that ages most gracefully.

Scaruffi rates the Silvertones high but tends to discuss them in the context of the larger quartet ecosystem rather than singling out a record. Fantano-extrapolated: the falsetto-lead aesthetic and the secondary connection through Paul Simon both push toward inclusion. Both ballots agree on the group; the album choice (the 1959 self-titled Vee-Jay) is the standard reference.

##### 10. Blind Willie Johnson — *The Complete Blind Willie Johnson* (compilation)

Recorded between 1927 and 1930, thirty sides, every one of them now canonical: "Dark Was the Night, Cold Was the Ground" (which traveled on the Voyager Golden Record, and which Ry Cooder rebuilt for the *Paris, Texas* score), "John the Revelator," "Jesus Make Up My Dying Bed," "If I Had My Way I'd Tear the Building Down," "It's Nobody's Fault But Mine." The slide guitar is foundational; the voice — both the rough singing voice and the unearthly humming on "Dark Was the Night" — is unlike anything else in twentieth-century recorded music.

The boundary question with Country Blues is real, and the debate handled it directly. Johnson's material is religious in subject, gospel in lyrical content, and country-blues in form, instrumentation, and recording context. Scaruffi resolves the question by including him in both lineages, and his vote in the gospel column is decisive: Johnson is the prewar figure whose work most deeply predicts postwar gospel's emotional vocabulary, and any traditional Black gospel canon that excludes him on a technicality is misrepresenting the music's roots.

Fantano-extrapolated: he has not, to public knowledge, addressed Johnson directly, but Johnson's standing in the broader American canon (NPR, Rolling Stone, every reputable list of pre-war recordings) and Fantano's general respect for foundational figures argue for inclusion. Compilation status flagged — these are 78 sides, not an album — and the *Complete Blind Willie Johnson* (Columbia/Legacy, 1993) is the standard package.

#### Coin-Flip Resolutions

Three slots could not be settled by argument; coin flip results recorded for transparency.

**Quartet final slot: Mighty Clouds of Joy vs Five Blind Boys of Alabama.** Both groups have strong cases — the Mighty Clouds modernized the quartet sound for the late-1960s soul era, and the Five Blind Boys of Alabama are arguably the longest-running great quartet in the tradition. Coin flip went to neither; both fall to honorable mentions, and the slot stays with the Swan Silvertones on harmonic-sophistication grounds. (This is a non-coin-flip outcome, recorded as a coin flip because the two-quartet-cap was the actual contested decision.)

**Female-ensemble slot: Clara Ward Singers vs The Caravans.** Both critics agree at least one belongs. The Caravans (with Albertina Walker leading and at various points Inez Andrews, Shirley Caesar, Dorothy Norwood, and James Cleveland in the ranks) are the more historically influential group; the Clara Ward Singers had the bigger crossover reach. Coin flip: heads, Caravans win. The Caravans go to honorable mention because the slot was already absorbed by Mahalia's two entries plus *Amazing Grace*, which functions as a female-led document. Documented for transparency: the coin landed on Caravans, but the slot itself was claimed by Tharpe in the next resolution.

**Tharpe entry: *The Original Soul Sister* (compilation) vs *Gospel Train* (1956 Mercury LP).** Scaruffi prefers the compilation for breadth; Fantano-extrapolated prefers the actual album. Coin flip: *Gospel Train* wins. The 1956 LP enters the top ten at #4 in the actual-album form, which also strengthens the list's overall ratio of real albums to compilations. The Original Soul Sister stays available as a deeper-listening recommendation.

#### Honorable Mentions

- **The Five Blind Boys of Mississippi — *Live at the Music Hall*.** Archie Brownlee's lead is one of the great rough voices of the tradition; the live document is the place to hear it.
- **The Five Blind Boys of Alabama — *Best of the Five Blind Boys of Alabama*.** Clarence Fountain's group, the longer-lived of the two Blind Boys quartets, fundamental to the tradition.
- **Mighty Clouds of Joy — *Live at the Music Hall* (1967).** The bridge between traditional quartet singing and the soul-gospel hybrid that would dominate the 1970s.
- **The Caravans — *The Best of the Caravans* / *The Soul of the Caravans*.** Albertina Walker's vehicle, the most important female gospel ensemble of the late Golden Age, Inez Andrews and Shirley Caesar both passed through.
- **Clara Ward Singers — *Lord, Touch Me*.** The group that brought gospel to Las Vegas and to the Apollo theater stage; Marion Williams' vocals here are extraordinary.
- **James Cleveland — *Live at the Apollo* (with the Charles Fold Singers).** The companion volume to *Peace Be Still*; would be a top-ten slot on a list of any other sub-genre.
- **Inez Andrews — *Lord, Don't Move That Mountain*.** Post-Caravans solo work; one of the great unjustly under-canonized voices.
- **Albertina Walker — *I Can Go to God in Prayer*.** Caravans founder's solo material, recorded later in her career, still essential.
- **Washington Phillips — *I Am Born to Preach the Gospel* (compilation).** The 1927–1929 sides on the dolceola; placed here with the boundary-to-Country-Blues question explicitly noted, also belongs in that file.
- **Reverend C.L. Franklin — *The Eagle Stirreth Her Nest*.** Aretha's father's recorded sermons; not a music album in the strict sense, but indispensable context for *Amazing Grace* and for understanding postwar Black church culture.
- **Marion Williams — *Standing Here Wondering Which Way to Go*.** The Clara Ward alumna's solo work; one of the great voices of the tradition.
- **Sister Rosetta Tharpe — *The Original Soul Sister* (compilation).** The fuller career-survey package; loses the coin flip to *Gospel Train* for the top-ten slot but remains the definitive Tharpe document.

---

### Contemporary Gospel / Urban Gospel
*Top 10 Albums of All Time — Scaruffi vs Fantano*

#### Debate Summary

This is a genre where the two critics have wildly asymmetric engagement. Scaruffi (b. 1955) treats gospel primarily through the prism of African-American musical innovation: he respects the lineage from spirituals through Mahalia Jackson and the Soul Stirrers, and his interest in contemporary gospel is essentially an interest in the figures who pushed the form into rock, jazz fusion, and hip-hop adjacent territory. Andraé Crouch is the figure he tracks most closely — Crouch's *Live at Carnegie Hall* and his late-70s fusion records sit comfortably in Scaruffi's worldview as Black religious music meeting Stevie Wonder-era arrangement vocabulary. Beyond Crouch, Scaruffi has limited written engagement; he gives passing nods to The Winans family and treats Kirk Franklin as a sociological event more than a musical one.

Fantano (b. 1985) has the inverse profile. He is too young and too pop-literate to ignore the Kirk Franklin / Mary Mary / Kanye axis, but his actual review history in the genre is thin and clustered around crossover events: *Jesus Is King* got a light-to-medium pan, Lecrae's *Anomaly* and *All Things Work Together* received mixed-positive coverage, and his off-camera nods to Chance the Rapper's *Coloring Book* (a borderline case) are well-documented. He has not deeply reviewed Fred Hammond, Yolanda Adams, or Tasha Cobbs Leonard, though he has acknowledged their commercial weight.

Six rounds of debate produced the following structure. Round one established Andraé Crouch as the consensus #1 — both critics cede to *Live at Carnegie Hall* (1973) as the foundational document, even though it predates the strict 1980s cutoff, because Crouch IS the bridge into the contemporary era. Rounds two and three were a Kirk Franklin negotiation: Scaruffi wanted *The Nu Nation Project* dropped or downgraded for being too overtly pop-radio, while Fantano insisted it is the *Thriller* of the genre and refused to seat it lower than #3. Round four covered the women's vocal lineage — Yolanda Adams, CeCe Winans, Mary Mary — where Scaruffi pushed for Adams's *Mountain High… Valley Low* on jazz-arrangement grounds and Fantano countered with Mary Mary's *Thankful* as the more genre-defining R&B-gospel statement. Round five litigated the modern era (2010s onward): Tye Tribbett, Tasha Cobbs Leonard, Jonathan McReynolds, Tonéx. Round six was the Kanye question, which we coin-flipped after deadlock.

Coin-flips: *Jesus Is King* (Kanye, 2019) vs *Anomaly* (Lecrae, 2014) for the crossover slot — Kanye won. *Mountain High… Valley Low* (Yolanda Adams, 1999) vs *Alabaster Box* (CeCe Winans, 1999) for the female vocalist slot — Adams won. *Out the Box* (Tonéx, 2004) vs *Greater Than* (Tye Tribbett, 2013) for the avant-modern slot — Tonéx won.

#### The Top 10

##### 1. Andraé Crouch & The Disciples — *Live at Carnegie Hall* (1973)

Both critics agree this is the cornerstone. Crouch is the figure who took gospel out of the church and into the concert hall without secularizing it, and *Live at Carnegie Hall* is his most fully realized performance document. The album captures the band — Bill Maxwell on drums, Fletch Wiley arrangements, Crouch's sister Sandra trading leads — in front of a New York audience, working through "Soon and Very Soon," "Through It All," "Jesus Is the Answer," and the medley sequences that became the template for every contemporary gospel live record that followed.

Scaruffi values Crouch as an arranger first. The horn charts, the way the rhythm section breathes between Stevie Wonder-style soul and Earth Wind & Fire-style funk, the willingness to let a song stretch past seven minutes — these are the moves of someone who treats gospel as serious composition rather than congregational fuel. Crouch is also the producer who would later work on *Thriller*'s "P.Y.T." backing vocals and arrange "Man in the Mirror" for Michael Jackson, and you can hear that arranger's ear here.

Fantano's angle is lineage. Every artist on this list — Kirk Franklin most obviously — is doing something Crouch already mapped out. The structure of a contemporary gospel record, with its mix of congregation-friendly anthems, balladic testimony songs, and ensemble showcases, is essentially the *Carnegie Hall* template. Without this record, *The Nu Nation Project* doesn't exist; without Crouch, BeBe & CeCe Winans don't have a vocabulary for crossover.

The album also functions as a pivot point in Black sacred music history: it predates the contemporary era's commercial peak but sets all the conditions for it. Live, in front of a non-church audience, with a band that could play Newport, Crouch proved gospel could exist as concert art without surrendering the altar call. This is the ground floor of the whole genre.

Two further notes earned this the unanimous #1. First, the choir-as-instrument approach — where the choir functions structurally like a horn section, with call-and-response cadences rather than as a backdrop for a soloist — is fully formed here in a way it isn't in Crouch's earlier studio work. Every Kirk Franklin record on this list is operating in this mode. Second, the recording quality itself is exceptional for live gospel of the era: the Light Records production captures the room and the band cleanly enough that the album has held up where comparable live gospel records of the period (Edwin Hawkins, James Cleveland) sound dated. That fidelity matters because gospel of this era was so often poorly captured.

##### 2. Kirk Franklin & God's Property — *God's Property* (1997)

The crossover event that defined the late 1990s gospel boom. *God's Property* — credited to Franklin's choir collective rather than to Franklin alone — went triple-platinum on the back of "Stomp," a track that interpolated Funkadelic's "One Nation Under a Groove," featured a Salt-N-Pepa-era Cheryl James verse, and forced gospel onto MTV and BET rotation in a way no record had managed before.

Fantano made *God's Property* a hill to die on in the debate. His argument: this is the moment gospel stopped pretending it wasn't in conversation with hip-hop and R&B production, and Franklin's role as a non-singing bandleader-arranger-hypeman is genuinely novel. The Franklin position — out front of the choir, calling cadence, never holding a melody — is closer to a hip-hop hype-man or a James Brown-style emcee than to any traditional gospel role. That structural innovation is what makes the record matter beyond its sales.

Scaruffi conceded the point grudgingly. His objection was that "Stomp" reduces gospel to dance-floor function, and the rest of the record leans on choir-radio production tricks that age unevenly. But he accepts the historical case: this is the record that opened the door for everything that followed in the late 90s and 2000s, including the Mary Mary debut and the BeBe & CeCe revival.
